The reaction follows the general stoichiometry: $$ \text{R–X} + \text{Nu–H} \longrightarrow \text{R–Nu} + \text{H–X} $$ where: - **R–X** is the alkylating agent (X is the leaving group, e.g., Cl⁻, Br⁻, OSO₃CH₃⁻) - **Nu–H** is the nucleophilic substrate - **R–Nu** is the alkylated product - **H–X** is the by-product (acid) > [!NOTE] > **Pharmaceutical Context:** In drug manufacturing, alkylation is rarely a one-step transformation. It is typically embedded in a multi-step synthetic route to introduce methyl (–CH₃), ethyl (–C₂H₅), benzyl (–CH₂C₆H₅), or isopropyl groups to alter lipophilicity, metabolic stability, receptor binding affinity, and patentable novelty of the drug molecule. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Key-Cutting" Analogy Imagine a master locksmith (the **alkylating agent**) cutting a new key (the **alkyl group**) and fitting it onto a keyring (the **drug scaffold**). - The **locksmith's tool** (leaving group, e.g., Cl⁻) is left behind as a discarded tool bit. - The **keyring** (the nucleophilic site — N, O, S, or C of the drug) decides *where* the new key attaches. - The **shape of the new key** (methyl, ethyl, benzyl) decides *how the drug behaves* in the body — whether it dissolves in fat, resists stomach acid, or binds tightly to its target receptor. Just as a watchmaker selects specific tools to modify watch components, a pharmaceutical chemist selects specific alkylating agents (e.g., dimethyl sulfate for methylation vs. benzyl chloride for benzylation) to fine-tune the drug's pharmacological profile. ### 1.3 Classification of Alkylation Reactions in Pharmaceutical Synthesis | Class | Nucleophile | Product Type | Pharmaceutical Example | |---|---|---|---| | **N-Alkylation** | Amines, amides, imides | Secondary/tertiary amines | Synthesis of **Caffeine** | | **O-Alkylation** | Alcohols, phenols, carboxylates | Ethers, esters | Synthesis of **Phenacetin** | | **C-Alkylation** | Activated aromatics, enolates | Alkylated C-skeletons | Synthesis of **Phenylbutazone** | | **S-Alkylation** | Thiols, thioethers | Thioethers | Synthesis of **Cimetidine** | > [!TIP] > **Memorization Shortcut:** The letters **N-O-C-S** (think: "**NOCS** — like 'knocks'") are the four nucleophilic centers targeted in pharmaceutical alkylation. Of these, **N-alkylation is the most frequently examined** in KTU end-semester papers. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Alkylation **Step 1 — Identification of the Nucleophilic Center** The pharmaceutical chemist first identifies the electron-rich atom in the drug intermediate. The most common nucleophilic centers ranked by reactivity are: 1. **Sulfur (thiolate, RS⁻)** — most reactive 2. **Nitrogen (amine, RNH₂)** — very common 3. **Oxygen (phenoxide, ArO⁻)** — moderate 4. **Carbon (aromatic π-system, enolate)** — requires activation **Step 2 — Selection of the Alkylating Agent** The agent must be reactive enough to alkylate efficiently but selective enough to avoid over-alkylation (e.g., formation of quaternary ammonium salts in N-alkylation). The reactivity follows: $$ \text{CH}_3\text{I} > \text{CH}_3\text{Br} > \text{CH}_3\text{Cl} > (\text{CH}_3)_2\text{SO}_4 $$ **Step 3 — Choice of Reaction Medium** Polar aprotic solvents (acetone, DMF, DMSO) favor **S_N2** pathways and are preferred for methylations. Polar protic solvents (water, ethanol) favor **S_N1** and are used for tertiary alkylations. **Step 4 — Control of Process Parameters** - **Temperature:** Methylations with dimethyl sulfate are run at **60–80 °C**; benzylations at **40–60 °C**. - **pH:** For N-alkylation of amines, pH is held at **8–10** to maintain nucleophilicity of the free amine. - **Molar Ratio:** Alkylating agent is typically used at **1.0–1.2 equivalents** to suppress over-alkylation. **Step 5 — Work-up and Purification** The alkylated product is isolated by **crystallization, distillation, or chromatography** depending on thermal stability and polarity. ### 2.2 Mechanism — The Two Principal Pathways #### 2.2.1 Bimolecular Nucleophilic Substitution (S_N2) — *Concerted, one-step* Used for **primary alkyl halides and dimethyl sulfate**. The nucleophile attacks the carbon from the *backside*, leading to **inversion of configuration** (Walden inversion). $$ \text{Nu}^- + \text{R–CH}_2\text{–X} \longrightarrow [\text{Nu}\cdots\text{CH}_2\cdots\text{X}]^{\ddagger} \longrightarrow \text{Nu–CH}_2\text{–R} + \text{X}^- $$ **Why it matters in pharmaceuticals:** Most drug intermediates are achiral at the alkylation site, so inversion is not a problem. S_N2 is favored for **methylation and ethylation** because of predictable kinetics. #### 2.2.2 Unimolecular Nucleophilic Substitution (S_N1) — *Stepwise, two-stage* Used for **tertiary, benzylic, and allylic systems**. The leaving group departs first, forming a **carbocation intermediate**, which is then attacked by the nucleophile. **Stage 1 (slow, rate-determining):** $$ \text{R}_3\text{C–X} \longrightarrow [\text{R}_3\text{C}^+ \cdots \text{X}^-]^{\ddagger} \longrightarrow \text{R}_3\text{C}^+ + \text{X}^- $$ **Stage 2 (fast):** $$ \text{R}_3\text{C}^+ + \text{Nu}^- \longrightarrow \text{R}_3\text{C–Nu} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Carbocation Rearrangement Trap (KTU Examiner's Pitfall):** In S_N1 alkylations, hydride or methyl shifts can occur, producing **regioisomeric impurities**. For example, attempted alkylation of neopentyl-type systems often yields rearranged products. Always check substrate structure before choosing S_N1 conditions. ### 2.3 Common Pharmaceutical Alkylating Agents — Properties Table | Agent | Formula | Molecular Mass (g/mol) | Boiling Point (°C) | Primary Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Dimethyl sulfate** | (CH₃O)₂SO₂ | 126.13 | 188 (with decomp.) | Methylation (O, N) | | **Methyl iodide** | CH₃I | 141.94 | 42 | Lab-scale methylation | | **Methyl bromide** | CH₃Br | 94.94 | 4 | Industrial methylation (gas) | | **Ethyl bromide** | C₂H₅Br | 108.97 | 38 | Ethylation | | **Benzyl chloride** | C₆H₅CH₂Cl | 126.58 | 179 | N-Benzylation (e.g., penicillin V precursor) | | **Diethyl sulfate** | (C₂H₅O)₂SO₂ | 154.18 | 208 | Ethylation (less toxic than EtI) | | **Isopropyl bromide** | (CH₃)₂CHBr | 122.99 | 59 | Isopropylation | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | **General alkylation** | R–X + NuH → R–Nu + HX | Stoichiometric | | **S_N2 rate law** | rate = k₂ [Nu⁻][R–X] | Second-order overall | | **S_N1 rate law** | rate = k₁ [R–X] | First-order (Nu independent) | | **Selectivity ratio (S_N2)** | k_CH3I / k_CH3Cl ≈ 100 | Methyl iodide is ~100× faster | | **Over-alkylation control** | Use NuH : R–X = 1.0 : 1.0–1.2 | Equivalents (mol basis) | | **Temperature effect (Eyring)** | k = (k_B T/h) · exp(–ΔG‡/RT) | k_B = **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** | | **pKa — nucleophilicity** | Lower pKa conjugate acid ⇒ weaker nucleophile | Used to choose N vs. O alkylation | | **Polarity index (solvent)** | Higher P′ ⇒ favors S_N2 | DMF: 6.4, Ethanol: 5.2 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Alkylation reactor design requires the rate law. For an S_N2 batch reactor operating isothermally, the **second-order integrated rate equation** is used for time-yield calculations — this is a common KTU Part B calculation item. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Integrated Rate Law for S_N2 Alkylation in a Batch Reactor We consider the pharmaceutical methylation of a primary amine with methyl iodide in a stirred batch reactor. Both substrates are consumed in a 1:1 stoichiometry; the reaction is elementary and bimolecular. **Starting point — elementary S_N2:** $$ \text{R–NH}_2 + \text{CH}_3\text{I} \longrightarrow \text{R–NHCH}_3 + \text{HI} $$ Let [A] = [R–NH₂] and [B] = [CH₃I]. With initial concentrations [A]₀ and [B]₀, and equal stoichiometry, the rate of disappearance is: $$ -\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k_2 [A][B] $$ **Step 1 — Apply stoichiometric balance.** For each mole of A consumed, one mole of B is also consumed. Define x = amount reacted at time t: $$ [A] = [A]_0 - x, \quad [B] = [B]_0 - x $$ **Step 2 — Substitute into the rate law:** $$ \frac{dx}{dt} = k_2 ([A]_0 - x)([B]_0 - x) $$ **Step 3 — Separate variables (partial fraction decomposition).** This requires the standard integral: $$ \int \frac{dx}{([A]_0 - x)([B]_0 - x)} = \frac{1}{[B]_0 - [A]_0} \ln\!\left(\frac{[A]_0 - x}{[B]_0 - x} \cdot \frac{[B]_0}{[A]_0}\right) = k_2 t $$ **Step 4 — Rearrange to the classic KTU form:** $$ k_2 \, t = \frac{1}{[A]_0 - [B]_0} \ln\!\left(\frac{[A][B]_0}{[B][A]_0}\right) $$ **Step 5 — Solve for conversion X_A = ([A]₀ – [A]) / [A]₀.** Define r = [B]₀ / [A]₀: $$ k_2 \, [A]_0 \, t = \frac{1}{1 - r} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - X_A}{1 - r X_A}\right) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation for Pharmaceutical Engineers:** This expression lets you predict **batch time** (t) for a target conversion (X_A) at known [A]₀, [B]₀, and k₂ (Arrhenius-obtained). For a 95% conversion (X_A = 0.95) with r = 1.0, the time is t = 19.5 / (k₂[A]₀). Multiply by reactor volume and density to get throughput — directly useful in plant design. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style **Problem (Typical KTU Part B 7-mark sub-question):** *In the N-methylation of theophylline to caffeine (using dimethyl sulfate in DMF at 65 °C), the rate constant is k₂ = 4.2 × 10⁻³ L mol⁻¹ min⁻¹. If the initial concentrations of theophylline and dimethyl sulfate are both 0.20 mol/L, calculate the time required for 90% conversion of theophylline.* **Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify parameters:** - [A]₀ = 0.20 mol/L, [B]₀ = 0.20 mol/L ⇒ r = 1.0 - X_A = 0.90, k₂ = 4.2 × 10⁻³ L mol⁻¹ min⁻¹ **Step 2 — For r = 1.0, the standard form reduces to a simpler second-order expression:** $$ k_2 \, t = \frac{X_A}{[A]_0 (1 - X_A)} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute values:** $$ t = \frac{0.90}{4.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.20 \times (1 - 0.90)} $$ **Step 4 — Compute denominator:** $$ 4.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.20 \times 0.10 = 8.4 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{L mol}^{-1} $$ **Step 5 — Compute t:** $$ t = \frac{0.90}{8.4 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.071 \times 10^{4} \, \text{min} \approx 178.6 \, \text{hours} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examination Pitfall:** Students often forget to use the *reduced* second-order formula when [A]₀ = [B]₀. Using the full logarithmic form gives the same answer only when r ≠ 1. For r = 1, the limit form must be used. Marks are commonly lost here. ### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Alkylation Conditions | Substrate Type | Recommended Agent | Solvent | Mechanism | Temp. Range (°C) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Primary aromatic amine | CH₃I or (CH₃)₂SO₄ | DMF / acetone | S_N2 | 40 – 80 | | Secondary amine | CH₃I (controlled equiv.) | EtOH / MeCN | S_N2 | 20 – 60 | | Phenol (deprotonated) | (CH₃)₂SO₄ | Aqueous NaOH | S_N2 | 50 – 80 | | Activated arene (Friedel–Crafts) | R–Cl + AlCl₃ | CS₂ or CH₂Cl₂ | Electrophilic | 0 – 40 | | Tertiary alcohol → ether | R–X (tertiary) | None (neat) | S_N1 | 25 – 50 | | Thiol | R–Br | EtOH / acetone | S_N2 | 20 – 40 | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 General Alkylation Process Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Substrate
Drug Intermediate] --> B1[Reactor Feed Tank] A2[Alkylating Agent
CH3I or Me2SO4] --> B1 B1 --> C1[Stirred Batch Reactor
with Temp Control] C1 --> D1[Quench Tank
Water or Dilute Acid] D1 --> E1[Extraction Unit
Organic Solvent] E1 --> F1[Drying Tower
Na2SO4 / Molecular Sieves] F1 --> G1[Crystallizer
or Distillation Column] G1 --> H1[Final API
Purity >= 99 percent] C1 -. Heat Exchanger .-> C1 D1 -. Neutralization .-> D1 classDef input fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000 classDef process fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000 classDef output fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 class A1,A2 input class B1,C1,D1,E1,F1,G1 process class H1 output ``` ### 4.2 Mechanistic Decision Flow — S_N1 vs S_N2 ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Step 1: Examine Substrate] --> P1{Is the carbon
primary?} P1 -- Yes --> P2[Step 2: Examine Leaving Group] P1 -- No --> P3{Is the carbon
tertiary or benzylic?} P3 -- Yes --> P4[S_N1 Pathway
Carbocation forms] P3 -- No --> P5{Is the carbon
secondary?} P5 -- Yes --> P6[Step 3: Examine Solvent] P2 --> P6 P6 --> P7{Polar protic
solvent?} P7 -- Yes --> P4 P7 -- No --> P8[S_N2 Pathway
Concerted attack] P4 --> Q[Step 4: Watch for
rearrangements] P8 --> R[Step 4: Watch for
inversion of configuration] Q --> S[Select Reaction Conditions] R --> S classDef decision fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 classDef pathway fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 class P1,P3,P5,P7 decision class P4,P8 pathway ``` ### 4.3 Industrial Example — Caffeine Synthesis Route Map ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Xanthine
Parent Core] --> B[Step 1: N-Methylation
CH3I or Me2SO4] B --> C[7-Methylxanthine] C --> D[Step 2: N-Methylation
Position 3] D --> E[Theobromine] E --> F[Step 3: N-Methylation
Position 1] F --> G[Caffeine
1,3,7-Trimethylxanthine] G --> H[Purification
Crystallization from Water] H --> I[Pharmaceutical Grade
Caffeine] classDef process fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 classDef product fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 class B,D,F process class A,C,E,G,H,I product ``` ### 4.4 Energy Profile — S_N1 vs S_N2 Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SN2[S_N2 Profile - Single Transition State] R2[Reactants
R-Nu + R-X] --> TS2[Transition State
Nu...C...X] TS2 --> P2[Product
Nu-R + X minus] end subgraph SN1[S_N1 Profile - Two Transition States] R1[Reactants] --> TS1a[TS1 - Ionization] TS1a --> INT[Carbocation
Intermediate] INT --> TS1b[TS2 - Nucleophilic Attack] TS1b --> P1[Product] end classDef sn2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 classDef sn1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 classDef peak fill:#fff176,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 class R2,P2 sn2 class R1,P1 sn1 class TS2,TS1a,TS1b,INT peak ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *Define alkylation. Give two examples of alkylating agents commonly used in pharmaceutical industry.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Alkylation is the chemical conversion process in which an alkyl group (R–) is transferred from an alkylating agent (R–X) to a nucleophilic substrate (Nu–H) to form a new C–Nu bond, releasing H–X as a by-product. (2 marks) Two examples of pharmaceutical alkylating agents: 1. **Dimethyl sulfate** (CH₃O)₂SO₂ — used for methylation of phenols and amines (e.g., in the synthesis of codeine from morphine). (½ mark) 2. **Benzyl chloride** C₆H₅CH₂Cl — used for N-benzylation in penicillin V precursor manufacture. (½ mark) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *Distinguish between S_N1 and S_N2 mechanisms with respect to kinetics and stereochemistry.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | S_N1 | S_N2 | |---|---|---| | **Kinetics** | First-order; rate = k[R–X] (1 mark) | Second-order; rate = k[Nu][R–X] (1 mark) | | **Stereochemistry** | Racemization (planar carbocation) (½ mark) | Inversion of configuration (Walden) (½ mark) | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Pattern #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]* **(a)** With a suitable reaction scheme, explain the **N-alkylation of theophylline to caffeine** using dimethyl sulfate. Discuss the role of solvent and temperature. (7 marks) **(b)** Derive the integrated rate law for a **second-order S_N2 alkylation** in a batch reactor. A pharmaceutical N-methylation has [A]₀ = 0.30 mol/L, [B]₀ = 0.40 mol/L, k₂ = 2.5 × 10⁻³ L mol⁻¹ min⁻¹. Calculate the time for 80% conversion of A. (7 marks) #### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** Theophylline (1,3-dimethylxanthine) undergoes N-methylation at the N-7 position to yield caffeine. The reaction is performed in **dimethylformamide (DMF)** as solvent and **dimethyl sulfate (DMS)** as the methylating agent. **Reaction Scheme:** $$ \text{Theophylline} + (\text{CH}_3\text{O})_2\text{SO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{DMF, 60–80 °C, 4 h}} \text{Caffeine} + \text{CH}_3\text{OSO}_3\text{H} $$ **Step-by-step explanation:** 1. **Substrate activation (1 mark):** The N-7 position of theophylline is a secondary amine nitrogen; in the imidazole ring, it is more nucleophilic than N-1 or N-3 (which are already methylated). DMF solvates the cationic intermediates and accelerates the S_N2 step. 2. **Methylation step (2 marks):** DMS acts as a methyl donor through S_N2 attack. The methyl carbon is attacked from the back by the lone pair of N-7. The leaving group is methyl sulfate (CH₃OSO₃⁻), a weak base and excellent leaving group. 3. **Role of solvent (2 marks):** DMF is a polar aprotic solvent (dielectric constant = 36.7). It dissolves the partially protonated theophylline salt (formed by adding K₂CO₃ as base) and does *not* hydrogen-bond to the nucleophile, keeping it highly reactive. Aqueous solvents would protonate N-7 and kill nucleophilicity. 4. **Role of temperature (2 marks):** Reaction is conducted at **60–80 °C**. Lower temperatures (<50 °C) give incomplete conversion in acceptable time; higher temperatures (>100 °C) cause DMS decomposition and over-methylation at theobromine. The Arrhenius dependence of k₂ in this range gives a doubling of rate per ~10 °C rise. **Yield and purity:** The industrial yield is 85–92%, and the product is crystallized from hot water to pharmaceutical grade (≥ 99.0% by HPLC). #### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** **Derivation (4 marks):** For an elementary S_N2 reaction A + B → products, with [A]₀ ≠ [B]₀: $$ -\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k_2 [A][B] $$ Let x = extent of reaction: [A] = [A]₀ – x, [B] = [B]₀ – x. $$ \frac{dx}{dt} = k_2 ([A]_0 - x)([B]_0 - x) $$ Separating variables and integrating (from 0 to x, 0 to t): $$ k_2 t = \frac{1}{[B]_0 - [A]_0} \ln\!\left(\frac{[A]_0([B]_0 - x)}{[B]_0([A]_0 - x)}\right) $$ In terms of conversion X_A = x/[A]₀: $$ k_2 [A]_0 t = \frac{1}{1 - r} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - X_A}{1 - r X_A}\right), \quad r = \frac{[B]_0}{[A]_0} $$ **Numerical Solution (3 marks):** Given: [A]₀ = 0.30 mol/L, [B]₀ = 0.40 mol/L, k₂ = 2.5 × 10⁻³ L mol⁻¹ min⁻¹, X_A = 0.80. - r = 0.40 / 0.30 = 1.3333 - 1 – r X_A = 1 – (1.3333)(0.80) = 1 – 1.0667 = –0.0667 - 1 – X_A = 0.20 - (1 – X_A) / (1 – r X_A) = 0.20 / (–0.0667) = –2.998 ≈ –3.0 - ln(–3.0) is invalid — this signals that 80% conversion is **physically unattainable** because [B]₀ is less than the amount of A that must be consumed. Maximum possible X_A is [B]₀/[A]₀ = 1.333, but we need [A]₀·X_A = 0.24 mol/L consumed, while only 0.40 – 0.10 = 0.30 mol/L of B is initially available (final [B] would be 0.30 – 0.24 = 0.06 mol/L — actually possible). **Re-checking:** - 1 – rX_A = 1 – (0.40/0.30)(0.80) = 1 – 1.0667 = **–0.0667** The negative argument arises because 80% × r = 1.067 > 1, meaning the **reactant B would have to become negative**, which is impossible. - **Physical limit:** X_A(max) = 1/r = [A]₀/[B]₀ = 0.30/0.40 = 0.75 ⇒ only **75% conversion** is possible. **Re-calculated time for X_A = 0.75 (achievable maximum):** - 1 – r X_A = 1 – 1.333 × 0.75 = 1 – 1.0 = 0 - The expression diverges → infinite time. Hence X_A = 0.75 is the *stoichiometric limit* requiring infinite time. **For a feasible X_A = 0.70:** - 1 – rX_A = 1 – 1.333 × 0.70 = 1 – 0.9333 = 0.0667 - (1 – X_A)/(1 – r X_A) = 0.30 / 0.0667 = 4.50 - ln(4.50) = 1.5041 - (1 – r) = 1 – 1.333 = –0.333 - k₂ [A]₀ t = 1.5041 / (–0.333) = –4.516 This is **negative**, indicating a sign convention error. The correct expression when [B]₀ > [A]₀ is: $$ k_2 [A]_0 t = \frac{1}{r - 1} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - X_A}{1 - r X_A}\right) \quad \text{(with } r > 1\text{)} $$ - (r – 1) = 0.333 - k₂ [A]₀ t = 1.5041 / 0.333 = 4.516 - t = 4.516 / (2.5 × 10⁻³ × 0.30) = 4.516 / 7.5 × 10⁻⁴ = **6022 min ≈ 100.4 hours** (for X_A = 0.70) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Critical Pitfall):** > 1. **Sign convention in the rate expression:** Students lose 1–2 marks by using the wrong sign when [B]₀ > [A]₀. Always check: if r > 1, the denominator must be (r – 1), not (1 – r). > 2. **Stoichiometric feasibility check:** Many students blindly plug in any X_A without verifying that 1 – r X_A > 0. The examiner awards 1 mark for *identifying* the feasibility limit explicitly. > 3. **Units audit:** k₂ must be in L mol⁻¹ min⁻¹ (or equivalent); if converted to seconds, also convert t to seconds. Mixing units costs full credit. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** Compare **N-alkylation vs. O-alkylation** in terms of nucleophilicity, conditions, and pharmaceutical applications. Give one named drug example for each. (7 marks) **(b)** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **industrial manufacture of phenacetin** starting from *p*-aminophenol. Identify the alkylating agent and write the balanced equation. Discuss the role of the base used. (7 marks) #### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** **N-Alkylation (3.5 marks):** - **Nucleophilicity:** Nitrogen (sp³ amine) is more nucleophilic than oxygen of alcohols/phenols at neutral pH due to lower electronegativity and higher polarizability. - **Conditions:** Polar aprotic solvents (DMF, DMSO), 40–80 °C, with a mild base (K₂CO₃, NaHCO₃) to keep the amine deprotonated. Alkylating agents: CH₃I, (CH₃)₂SO₄, benzyl chloride. - **Pharmaceutical example:** **Caffeine** synthesis — N-methylation of theophylline. **O-Alkylation (3.5 marks):** - **Nucleophilicity:** Oxygen of alcohols is *less* nucleophilic than N of amines; phenoxide (ArO⁻) is comparable to amine N. O-alkylation often requires deprotonation first (NaOH, NaH, or K₂CO₃). - **Conditions:** Aqueous alkali (for phenols) or anhydrous conditions with NaH/DMF (for sensitive alcohols). Alkylating agents: (CH₃)₂SO₄, CH₃Cl, ethyl bromide. - **Pharmaceutical example:** **Phenacetin** synthesis — O-ethylation of *p*-hydroxyacetanilide with diethyl sulfate. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** **Balanced equation (2 marks):** $$ \text{p-Hydroxyacetanilide} + (\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{O})_2\text{SO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH, H}_2\text{O, 60 °C}} \text{Phenacetin} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OSO}_3\text{H} $$ **Step-by-step process (4 marks):** 1. *p*-Aminophenol is first **acetylated** with acetic anhydride to give *p*-hydroxyacetanilide (protects the amine from over-alkylation). 2. The phenolic –OH is **deprotonated** by NaOH (the base) to form the phenoxide ion, which is a much stronger nucleophile than the neutral phenol. 3. **Diethyl sulfate** (the alkylating agent) is added slowly at **60 °C** under agitation. S_N2 attack of phenoxide on the ethyl carbon releases ethyl sulfate anion. 4. The mixture is **acidified** to neutralize excess base, and phenacetin crystallizes on cooling. It is recrystallized from aqueous ethanol to **pharmaceutical grade**. **Role of base (1 mark):** NaOH converts the weakly nucleophilic phenol (pKa ≈ 10) to the highly nucleophilic phenoxide (pKa of conjugate acid ≈ –7), increasing the rate of S_N2 by a factor of **10⁶–10⁸**. It also neutralizes the H₂SO₄ by-product, preventing acid-catalyzed decomposition of the product. **Flow diagram (already covered in Section 4.1 of these notes — refer to the same Mermaid block for reactor configuration).** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Q.B specific):** > - Students frequently **forget to write the protecting step (acetylation)** of the amine before O-alkylation. Marks are reserved for showing the *complete* route, not just the alkylation step. > - **Balancing the equation with charge states** (showing phenoxide as the nucleophile, not phenol) earns 1 extra mark and demonstrates conceptual clarity. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):** - ☐ Alkylation = transfer of R– from R–X to a nucleophile (N, O, C, S). - ☐ Two main mechanisms: **S_N2** (concerted, primary substrates, inversion) and **S_N1** (stepwise, tertiary/benzylic, racemization). - ☐ Pharmaceutical alkylating agents in order of reactivity: **CH₃I > (CH₃)₂SO₄ > CH₃Br > CH₃Cl**. - ☐ Polar aprotic solvents (DMF, DMSO) favor S_N2; polar protic (H₂O, EtOH) favor S_N1. - ☐ N-Alkylation: most common in pharma; **over-alkylation** controlled by stoichiometry (1.0–1.2 eq.). - ☐ O-Alkylation: requires deprotonation (phenoxide); example: **phenacetin** from *p*-hydroxyacetanilide + diethyl sulfate. - ☐ C-Alkylation: requires activated substrate (enolate or Friedel–Crafts conditions). - ☐ For [A]₀ = [B]₀, use the **reduced** second-order form: k₂t = X_A / ([A]₀(1 – X_A)). - ☐ For [A]₀ ≠ [B]₀, use the **logarithmic form** with sign caution: denominator is ([B]₀ – [A]₀). - ☐ Stoichiometric feasibility: X_A(max) = [B]₀ / [A]₀ when [B]₀ < [A]₀; 1 – r X_A must stay positive. - ☐ KTU expects: mechanism (2 marks) + reagent/solvent choice (2 marks) + numerical (3 marks) in a 7-mark sub-question. - ☐ Named drug examples to remember: **Caffeine** (N-alkylation), **Phenacetin** (O-alkylation), **Phenylbutazone** (C-alkylation), **Cimetidine** (S-alkylation). --- > **End of Module 1 Notes — Chemical Conversion Processes: Alkylation** > *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | PECHT751 | Outcome-Based Education Framework*

topics.title: Carboxylation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Carboxylation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Carboxylation** is a fundamental chemical transformation in pharmaceutical chemistry defined as the introduction of a **carboxyl functional group (–COOH)** into an organic substrate molecule. The reaction is broadly classified as a *functional group interconversion* and may be achieved through chemical, electrochemical, or biochemical (enzymatic) pathways. In the context of **KTU PECHT751 – Drugs and Pharmaceuticals Technology**, the term carries dual operational meaning:

1. **Synthetic Carboxylation** – An industrial unit reaction used to manufacture drug intermediates (e.g., the **Kolbe–Schmitt reaction** for salicylic acid, a key precursor to aspirin).
2. **Metabolic / Phase-I Carboxylation** – A *biotransformation pathway* in drug metabolism where xenobiotics acquire –COOH groups to enhance renal excretion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 1 coverage of *Carboxylation* is most frequently tested via the **Kolbe–Schmitt Reaction**, the synthesis of **Salicylic Acid**, and its downstream conversion to **Acetylsalicylic Acid (Aspirin)**. Memorize the catalyst, pressure range, temperature, and regioselectivity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a drug molecule as a **wooden key** designed to fit a biological **lock (receptor/enzyme)**. Now, the key may be made of *hydrophobic wood* — it does not dissolve well in the watery bloodstream. By attaching a **–COOH "molecular hook"** to the key, the chemist transforms the molecule into a **water-compatible, ionizable form** that can travel through plasma, bind plasma proteins reversibly, and eventually be filtered by the kidneys. **Carboxylation is, therefore, the chemist's way of giving a drug molecule a "swimming lesson."**

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **pKa of –COOH group** | **≈ 4.0 – 5.0** | Determines ionization at physiological pH (7.4) |
| **Bond energy C=O (carbonyl)** | **745 kJ/mol** | Explains thermodynamic stability of carboxyl group |
| **Bond energy C–O (hydroxyl)** | **358 kJ/mol** | Cleaved during esterification (aspirin synthesis) |
| **Kolbe–Schmitt CO₂ pressure** | **5 – 7 atm** | Standard industrial carboxylation pressure |
| **Kolbe–Schmitt temperature** | **120 – 125 °C** | Sodium phenoxide dry-state carboxylation window |
| **Molecular weight –COOH** | **45.02 g/mol** | Used in stoichiometric calculations |

> [!NOTE]
> The carboxyl group exists in **equilibrium** between the *protonated (–COOH)* and *deprotonated (–COO⁻)* forms. At blood pH 7.4, **>99% of acidic drug molecules are ionized**, a principle exploited in Henderson–Hasselbalch calculations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Carboxylation Reactions

Carboxylation reactions are categorized based on the **carbon substrate type** and the **mechanistic pathway** adopted:

- **Electrophilic Aromatic Carboxylation** – e.g., Kolbe–Schmitt reaction on phenols → hydroxybenzoic acids.
- **Nucleophilic Carboxylation** – Grignard reagents + CO₂ → carboxylic acids.
- **Electrochemical Carboxylation (Electrocarboxylation)** – Cathodic reduction of organic substrates in CO₂-saturated electrolytes.
- **Enzymatic Carboxylation** – Biotin-dependent carboxylases (e.g., pyruvate carboxylase, acetyl-CoA carboxylase).
- **Reppe Carbonylation (with carboxylation variant)** – Transition-metal-catalyzed addition of CO₂ to alkenes/alkynes.

### 2.2 The Kolbe–Schmitt Reaction – Operational Logic

The **Kolbe–Schmitt reaction** is the most industrially significant carboxylation in pharmaceutical manufacturing. The operational logic unfolds in five structured steps:

1. **Substrate Activation:** Sodium phenoxide is generated by treating phenol with aqueous NaOH.
2. **Drying:** Water is removed to yield *anhydrous* sodium phenoxide — critical, since water suppresses the reaction.
3. **CO₂ Absorption:** Dry sodium phenoxide absorbs CO₂ under **5–7 atm pressure** at **120–125 °C**, forming *sodium salicylate*.
4. **Acidification:** The sodium salt is acidified with mineral acid (H₂SO₄) to liberate *salicylic acid*.
5. **Purification:** Recrystallization from hot water yields pure salicylic acid (m.p. **158–160 °C**).

### 2.3 Why Dry Conditions Are Critical

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Anhydrous Conditions:** Water molecules compete with phenoxide ions for CO₂ solvation, forming H₂CO₃ (carbonic acid) instead of the desired C–C bond. This reduces yield from ~90% (dry) to <30% (wet). Industrial Kolbe–Schmitt reactors are therefore equipped with **dehydration trays** above the reaction bed.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Henderson–Hasselbalch | $\mathrm{pH} = \mathrm{pK_a} + \log \frac{[\mathrm{A^-}]}{[\mathrm{HA}]}$ | Ionization of drug –COOH groups |
| Carboxylation stoichiometry | $\mathrm{ArONa} + \mathrm{CO_2} \rightarrow \mathrm{Ar(OH)COONa}$ | Kolbe–Schmitt mass balance |
| Aspirin synthesis | $\mathrm{Salicylic\,acid + Ac_2O \rightarrow Aspirin + CH_3COOH}$ | Acetylation step |
| Theoretical yield | $\mathrm{Yield\,\%} = \frac{\mathrm{Actual}}{\mathrm{Theoretical}} \times 100$ | Industrial QA/QC |
| Degree of ionization | $\alpha = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(\mathrm{pK_a} - \mathrm{pH})}}$ | Drug absorption studies |
| Salicylic acid pKa | $\mathrm{pK_a} = 2.97$ | Acid strength reference |
| Aspirin pKa | $\mathrm{pK_a} = 3.5$ | Acid strength reference |

### 2.5 Real-World Pharmaceutical Utility

- **NSAID Synthesis:** Salicylic acid → Aspirin → modern NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen all contain –COOH).
- **Prodrug Design:** Ester prodrugs of carboxylic acids (e.g., enalapril → enalaprilat) leverage carboxylate bioactivation.
- **Pharmacokinetics:** Carboxylation by hepatic cytochrome P450 enzymes is a primary *Phase-I clearance pathway* for many drugs.
- **Chiral Resolution:** Carboxylate salts (e.g., tartaric acid, mandelic acid) are used to resolve racemic drug mixtures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Mechanism of the Kolbe–Schmitt Reaction (Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution)

The Kolbe–Schmitt reaction proceeds via an **electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS)** pathway. Below is the exhaustive, step-by-step electronic mechanism:

**Step 1 — Generation of the phenoxide nucleophile:**

Phenol is deprotonated by sodium hydroxide to form the resonance-stabilized **sodium phenoxide**.

$$\mathrm{C_6H_5OH + NaOH \rightarrow C_6H_5O^-Na^+ + H_2O}$$

The phenoxide anion is a strong *ortho/para director* due to the negative charge being delocalized onto the ortho and para carbons via resonance.

**Step 2 — Drying of sodium phenoxide:**

Water is removed by heating under vacuum. This is a *thermodynamic prerequisite*, not merely a procedural one.

$$\mathrm{C_6H_5O^-Na^+ \xrightarrow{100-110\,°C,\,vacuum} \text{dry } C_6H_5O^-Na^+}$$

**Step 3 — Nucleophilic attack on CO₂ (the carboxylation step):**

The *electron-rich ortho carbon* of phenoxide attacks the **electrophilic carbon of CO₂** (which bears a partial positive charge due to polarization, $\delta^+$ on C and $\delta^-$ on each O).

$$\mathrm{C_6H_5O^-Na^+ + CO_2 \rightarrow \text{sodium } o\text{-hydroxybenzoate (chelate complex)}}$$

This intermediate is a **six-membered chelate** in which Na⁺ is coordinated to both the phenoxide oxygen and the new carboxylate oxygen — this chelation is the **driving force** for *ortho-selectivity*.

**Step 4 — Acidification to liberate the free acid:**

$$\mathrm{Ar(OH)COONa + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow Ar(OH)COOH + NaHSO_4}$$

The free **salicylic acid** is precipitated, filtered, and recrystallized from hot water.

**Step 5 — Acetylation to Aspirin (downstream pharmaceutical reaction):**

$$\mathrm{C_6H_4(OH)COOH + (CH_3CO)_2O \xrightarrow{H^+} C_6H_4(OCOCH_3)COOH + CH_3COOH}$$

Acetic anhydride acetylates the phenolic –OH, blocking the gastric-irritant activity and producing **acetylsalicylic acid (Aspirin)**.

### 3.2 Regioselectivity Derivation — *Ortho* vs. *Para* Carboxylation

The thermodynamic product of Kolbe–Schmitt is governed by the **stability of the intermediate chelate**:

- **Ortho pathway** → forms a *6-membered cyclic chelate* stabilized by Na⁺ coordination.
- **Para pathway** → no such chelation possible; transition state energy is higher.

The activation energy difference is approximately:

$$\Delta E_a \approx 8 - 12 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

favoring ortho substitution. Under forcing conditions (potassium salt, higher temperature), the para-isomer (**p-hydroxybenzoic acid**) becomes dominant — this is the basis for industrial branching.

### 3.3 Worked Industrial Mass-Balance Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A pharmaceutical plant reacts **500 kg of phenol** (M = 94.11 g/mol) with NaOH and CO₂ via the Kolbe–Schmitt reaction. The actual yield of salicylic acid (M = 138.12 g/mol) recovered is **580 kg**. Calculate (a) theoretical yield, (b) percent yield.

**Step 1 — Moles of phenol:**

$$n_{\mathrm{phenol}} = \frac{500{,}000 \text{ g}}{94.11 \text{ g/mol}} = 5313.56 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric conversion (1:1):**

From the balanced equation, 1 mol phenol → 1 mol salicylic acid.

$$n_{\mathrm{theoretical}} = 5313.56 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical mass:**

$$m_{\mathrm{theoretical}} = 5313.56 \times 138.12 = 733{,}930 \text{ g} \approx 733.93 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Percent yield:**

$$\mathrm{Yield\,\%} = \frac{580}{733.93} \times 100 = 79.0\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** When asked to *comment on yield*, students must mention the *losses due to* (i) para-isomer formation (~10%), (ii) decarboxylation side reaction at >130 °C, and (iii) incomplete acidification. A bare 79% figure without explanation attracts a **2-mark penalty**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Block Diagram — Industrial Kolbe–Schmitt Manufacturing of Salicylic Acid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Phenol Storage Tank] --> B1[NaOH Dissolution Reactor]
    B1 --> C1[Vacuum Dryer - 100-110 C]
    C1 --> D1[Carboxylation Autoclave - 125 C, 5-7 atm CO2]
    D1 --> E1[Cooling and Discharge]
    E1 --> F1[Acidification Vessel - H2SO4]
    F1 --> G1[Filtration Unit]
    G1 --> H1[Recrystallization - Hot Water]
    H1 --> I1[Pure Salicylic Acid - m.p. 158-160 C]
    D1 --> J1[By-product: p-Hydroxybenzoic acid - para-isomer]
    F1 --> K1[Wastewater Treatment - Na2SO4 removal]
    I1 --> L1[Aspirin Synthesis Reactor - Ac2O / H plus]
    L1 --> M1[Crude Aspirin Crystallizer]
    M1 --> N1[Final API - Acetylsalicylic Acid]

    style A1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style D1 fill:#ffe1e1
    style I1 fill:#d4edda
    style N1 fill:#fff3cd
    style J1 fill:#f8d7da
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Carboxylation Pathways in Drug Discovery

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Synthetic[Synthetic Carboxylation Routes]
        S1[Kolbe Schmitt - Phenol plus CO2] --> S2[Salicylic Acid]
        S3[Grignard plus CO2] --> S4[Carboxylic Acid]
        S5[Electrocarboxylation] --> S4
        S2 --> S6[Aspirin NSAID]
        S4 --> S7[Drug Intermediates]
    end

    subgraph Biological[Biological Carboxylation]
        B1[Pyruvate Carboxylase - Biotin] --> B2[Oxaloacetate - TCA cycle]
        B3[Acetyl CoA Carboxylase] --> B4[Malonyl CoA - Fatty acid synthesis]
        B5[CYP450 Phase I] --> B6[Hydroxy then Carboxylate metabolite]
    end

    subgraph Pharma[Pharmaceutical Applications]
        P1[NSAID family - Aspirin Ibuprofen Naproxen]
        P2[ACE inhibitors - Enalapril Lisinopril]
        P3[Statins - Atorvastatin Lovastatin]
        P4[Prodrug esters - Bioactivation]
        P6[Chiral resolving agents - Tartaric Mandelic acids]
    end

    S2 --> P1
    S4 --> P2
    S4 --> P3
    S4 --> P4
    B6 --> P1
    B6 --> P2
    S4 --> P6

    style Synthetic fill:#f0f8ff
    style Biological fill:#fff8dc
    style Pharma fill:#e6f7e6
```

### 4.3 Mechanistic Cycle — Kolbe–Schmitt Six-Membered Chelate Intermediate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Step 1: Phenoxide formation] --> M2[Step 2: Drying]
    M2 --> M3[Step 3: ortho attack on CO2]
    M3 --> M4[Step 4: Six-membered Na chelate intermediate]
    M4 --> M5[Step 5: Acidification]
    M5 --> M6[Step 6: Salicylic acid - ortho product]
    M3 --> M7[Minor: para attack]
    M7 --> M8[p-Hydroxybenzoic acid - para byproduct]

    style M1 fill:#cfe2ff
    style M4 fill:#ffe1e1
    style M6 fill:#d4edda
    style M8 fill:#f8d7da
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define carboxylation. Mention any two pharmaceutical drugs whose synthesis involves a carboxylation step.*

**Model Answer:**
Carboxylation is a chemical reaction in which a **carboxyl group (–COOH)** is introduced into an organic substrate, typically through reaction with CO₂ or a carboxylating agent under catalytic conditions.
*Pharmaceutical examples:*
1. **Salicylic acid** (synthesized via Kolbe–Schmitt reaction from phenol + CO₂), a precursor to **Aspirin**.
2. **Ibuprofen** — final step involves catalytic carboxylation of an aryl carbon.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for example 1, 1 for example 2.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is the Kolbe–Schmitt reaction carried out under strictly anhydrous conditions?*

**Model Answer:**
The Kolbe–Schmitt reaction proceeds via a sodium phenoxide intermediate. In the presence of water, two detrimental effects occur:
1. **Reverse hydrolysis:** Water shifts the equilibrium *ArO⁻ + CO₂ ⇌ ArO–COO⁻* back toward phenoxide, lowering yield.
2. **CO₂ solvation:** Water hydrates CO₂ to H₂CO₃, which is non-electrophilic and does not participate in C–C bond formation.
Anhydrous conditions maintain the integrity of the **six-membered Na⁺ chelate intermediate**, which is essential for *ortho-selectivity* and high yield (~80–90%).

*(3 marks: 1 for stating the requirement, 1 each for the two mechanistic reasons.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A [14 Marks] — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**
**(a)** Describe the **Kolbe–Schmitt reaction** in detail with reference to the synthesis of **salicylic acid**. Discuss the reaction conditions, mechanism, and the role of the sodium phenoxide intermediate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** How is **salicylic acid converted to aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid)**? Write the reaction, mechanism, and explain why aspirin is less irritating to the gastric mucosa than salicylic acid. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Answer — Part (a):**

1. **Reaction:** Sodium phenoxide + CO₂ → sodium salicylate (which on acidification gives salicylic acid).
2. **Conditions:** NaOH as base, CO₂ pressure = **5–7 atm**, temperature = **120–125 °C**, *anhydrous conditions*.
3. **Mechanism (EAS):**
   - Phenoxide ion is the nucleophile.
   - *Ortho*-carbon attacks the electrophilic C of CO₂.
   - A **6-membered Na⁺ chelate** is formed as the key intermediate.
   - The chelate determines *ortho* regioselectivity.
4. **Role of Na⁺:** Coordinates with both the phenoxide oxygen and the incoming carboxylate oxygen, lowering the transition-state energy by ~10 kJ/mol and locking the geometry for *ortho* attack.
5. **Industrial yield:** ~75–85% based on phenol.

#### **Model Answer — Part (b):**

1. **Reaction:**

$$\mathrm{Salicylic\,acid + (CH_3CO)_2O \xrightarrow{H_2SO_4} Aspirin + CH_3COOH}$$

2. **Mechanism (Nucleophilic Acyl Substitution):**
   - The phenolic –OH attacks acetic anhydride's carbonyl carbon.
   - Tetrahedral intermediate forms.
   - Phenoxide leaves, picking up H⁺ to give free acetic acid.
3. **Gastric Tolerance Explanation:**
   - Free –OH of salicylic acid is **directly irritating** to gastric mucosa and also inhibits the protective prostaglandin synthesis locally.
   - **Acetylation caps the –OH** group, preventing this local irritant effect.
   - The acetyl group is hydrolyzed *only after* absorption, releasing salicylic acid systemically.

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks] — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Explain the **classification of carboxylation reactions** with suitable examples. Discuss the role of the carboxyl group in drug–receptor interactions and pharmacokinetics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a short note on **enzymatic carboxylation** with reference to biotin-dependent carboxylases. Give two examples of drugs whose metabolism involves carboxylation as a Phase-I biotransformation. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Answer — Part (a):**

1. **Classification:**
   - *Electrophilic* — Kolbe–Schmitt (EAS on phenols).
   - *Nucleophilic* — Grignard + CO₂.
   - *Electrochemical* — Cathodic reduction in CO₂-saturated media.
   - *Reppe-type* — Metal-catalyzed addition of CO₂ to π-systems.
2. **Drug–Receptor Role:**
   - The –COOH group forms **ionic / hydrogen bonds** with basic amino acid residues (Lys, Arg, His) at the receptor site — a key anchoring interaction in NSAIDs (COX-1/COX-2 active site contains Arg120 and Tyr355).
3. **Pharmacokinetic Role:**
   - Ionization at physiological pH improves water solubility.
   - **Plasma protein binding** (~98% for acidic drugs like warfarin) is mediated by albumin recognition of –COO⁻.
   - **Renal excretion** of ionized carboxylates is efficient.

#### **Model Answer — Part (b):**

1. **Biotin-Dependent Enzymatic Carboxylation:**
   - Biotin (vitamin B7) acts as a *mobile carboxyl carrier*.
   - ATP-driven carboxylation of biotin's ureido ring yields *carboxybiotin*.
   - Carboxybiotin transfers –COO⁻ to the substrate's nucleophilic carbon.
2. **Examples of Enzymes:** *Pyruvate carboxylase* (pyruvate → oxaloacetate), *Acetyl-CoA carboxylase* (acetyl-CoA → malonyl-CoA).
3. **Drug Metabolism Examples:**
   - **Diazepam** undergoes CYP3A4-mediated carboxylation to form *temazepam* (active metabolite).
   - **Losartan** is metabolized to *EXP3174*, a carboxylic acid metabolite with stronger AT₁ receptor antagonism.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently lose marks on this topic for the following reasons:
> 1. **Confusing *carboxylation* with *carbonylation*.** Carbonylation inserts *C=O* (without –OH). Carboxylation inserts *–COOH*. KTU examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for this interchange.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify ortho vs. para selectivity** in the Kolbe–Schmitt mechanism.
> 3. **Omitting the role of Na⁺ chelation** — the chelate is the *core mechanistic feature*, not an optional detail.
> 4. **Not balancing the aspirin synthesis equation** — unbalanced equations cost 1 mark.
> 5. **Writing the aspirin mechanism as SN2** — it is *Nucleophilic Acyl Substitution (Addition–Elimination)*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Carboxylation = introduction of **–COOH** group; distinct from carbonylation.
- **Kolbe–Schmitt Reaction:** Phenol + NaOH + CO₂ (5–7 atm, 120–125 °C, dry) → *ortho*-hydroxybenzoic acid (salicylic acid).
- **Catalyst/Base:** NaOH (sodium salt) → ortho-product; KOH (potassium salt) → para-product.
- **Mechanism Type:** Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution (EAS) with a **6-membered Na⁺ chelate intermediate**.
- **Anhydrous Condition:** Mandatory; water destroys CO₂ electrophilicity and reverses equilibrium.
- **Industrial Yield:** ~80%; major loss is para-isomer and decarboxylation at >130 °C.
- **Aspirin Synthesis:** Salicylic acid + acetic anhydride + catalytic H₂SO₄ → acetylsalicylic acid.
- **Aspirin Advantage:** Acetylation of phenolic –OH reduces gastric irritation.
- **Enzymatic Carboxylation:** Biotin is the cofactor; ATP-dependent; examples — pyruvate carboxylase, acetyl-CoA carboxylase.
- **Metabolic Carboxylation:** Phase-I biotransformation; examples — diazepam, losartan, ibuprofen.
- **pKa Range:** Drug –COOH groups typically pKa = 3–5; >99% ionized at blood pH 7.4.
- **Key Formula:** Henderson–Hasselbalch $\mathrm{pH} = \mathrm{pK_a} + \log \frac{[\mathrm{A^-}]}{[\mathrm{HA}]}$ is the most-tested equation.
- **Pharma Importance:** Carboxyl group is the **most common ionizable handle** in drug design, present in ~25% of all marketed drugs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Condensation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Condensation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Condensation** is a unit operation in chemical engineering involving the phase transition of a substance from its **vapor (gaseous) state** into its **liquid state** by the removal of **latent heat of vaporization**. In pharmaceutical and chemical process industries, condensation is a critical mass and heat transfer operation employed for solvent recovery, product purification, reflux generation in distillation columns, and recovery of valuable condensates from reaction off-gases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Condensation belongs to the family of **phase-change heat transfer** operations. It is governed by the simultaneous transfer of **mass and heat** across a vapor–liquid interface, driven by a temperature gradient between the bulk vapor (saturated, at $T_{sat}$) and the cooler condensing surface (at $T_w$).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Think of condensation as **"the sweat on a cold glass of water on a hot summer day."**

When warm, humid air (carrying water vapor) comes into contact with the cold outer surface of a water glass, the air near the glass cools down. Since cold air cannot "hold" as much moisture as warm air, the water vapor molecules lose energy, slow down, and clump together to form tiny liquid droplets — that is **condensation** in action.

In a pharmaceutical plant, the same physics occurs inside the **shell-and-tube condensers** of a solvent recovery train:
- **Warm solvent vapor** (e.g., methanol, ethanol, acetone, IPA) leaves the top of a distillation column.
- It enters the condenser where it meets a **cool cooling medium** (water, brine, or glycol) flowing on the other side of the tubes.
- The solvent vapor gives up its **latent heat** to the coolant, condenses back to liquid, and is collected for reuse.

## 1.3 Two Physical Modes of Condensation

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Classification (Nusselt, 1916):**
> Condensation on a cooled surface can occur in **two distinct physical modes**, each with drastically different heat transfer rates.

| Mode | Description | Heat Transfer Coefficient ($h$) |
|------|-------------|----------------------------------|
| **Filmwise Condensation** | Liquid wets the surface and forms a continuous film that grows thicker under gravity | **Low** (resistance increases with film thickness) |
| **Dropwise Condensation** | Liquid forms discrete droplets that grow, coalesce, and roll off, exposing fresh surface | **Very High** (often **5–10×** higher than filmwise) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter in pharma?**
> In API manufacturing, **dropwise condensation** is desirable because it allows for more compact condensers and lower coolant consumption. Surface treatments (e.g., silicone coatings, PTFE, or noble metal plating) are often used to **promote dropwise condensation** and enhance energy efficiency.

## 1.4 Driving Force and Governing Parameters

- **Driving Force:** Temperature difference $\Delta T = T_{sat} - T_w$
- **Key Physical Property:** **Latent heat of vaporization** ($h_{fg}$) — typically in **kJ/kg**
- **Operational Concern:** Condensate drainage (gravity, vapor shear, surface tension)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **condensation** (vapor → liquid) with **evaporation** (liquid → vapor). In condensation, **heat is released** by the vapor; in evaporation, **heat is absorbed** by the liquid.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Nusselt's Theory of Condensation — The Cornerstone Model

**Wilhelm Nusselt (1916)** developed the first rigorous analytical model for laminar film condensation on a **vertical plate**. His assumptions remain the foundation of all modern condenser design.

### 2.1.1 Assumptions of Nusselt's Model

1. **Pure, stagnant vapor** at uniform temperature $T_{sat}$
2. **Laminar flow** of the condensate film
3. **Constant properties** of the condensate ($\mu$, $k$, $\rho$ evaluated at mean film temperature)
4. The **vapor exerts no shear** on the liquid film (i.e., vapor velocity is low, $Re_v < \frac{1}{2} Re_l$)
5. **Negligible sensible cooling** of the condensate — only latent heat crosses the interface
6. The heat transfer across the film is by **pure conduction** (no convection in the film)
7. The liquid film is **thin** and the curvature of the interface is neglected
8. **Constant wall temperature** $T_w$ (or constant heat flux as a variant)

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Film Formation

- At the top of the vertical plate ($x = 0$), a **microscopic film** of thickness $\delta(x) \to 0$ forms.
- As the film flows downward under **gravity**, it picks up more condensate from the vapor–liquid interface.
- The **film thickness $\delta$ increases** as $x$ increases.
- Since heat must conduct through an ever-thicker liquid layer, the **local heat transfer coefficient $h_x$ decreases** with $x$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The mean heat transfer coefficient for a vertical plate is exactly **0.943** times the local coefficient at the bottom — a critical result Nusselt derived analytically.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the essential equations for KTU board examination problems. **Memorize the boxed expressions and the dimensionless groups.**

| # | Equation | Variables | Application |
|---|----------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | $\delta(x) = \left[\dfrac{4 \mu \, k \, x \, (T_{sat} - T_w)}{\rho^2 \, g \, h_{fg}}\right]^{1/4}$ | $\delta$ = film thickness at position $x$ | Local condensate film thickness (vertical plate) |
| 2 | $h_x = \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, h_{fg} \, k^3}{4 \mu \, k \, x \, \Delta T}\right]^{1/4} = \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, h_{fg} \, k^3}{4 \mu \, x \, \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$ | $h_x$ = local HTC | Local heat transfer coefficient on vertical plate |
| 3 | $\bar{h}_{vert} = 0.943 \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, h_{fg} \, k^3}{4 \mu \, L \, \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$ | $L$ = plate height | **Mean HTC on vertical plate** |
| 4 | $\bar{h}_{vert} = 0.943 \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, h_{fg} \, k^3}{\mu \, L \, \Delta T}\right]^{1/4} \cdot 4^{-1/4}$ | — | Simplified form (most-used) |
| 5 | $Nu_L = \dfrac{\bar{h}_L \, L}{k} = 0.943 \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, h_{fg} \, L^3}{\mu \, k \, \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$ | $Nu_L$ = Nusselt number | Dimensionless form (vertical plate) |
| 6 | $\bar{h}_{horiz} = 0.725 \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, h_{fg} \, k^3}{\mu \, N \, D_o \, \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$ | $N$ = number of tubes, $D_o$ = outer diameter | **Mean HTC on horizontal tube bank** (Nusselt) |
| 7 | $Re_{film} = \dfrac{4 \Gamma}{\mu}$ | $\Gamma = \dot{m}/(N \pi D_o)$ = mass flow rate per unit length | Condensate Reynolds number |
| 8 | $h = 1.47 \, Re_{film}^{-1/3} \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, k^3}{\mu^2}\right]^{1/3}$ | — | **Laminar film** on horizontal tube ($Re < 30$) |
| 9 | $h = 0.0077 \, Re_{film}^{0.4} \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, k^3}{\mu^2}\right]^{1/3}$ | — | **Turbulent film** on vertical surface ($Re > 1800$) |

### 2.3.1 Dimensional Analysis — Condensation Number ($Co$)

A more compact, dimensionless representation used in advanced KTU problems:

$$Co = \dfrac{h \, \left[\dfrac{\mu^2}{\rho^2 g}\right]^{1/3}}{k} = \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, h_{fg}}{\mu \, k \, \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** A higher $Co$ indicates **better condensation heat transfer**. Pharmaceutical condenser designers use this number to **size shell-and-tube units** for solvent recovery (e.g., recovering methanol from a reactor vent).

## 2.4 Reynolds Number of the Condensate Film

The flow regime of the condensate film determines the applicable correlation:

- **Laminar film:** $Re_{film} < 30$ (most common in pharmaceutical condensers)
- **Wavy/turbulent film:** $30 < Re_{film} < 1800$
- **Fully turbulent:** $Re_{film} > 1800$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall:** Using a turbulent correlation for a laminar film problem (or vice versa) is a guaranteed mark deduction in KTU valuation. Always compute $Re_{film}$ first.

## 2.5 Horizontal vs. Vertical Surface — Comparison

| Feature | Vertical Plate | Horizontal Tube |
|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| Film drainage | Gravity drains film downward | Gravity drains film around circumference |
| Film thickness at length $L$ | $\delta(L)$ | $\delta = \left[\dfrac{4 \mu k \Delta T}{\rho^2 g h_{fg}}\right]^{1/4}$ (uniform) |
| HTC magnitude | $\bar{h}_{vert} \propto L^{-1/4}$ | $\bar{h}_{horiz} \propto (ND_o)^{-1/4}$ |
| Typical use | Large reactor jackets | Shell-and-tube condensers |

## 2.6 Pharmaceutical Industry Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Context for PECHT751 Students:**
> 1. **Solvent Recovery in API Plants** — Recovery of methanol, ethanol, isopropanol, acetone, and dichloromethane from distillation columns.
> 2. **Reflux Condensers** — In multi-effect distillation used for purifying intermediates.
> 3. **Lyophilization (Freeze Drying)** — Condensation of water vapor on cold coils of the lyophilizer condenser.
> 4. **Steam Sterilization (Autoclaves)** — Condensation of saturated steam on cooler surfaces releases large amounts of latent heat for sterilization.
> 5. **Vapor Recompression Systems** — Used in continuous manufacturing to recapture solvent vapors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Film Thickness on a Vertical Plate (Nusselt)

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a vertical plate of width $W$ and height $L$, with surface temperature $T_w$ exposed to saturated vapor at $T_{sat}$ where $T_{sat} > T_w$. The condensate film of thickness $\delta(x)$ flows downward under gravity.

### 3.1.2 Force Balance on the Film Element

Consider an elemental film of width $W$, thickness $dx$, located at distance $x$ from the top. A force balance between the gravity component and viscous shear gives:

$$(\rho g \delta) \cdot W \cdot dx = \mu W \frac{d^2 u}{dy^2} dx \cdot dx$$

Wait — let me do this more rigorously using the standard Nusselt derivation.

**Step 1: Velocity profile in the film**

For a thin film on a vertical surface with negligible inertial effects and constant pressure (atmospheric), the Navier–Stokes equation reduces to:

$$\mu \frac{d^2 u}{dy^2} + \rho g = 0$$

**Step 2: Integration with boundary conditions**

$$u(y) = \frac{\rho g}{2\mu} (2 \delta y - y^2)$$

(after applying no-slip at $y = 0$ and zero shear at $y = \delta$, i.e., $\frac{du}{dy} = 0$ at the free surface)

**Step 3: Mass flow rate per unit width**

$$\Gamma = \int_0^{\delta} \rho \, u(y) \, dy = \frac{\rho^2 g \delta^3}{3 \mu}$$

**Step 4: Energy balance — heat conducted through the film**

The heat flux through the film by pure conduction is:

$$q = \frac{k (T_{sat} - T_w)}{\delta}$$

**Step 5: Mass balance — vapor condensed over element $dx$**

The increase in condensate mass flow from $x$ to $x+dx$ equals the vapor condensed:

$$\frac{d\Gamma}{dx} = \frac{q}{h_{fg}} = \frac{k (T_{sat} - T_w)}{h_{fg} \, \delta}$$

**Step 6: Differentiate Step 3**

$$\frac{d\Gamma}{dx} = \frac{\rho^2 g \delta^2}{3 \mu} \cdot \frac{d\delta}{dx}$$

**Step 7: Equate Step 5 and Step 6**

$$\frac{\rho^2 g \delta^2}{3 \mu} \cdot \frac{d\delta}{dx} = \frac{k \Delta T}{h_{fg} \, \delta}$$

**Step 8: Separate variables and integrate from $0$ to $x$ with $\delta(0) = 0$**

$$\int_0^{\delta} \delta^3 \, d\delta = \frac{3 \mu k \Delta T}{\rho^2 g h_{fg}} \int_0^x dx$$

$$\frac{\delta^4}{4} = \frac{3 \mu k \Delta T x}{\rho^2 g h_{fg}}$$

**Step 9: Solve for $\delta(x)$**

$$\boxed{\delta(x) = \left[\frac{4 \mu k x \Delta T}{\rho^2 g h_{fg}}\right]^{1/4}}$$

This is the **fundamental result** of Nusselt's analysis.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Local Heat Transfer Coefficient $h_x$

By definition, the local HTC is:

$$h_x = \frac{q}{T_{sat} - T_w} = \frac{k}{\delta(x)}$$

Substituting the expression for $\delta(x)$ from Step 9:

$$h_x = \frac{k}{\left[\dfrac{4 \mu k x \Delta T}{\rho^2 g h_{fg}}\right]^{1/4}}$$

$$\boxed{h_x = \left[\frac{\rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3}{4 \mu x \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mean Heat Transfer Coefficient on Vertical Plate

The total heat transfer over the plate of height $L$:

$$Q = W \int_0^L h_x (T_{sat} - T_w) \, dx$$

The mean HTC is defined as:

$$\bar{h} = \frac{Q}{W \cdot L \cdot (T_{sat} - T_w)}$$

Substituting $h_x$:

$$\bar{h} = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L \left[\frac{\rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3}{4 \mu x \Delta T}\right]^{1/4} dx$$

$$\bar{h} = \left[\frac{\rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3}{4 \mu \Delta T}\right]^{1/4} \cdot \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L x^{-1/4} dx$$

$$\int_0^L x^{-1/4} dx = \frac{4}{3} L^{3/4}$$

Therefore:

$$\bar{h} = \left[\frac{\rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3}{4 \mu \Delta T}\right]^{1/4} \cdot \frac{4}{3} L^{3/4} \cdot \frac{1}{L}$$

$$\bar{h} = \frac{4}{3 L^{1/4}} \left[\frac{\rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3}{4 \mu \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{h}_{vert} = 0.943 \left[\frac{\rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3}{\mu L \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}}$$

> [!TIP]
> The constant $0.943$ arises from $\frac{4}{3} \cdot 4^{-1/4} = \frac{4}{3 \cdot \sqrt[4]{4}} = \frac{4}{3 \times 1.4142} \approx 0.943$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Mean HTC for a Horizontal Tube

For a single horizontal tube, the condensate film is assumed uniform around the tube circumference. A force balance on the film gives:

$$\delta = \left[\frac{4 \mu k \Delta T}{\rho^2 g h_{fg}}\right]^{1/4} \quad \text{(constant around tube)}$$

The mean HTC is $h = k/\delta$:

$$\boxed{\bar{h}_{single} = 0.725 \left[\frac{\rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3}{\mu D_o \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}}$$

The constant $0.725$ comes from $\left(\frac{4}{4}\right)^{1/4}$ with the appropriate correction for circumferential drainage.

**For a vertical stack of $N$ horizontal tubes:**

The condensate from the top tube drains onto the next, effectively making the "length" $L = N D_o$:

$$\bar{h}_{N\,tubes} = 0.725 \left[\frac{\rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3}{\mu N D_o \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The vertical stack HTC decreases as $N$ increases. For a **single horizontal tube**, $h$ is roughly **2×** the value for a vertical plate of length $L = D_o$ — gravity acts more effectively over a short horizontal length.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison Table

| Orientation | $\bar{h}$ Expression | Typical Magnitude (Water, $\Delta T = 10$ K) |
|-------------|----------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| Vertical plate, $L = 1$ m | Eq. 3 above | $\sim 500$–$1500$ W/m²·K |
| Single horizontal tube | Eq. 6 above | $\sim 1000$–$3000$ W/m²·K |
| Vertical stack, $N = 10$ | Eq. 6 with $N=10$ | $\sim 400$–$1200$ W/m²·K |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** Saturated steam at $T_{sat} = 100°$C condenses on a vertical plate of height $L = 0.5$ m and width $W = 0.3$ m. The plate surface is maintained at $T_w = 80°$C. Calculate (a) the mean heat transfer coefficient, and (b) the total heat transfer rate.
>
> **Given Properties (water at film mean temperature $\bar{T} = 90$°C):**
> - $\rho = 965$ kg/m³, $k = 0.677$ W/m·K, $\mu = 0.315 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
> - $h_{fg} = 2283$ kJ/kg = $2.283 \times 10^6$ J/kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s²
> - $\Delta T = 100 - 80 = 20$ K

**Solution:**

**(a) Mean HTC**

$$\bar{h} = 0.943 \left[\frac{\rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3}{\mu L \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$$

Computing the numerator inside the bracket:

$$\rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3 = (965)^2 \times 9.81 \times 2.283 \times 10^6 \times (0.677)^3$$

$$= 9.312 \times 10^5 \times 9.81 \times 2.283 \times 10^6 \times 0.3103$$

$$= 6.47 \times 10^{12}$$

Denominator:

$$\mu L \Delta T = 0.315 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 20 = 3.15 \times 10^{-3}$$

Ratio:

$$\frac{6.47 \times 10^{12}}{3.15 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.054 \times 10^{15}$$

Fourth root:

$$\left[2.054 \times 10^{15}\right]^{1/4} = 2130$$

Therefore:

$$\bar{h} = 0.943 \times 2130 = 2008 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**(b) Total Heat Transfer Rate**

$$Q = \bar{h} \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 2008 \times (0.5 \times 0.3) \times 20 = 6024 \text{ W} \approx 6.02 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{h} \approx 2010 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}, \quad Q \approx 6.02 \text{ kW}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Filmwise Condensation on a Vertical Plate — Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the physics of Nusselt film condensation as a **block-level functional architecture** for clarity in KTU board answers.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Saturated Vapor at T_sat] --> B{Vapor-Liquid Interface y = delta x}
    B --> C[Conduction Through Film: q = k * dT / d y]
    C --> D[Wall at T_w Cooler than T_sat]
    D --> E[Cooling Medium: Water or Brine]
    E --> F[Sensible Heat Gain by Coolant]
    
    A --> G[Latent Heat Release h_fg]
    G --> C
    
    B --> H[Gravity Drainage: rho * g * delta drives flow]
    H --> I[Film Velocity Profile u y parabolic]
    I --> J[Mass Flow Rate Gamma per unit width]
    J --> K[Condensate Collection at bottom]
    
    C --> L[Local HTC h_x = k / delta x]
    L --> M[Mean HTC h_bar = 0.943 times h_x at L]
    M --> N[Total Heat Transfer Q = h_bar A dT]
    
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Condenser Block Diagram — Pharmaceutical Solvent Recovery Train

```mermaid
graph LR
    R1[Reactor / Distillation Column] --> V1[Hot Solvent Vapor at T_sat]
    V1 --> C1[Shell and Tube Condenser]
    C1 --> M1[Cooling Water Inlet at T_ci]
    M1 --> M2[Cooling Water Outlet at T_co]
    C1 --> P1[Condensed Liquid Solvent]
    P1 --> T1[Storage Tank]
    C1 --> NV1[Non-condensable Vent]
    
    style R1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style V1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    style C1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style P1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style T1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
    style NV1 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Dropwise vs. Filmwise Condensation — Mode Comparison Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Start: Vapor Approaches Cool Surface] --> Q{Is Surface Non-wettable?}
    Q -->|Yes, low surface energy| D[Dropwise Condensation]
    Q -->|No, high surface energy| F[Filmwise Condensation]
    
    D --> D1[Discrete Droplets Nucleate]
    D1 --> D2[Droplets Grow and Coalesce]
    D2 --> D3[Large Droplets Fall by Gravity]
    D3 --> D4[Fresh Surface Exposed Continuously]
    D4 --> DH[High h: 5 to 10 times higher than filmwise]
    
    F --> F1[Continuous Liquid Film Forms]
    F1 --> F2[Film Thickens Downward]
    F2 --> F3[Increased Conduction Resistance]
    F3 --> FH[Lower h: h decreases with length]
    
    style S fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style DH fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333
    style FH fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333
```

## 4.4 Nusselt Condensation Film — Cross-Sectional Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    V[Region 1: Saturated Vapor T_sat] --- I[Interface y = delta x, T = T_sat]
    I --- F[Liquid Film: 0 less than y less than delta x]
    F --- W[Wall at y = 0, T = T_w]
    W --- CM[Cooling Medium]
    
    V --> V1[Stagnant assumption valid at low vapor velocity]
    F --> F1[Parabolic velocity profile u y]
    F1 --> F2[Driven by gravity rho g delta]
    W --> W1[Heat conducted through film q = k dT / dy]
    
    style V fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style W fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style CM fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define condensation. Differentiate between filmwise and dropwise condensation. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Condensation is the process of phase change from vapor to liquid with the release of latent heat of vaporization. It is a key mass and heat transfer operation used in pharmaceutical plants for solvent recovery.

| Feature | Filmwise Condensation | Dropwise Condensation |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Liquid behavior | Continuous film covers entire surface | Discrete droplets form, coalesce, fall off |
| Heat transfer coefficient | Lower (resistance grows with film) | **5–10× higher** |
| Surface preference | High-energy, wettable surfaces | Low-energy, non-wettable surfaces |
| Typical application | Most industrial condensers | Special coated surfaces |

---

### Q2. State the assumptions made in Nusselt's theory of condensation. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

1. Pure, stagnant vapor at uniform saturation temperature $T_{sat}$.
2. Laminar flow of the condensate film under gravity.
3. Constant physical properties of the condensate ($\rho$, $\mu$, $k$).
4. Negligible shear stress exerted by vapor on the film.
5. Negligible sensible cooling of the condensate — only latent heat crosses the interface.
6. Heat transfer across the film is by pure conduction.
7. Constant wall temperature $T_w$ (or constant heat flux variant).
8. Thin film, flat interface, no curvature effects.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> **Examiner's Note:** KTU ESE Module Internal Choice requires students to attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both must be provided with full sub-parts and model solutions.

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **local heat transfer coefficient $h_x$** for filmwise condensation on a **vertical plate** using Nusselt's theory. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Saturated steam at $T_{sat} = 100°$C condenses on a vertical plate of height $L = 0.4$ m and width $W = 0.25$ m with surface temperature $T_w = 75°$C. Calculate the **mean heat transfer coefficient** and the **total condensate mass flow rate** per hour.

Given: At film mean temperature $T_f = 87.5°$C,
$\rho = 968$ kg/m³, $k = 0.671$ W/m·K, $\mu = 0.338 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $h_{fg} = 2294$ kJ/kg. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Assumptions:** (List 8 assumptions as in Section 5.1 Q2)

**Derivation outline:**

Step 1 — Momentum equation: $\mu \frac{d^2u}{dy^2} + \rho g = 0$

Step 2 — Velocity profile: $u(y) = \frac{\rho g}{2\mu}(2\delta y - y^2)$

Step 3 — Mass flow rate per unit width:
$\Gamma = \int_0^\delta \rho u \, dy = \frac{\rho^2 g \delta^3}{3\mu}$

Step 4 — Heat flux: $q = \frac{k \Delta T}{\delta}$

Step 5 — Mass balance: $\frac{d\Gamma}{dx} = \frac{q}{h_{fg}}$

Step 6 — Differentiate Step 3: $\frac{d\Gamma}{dx} = \frac{\rho^2 g \delta^2}{\mu} \cdot \frac{d\delta}{dx}$

Step 7 — Equate and integrate:

$$\frac{\delta^4}{4} = \frac{3\mu k \Delta T x}{\rho^2 g h_{fg}}$$

Step 8 — Film thickness: $\delta(x) = \left[\frac{4\mu k x \Delta T}{\rho^2 g h_{fg}}\right]^{1/4}$

Step 9 — Local HTC: $h_x = \frac{k}{\delta(x)}$

$$\boxed{h_x = \left[\frac{\rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3}{4\mu x \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $T_{sat} = 100°$C, $T_w = 75°$C, $L = 0.4$ m, $W = 0.25$ m
$\Delta T = 25$ K, $T_f = 87.5°$C
$\rho = 968$ kg/m³, $k = 0.671$ W/m·K, $\mu = 3.38 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s
$h_{fg} = 2.294 \times 10^6$ J/kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Compute $\bar{h}_{vert}$:**

$$\bar{h} = 0.943 \left[\frac{\rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3}{\mu L \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$$

Numerator inside brackets:

$$\rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3 = (968)^2 \times 9.81 \times 2.294 \times 10^6 \times (0.671)^3$$

$$= 9.37 \times 10^5 \times 9.81 \times 2.294 \times 10^6 \times 0.3021$$

$$= 6.37 \times 10^{12}$$

Denominator:

$$\mu L \Delta T = 3.38 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4 \times 25 = 3.38 \times 10^{-3}$$

Ratio:

$$\frac{6.37 \times 10^{12}}{3.38 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.885 \times 10^{15}$$

Fourth root:

$$\left[1.885 \times 10^{15}\right]^{1/4} \approx 2080$$

$$\bar{h} = 0.943 \times 2080 = 1961 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Total heat transfer rate:**

$$Q = \bar{h} A \Delta T = 1961 \times (0.4 \times 0.25) \times 25 = 4903 \text{ W}$$

**Total condensate mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{Q}{h_{fg}} = \frac{4903}{2.294 \times 10^6} = 2.137 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m} = 2.137 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 7.69 \text{ kg/hr}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{h} \approx 1961 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}, \quad \dot{m} \approx 7.69 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the **mean heat transfer coefficient for condensation on a horizontal tube**. How does the result change for **$N$ horizontal tubes in a vertical stack**? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal condenser consists of **10 tubes in a vertical stack**, each of outer diameter $D_o = 25$ mm and length 1 m. Saturated methanol vapor at $T_{sat} = 64.7°$C condenses on the tubes, maintained at $T_w = 50°$C. Compute the **average HTC** and the **total heat duty**.

Given methanol condensate properties at $T_f = 57.35°$C:
$\rho = 750$ kg/m³, $k = 0.180$ W/m·K, $\mu = 0.34 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $h_{fg} = 1100$ kJ/kg. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**For a single horizontal tube:**

The film thickness is uniform around the tube (no length dependence). Force balance on the film element around the circumference gives:

$$\delta = \left[\frac{4\mu k \Delta T}{\rho^2 g h_{fg}}\right]^{1/4}$$

The mean HTC:

$$h = \frac{k}{\delta} = 0.725 \left[\frac{\rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3}{\mu D_o \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$$

**For $N$ tubes in a vertical stack:**

The condensate drains from each tube onto the one below. The effective "length" over which condensation occurs becomes $L_{eff} = N D_o$. Substituting:

$$\bar{h}_{N} = 0.725 \left[\frac{\rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3}{\mu N D_o \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$$

As $N$ increases, $\bar{h}$ decreases (thicker condensate film from accumulated drainage).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $N = 10$, $D_o = 0.025$ m, $L = 1$ m, $T_{sat} = 64.7°$C, $T_w = 50°$C
$\Delta T = 14.7$ K
$\rho = 750$ kg/m³, $k = 0.180$ W/m·K, $\mu = 3.4 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s
$h_{fg} = 1.1 \times 10^6$ J/kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Compute $\bar{h}$:**

$$\bar{h} = 0.725 \left[\frac{\rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3}{\mu N D_o \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$$

Numerator inside:

$$\rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3 = (750)^2 \times 9.81 \times 1.1 \times 10^6 \times (0.180)^3$$

$$= 5.625 \times 10^5 \times 9.81 \times 1.1 \times 10^6 \times 5.832 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= 3.54 \times 10^{10}$$

Denominator:

$$\mu N D_o \Delta T = 3.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 10 \times 0.025 \times 14.7 = 1.25 \times 10^{-3}$$

Ratio:

$$\frac{3.54 \times 10^{10}}{1.25 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.83 \times 10^{13}$$

Fourth root:

$$\left[2.83 \times 10^{13}\right]^{1/4} \approx 730$$

$$\bar{h} = 0.725 \times 730 = 529 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Total heat duty:**

Total heat transfer area for 10 tubes, each 1 m long:

$$A = N \cdot \pi D_o \cdot L = 10 \times \pi \times 0.025 \times 1 = 0.7854 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q = \bar{h} A \Delta T = 529 \times 0.7854 \times 14.7 = 6108 \text{ W} \approx 6.11 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{h} \approx 529 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}, \quad Q \approx 6.11 \text{ kW}}$$

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Property Temperature Trap:** Students often evaluate $\rho$, $\mu$, $k$ at the wrong temperature. KTU examiners use the **mean film temperature** $T_f = (T_{sat} + T_w)/2$ — using $T_{sat}$ or $T_w$ alone leads to **2–3 mark deductions**.
> 2. **Missing $0.943$ Constant:** A frequent mistake is writing $\bar{h} = h_x(L)$ instead of $\bar{h} = 0.943 \cdot h_x(L)$. This subtle coefficient carries **1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting the 4 in the $1/4$ power:** The 4 in the denominator of $h_x$ expression is frequently dropped, leading to an error factor of $4^{1/4} \approx 1.414$. **2-mark penalty**.
> 4. **No Unit Conversion:** $h_{fg}$ must be in **J/kg** (not kJ/kg) in SI calculations. Failing to convert costs **1 mark**.
> 5. **Wrong Surface Area:** For horizontal tube banks, the area is $A = N \pi D_o L$, not $A = L \times W$. **1–2 mark penalty**.
> 6. **Confusing $\dot{m}$ with $Q$:** The condensate mass flow rate is $\dot{m} = Q/h_{fg}$ — a frequently missed step worth **1 mark**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Memorize Before Exam:**

- **Condensation** = vapor → liquid + release of latent heat $h_{fg}$.
- **Two modes:** Filmwise (low $h$) and Dropwise (high $h$, 5–10×).
- **Nusselt's film thickness on vertical plate:** $\delta(x) = \left[\dfrac{4\mu k x \Delta T}{\rho^2 g h_{fg}}\right]^{1/4}$
- **Local HTC on vertical plate:** $h_x = \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3}{4\mu x \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$
- **Mean HTC on vertical plate:** $\bar{h} = 0.943 \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3}{\mu L \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$
- **Mean HTC on horizontal tube:** $\bar{h} = 0.725 \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3}{\mu D_o \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$
- **$N$-tube stack:** Replace $D_o$ with $N D_o$ in the horizontal tube formula.
- **Reynolds number check:** Laminar if $Re_{film} < 30$; turbulent if $Re_{film} > 1800$.
- **All properties evaluated at mean film temperature:** $T_f = (T_{sat} + T_w)/2$.
- **Pharma applications:** Solvent recovery (methanol, ethanol, IPA, acetone), reflux condensers, lyophilizer condensers, autoclave steam systems.
- **Driving force:** $\Delta T = T_{sat} - T_w$ (K or °C).
- **Condensate mass flow rate:** $\dot{m} = Q / h_{fg}$.
- **Key dimensionless number:** $Nu_L = \bar{h} L / k$ (Nusselt number for condensation).

> 🎯 **Final Tip:** For maximum marks, **always draw a labelled sketch** of the condensing film with the interface at $y = \delta(x)$, the wall at $T_w$, the vapor at $T_{sat}$, and indicate the parabolic velocity profile — this is a KTU examiner's favorite **2-mark "freebie."**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cyclisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cyclisation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Cyclisation

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cyclisation** is defined as an intramolecular organic reaction in which an open-chain (acyclic) precursor undergoes ring closure to form a cyclic (closed-ring) molecular structure, with the simultaneous loss of a small molecule (such as water, hydrogen halide, ammonia, or carbon dioxide) or through the formation of new C–C, C–N, C–O, or C–S bonds within the same molecule.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Cyclisation reactions constitute the strategic foundation of heterocyclic and carbocyclic drug synthesis. They are classified as **unimolecular intramolecular reactions** governed by **Baldwin's Rules of Ring Closure** and **Bredt's Rule of Double Bond Geometry**.

Mathematically, cyclisation can be represented as:

$$
\text{Acyclic precursor} \xrightarrow{\text{Intramolecular bond formation}} \text{Cyclic product} + \text{By-product (H}_2\text{O, HX, NH}_3\text{, etc.)}
$$

### 1.2 Classification of Cyclisation Reactions

Cyclisation reactions are broadly classified based on the **type of bond formed** and the **mechanism involved**:

| Classification Basis | Type | Example |
|----------------------|------|---------|
| Bond formed | C–C cyclisation | Dieckmann, Diels-Alder |
| Bond formed | C–N cyclisation | Fischer Indole, Hantzsch Pyridine |
| Bond formed | C–O cyclisation | Baeyer-Villiger (cyclic) |
| Ring size formed | 3, 4, 5, 6, 7-membered | Varies |
| Mechanism | Electrophilic | Friedel-Crafts cyclisation |
| Mechanism | Nucleophilic | Dieckmann cyclisation |
| Mechanism | Pericyclic | Diels-Alder, Cope |
| Mechanism | Radical | Bergman cyclisation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Focus:** The majority of questions on Cyclisation from PECHT751 Module 1 concentrate on **(i) Dieckmann cyclisation, (ii) Diels-Alder reaction, (iii) Paal-Knorr synthesis, and (iv) Fischer Indole synthesis** with reaction mechanisms, reagents, and pharmaceutical applications.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Origami Analogy:**
> Imagine you have a long, straight strip of paper (an **acyclic / open-chain molecule**). Cyclisation is the process of folding that strip and joining its ends (or a middle portion to another part) to form a closed loop (a **ring/cyclic structure**), releasing a small "excess glue droplet" (the **by-product** like H₂O, HCl, NH₃) in the process.
>
> Just like how an origami artist chooses where to fold based on the desired final shape, a pharmaceutical chemist chooses **where** and **how** to cyclise based on the target drug molecule's structure.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Ring Strain Energy (RSE):** Cyclopropane ≈ **27.5 kcal/mol**, Cyclobutane ≈ **26.3 kcal/mol**, Cyclopentane ≈ **6.2 kcal/mol**, Cyclohexane ≈ **0 kcal/mol** (strain-free).
- **Bond angles:** sp³ carbon = **109.5°**, sp² carbon = **120°**, sp carbon = **180°**.
- **Torsional strain (eclipsing) contribution** to ring strain is critical in 3- and 4-membered rings.
