## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define a pharmaceutical indicator. Give the pH range, color change, and one pharmaceutical use of Methyl Orange.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A pharmaceutical indicator is a substance (usually a weak acid or weak base) that changes color sharply near the equivalence point of an acid-base titration, thereby signaling the completion of the reaction. Indicators are classified as internal, external, or fluorescent; Methyl Orange is an **internal, monoprotic acid-type** indicator.

- **pH range:** 3.1 – 4.4
- **Color change:** Red (acidic) → Yellow (basic)
- **Pharmaceutical use:** Used as an indicator in the assay of antacids (e.g., aluminium hydroxide gel, calcium carbonate) titrated against standard HCl.

*(Mark Split: 1 mark definition + 1 mark pH/color + 1 mark pharmaceutical use)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*Why is Methyl Orange preferred over phenolphthalein in the titration of sodium carbonate against HCl? Justify with pH data.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Sodium carbonate ($\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$) is a **diprotic weak base** that has two equivalence points when titrated with HCl:

- **First equivalence point** (Na₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃): pH ≈ **8.3**
- **Second equivalence point** (NaHCO₃ → H₂CO₃): pH ≈ **3.9**

Phenolphthalein changes color at **8.2 – 10.0**, which is suitable for detecting the *first* endpoint only. For total carbonate estimation (both stages), we need an indicator that changes near pH 3.9. **Methyl Orange's range of 3.1 – 4.4 perfectly brackets this second equivalence point**, making it the correct choice for complete neutralization of carbonate.

*(Mark Split: 1 mark equivalence point data + 1 mark phenolphthalein mismatch + 1 mark Methyl Orange justification)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**(a)** Describe the chemical structure, classification, and synthesis of Methyl Orange with a neat labeled diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the theory of acid-base indicators and derive the relationship between pKa and the pH transition range of Methyl Orange. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q.A

**(a) Chemical Structure, Classification & Synthesis (7 marks)**

**Structure:**

Methyl Orange is **sodium 4-{[4-(dimethylamino)phenyl]diazenyl}benzenesulfonate}**. It is an **azo dye** in which the chromophoric $-\text{N}=\text{N}-$ group links two aromatic rings — one bearing a dimethylamino auxochrome ($-N(\text{CH}_3)_2$) and the other bearing a sulfonate group ($-\text{SO}_3^-\text{Na}^+$) for water solubility. The molecular formula is $\text{C}_{14}\text{H}_{14}\text{N}_3\text{NaO}_3\text{S}$ with molecular weight 327.33 g/mol.

**Classification:**

- **Type:** Synthetic, internal, acid-type pH indicator
- **Dye class:** Azo dye (diazo class)
- **Solubility:** Soluble in water, ethanol
- **Physical form:** Orange-yellow crystalline powder

**Synthesis (Diazotization-Coupling):**

**Step 1 — Diazotization:** Sulfanilic acid is dissolved in sodium carbonate solution, then sodium nitrite ($\text{NaNO}_2$) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) are added at **0–5 °C** to form the **diazonium salt** (4-diazoniobenzenesulfonate).

$$
\text{HO}_3\text{S}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{NH}_2 + \text{NaNO}_2 + 2\text{HCl} \xrightarrow{0-5\,°\text{C}} \text{HO}_3\text{S}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{N}_2^+\text{Cl}^- + \text{NaCl} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 2 — Coupling:** The freshly prepared diazonium salt is then coupled with **N,N-dimethylaniline** in a mildly acidic medium to form Methyl Orange (as the free acid), which is then converted to its sodium salt by adding NaOH.

$$
\text{HO}_3\text{S}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{N}_2^+\text{Cl}^- + \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-N}(\text{CH}_3)_2 \rightarrow \text{HO}_3\text{S}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{N}=\text{N}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{N}(\text{CH}_3)_2 + \text{HCl}
$$

**Step 3 — Salt formation and purification:** Treatment with NaOH gives the sodium salt (true Methyl Orange), which is precipitated by salting out with NaCl and recrystallized from hot water.

> *(Mark Split: 2 marks structure + 2 marks classification + 3 marks synthesis equations)*

---

**(b) Theory of Acid-Base Indicators and Derivation of pH Range (7 marks)**

**Theory (Ostwald's Theory):**

According to **Ostwald's theory (1891)**, acid-base indicators are themselves weak acids (or weak bases) whose dissociated and undissociated forms have different colors. The equilibrium is:

$$
\text{HIn} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{H}^+ + \text{In}^-
$$

(acid form, color A) (base form, color B)

The color visible to the eye is determined by whichever form is present in **at least 10 times** the concentration of the other (perceptual threshold of human eye).

**Derivation:**

Applying the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation:

$$
\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log \frac{[\text{In}^-]}{[\text{HIn}]}
$$

**Case 1 — Lower transition limit (red form still dominant, ratio 1:10):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{pH}_{\text{lower}} &= \text{p}K_a + \log\left(\frac{1}{10}\right) = \text{p}K_a - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 2 — Upper transition limit (yellow form dominant, ratio 10:1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{pH}_{\text{upper}} &= \text{p}K_a + \log(10) = \text{p}K_a + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore, the pH transition range is theoretically $\text{p}K_a \pm 1$.**

**For Methyl Orange (pKa = 3.47):**

$$
\text{Range} = 3.47 \pm 1 = 2.47 \text{ to } 4.47
$$

Experimentally reported range is **3.1 – 4.4**, which closely matches the theoretical prediction. The small deviation arises from activity coefficient effects and the eye's sensitivity threshold.

**Conclusion:** An indicator changes color over a range of approximately **2 pH units centered on its pKa**, making pKa the key selection criterion for choosing an indicator for a given titration.

> *(Mark Split: 2 marks Ostwald theory + 3 marks derivation + 2 marks numerical substitution and conclusion)*

---

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme, Module 1 – Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the working mechanism of Methyl Orange as a pH indicator with reference to its chromophore and auxochrome. Discuss why its color changes from red to yellow as pH increases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe in detail the pharmaceutical applications of Methyl Orange. Why is it not used in oral pharmaceutical formulations today, but is still used in external preparations? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q.B

**(a) Working Mechanism — Chromophore and Auxochrome (7 marks)**

**The Concept of Chromophore and Auxochrome:**

A **chromophore** is a functional group that absorbs light in the visible region, producing color. In Methyl Orange, the chromophore is the **azo group ($-\text{N}=\text{N}-$)** which contains π-electrons easily excitable by visible light photons.

An **auxochrome** is a group that does not itself produce color but intensifies or modifies the color produced by the chromophore. The **dimethylamino group ($-N(\text{CH}_3)_2$)** is the auxochrome in Methyl Orange — it donates electrons, extending the conjugated π-system.

**Mechanism of Color Change:**

In **acidic medium (pH < 3.1):** The β-azo nitrogen (the nitrogen farther from the dimethylamino group) accepts a proton ($\text{H}^+$). This protonation **extends the π-conjugation** to include the protonated nitrogen, producing a **quinoid (azenium) resonance structure**. The extended conjugation lowers the energy gap between HOMO and LUMO, so the molecule absorbs light of **longer wavelength (~507 nm)** — the color perceived is **red**.

In **basic medium (pH > 4.4):** The proton is removed. The molecule reverts to its **benzenoid azo form** with shorter conjugation length. The energy gap increases, and absorption shifts to **shorter wavelength (~460 nm)** — the color perceived is **yellow**.

**Resonance transition summary:**

$$
\text{Azo form (yellow)} \;\xrightleftharpoons[\text{OH}^-]{\text{H}^+}\; \text{Azenium form (red)}
$$

The pH at which both forms exist in equal proportion is the **pKa = 3.47**, where the solution appears **orange** (the midpoint of the color transition).

> *(Mark Split: 2 marks chromophore/auxochrome + 3 marks mechanism + 2 marks red-yellow explanation with conjugation logic)*

---

**(b) Pharmaceutical Applications and Restrictions (7 marks)**

**Pharmaceutical Applications:**

1. **Indicator in Antacid Assay:** Used in the IP/USP titration of antacids such as aluminium hydroxide, magnesium hydroxide, and calcium carbonate against standardized HCl.
2. **Indicator in Carbonate/Bicarbonate Assays:** Sodium bicarbonate injections and oral rehydration salts (ORS) are assayed using Methyl Orange indicator.
3. **Diagnostic Stain:** Used in bacteriology labs as a pH indicator in culture media (e.g., to detect acid production by fermenting bacteria).
4. **Quality Control of Water:** Used in "Methyl Orange neutral" test for detecting acidic impurities in distilled and purified water.
5. **Karl Fischer and Bromometric Assays:** Used in some bromometric titrations of sulfa drugs and phenols.

**Why Not in Oral Formulations Today:**

Methyl Orange (along with other azo dyes like Tartrazine / Sunset Yellow) has been **restricted or banned in oral pharmaceutical formulations** in several countries due to:

- **Hypersensitivity reactions:** Azo dyes can cause allergic reactions, asthma exacerbation, and urticaria in susceptible individuals (especially asthmatic and aspirin-intolerant patients).
- **Carcinogenicity concerns:** Certain azo dyes can be metabolized by intestinal microflora to release **aromatic amines**, some of which are potential carcinogens.
- **Pediatric safety:** Banned in pediatric syrups and chewable tablets due to hyperactivity and behavioral issues reported in children.

**Why Still Used Externally:**

In **external preparations** (antiseptic solutions, topical disinfectants, diagnostic stains), the dye is not ingested, so systemic toxicity is negligible. The risk-benefit ratio remains favorable, and Methyl Orange is permitted in:
- Topical antiseptic solutions
- Diagnostic staining reagents
- External-use disinfectants (e.g., for skin preparation)

> *(Mark Split: 3 marks applications + 2 marks oral restriction reasons + 2 marks external use justification)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **"pH range vs pKa" confusion:** Students often write *"Methyl Orange changes at pH 3.47"* — this is WRONG. The range is **3.1–4.4**; 3.47 is the **midpoint**.
> 2. **"Indicator" vs "Drug":** Methyl Orange is **not a drug**. Calling it a drug will cost you marks in any question that uses the word "pharmaceutical."
> 3. **Missing temperature data:** pKa values shift with temperature. Always mention **25 °C** when quoting pKa or pH range.
> 4. **Synthesis step skip:** Examiners expect **all three steps** of synthesis (diazotization, coupling, salt formation). Skipping "salt formation" costs ~1 mark.
> 5. **Color–conjugation link:** The color change is due to **conjugation length change**, not due to the indicator gaining/losing mass. Many students write vague "structural change" answers — be specific about *extended* vs *contracted* π-system.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Methyl Orange** is an **azo dye** pH indicator, **not a drug**.
- **Formula:** $\text{C}_{14}\text{H}_{14}\text{N}_3\text{NaO}_3\text{S}$, **MW = 327.33 g/mol**, **CAS = 547-58-0**.
- **pKa = 3.47** (the midpoint of color change).
- **Transition range: 3.1 (red) → 4.4 (yellow)**.
- **Theory:** Ostwald's indicator theory — color change is due to ratio of HIn (acid) and In⁻ (base) forms.
- **Range formula:** $\text{pH transition} = \text{p}K_a \pm 1$.
- **Chromophore:** Azo group $-\text{N}=\text{N}-$.
- **Auxochrome:** Dimethylamino group $-N(\text{CH}_3)_2$.
- **Solubilizing group:** Sodium sulfonate $-\text{SO}_3^-\text{Na}^+$.
- **Synthesis:** Diazotization of sulfanilic acid → coupling with N,N-dimethylaniline → salt formation with NaOH.
- **Titration use:** Strong acid vs weak base titrations (e.g., HCl vs Na₂CO₃, HCl vs NH₃, antacid QC).
- **Not suitable for:** Weak acid vs strong base titrations (use phenolphthalein).
- **Pharmaceutical role:** Analytical indicator in QC, diagnostic stain, water purity test — **not a therapeutic agent**.
- **Regulatory status:** Restricted/banned in oral formulations in many countries due to azo-dye hypersensitivity; permitted in external preparations.
- **Beer–Lambert Law:** $A = \varepsilon c l$ — used in spectrophotometric assay of Methyl Orange concentration.
- **Stock solution:** 0.1% w/v in water; 1–2 drops per titration.
- **Eye-perceptual threshold:** Color visible when one form is **10×** the other — gives the ±1 pH unit range.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Riboflavin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Riboflavin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Riboflavin (Vitamin B2 / Lactoflavin / Vitamin G)** is a water-soluble, thermally stable but photosensitive B-complex vitamin chemically designated as **7,8-dimethyl-10-(D-ribo-2,3,4,5-tetrahydroxypentyl)isoalloxazine**, possessing the molecular formula **C₁₇H₂₀N₄O₆** and a molecular weight of **376.36 g/mol**. It is the precursor for the two essential flavin coenzymes—**Flavin Mononucleotide (FMN)** and **Flavin Adenine Dinucleotide (FAD)**—which serve as indispensable electron carriers in cellular redox metabolism.

The structure consists of a tricyclic **isoalloxazine ring system** (a benzopteridine) substituted with methyl groups at positions 7 and 8, and bearing a **D-ribitol** (a sugar alcohol) side chain at the N-10 position.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Riboflavin as the "Yellow Battery" of every living cell.**

Imagine every cell in your body is a tiny factory. Factories need batteries to power their machines. Riboflavin is one of the most common "batteries" — it picks up electrons (negative charges) from food you eat, carries them to other parts of the factory, and drops them off to make energy (ATP) or detoxify harmful substances. The bright yellow-green color of riboflavin is the giveaway that it is "charged up" with energy electrons. Without this yellow battery, your body cannot properly burn carbohydrates, fats, or proteins, and your skin, eyes, and mouth tissues suffer first.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **IUPAC Name:** 7,8-Dimethyl-10-[(2S,3S,4R)-2,3,4,5-tetrahydroxypentyl]benzo[g]pteridine-2,4(3H,10H)-dione
> - **Molecular Formula:** C₁₇H₂₀N₄O₆
> - **Molar Mass:** 376.36 g/mol
> - **Melting Point:** ≈ **280 °C** (with decomposition)
> - **UV Absorption Maxima:** **223 nm, 267 nm, 373 nm, 444 nm**
> - **Fluorescence Emission:** **525–530 nm** (intense yellow-green)
> - **Daily RDA (Adults):** **1.1 – 1.7 mg/day**
> - **Water Solubility:** **0.067 mg/mL at 25 °C** (very poor — improves dramatically with salt formation)

## 1.4 Sources of Riboflavin

| Natural Source | Approximate Content |
|---|---|
| Milk & Dairy products | 0.18 – 0.5 mg/100 g |
| Egg yolk | 0.5 mg/100 g |
| Liver (beef) | 2.9 mg/100 g |
| Green leafy vegetables | 0.1 – 0.3 mg/100 g |
| Yeast extract | 5.0 mg/100 g |
| Fortified cereals | 0.3 – 1.0 mg/serving |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Architecture of Riboflavin

Riboflavin's biological activity stems from its **isoalloxazine ring**, which can undergo reversible two-electron (and one-electron) redox reactions. The molecule has three principal regions:

1. **Isoalloxazine nucleus** (the redox-active "head"):
   - Positions N-1, N-3, N-5, N-10 carry the redox chemistry.
   - N-5 and N-10 are the electron-carrying nitrogens.
2. **Ribitol side chain** (the anchoring "tail" at N-10):
   - A five-carbon polyhydroxy chain (C-2′, C-3′, C-4′, C-5′) that determines the vitamin's water solubility.
   - It is the site of phosphorylation to form FMN.
3. **Methyl groups at C-7 and C-8**:
   - Influence redox potential (E₀′ ≈ **–0.219 V** for free riboflavin).
   - Block the C-7/C-8 positions from covalent side reactions.

## 2.2 Conversion to Coenzymes FMN and FAD

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is riboflavin biologically active?**
> It is *not* riboflavin itself, but its phosphorylated derivatives **FMN** and **FAD** that act as coenzymes for flavoenzymes.

The bioconversion sequence is:

$$\text{Riboflavin} \xrightarrow[\text{(Riboflavin kinase)}]{\text{ATP, Mg}^{2+}} \text{FMN}$$

$$\text{FMN} \xrightarrow[\text{(FAD synthetase)}]{\text{ATP, Mg}^{2+}} \text{FAD}$$

These coenzymes are tightly (often covalently) bound to **flavoproteins**, which include:
- **Succinate dehydrogenase** (Complex II, TCA cycle)
- **NADH dehydrogenase** (Complex I, ETC)
- **Glutathione reductase** (antioxidant defense)
- **Acyl-CoA dehydrogenase** (β-oxidation)
- **Xanthine oxidase** (purine catabolism)

## 2.3 Physicochemical Properties (Detailed)

| Property | Observation | Pharmaceutical Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| Appearance | Yellow to orange-yellow crystalline powder | Useful as a food colorant (E101) |
| Odor & Taste | Slight odor, bitter taste | Affects formulation palatability |
| Solubility in water | 0.067 mg/mL (25 °C) | Often converted to **Riboflavin 5′-phosphate sodium (FMN-Na)** for injections |
| Solubility in alcohol | Slightly soluble in ethanol, insoluble in ether/chloroform | Influences extraction and purification |
| pH stability | Most stable at **pH 2 – 5** | Buffers used in oral liquid dosage forms |
| Light stability | Photolabile → forms **lumichrome / lumiflavin** | Requires amber glass, Al-foil blister packaging |
| Heat stability | Stable up to 120 °C (no air) | Survives pasteurization and autoclaving if pH is acidic |
| Redox potential | E₀′ ≈ **–0.219 V** (free); +0.0 to –0.06 V (protein-bound) | Determines direction of electron flow in metabolic pathways |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these are the high-yield facts tested in KTU ESE.**

| Concept | Key Equation / Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Molar mass of riboflavin | **M = 376.36 g/mol** | g/mol |
| Beer–Lambert law (assay) | $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | A = absorbance; ε ≈ **12,500 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹** at 444 nm |
| Fluorescence assay | $I_f = k \cdot \Phi \cdot P_0 \cdot (1 - 10^{-A})$ | λ_ex = 444 nm; λ_em = **525 nm** |
| Redox potential (free) | E₀′ = **–0.219 V** | vs. SHE at pH 7 |
| Riboflavin → FMN | $K_m$ (riboflavin kinase) ≈ **0.16 µM** | Michaelis constant |
| Daily adult RDA | **1.1 mg (women), 1.3 mg (men)** | 1.7 mg during pregnancy/lactation |
| Therapeutic dose (deficiency) | **5 – 25 mg/day** (oral) | Higher in parenteral therapy |
| Photolysis product (alkaline) | **Lumiflavin** (C₁₃H₁₂N₄O₂) | Detected via fluorescence loss |
| Photolysis product (neutral/acidic) | **Lumichrome** (C₁₂H₁₀N₄O₂) | Detected via loss of yellow color |
| Solubility in water | **67 mg/L** (25 °C) | Increases 100× as **riboflavin 5′-phosphate Na** |

> **Note:** Use `\vert` for absolute value signs in your own handwritten solutions to avoid table breakage during copying.

## 2.5 Real-World Pharmaceutical & Engineering Utility

1. **Injectable formulations:** Riboflavin 5′-phosphate sodium (FMN-Na) is used in parenteral multivitamin preparations (e.g., TPN bags) due to its 100× higher water solubility.
2. **Photodynamic therapy (PDT):** Riboflavin + UV-A (365 nm) induces **collagen cross-linking in corneal tissue** — a treatment for *keratoconus*.
3. **Antimicrobial agent:** Blue light (450 nm) + riboflavin inactivates pathogens in **blood products** (plasma, platelets) — replacing some chemical pathogen-reduction steps.
4. **Food industry:** Acts as a natural yellow colorant (E101) and as a fortificant in cereals, baby foods, and beverages.
5. **Diagnostic tool:** Used in the **erythrocyte glutathione reductase activity coefficient (EGRAC)** test to assess riboflavin status.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Chemical Synthesis of Riboflavin (KTU High-Weight Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Tishchenko–Yoneda industrial route** (or its modern variants) is the standard method tested. Memorize the steps.

### 3.1.1 Step-Wise Derivation of the Synthetic Sequence

**Step 1: Preparation of D-Ribose from D-Glucose**

D-Glucose is first converted to D-ribose via the **epimerization–oxidation–reduction** route or via fermentation using *Bacillus pumilus*. The product is purified by ion-exchange chromatography.

**Step 2: Reduction of D-Ribose to D-Ribitol**

$$\text{D-Ribose} \xrightarrow[\text{or LiAlH}_4]{\text{NaBH}_4,\ \text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{D-Ribitol}$$

This is a chemical reduction of the aldehyde (C-1) to a primary alcohol. The yield is ≈ 90 – 95 %.

**Step 3: Selective Acetonide Protection of Ribitol**

Ribitol is reacted with **acetone / ZnCl₂** (or p-toluenesulfonic acid) to form the **2,3-O-isopropylidene acetonide**, leaving the 5′-OH free for later attachment to the aromatic ring.

$$\text{D-Ribitol} + 2\,\text{Me}_2\text{C=O} \xrightarrow{\text{ZnCl}_2} \text{2,3-O-Isopropylidene-D-ribitol} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 4: Construction of the o-Xylylene Diamine Intermediate**

Starting from **3,4-dimethylaniline (o-xylidine)**, the sequence is:
1. N-alkylation with chloroacetic acid → (3,4-dimethylanilino)acetic acid.
2. Nitrosation at the ortho position → 2-nitroso derivative.
3. Reduction of the nitroso group → **2-amino-4,5-dimethyl-anilinoacetic acid** (the "ribityl-acceptor" intermediate).

**Step 5: Coupling with D-Ribitol Acetonide**

The 2-amino-arylamine is condensed with the 5′-chloro-2,3-O-isopropylidene-D-ribitol to install the ribitol chain at N-10.

**Step 6: Condensation with Alloxan (The Key Cyclization)**

The N-1 position is freed, and condensation with **alloxan (2,4,5,6-tetraoxohexahydropyrimidine)** in **boric acid / glacial acetic acid** forms the isoalloxazine ring.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ArNH-CO-Ribitol} + \text{Alloxan} &\xrightarrow{\text{H}_3\text{BO}_3,\ \text{AcOH}} \\
&\text{Riboflavin 2′,3′,4′,5′-tetraacetate}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Deprotection and Final Purification**

The acetonide/acetyl groups are removed by mild acid hydrolysis, and the crude riboflavin is recrystallized from hot water or 2N acetic acid to give **pure yellow-orange crystals**.

### 3.1.2 Comprehensive Reaction Flow Table

| Stage | Reagent / Condition | Transformation | Yield (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | D-Glucose → fermentation | D-Glucose → D-Ribose | 60 – 75 |
| 2 | NaBH₄, H₂O | D-Ribose → D-Ribitol | 90 – 95 |
| 3 | Acetone, ZnCl₂ | Ribitol → 2,3-O-Isopropylidene-D-ribitol | 80 – 85 |
| 4 | 3,4-Dimethylaniline + ClCH₂COOH | Formation of arylglycine | 70 – 80 |
| 5 | HNO₂ (nitrosation) | Aromatic nitrosation | 65 – 75 |
| 6 | Reduction (Zn / AcOH) | Nitroso → Amine | 85 – 90 |
| 7 | Coupling with ribitol-Cl | N-alkylation | 75 – 85 |
| 8 | Alloxan, H₃BO₃, AcOH | Ring closure → Riboflavin tetraacetate | 55 – 70 |
| 9 | H⁺ hydrolysis | Deprotection → Riboflavin | 90 – 95 |

## 3.2 Analytical Assay: Fluorometric Determination (USP Method)

The USP monograph specifies a **fluorometric assay** based on the natural yellow-green fluorescence of riboflavin in aqueous solution.

### 3.2.1 Derivation of the Working Equation

The fluorescence intensity $I_f$ at 525 nm is proportional to the radiant power absorbed and the **quantum yield** $\Phi$:

$$I_f = k \cdot \Phi \cdot P_0 \cdot (1 - 10^{-A_{ex}})$$

At low concentrations ($A_{ex} < 0.05$), the Beer–Lambert law simplifies this to:

$$I_f \approx k' \cdot \Phi \cdot P_0 \cdot 2.303 \cdot \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$

Because $k'$, $\Phi$, $P_0$, $\varepsilon$, and $l$ are constants in a given instrument, we obtain the working linear relationship:

$$I_f = K \cdot c$$

where $c$ is the riboflavin concentration. The K-factor is calibrated using a **USP Riboflavin Reference Standard** at known concentration.

### 3.2.2 Sample Calculation (KTU Problem-Style)

> **Problem:** A riboflavin tablet weighing **150 mg** is dissolved and diluted to **250 mL**. A **5.00 mL** aliquot is further diluted to **50.00 mL** and its fluorescence is measured. The reading is **62.5 %** of a reference standard of **0.500 µg/mL**. Calculate the % purity of the tablet.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Find the concentration in the final diluted solution.

$$c_{\text{sample}} = 0.625 \times 0.500\ \mu\text{g/mL} = 0.3125\ \mu\text{g/mL}$$

**Step 2:** Convert back to the original (pre-dilution) solution.

$$c_{\text{stock}} = 0.3125\ \mu\text{g/mL} \times \frac{50.00\ \text{mL}}{5.00\ \text{mL}} = 3.125\ \mu\text{g/mL}$$

**Step 3:** Total mass of riboflavin in the tablet.

$$m = 3.125\ \mu\text{g/mL} \times 250\ \text{mL} = 781.25\ \mu\text{g} = 0.78125\ \text{mg}$$

**Step 4:** % Purity.

$$\%\, \text{purity} = \frac{0.78125\ \text{mg}}{150\ \text{mg}} \times 100 = 0.521\,\%$$

> This is unrealistically low — meaning a real tablet would have a much higher concentration; the dilution factor would be lower. The method itself is correct.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Riboflavin Metabolism & Drug Action Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FOOD SOURCES\nMilk, Egg, Liver, Green Vegetables] --> B[DIETARY RIBOFLAVIN\nFree Vitamin B2]
    B --> C{INTESTINAL\nABSORPTION\nNa-dependent carrier}
    C --> D[BLOOD PLASMA\nBound to Albumin\nand IgG]
    D --> E[TARGET CELLS\nUptake by Riboflavin\nTransporters RFVT1/RFVT2]
    E --> F[CYTOSOLIC\nCONVERSION]
    F --> G[FMN\nFlavin Mononucleotide]
    G --> H[FAD\nFlavin Adenine Dinucleotide]
    H --> I[FLAVOENZYMES\nSuccinate DH, NADH DH,\nAcyl-CoA DH, Glutathione R]
    I --> J[ATP PRODUCTION\nAND DETOXIFICATION]
    I --> K[MAINTENANCE OF\nSKIN, EYES, MUCOUS\nMEMBRANES]
    L[DEGRADATION\nBY LIGHT] --> M[LUMIFLAVIN\nalkaline]
    L --> N[LUMICHROME\nneutral or acidic]
    O[THERAPEUTIC USE\nPhototherapy, TPN, Multivitamins] --> B
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Industrial Synthesis Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[D-Glucose] -->|fermentation| B[D-Ribose]
    B -->|NaBH4| C[D-Ribitol]
    C -->|acetone, ZnCl2| D[2,3-O-Isopropylidene-D-ribitol]
    E[3,4-Dimethylaniline] --> F[N-alkylation with ClCH2COOH]
    F --> G[Nitrosation HNO2]
    G --> H[Reduction to 2-Aminoarylamine]
    H --> I[Coupling with D acetonide]
    I --> J[Condensation with Alloxan\nH3BO3, AcOH]
    J --> K[Riboflavin 2',3',4',5'-tetraacetate]
    K -->|acid hydrolysis| L[Pure Riboflavin crystals]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Functional Coenzyme Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FMN_BLOCK ["FMN STRUCTURE"]
        F1[Isoalloxazine Ring] --- F2[Ribitol 5'-Phosphate]
        F2 --- F3[Active Redox Center]
    end
    subgraph FAD_BLOCK ["FAD STRUCTURE"]
        F1 --- F4[Pyrophosphate Bridge]
        F4 --- F5[Adenosine 5'-Monophosphate]
    end
    subgraph ENZYME_BLOCK ["APOENZYME"]
        E1[Succinate Dehydrogenase] -.binds.-> F1
        E2[Glutathione Reductase] -.binds.-> F1
        E3[Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase] -.binds.-> F1
    end
    F3 -->|transfers 2 e- and 2 H+| G[Substrate Reduction]
    G --> H[Cellular Energy and Antioxidant Defense]
```

## 4.4 Diagram: Stability Zones of Riboflavin in Dosage Forms

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[STABLE ZONE\npH 2 to 5\nDark, Amber glass\nOxygen-free] --> S1[Liquid Oral / Injectable\nUse riboflavin 5'-phosphate Na]
    P2[CAUTIOUS ZONE\npH 5 to 7\nDiffused light] --> S2[Tablets, Capsules\nUse opaque blister packs]
    P3[UNSTABLE ZONE\npH > 7\nDirect sunlight\nHeat above 100 C] --> S3[Photolysis to lumichrome\nLoss of vitamin activity]
    P4[FOOD FORTIFICATION\npH 4 to 5\nDry state] --> S4[Cereal, Flour, Powdered Milk]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define Riboflavin. List any two pharmaceutical applications of it.

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Similar)]**

**Model Answer:**

Riboflavin, chemically known as **7,8-dimethyl-10-(D-ribo-2,3,4,5-tetrahydroxypentyl)isoalloxazine**, is a water-soluble B-complex vitamin (Vitamin B2) with molecular formula **C₁₇H₂₀N₄O₆**. It functions as the precursor of flavin coenzymes FMN and FAD, which act as electron carriers in cellular redox reactions.

**Pharmaceutical applications:**
1. Treatment and prevention of **ariboflavinosis** (angular stomatitis, cheilosis, glossitis).
2. Used as a **flavoprotein cofactor in parenteral nutrition (TPN)** and in multivitamin formulations, often as the more soluble **riboflavin 5′-phosphate sodium** salt.

### **Q2.** Why is riboflavin stored in amber-colored glass containers?

**Model Answer:**

Riboflavin is **highly photosensitive**. When exposed to light (especially UV and blue light, 400–470 nm), it undergoes **photolysis**:
- In **neutral or acidic medium** → forms **lumichrome** (loses the ribitol side chain).
- In **alkaline medium** → forms **lumiflavin**.

Both photoproducts are **biologically inactive**. To prevent this, riboflavin is stored in **amber-colored glass containers or aluminum-foil blister packs** that filter out the activating wavelengths, thereby preserving its vitamin potency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Adapted)]*

#### **(a) Discuss the chemistry, structure, and physicochemical properties of Riboflavin. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Chemistry & Structure:**
Riboflavin has the molecular formula **C₁₇H₂₀N₄O₆** (MW = 376.36 g/mol). It consists of an **isoalloxazine (benzopteridine) ring system** substituted with two methyl groups at positions 7 and 8, and a **D-ribitol side chain** at N-10. The isoalloxazine ring is the redox-active center, while the ribitol chain imparts water solubility and serves as the attachment point for phosphorylation to form FMN and FAD.

**2. Physicochemical Properties:**
- **Appearance:** Yellow to orange-yellow crystalline powder.
- **Odor/Taste:** Slight odor, bitter taste.
- **Solubility:** Sparingly soluble in water (0.067 mg/mL), very slightly soluble in ethanol, insoluble in chloroform and ether.
- **Melting point:** ≈ 280 °C (with decomposition).
- **UV absorption maxima:** 223, 267, 373, 444 nm.
- **Fluorescence:** Intense yellow-green fluorescence at λ_em ≈ 525 nm.
- **Stability:** Stable to heat and air in dry form and acidic solution; unstable to light and alkaline pH.
- **Redox potential:** E₀′ ≈ –0.219 V (free riboflavin).

**3. Photolysis:**
- Neutral/acidic medium → **lumichrome**.
- Alkaline medium → **lumiflavin**.

#### **(b) Explain the industrial synthesis of Riboflavin. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Tishchenko route (modified by Yoneda)** is the main industrial method:

**Step 1:** D-Glucose → D-Ribose (via fermentation using *Bacillus pumilus*).

**Step 2:** D-Ribose + NaBH₄ → D-Ribitol.

**Step 3:** D-Ribitol + acetone / ZnCl₂ → **2,3-O-isopropylidene-D-ribitol** (selective protection of 2′ and 3′ hydroxyls).

**Step 4:** 3,4-Dimethylaniline + ClCH₂COOH → N-carboxymethyl derivative.

**Step 5:** Nitrosation with NaNO₂ / HCl at the 2-position.

**Step 6:** Reduction of the nitroso group to a primary amine.

**Step 7:** N-Alkylation of the aromatic amine with **5′-deoxy-5′-chloro-2,3-O-isopropylidene-D-ribitol** to give the N-10 substituted compound.

**Step 8:** Condensation with **alloxan** in presence of **boric acid and glacial acetic acid** to form the isoalloxazine ring → **riboflavin 2′,3′,4′,5′-tetraacetate**.

**Step 9:** Acid hydrolysis to remove protecting groups → pure riboflavin, recrystallized from hot water.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Similar Style)]*

#### **(a) Describe the role of Riboflavin as a coenzyme in cellular metabolism. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Riboflavin itself is not biologically active; it is converted in the body to the **flavin coenzymes FMN** and **FAD** by the enzymes **riboflavin kinase** and **FAD synthetase**, respectively.

**Major flavoenzyme systems and their metabolic roles:**

| Coenzyme | Flavoenzyme | Metabolic Function |
|---|---|---|
| FAD | Succinate dehydrogenase (Complex II) | TCA cycle — oxidation of succinate to fumarate |
| FMN | NADH-CoQ reductase (Complex I) | Electron transport chain — transfers e⁻ to CoQ |
| FAD | Acyl-CoA dehydrogenase | β-oxidation of fatty acids |
| FAD | Glutathione reductase | Regenerates reduced glutathione (antioxidant) |
| FAD | Xanthine oxidase | Purine catabolism — hypoxanthine → uric acid |
| FAD | Dihydrolipoyl dehydrogenase | Pyruvate and α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase complexes |
| FAD/FMN | Monoamine oxidase (MAO) | Catabolism of biogenic amines |

The reduced forms (FMNH₂ and FADH₂) carry two hydrogen atoms (2e⁻ + 2H⁺) and are reoxidized by oxygen or by other electron acceptors such as ubiquinone, completing the redox cycle.

#### **(b) Discuss deficiency symptoms, therapeutic uses, and the formulation aspects of Riboflavin. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Deficiency (Ariboflavinosis):**
- Angular stomatitis (cracks at corners of mouth)
- Cheilosis (swollen, cracked lips)
- Glossitis (magenta tongue)
- Corneal vascularization
- Seborrheic dermatitis
- Normocytic-normochromic anemia (impaired iron metabolism)
- Photophobia, tearing, burning eyes

**2. Therapeutic Uses:**
- Prophylaxis and treatment of riboflavin deficiency.
- Component of **multivitamin and B-complex formulations**.
- **Phototherapy** — UV-A + riboflavin eye drops for **corneal cross-linking in keratoconus**.
- **Pathogen reduction** in blood plasma and platelet concentrates.
- Adjunct in **migraine prophylaxis** (400 mg/day, off-label).

**3. Formulation Aspects:**
- **Oral:** Tablets (5 – 25 mg) and capsules of plain riboflavin or its 5′-phosphate salt.
- **Parenteral:** **Riboflavin 5′-phosphate sodium (FMN-Na)** is preferred (100× more water soluble) in IV multivitamins.
- **Stability concern:** Photosensitivity — must use **amber glass vials, Al-foil blisters, opaque HDPE bottles**.
- **Storage:** Cool, dry, dark place; protect from light at all stages.
- **Synergistic combinations:** Usually combined with other B-vitamins (B1, B3, B6, B12, folic acid).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Common Error 1:** Writing the structure of riboflavin without showing the **2,3,4-trihydroxy** nature of the ribitol chain (D-ribitol, not D-ribose) → loss of 1 mark.
> 2. **Common Error 2:** Confusing **lumichrome** (acidic/neutral photolysis) with **lumiflavin** (alkaline photolysis) → 0.5 mark loss each.
> 3. **Common Error 3:** Mentioning "Vitamin B complex" without naming the **coenzymes FMN and FAD** → up to 2 marks lost in coenzyme questions.
> 4. **Common Error 4:** Writing the RDA as "1.7 mg" for everyone — it is **1.1 mg (women), 1.3 mg (men), 1.7 mg (pregnant/lactating)**. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for a single value.
> 5. **Common Error 5:** In synthesis questions, skipping the **protection–deprotection strategy** (acetonide formation) — this step is the hallmark of the Yoneda method and is the most important high-weight answer.
> 6. **Common Error 6:** Not mentioning that riboflavin injections use the **sodium phosphate ester (FMN-Na)** rather than free riboflavin — this is a frequently asked distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read Before Exam Day**

- **Identity:** Riboflavin = Vitamin B2; formula **C₁₇H₂₀N₄O₆**; MW **376.36**; IUPAC name contains **7,8-dimethyl-10-isoalloxazine**.
- **Core structure:** **Isoalloxazine (tricyclic, 3 fused rings)** + **D-ribitol** side chain at N-10 + 2 methyl groups at C-7, C-8.
- **Coenzymes formed:** **FMN (flavin mononucleotide)** and **FAD (flavin adenine dinucleotide)** — both are **prosthetic groups**, not cosubstrates.
- **Redox function:** Carries **2 electrons and 2 protons** (or 1 e⁻ in radicals); E₀′ ≈ –0.219 V.
- **Color:** Yellow-orange in solution; **intense yellow-green fluorescence** at 525 nm (used in assay).
- **Photolysis products:** **Lumichrome** (acidic/neutral) and **Lumiflavin** (alkaline) — both are **biologically inactive**.
- **Storage:** **Amber glass / Al-foil / dark HDPE**; cool, dry, away from light.
- **Heat stability:** Yes (no air, acidic pH).
- **Solubility issue:** Free riboflavin poorly soluble; use **sodium riboflavin 5′-phosphate (FMN-Na)** for injectables.
- **Daily RDA:** **1.1 mg (women), 1.3 mg (men), 1.7 mg (pregnant)**.
- **Deficiency name:** **Ariboflavinosis**; classic signs: **cheilosis, angular stomatitis, glossitis, corneal vascularization**.
- **Therapeutic dose:** **5 – 25 mg/day** orally.
- **Synthesis key steps (Yoneda method):** D-Glucose → D-Ribose → D-Ribitol → 2,3-O-isopropylidene-D-ribitol → N-arylation with dimethylaniline derivative → alloxan cyclization → deprotection → pure riboflavin.
- **Alloxan** is the C-2, C-4 ring-closing reagent; **boric acid / acetic acid** is the cyclization catalyst.
- **Important pharmaceutical uses beyond nutrition:** Corneal cross-linking in **keratoconus** (UV-A + riboflavin eye drops); pathogen reduction in **plasma and platelet** units.
- **Important flavoenzymes to remember for exams:** Succinate DH, NADH-CoQ reductase, Acyl-CoA DH, Glutathione reductase, Xanthine oxidase.
- **Solvent note for extraction:** Free riboflavin → sparingly soluble in water, slightly soluble in alcohol; **insoluble in ether, chloroform, acetone**.
- **UV maxima (memorize):** 223, 267, 373, **444 nm** — the 444 nm peak is used for colorimetric and fluorometric assay excitation.
- **Pregnancy/lactation:** Requirement increases to **1.7 mg/day** — combination prenatal vitamins always include extra riboflavin.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nicotinamide
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nicotinamide

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Nicotinamide** (also known as **niacinamide** or **pyridine-3-carboxamide**) is the **amide derivative of nicotinic acid (Vitamin B3 / Niacin)**. It is a water-soluble vitamin belonging to the **Vitamin B-complex group** and serves as a **biological precursor** for the coenzymes **nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD⁺)** and **nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP⁺)**. 

It is officially recognized in the **Indian Pharmacopoeia (IP)**, **United States Pharmacopoeia (USP)**, and **British Pharmacopoeia (BP)** as an essential pharmaceutical raw material used in the prophylaxis and treatment of **pellagra** and as a vitamin supplement in multivitamin formulations.

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **IUPAC Name** | Pyridine-3-carboxamide |
| **Molecular Formula** | C₆H₆N₂O |
| **Molecular Weight** | 122.12 g/mol |
| **Synonyms** | Niacinamide, Vitamin B₃, Vitamin PP (Pellagra Preventive) |
| **ATC Classification** | A11HA01 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT751, Module 1):** Nicotinamide is studied under the unit *"Drugs & Pharmaceuticals – Study of important drugs/agents with respect to their chemistry, nomenclature, manufacturing/processing, uses, and mode of action."* Students must remember its structure, synthesis (with emphasis on the **3-cyanopyridine route**), and deficiency disease association.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Nicotinamide as a "rechargeable battery"** for the human body. Just like a battery stores energy that can be released when needed, nicotinamide gets converted inside cells into **NAD⁺** and **NADP⁺** — the two most important "energy currency" molecules in the body. Every metabolic reaction that releases or transfers energy uses these coenzymes. Without nicotinamide, our cellular "power plants" (mitochondria) shut down, leading to the deficiency disease **pellagra** (dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia, and death).

In **pharmaceutical manufacturing**, nicotinamide acts as a **"versatile building block"** — small in size, stable in formulation, and easily combined with other vitamins (B-complex capsules) and therapeutic agents (anti-tuberculosis drugs, lipid-lowering combinations).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** Nicotinamide = the amide cousin of niacin → water-soluble → turned into NAD⁺ → fuels hundreds of enzymatic reactions → deficiency = pellagra.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Chemical Constants

- **Melting Point:** 128 °C – 131 °C
- **Boiling Point:** 157 °C (at 0.0005 mmHg) / approximately 224 °C (sublimes)
- **Density:** 1.40 g/cm³
- **Solubility in Water:** **500 g/L (highly soluble) at 20 °C**
- **Solubility in Ethanol:** Freely soluble
- **pKa:** 3.35 (conjugate acid)
- **Appearance:** White, crystalline, odorless powder with a bitter taste
- **UV λmax:** 261.5 nm (in 0.1N HCl) — used in **spectrophotometric assay**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### (A) Chemistry and Nomenclature Logic
- The molecule consists of a **pyridine ring** (6-membered aromatic N-heterocycle) with a **carboxamide functional group (–CONH₂)** attached at the **3-position**.
- The numbering of the pyridine ring starts at the **nitrogen atom = position 1**, and the carbon bearing the –CONH₂ group is at **position 3**.
- The IUPAC name **"pyridine-3-carboxamide"** is directly derived: parent = pyridine; substituent = carboxamide; locant = 3.

### (B) Step-by-Step Reasoning: Why is Nicotinamide biologically active?
1. **Step 1 — Ingestion:** Nicotinamide is absorbed directly from the GI tract via passive diffusion (no phosphorylation required, unlike niacin).
2. **Step 2 — Cellular Uptake:** It enters cells through nucleoside transporters.
3. **Step 3 — Coenzyme Conversion:** Inside the cell, it is converted by the enzyme **nicotinamide phosphoribosyltransferase (NAMPT)** to **nicotinamide mononucleotide (NMN)**.
4. **Step 4 — Final Activation:** NMN is further converted by **NMN adenylyltransferase** to **NAD⁺** (and subsequently NADP⁺ via NAD⁺ kinase).
5. **Step 5 — Biological Function:** NAD⁺/NADP⁺ participate in **redox reactions** (electron transfer), **DNA repair** (PARP enzymes), and **cell signaling** (sirtuins).

### (C) Pharmaceutical Mechanism of Action
- **Anti-pellagra action:** Restores NAD⁺/NADP⁺ levels in deficient patients.
- **Anti-inflammatory action (skin):** Inhibits **poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase-1 (PARP-1)** and reduces pro-inflammatory cytokine release.
- **Photoprotection:** Enhances DNA repair in UV-damaged keratinocytes.
- **Antioxidant effect:** Boosts cellular NADPH pool → regenerates glutathione.

### (D) Structure–Activity Relationship (SAR) — KTU High-Yield
| Structural Feature | Effect on Activity |
|---|---|
| **Pyridine ring** | Essential for coenzyme binding; aromaticity critical |
| **3-position substitution** | Optimal for biological activity (2- or 4- isomers are inactive) |
| **–CONH₂ group (amide)** | Better tolerated than –COOH (niacin); no flushing |
| **Unsubstituted ring** | Required for NAD⁺ biosynthesis pathway |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Niacin (= nicotinic acid = –COOH) causes "niacin flush" due to prostaglandin release. **Nicotinamide (–CONH₂) does NOT cause flushing** — this is a high-yield comparison question.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Molecular weight** | $M = 6(12) + 6(1) + 2(14) + 16 = 122.12$ | g/mol | Stoichiometric calculations in synthesis |
| **Percent yield** | $\% \text{Yield} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Yield}}{\text{Theoretical Yield}} \times 100$ | % | Industrial batch assessment |
| **Molarity of stock solution** | $M = \dfrac{w \times 1000}{M_w \times V_{mL}}$ | mol/L | Analytical assay preparation |
| **Beer–Lambert Law (UV assay)** | $A = \varepsilon \, c \, \ell$ | dimensionless | Assay of nicotinamide at **261.5 nm** |
| **Pellagra Symptom Mnemonic** | **"3 D's"** = Dermatitis, Diarrhea, Dementia (+ Death) | — | Clinical identification |
| **Coenzyme ratio** | $\text{NAD}^+ \rightleftharpoons \text{NADH} + H^+ + 2e^-$ | — | Redox biochemistry |
| **Vital Amines** (origin) | **Vitamin = "vital amine"** | — | Historical naming |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Beer–Lambert equation $A = \varepsilon c \ell$ is directly applied in the **IP/USP assay of nicotinamide tablets** by UV-Visible spectrophotometry at **261.5 nm in 0.1N HCl**, which is a routine quality control test in pharmaceutical industry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Industrial Synthesis Route — The 3-Cyanopyridine Pathway (KTU Most Important)

The **most industrially significant** synthesis of nicotinamide proceeds through **3-cyanopyridine (nicotinonitrile)** as a key intermediate. This is a **two-step process**.

### **Step 1: Preparation of 3-Cyanopyridine (from 3-Methylpyridine / β-Picoline)**

**Raw material:** 3-Methylpyridine (β-picoline, C₆H₇N)

**Reaction type:** Catalytic **ammoxidation** (also called "Andrussow-type" reaction adapted to methylpyridines).

**Conditions:** 
- Catalyst: **Vanadium pentoxide (V₂O₅)** supported on **alumina or titania** 
- Or **fluidized bed with Mo-Bi-O / Sb-Fe-O catalysts**
- Temperature: **350 – 400 °C**
- Reactants: β-picoline + **NH₃ + O₂ (air)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_6\text{H}_7\text{N} \; + \; \text{NH}_3 \; + \; \tfrac{3}{2}\, \text{O}_2 \;&\xrightarrow{\text{V}_2\text{O}_5 / \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3,\; 380^\circ\text{C}} \; \text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{N-CN} \; + \; 3\, \text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{(3-Methylpyridine)} \;&& \text{(3-Cyanopyridine)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of each step:**
- The methyl group (–CH₃) at the 3-position is **oxidatively dehydrogenated**.
- Ammonia provides the **nitrogen** for the –C≡N group.
- Oxygen acts as the **oxidant** (water is released as byproduct).
- The V₂O₅ catalyst provides **redox active sites** to facilitate methyl activation.

**Yield:** ~ 90 – 95% (industrial scale).

---

### **Step 2: Hydrolysis of 3-Cyanopyridine to Nicotinamide**

This is a **controlled partial hydrolysis** — the challenge is to stop at the **amide** stage and not over-hydrolyze to nicotinic acid.

**Reaction:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{N-CN} \; + \; \text{H}_2\text{O} \;&\xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 / \text{NaOH, or enzymatic,}\; 60-80^\circ\text{C}} \; \text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{N-CONH}_2 \\
\text{(3-Cyanopyridine)} && \text{(Nicotinamide)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mechanism — Why amide and not acid?**

The **nitrile group (–C≡N)** undergoes nucleophilic addition of water:
1. **Protonation** of nitrile nitrogen by H⁺ (from H₂O₂ or dilute acid).
2. **Water attacks** the electrophilic carbon of C≡N.
3. Forms an unstable **iminol intermediate** (R–C(=NH)–OH).
4. **Tautomerization** (keto-enol type) of the iminol gives the **amide** (R–CONH₂).
5. **If hydrolysis proceeds further** with stronger acid/heat, the amide → **carboxylic acid** (nicotinic acid) + NH₃.

**Therefore:** The industrial process uses **mild, controlled conditions** (often **enzymatic hydrolysis using nitrile hydratase** from *Rhodococcus* or *Pseudomonas*) to selectively obtain the amide.

**Overall Yield:** ~ 85 – 90% (overall route ~ 75 – 85%).

---

## 3.2 Alternative Synthesis Route — From Nicotinic Acid (Niacin)

This is a **two-step route** starting from the cheaper, more abundant **nicotinic acid**.

### **Step 1: Conversion of Nicotinic Acid → Acid Chloride**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{N-COOH} \; + \; \text{SOCl}_2 \;&\longrightarrow \; \text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{N-COCl} \; + \; \text{SO}_2 \; + \; \text{HCl} \\
\text{(Nicotinic acid)} && \text{(Nicotinoyl chloride)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 2: Ammonolysis of Acid Chloride**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{N-COCl} \; + \; 2\, \text{NH}_3 \;&\longrightarrow \; \text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{N-CONH}_2 \; + \; \text{NH}_4\text{Cl} \\
\text{(Nicotinoyl chloride)} && \text{(Nicotinamide)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** 
- One mole of NH₃ acts as the **nucleophile** that attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon.
- The second mole of NH₃ acts as the **base**, neutralizing the HCl released.
- This is the classical **Schotten–Baumann type amidation**.

**Limitation:** Generates corrosive SO₂/HCl gases; requires stainless-steel reactors. Costlier than 3-cyanopyridine route.

---

## 3.3 Assay of Nicotinamide — IP/USP Method (UV-Vis)

**Procedure Outline:**
1. Weigh accurately **~ 0.1 g** of nicotinamide, dissolve in **0.1N HCl** and make up to **100 mL** (stock).
2. Dilute 10 mL of stock to 100 mL with 0.1N HCl → working solution.
3. Measure **absorbance (A)** at **λmax = 261.5 nm** in a 1 cm quartz cuvette.

**Calculation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Assay} \;&= \; \dfrac{A_{\text{sample}}}{A_{\text{standard}}} \;\times\; \dfrac{W_{\text{standard}}}{W_{\text{sample}}} \;\times\; \dfrac{P_{\text{standard}}}{100} \;\times\; 100
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $A$ = absorbance
- $W$ = weight (g)
- $P$ = potency / purity (%) of the standard

**Acceptance limit (IP):** **98.5 – 101.5%** of stated amount on dried basis.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quality Control Tests

| Test | Method | Specification (IP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Description** | Visual | White crystalline powder |
| **Identification (A)** | Melting point | 128 – 131 °C |
| **Identification (B)** | UV absorption max | 261 – 263 nm (in 0.1N HCl) |
| **Identification (C)** | IR spectrum | Conforms to reference spectrum |
| **Loss on drying** | 105 °C / 2 h | ≤ 0.5% w/w |
| **Heavy metals** | IP limit test | ≤ 20 ppm |
| **Sulphated ash** | Ignition at 600 °C | ≤ 0.1% |
| **Assay** | Non-aqueous titration (with HClO₄) | 98.5 – 101.5% |
| **Related substances** | HPLC (IP/BP) | Single impurity ≤ 0.1%, Total ≤ 0.2% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Molecular Structure of Nicotinamide (Mermaid Block Schematic)

```mermaid
    nh2["NH2 AMINO"]:::part

    pyridineCore --- c2

```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Industrial Synthesis (3-Cyanopyridine Route)

```mermaid

    start["RAW MATERIAL BETA PICOLINE 3 METHYLPYRIDINE"]:::raw
    mix["MIX WITH AMMONIA AND AIR NH3 PLUS O2"]:::step

    inter["INTERMEDIATE 3 CYANOPYRIDINE NICOTINONITRILE"]:::mid

    purify["RECRYSTALLISATION FROM WATER OR ETHANOL"]:::step

    qc["QUALITY CONTROL IP USP ASSAY UV AND HPLC"]:::check
    final["PHARMA GRADE NICOTINAMIDE 98.5 TO 101.5 PERCENT"]:::final

    start --> mix --> reactor1 --> inter --> h2o --> reactor2 --> crude --> purify --> dry --> qc --> final

```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Biological Conversion Pathway

```mermaid

    intake["ORAL INTAKE TABLET OR CAPSULE"]:::intake
    absorb["GASTROINTESTINAL ABSORPTION PASSIVE DIFFUSION"]:::process

    cell["CELLULAR UPTAKE VIA NUCLEOSIDE TRANSPORTERS"]:::process
    nampt["ENZYME NAMPT NICOTINAMIDE PHOSPHORIBOSYLTRANSFERASE"]:::enzyme
    nmn["NICOTINAMIDE MONONUCLEOTIDE NMN"]:::metab
    nmnat["ENZYME NMN ADENYLYLTRANSFERASE"]:::enzyme
    nad["NAD PLUS COENZYME ACTIVE FORM"]:::active
    nadp["NADP PLUS VIA NAD KINASE"]:::active
    redox["REDOX REACTIONS ENERGY METABOLISM"]:::function
    dnaRepair["DNA REPAIR VIA PARP ENZYMES"]:::function
    sirtuins["SIRTUIN MEDIATED CELL SIGNALLING"]:::function

    intake --> absorb --> transport --> cell --> nampt --> nmn --> nmnat --> nad
    nad --> nadp
    nad --> redox
    nad --> dnaRepair
    nadp --> sirtuins
```

## 4.4 Schematic of Deficiency–Treatment Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    diet["INADEQUATE DIET LACK OF B3 FOODS"]:::cause
    mal["MALABSORPTION DISORDERS"]:::cause
    etoh["CHRONIC ALCOHOLISM"]:::cause
    def["NICOTINAMIDE DEFICIENCY"]:::state
    pellagra["PELLAGRA DERMATITIS DIARRHEA DEMENTIA"]:::symptom
    treat["ADMINISTER NICOTINAMIDE 100 TO 500 MG PER DAY"]:::treat
    restore["RESTORE NAD PLUS AND NADP PLUS LEVELS"]:::restore
    cure["RESOLUTION OF SYMPTOMS"]:::outcome

    diet --> def
    mal --> def
    etoh --> def
    def --> pellagra
    pellagra --> treat
    treat --> restore
    restore --> cure

    classDef cause fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:1px,color:#3E0A0A
```
