
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PECHT751]**
> **Define Nicotinamide. Give its IUPAC name, molecular formula, and the deficiency disease it prevents.**

**Model Answer:**
Nicotinamide is the **amide form of Vitamin B₃ (Niacin)**, a water-soluble vitamin. 
- **IUPAC Name:** Pyridine-3-carboxamide 
- **Molecular Formula:** C₆H₆N₂O 
- **Molecular Weight:** 122.12 g/mol 
- **Deficiency Disease:** **Pellagra** (also called "the 3 D's disease": Dermatitis, Diarrhea, Dementia; 4th D is Death if untreated).
It functions as a precursor of the coenzymes **NAD⁺** and **NADP⁺**, essential for cellular redox reactions. *(3 marks)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, PECHT751]**
> **Differentiate between Niacin (Nicotinic acid) and Nicotinamide. Why is Nicotinamide preferred in multivitamin formulations?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Niacin (Nicotinic acid) | Nicotinamide |
|---|---|---|
| **Functional group** | –COOH (carboxylic acid) | –CONH₂ (carboxamide) |
| **Side effect** | Causes "niacin flush" (cutaneous vasodilation) due to prostaglandin release | **No flushing** — well tolerated |
| **Lipid-lowering use** | Yes (↓ LDL, ↑ HDL) | **No** — lacks lipid effect |
| **Use in formulations** | Limited (GI irritation) | **Preferred** in multivitamins |

Nicotinamide is preferred in multivitamin formulations because it is **better tolerated, does not cause flushing, and is more stable** in combination with other B-vitamins. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECHT751, Module 1]**
> **(a) Describe the industrial synthesis of Nicotinamide from 3-Methylpyridine (β-Picoline) via the 3-Cyanopyridine route. Include the reaction conditions, catalyst used, and mechanism of nitrile hydrolysis. (7 marks)**
> **(b) Explain the biological role of Nicotinamide as a precursor of NAD⁺ and NADP⁺. How does its deficiency lead to pellagra, and what is the recommended dosage for treatment? (7 marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — Industrial Synthesis (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Preparation of 3-Cyanopyridine via ammoxidation of β-picoline (4 marks)**
- **Raw material:** 3-Methylpyridine (β-picoline)
- **Reagents:** NH₃ + O₂ (air)
- **Catalyst:** V₂O₅ supported on Al₂O₃ (or Mo-Bi-O, Sb-Fe-O in commercial fluidized beds)
- **Temperature:** 350 – 400 °C
- **Reaction:**
  $$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_7\text{N} + \text{NH}_3 + \tfrac{3}{2}\, \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{V}_2\text{O}_5,\; 380^\circ\text{C}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{N-CN} + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$
- The methyl group is oxidatively converted to a –C≡N group; the nitrogen comes from NH₃.
- **Yield:** ~ 90 – 95% in industrial scale.

**Step 2: Controlled hydrolysis of 3-cyanopyridine to nicotinamide (3 marks)**
- **Reagent:** H₂O₂ in dilute NaOH (or **enzymatic hydrolysis** using *Rhodococcus* nitrile hydratase at 30 – 40 °C, pH 7.0).
- **Reaction:**
  $$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{N-CN} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{N-CONH}_2 $$
- **Mechanism:** (i) Protonation of –C≡N; (ii) Nucleophilic attack by H₂O; (iii) Formation of iminol (R–C(=NH)–OH); (iv) Tautomerization to amide (R–CONH₂).
- **Industrial preference:** Enzymatic method gives > 99% selectivity to amide; chemical method gives ~ 90% with side-product nicotinic acid.
- **Purification:** Recrystallization from water/ethanol; vacuum drying at ~ 50 °C.

---

#### **Part (b) — Biological Role & Deficiency (7 marks)**

**Conversion to NAD⁺ (3 marks):**
1. **Absorption:** Nicotinamide is absorbed from the GI tract by **passive diffusion**.
2. **NAMPT reaction:** Inside the cell, the enzyme **nicotinamide phosphoribosyltransferase (NAMPT)** converts nicotinamide + PRPP → **nicotinamide mononucleotide (NMN)**.
3. **NMNAT reaction:** **NMN adenylyltransferase** converts NMN + ATP → **NAD⁺**.
4. **NADPH production:** NAD⁺ is phosphorylated by **NAD⁺ kinase** → **NADP⁺**.

**Biological functions of NAD⁺/NADP⁺ (2 marks):**
- **Redox reactions** — electron transfer in glycolysis, TCA cycle, oxidative phosphorylation.
- **DNA repair** — substrate for **PARP enzymes**.
- **Cell signaling** — cofactor for **sirtuins** (longevity/deacetylase enzymes).

**Pellagra (1 mark):**
- Deficiency causes pellagra = **3 D's: Dermatitis, Diarrhea, Dementia (+ Death)**.
- Common in chronic alcoholics, malnourished, Hartnup disease patients.

**Dosage (1 mark):**
- **Prophylaxis:** 15 – 30 mg/day (RDA for adults).
- **Treatment of pellagra:** 100 – 500 mg/day orally in divided doses for 1 – 2 weeks, then maintenance dose.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PECHT751, Module 1]**
> **(a) Discuss the various physicochemical properties of Nicotinamide used in its identification and quality control. List the IP limit tests with specifications. (7 marks)**
> **(b) Outline the synthesis of Nicotinamide from Nicotinic acid via acid chloride route. Compare this route with the 3-cyanopyridine route on the basis of cost, yield, and environmental impact. (7 marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — Physicochemical Properties & QC (7 marks)**

**Identification Parameters (4 marks):**
- **Appearance:** White, crystalline, odorless powder with a bitter taste.
- **Melting point:** 128 – 131 °C (matches IP).
- **UV λmax:** 261 – 263 nm in 0.1N HCl (E¹%₁cm ≈ 460) — used in assay.
- **IR spectroscopy:** Characteristic amide-I band at ~ 1680 cm⁻¹ (C=O stretch); amide-II band at ~ 1620 cm⁻¹ (N–H bend); aromatic C=N stretch at ~ 1580 cm⁻¹.
- **Solubility:** Very soluble in water (500 g/L); freely soluble in ethanol, glycerol; sparingly soluble in ether.
- **Hygroscopicity:** Slightly hygroscopic — should be stored in airtight containers.

**IP Quality Control Tests (3 marks):**

| Test | Specification |
|---|---|
| Description | White crystalline powder |
| Melting point | 128 – 131 °C |
| Loss on drying (105 °C, 2 h) | ≤ 0.5% w/w |
| Heavy metals | ≤ 20 ppm |
| Sulphated ash | ≤ 0.1% |
| Assay (non-aqueous titration with HClO₄) | 98.5 – 101.5% |
| Related substances (HPLC) | Single impurity ≤ 0.1%; Total ≤ 0.2% |

---

#### **Part (b) — Synthesis from Nicotinic Acid & Comparison (7 marks)**

**Synthesis from Nicotinic Acid (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Nicotinic acid + SOCl₂ → Nicotinoyl chloride + SO₂↑ + HCl↑
**Step 2:** Nicotinoyl chloride + 2 NH₃ → Nicotinamide + NH₄Cl

**Reaction scheme:**
$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{N-COOH} \xrightarrow{\text{SOCl}_2} \text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{N-COCl} \xrightarrow{\text{NH}_3} \text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{N-CONH}_2 $$

This is **Schotten–Baumann amidation**.

**Comparison (4 marks):**

| Parameter | 3-Cyanopyridine route | Nicotinic acid route |
|---|---|---|
| **Raw material cost** | Lower (β-picoline is cheap) | Higher (nicotinic acid costs more) |
| **Yield** | ~ 80 – 90% overall | ~ 70 – 80% |
| **Catalyst** | V₂O₅ (heterogeneous, reusable) | SOCl₂ (corrosive, stoichiometric) |
| **By-products** | H₂O (clean) | SO₂, HCl (corrosive gases) |
| **Environmental impact** | **Greener** | Polluting — gas scrubbers needed |
| **Industrial preference** | **Preferred** (Lonza, BASF, DSM use this) | Used mainly in small-scale / lab synthesis |

**Conclusion:** The **3-cyanopyridine route** is the **green, cost-effective, industrial process of choice**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Niacin with Nicotinamide** — they are NOT the same. Niacin = –COOH; Nicotinamide = –CONH₂. Mixing them up costs full marks.
> 2. **Writing the molecular formula as C₆H₆NO instead of C₆H₆N₂O** — students frequently forget the second nitrogen of the amide group.
> 3. **Calling pellagra a "skin disease" only** — it is a multi-system disease. Use the **"3 D's"** mnemonic to show completeness.
> 4. **Omitting catalyst name** in the ammoxidation step — V₂O₅ is **mandatory** in the answer.
> 5. **Forgetting the controlled conditions** of nitrile hydrolysis — students often write "HNO₃" or "H₂SO₄" (too harsh, will over-hydrolyze to acid).
> 6. **UV λmax value** — 261.5 nm in 0.1N HCl is the IP value. Many students write 260 nm or 262 nm — close, but not exact.
> 7. **Drawing the structure with N at the wrong position** — always number from the ring nitrogen.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Nicotinamide = pyridine-3-carboxamide = Vitamin B₃ (amide form)**, MF: C₆H₆N₂O, MW: 122.12 g/mol.
- **Deficiency disease = Pellagra** (3 D's: Dermatitis, Diarrhea, Dementia + Death).
- **Coenzymes derived:** NAD⁺ (via NAMPT → NMN → NMNAT pathway) and NADP⁺.
- **Industrial synthesis (preferred):** 3-Methylpyridine → **ammoxidation (V₂O₅, 380 °C)** → 3-Cyanopyridine → **controlled hydrolysis (H₂O₂ or nitrile hydratase)** → Nicotinamide.
- **Alternative route:** Nicotinic acid → SOCl₂ → Nicotinoyl chloride → NH₃ → Nicotinamide (Schotten–Baumann).
- **UV assay:** λmax = 261.5 nm in 0.1N HCl; Beer–Lambert: $A = \varepsilon c \ell$.
- **Quality:** IP assay 98.5 – 101.5%; MP 128 – 131 °C; LOD ≤ 0.5%.
- **Pharmacological actions:** Anti-pellagra, anti-inflammatory, photoprotective, antioxidant, supports cellular energy metabolism.
- **Why preferred over niacin:** **No flushing** (no prostaglandin release).
- **Therapeutic dose:** 100 – 500 mg/day for pellagra; RDA: 15 – 30 mg/day.
- **SAR key point:** Carboxamide group at **3-position** of pyridine is **essential** for activity.
- **Tablet formulations:** Often combined with B₁, B₂, B₆, B₁₂, folic acid, and vitamin C.
- **Solubility:** ~ 500 g/L in water → makes it ideal for liquid/oral dosage forms.
- **Storage:** Protect from light and moisture; keep in **airtight containers** at < 25 °C.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Procaine Hydrochloride
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Procaine Hydrochloride

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Procaine Hydrochloride** is the hydrochloride salt of the *p*-aminobenzoic acid ester of diethylaminoethanol, chemically designated as **4-aminobenzoic acid 2-(diethylamino)ethyl ester hydrochloride**. It is classified under the **amino-ester** class of synthetic local anesthetic agents and serves as the prototypical injectable local anesthetic introduced into clinical medicine in **1905** under the trade name **Novocaine®**.

The compound has the following rigorous physico-chemical descriptors:

- **Molecular Formula:** $C_{13}H_{20}N_2O_2 \cdot HCl$
- **Molecular Weight:** **272.77 g/mol**
- **pKa:** **8.9** (tertiary amine)
- **Melting Point Range:** **154 °C – 158 °C**
- **Aqueous Solubility (20 °C):** Approximately **1 g per 1 mL of water**
- **Log P (partition coefficient, octanol/water):** **1.92**
- **pH of 1% aqueous solution:** **5.0 – 6.5**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight (CO1 – Pharmaceutical Chemistry Foundations):** Students are expected to identify the **three structural domains** of a local anesthetic molecule — the *lipophilic aromatic ring*, the *intermediate linker* (ester or amide), and the *hydrophilic amine side chain* — and recognize how Procaine embodies the **"LIFA" model** (Lipophilic-Intermediate-Hydrophilic Amine) proposed by **Löfgren (1948)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Lamp Switch" Concept

Think of a nerve fiber as a long electrical wire (the lamp cord) that carries tiny bio-electrical signals (the current) from one part of the body to the brain. Sodium ions ($\text{Na}^+$) act like the **"ON switch"** — they rush into the nerve cell and propagate the pain signal forward.

**Procaine Hydrochloride** acts like a **broken fuse or a jammed ON-switch**. It physically **blocks the sodium channel gate** (the "switch") so that $\text{Na}^+$ cannot enter the nerve cell. As a result, the pain signal **cannot travel**, and the patient feels numbness in the region where Procaine was injected.

> [!TIP]
> **Easy Memory Hook:** **"Procaine = Pro-Block-Caine"** — *Pro* (favorably) + *Block* (sodium channel blocker) + *caine* (family name for local anesthetics).

## 1.3 Structural Identity Card

| Structural Domain | Chemical Identity | Functional Role |
|---|---|---|
| Lipophilic group | *p*-Aminobenzoic acid (PABA) ring | Membrane penetration, π-stacking |
| Intermediate linker | **Ester bond** (−COO−) | Hydrolyzable by plasma esterases |
| Hydrophilic group | Diethylaminoethyl side chain | Protonation, water solubility |
| Salt former | Hydrochloride (HCl) | Pharmaceutical stability, aqueous solubility |

> [!CAUTION]
> The **ester linkage** is the defining *weakness* and *strength* of Procaine — *weak* because it is rapidly hydrolyzed in plasma (short duration of action ≈ **30–60 minutes**), and *strength* because it ensures low systemic toxicity and allergenicity compared to amide-type anesthetics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deconstruction of the Molecule

Procaine can be chemically parsed into **two parent fragments** joined by an ester bond:

1. **Acid Fragment:** 4-Aminobenzoic Acid (PABA)
2. **Alcohol Fragment:** 2-(Diethylamino)ethanol

### 2.1.1 The Acid Fragment — *p*-Aminobenzoic Acid (PABA)

- Aromatic benzene ring bearing **−NH₂ at the para position (C-4)** and **−COOH at C-1**.
- The −NH₂ group is an **electron-donating group (+M effect)**, which **raises the electron density** on the carbonyl carbon, thereby making the carboxyl carbon **more electrophilic** and accelerating nucleophilic esterification.

### 2.1.2 The Alcohol Fragment — 2-(Diethylamino)ethanol

- Two-carbon ethylene bridge terminated by a **tertiary amine** $\text{−N(C}_2\text{H}_5\text{)}_2$.
- This tertiary amine has a **proton sponge** function: at physiological pH (7.4), it is **partially protonated** ($\text{pKa} = 8.9$, so ≈ **8.9% protonated** at pH 7.4).

### 2.1.3 The Ester Linkage — The Pharmacokinetic Switch

The ester bond is **electronically stabilized** by conjugation with the aromatic ring, but is **kinetically vulnerable** to **pseudocholinesterase (butyrylcholinesterase)** hydrolysis in plasma.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The half-life of Procaine in plasma is approximately **7.7 minutes** — this is *the* reason Procaine is a **short-acting** local anesthetic.

## 2.2 The LIFA Pharmacophore — Why Each Fragment Matters

| Fragment | Physico-chemical Property | Pharmacological Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| PABA ring | Aromatic, lipophilic (Log P ≈ 0.92) | Crosses nerve membrane (lipid bilayer) |
| −COO− ester | Polar, hydrolyzable | Cleared rapidly → low toxicity |
| −CH₂CH₂− spacer | Torsional flexibility | Allows proper receptor fit |
| −N(Et)₂ | Basic tertiary amine | Exists as cationic form (BH⁺) inside channel |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following equations are the *only* mathematical relations* required for Module 1 (Local Anesthetics) per the KTU 2024 PECHT751 syllabus. Master these and you master the chapter.*

### 2.3.1 Henderson–Hasselbalch Equation (Ionization of Local Anesthetics)

$$pH = pK_a + \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{B}]}{[\text{BH}^+]}\right)$$

where:
- $[\text{B}]$ = concentration of **unprotonated (free base)** form
- $[\text{BH}^+]$ = concentration of **protonated (cationic)** form

**Rearranged (fraction un-ionized, $\alpha$):**

$$\alpha = \frac{[\text{B}]}{[\text{B}] + [\text{BH}^+]} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(pK_a - pH)}}$$

### 2.3.2 Onset of Action (Correlation with pKa)

$$\text{Onset time} \;\propto\; \frac{[\text{BH}^+]}{[\text{B}]} \;=\; 10^{(pK_a - pH)}$$

- Higher pKa → more BH⁺ at tissue pH → **slower onset**
- Procaine (pKa = **8.9**) → at tissue pH 7.4 → onset time is **moderate to slow**

### 2.3.3 Lipid Solubility & Potency Approximation

$$\log P \;\approx\; \log\left(\frac{\text{Concentration in octanol}}{\text{Concentration in water}}\right)$$

> **Engineering Utility:** In pharmaceutical formulation, the **log P** is used by medicinal chemists to predict whether a candidate will *penetrate the blood-nerve barrier* (typically need $\log P \geq 2$) or remain in extracellular fluid.

### 2.3.4 Hydrolysis Rate of Ester Linkage (Pseudo-First Order)

$$\ln[\text{Procaine}]_t = \ln[\text{Procaine}]_0 - k_{\text{hyd}} \cdot t$$

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_{\text{hyd}}} = \frac{0.693}{k_{\text{hyd}}}$$

For Procaine in human plasma at **37 °C**, $t_{1/2} \approx \mathbf{7.7 \text{ min}}$.

### 2.3.5 Dose-Response Hill Equation (Anesthetic Block)

$$\theta = \frac{[\text{Drug}]^n}{EC_{50}^n + [\text{Drug}]^n}$$

where:
- $\theta$ = fractional sodium-channel block
- $EC_{50}$ ≈ **0.01 mM** for Procaine on isolated nerve
- $n$ (Hill coefficient) ≈ **1**

### 2.3.6 Critical QSP (Quantitative Structure–Property) Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Procaine Value | Engineering/Biological Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight | MW | 272.77 g/mol | Drug-likeness (Lipinski: ≤ 500) |
| Partition coefficient | Log P | 1.92 | Membrane permeability |
| Acid dissociation | pKa | 8.9 | Ionization at physiological pH |
| Plasma half-life | t₁/₂ | 7.7 min | Duration of action |
| Plasma protein binding | %PB | ≈ 6% | Low → freely diffusible |
| Therapeutic index | TI | ≈ 7–10 | Margin of safety |
| Topological polar surface area | tPSA | 55.56 Å² | BBB/cell permeability (Lipinski: ≤ 140) |

## 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering Practice

In the **pharmaceutical manufacturing industry**, the medicinal chemist must rapidly estimate whether a candidate anesthetic will:
1. Be **absorbed** into the nerve (depends on Log P and pKa via Henderson–Hasselbalch).
2. **Last long enough** in vivo (depends on hydrolysis rate $k_{\text{hyd}}$, dictated by the ester vs amide linker).
3. Be **safe** (depends on Therapeutic Index, related to $EC_{50}$ from Hill equation).

The Procaine molecule is the *textbook archetype* used to teach all three considerations simultaneously.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fraction of Un-Ionized Procaine at Physiological pH

**Problem (KTU-style):** Calculate the percentage of Procaine existing as the **un-ionized free base** (B) at physiological pH 7.4, given $\text{pKa} = 8.9$.

### Step-by-Step Deduction

Starting from the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation:

$$pH = pK_a + \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{B}]}{[\text{BH}^+]}\right)$$

**Step 1:** Rearrange to isolate the ratio.

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{B}]}{[\text{BH}^+]}\right) = pH - pK_a$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values.

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{B}]}{[\text{BH}^+]}\right) = 7.4 - 8.9 = -1.5$$

**Step 3:** Take antilog to extract the concentration ratio.

$$\frac{[\text{B}]}{[\text{BH}^+]} = 10^{-1.5}$$

$$10^{-1.5} = \frac{1}{10^{1.5}} = \frac{1}{31.623} \approx 0.0316$$

**Step 4:** Express as fraction of the total concentration.

Let total concentration be $[\text{B}] + [\text{BH}^+] = 1.0$ (normalized unit).

$$\alpha = \frac{[\text{B}]}{[\text{B}] + [\text{BH}^+]} = \frac{0.0316}{1 + 0.0316} = \frac{0.0316}{1.0316}$$

**Step 5:** Compute final numeric value.

$$\alpha = 0.0307 \;\;\text{or equivalently}\;\; 3.07\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Only **~3% of Procaine** is in the lipid-soluble, membrane-permeating free-base form at physiological pH, while **~97%** is in the cationic (BH⁺) form that binds to the *intracellular* sodium-channel receptor. This dual requirement — un-ionized to enter, ionized to block — explains Procaine's **moderate onset of action**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Plasma Half-Life from Hydrolysis Data

**Problem:** If the pseudo-first-order rate constant for Procaine hydrolysis by plasma pseudocholinesterase at 37 °C is $k_{\text{hyd}} = 0.09\ \text{min}^{-1}$, calculate the plasma half-life.

### Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1:** State the integrated first-order rate law.

$$\ln[\text{Procaine}]_t = \ln[\text{Procaine}]_0 - k_{\text{hyd}} \cdot t$$

**Step 2:** Define half-life condition. At $t = t_{1/2}$, the concentration drops to half:

$$[\text{Procaine}]_{t_{1/2}} = \frac{[\text{Procaine}]_0}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the rate law.

$$\ln\left(\frac{[\text{Procaine}]_0}{2}\right) = \ln[\text{Procaine}]_0 - k_{\text{hyd}} \cdot t_{1/2}$$

**Step 4:** Use logarithmic identity $\ln(\frac{x}{2}) = \ln x - \ln 2$.

$$\ln[\text{Procaine}]_0 - \ln 2 = \ln[\text{Procaine}]_0 - k_{\text{hyd}} \cdot t_{1/2}$$

**Step 5:** Cancel common term $\ln[\text{Procaine}]_0$.

$$-\ln 2 = -k_{\text{hyd}} \cdot t_{1/2}$$

**Step 6:** Solve algebraically for $t_{1/2}$.

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_{\text{hyd}}}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute $k_{\text{hyd}} = 0.09\ \text{min}^{-1}$.

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{0.09\ \text{min}^{-1}}$$

$$t_{1/2} \approx 7.7\ \text{minutes}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Compare this with **Lidocaine** (an amide, $t_{1/2} \approx 90$ min) — *esters are 10× shorter acting*. This is why ester anesthetics are preferred for **short dental procedures**, and amides for **longer surgeries**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hydrolysis Reaction Mechanism (Stoichiometric Balance)

Procaine undergoes hydrolysis at the **ester carbonyl**, catalyzed by plasma pseudocholinesterase. The stoichiometry is:

$$\text{Procaine} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{pseudocholinesterase}} \text{PABA} + \text{Diethylaminoethanol}$$

In molecular terms:

$$C_{13}H_{20}N_2O_2 + H_2O \longrightarrow C_7H_7NO_2 + C_6H_{15}NO$$

**Molecular-weight balance check:**

$$\text{LHS} = 236.31 + 18.02 = 254.33\ \text{g/mol (free base)}$$

$$\text{RHS} = 137.14 + 117.19 = 254.33\ \text{g/mol} \;\;\checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> For the **hydrochloride salt**, both product amines pick up a proton: PABA is zwitterionic (pKa ≈ 2.5 / 4.9), and diethylaminoethanol (pKa ≈ 10.5) is fully protonated. This pH-dependent speciation is critical for renal clearance.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Therapeutic Index Calculation (Worked Example)

**Problem:** For Procaine in a rodent assay, $LD_{50} = 80\ \text{mg/kg}$ (intravenous) and $ED_{50} = 10\ \text{mg/kg}$ (infiltration block). Calculate the Therapeutic Index.

$$TI = \frac{LD_{50}}{ED_{50}} = \frac{80\ \text{mg/kg}}{10\ \text{mg/kg}}$$

$$TI = 8$$

> [!CAUTION]
> A TI of 8 is **narrow** by modern pharmaceutical standards. Compare with **Lidocaine (TI ≈ 6–10)** and **Bupivacaine (TI ≈ 4)** — Procaine is relatively safer than amide analogues for simple infiltration.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Procaine Pharmacokinetic Journey (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Injection of Procaine HCl 1 percent solution] --> B[Dissolution in tissue fluid pH 7.4]
    B --> C{Ionization equilibrium at pKa 8.9}
    C -->|97 percent| D[Cationic form BH plus]
    C -->|3 percent| E[Free base B un-ionized]
    E --> F[Diffuses through nerve membrane lipid bilayer]
    D --> G[Re-protonated inside axoplasm]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Binds to intracellular Domain 4 of voltage gated Na plus channel]
    H --> I[Blocks Na plus influx]
    I --> J[No depolarization No action potential]
    J --> K[Loss of sensory and motor function LOCAL ANESTHESIA]
    H --> L[Plasma pseudocholinesterase hydrolysis]
    L --> M[PABA plus Diethylaminoethanol]
    M --> N[Renal excretion within 30 minutes]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** Trace a single Procaine molecule from the syringe → through the membrane → into the sodium channel → out via hydrolysis. Each node is a *step* a pharmaceutical engineer must optimize in formulation.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Industrial Synthesis Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[PABA raw material] --> S1[Activation with thionyl chloride or PCl5]
    S1 --> S2[PABA acid chloride]
    R2[Diethylaminoethanol] --> S3[Neutralization with sodium bicarbonate]
    S2 --> R3[Esterification coupling reaction]
    S3 --> R3
    R3 --> P1[Crude Procaine base]
    P1 --> C1[Purification by recrystallization]
    C1 --> C2[Conversion to hydrochloride salt using HCl gas]
    C2 --> Q1[Pharmaceutical grade Procaine HCl USP BP]
    Q1 --> ST[Stability testing and quality control]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Representation — The LIFA Pharmacophore

```
         Lipophilic Domain
              │
              ▼
         ┌─────────┐
         │ PABA    │ ← Aromatic ring + NH₂ (donor)
         │ ring    │
         └────┬────┘
              │ Intermediate linker (ester −COO−)
              ▼
         ┌─────────┐
         │ −CH₂−CH₂− │ ← Torsional spacer
         └────┬────┘
              │ Hydrophilic domain
              ▼
         ┌─────────────┐
         │ −N(C₂H₅)₂   │ ← Tertiary amine (protonatable)
         └─────────────┘
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **aromatic–ester–amine triad** is the *defining visual signature* of an ester-type local anesthetic. Any student seeing this triad in a structural diagram in the exam should immediately classify the drug as a Procaine-family anesthetic.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Structure-Activity Relationship (SAR) Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SAR[Procaine SAR Analysis] --> SAR1[Aromatic ring]
    SAR --> SAR2[Ester linkage]
    SAR --> SAR3[Alkyl spacer]
    SAR --> SAR4[Tertiary amine]

    SAR1 --> SAR1A[para NH2 is essential]
    SAR1 --> SAR1B[meta OH or NH2 reduces potency]
    SAR1 --> SAR1C[ortho bulky groups cause steric clash]

    SAR2 --> SAR2A[Ester gives short duration]
    SAR2 --> SAR2B[Amide replacement gives Lidocaine longer duration]
    SAR2 --> SAR2C[Thioester or ketone replacement inactive]

    SAR3 --> SAR3A[2 carbon spacer is optimum]
    SAR3 --> SAR3B[3 carbon spacer lowers potency 10 fold]
    SAR3 --> SAR3C[1 carbon spacer too rigid]

    SAR4 --> SAR4A[Diethyl is balanced]
    SAR4 --> SAR4B[Dimethyl more toxic]
    SAR4 --> SAR4C[Piperidine ring gives Etidocaine]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Define the term "**local anesthetic**" and classify Procaine Hydrochloride based on its chemical linkage type.

**Model Answer:**

A **local anesthetic** is a drug that reversibly blocks the conduction of nerve impulses in a localized region of the body without causing loss of consciousness. Procaine Hydrochloride belongs to the **amino-ester class** of local anesthetics because it contains an **ester linkage (−COO−)** connecting its *p*-aminobenzoic acid moiety to its diethylaminoethanol side chain. It is the prototype of all synthetic injectable ester anesthetics, with a pKa of **8.9**, plasma half-life of **7.7 minutes**, and short duration of action of **30–60 minutes**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Write the molecular formula, molecular weight, and clinical trade name of Procaine Hydrochloride.

**Model Answer:**

- **Molecular Formula:** $C_{13}H_{20}N_2O_2 \cdot HCl$
- **Molecular Weight:** **272.77 g/mol**
- **Clinical Trade Name:** **Novocaine®** (or Novocain®); introduced commercially in **1905** by the German chemist **Alfred Einhorn**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **chemistry, structure, and synthesis** of Procaine Hydrochloride with a neat reaction scheme. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **mechanism of action** of Procaine Hydrochloride on voltage-gated sodium channels, including the role of its un-ionized and ionized forms. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Chemistry & Structure:**

Procaine Hydrochloride is **4-aminobenzoic acid 2-(diethylamino)ethyl ester hydrochloride**. Its structure consists of three pharmacophoric regions:

1. **Lipophilic aromatic ring:** *p*-aminobenzoic acid (PABA)
2. **Intermediate linker:** an **ester bond (−COO−)**
3. **Hydrophilic side chain:** tertiary amine (diethylaminoethyl)

The free base is a colorless oil, but the **hydrochloride salt** is a stable white crystalline powder soluble in water, making it suitable for injection.

**Synthesis (Reaction Scheme):**

The most common industrial route is the **esterification of *p*-aminobenzoic acid with 2-chloroethanol** followed by amination, or directly via the **Einhorn modification**:

$$\text{4-NH}_2\text{-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-COOH} + \text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+, \text{reflux}} \text{4-NH}_2\text{-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-COO-CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{Above intermediate} + \text{HN(C}_2\text{H}_5\text{)}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{base}} \text{Procaine free base}$$

$$\text{Procaine free base} + \text{HCl}_{(\text{g})} \longrightarrow \text{Procaine HCl salt}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Mechanism of Action — Modulated Receptor Hypothesis:**

Step 1: At physiological pH 7.4, only ~3% of Procaine exists as the **lipid-soluble free base (B)**. This form **diffuses passively across the axonal membrane** (which is lipid-rich).

Step 2: Inside the axoplasm (intracellular pH ~7.0), the free base picks up a proton and converts to the **cationic form (BH⁺)**.

Step 3: The cationic form binds to a specific receptor site located on the **intracellular side of the voltage-gated sodium channel**, on the **S6 segment of Domain IV**.

Step 4: This binding **stabilizes the channel in its inactivated state**, preventing the conformational change needed for sodium ion influx.

Step 5: Without $\text{Na}^+$ influx, **depolarization cannot occur**, and the action potential fails to propagate. The patient experiences **sensory and motor block** in the innervated region.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (–2 marks commonly lost):** Many students write that Procaine "blocks the nerve" without specifying *where* (intracellular S6 segment of Domain IV) and *how* (cationic form binding). Always cite both the **molecular target** and the **active form**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **metabolism and elimination** of Procaine Hydrochloride in the human body, including the role of plasma pseudocholinesterase. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Draw the **LIFA pharmacophore** of Procaine and describe how **structural modification** of each region alters pharmacological activity (Structure–Activity Relationship). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Metabolism Pathway:**

Procaine is hydrolyzed **almost entirely in the plasma** by the enzyme **butyrylcholinesterase (pseudocholinesterase)** — *not* by acetylcholinesterase. The hydrolysis occurs at the ester carbonyl:

$$\text{Procaine} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{pseudocholinesterase}} \text{PABA} + \text{Diethylaminoethanol}$$

**Kinetic Data:**
- **Plasma half-life (t₁/₂):** **7.7 minutes**
- **Plasma clearance:** Approximately **60–70 mL/min/kg**
- **Hepatic metabolism:** Negligible (because plasma hydrolysis is so fast)
- **Renal excretion:** Less than 2% of unchanged Procaine appears in urine; >98% excreted as **PABA (conjugated as glycine or glucuronide)** and **diethylaminoethanol**.

**Clinical Significance:**
1. Patients with **atypical pseudocholinesterase** (genetic deficiency) may show **procaine toxicity** due to prolonged circulation.
2. PABA metabolite can inhibit sulfonamide antibiotics — caution in concurrent therapy.
3. Short duration makes Procaine unsuitable for prolonged surgical procedures; **epinephrine 1:100,000** is often co-administered to cause local vasoconstriction, slowing systemic absorption.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**LIFA Pharmacophore Diagram:**

```
  Lipophilic     Intermediate   Hydrophilic
       │              │              │
       ▼              ▼              ▼
   ┌───────┐      ┌──────┐     ┌─────────────┐
   │ PABA  │──COO─│ CH₂CH₂│──N(C₂H₅)₂     │
   │ ring  │      │ spacer│     │              │
   └───────┘      └──────┘     └─────────────┘
```

**SAR Analysis:**

| Structural Region | Modification | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Aromatic ring | Replace PABA with xylidine (2,6-dimethyl aniline) | Yields **Lidocaine** (amide type) |
| Aromatic ring | Add ortho-Cl or F to ring | Increases lipophilicity, potency |
| Linker | Replace ester by amide | **Increases t₁/₂ to 1.5–3 hours** (Lidocaine) |
| Linker | Replace ester by thioester | Loses activity (poor receptor fit) |
| Alkyl spacer | Shorten to 1 carbon | Rigid, low potency |
| Alkyl spacer | Lengthen to 3 carbons | 10× lower potency |
| Tertiary amine | Replace diethyl by piperidine | Yields **Etidocaine** (longer acting) |
| Tertiary amine | Quaternize (permanent cation) | Cannot penetrate membrane → no block |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (–2 marks commonly lost):** Students often describe SAR without **explicit examples of drug analogues** (Lidocaine, Etidocaine, Tetracaine). Always cite *one specific marketed drug* per modification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and pin it on your study wall before every KTU exam.**

- ✅ Procaine HCl = **Novocaine®**, the *first safe injectable* local anesthetic (1905, **Alfred Einhorn**).
- ✅ Class: **Amino-ester** local anesthetic (LIFA pharmacophore: PABA–ester–amine).
- ✅ Molecular formula: **$C_{13}H_{20}N_2O_2 \cdot HCl$**, MW = **272.77 g/mol**.
- ✅ pKa = **8.9** → only **~3%** un-ionized at pH 7.4 → moderate onset.
- ✅ Plasma half-life = **7.7 minutes** → short duration (**30–60 min**).
- ✅ Metabolism: **Plasma pseudocholinesterase** (butyrylcholinesterase) → PABA + diethylaminoethanol.
- ✅ Mechanism: Binds **intracellular S6 of Domain IV** of voltage-gated Na⁺ channel; stabilizes **inactivated state**.
- ✅ Log P ≈ **1.92**, Protein binding ≈ **6%**, Therapeutic Index ≈ **8**.
- ✅ Often combined with **epinephrine 1:100,000** to prolong action via vasoconstriction.
- ✅ Allergic reactions are due to the **PABA metabolite**, not Procaine itself.
- ✅ Trade name **Novocaine** is now largely replaced by **Lidocaine** in modern clinical practice.
- ✅ SAR rule of thumb: **Ester = short**, **Amide = long**; **para-NH₂** on aromatic ring is *essential* for activity.
- ✅ Remember the **Löfgren LIFA model** — it is the *single most-asked framework* in KTU theory papers on this topic.
- ✅ Always cite both **form (B or BH⁺)** and **target site** in mechanism answers to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Para-Amino Salicylic Acid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Para-Amino Salicylic Acid # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Para-Amino Salicylic Acid (PAS)**, chemically known as **4-Amino-2-hydroxybenzoic acid** (IUPAC nomenclature), is a synthetic, bacteriostatic, second-line **anti-tuberculosis (anti-TB) drug** belonging to the class of **folic acid synthesis inhibitors**. It is structurally analogous to *p*-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) and is used almost exclusively as an adjunct agent in the multi-drug regimen for the treatment of *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* infections, particularly in cases of **multi-drug resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB)**. $$ \text{Molecular Formula: } \mathbf{C_7H_7NO_3} $$ $$ \text{Molecular Weight: } \mathbf{153.14 \text{ g/mol}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** PAS is the historical prototype of the **anti-folate anti-tuberculosis agents**. It was discovered by **Jörgen Lehmann** in **1944** and was one of the first effective treatments for TB, predating even **streptomycin** and **isoniazid**. ## 1.2 Structural Blueprint PAS is a derivative of **salicylic acid** (ortho-hydroxybenzoic acid) bearing an additional **amino group (–NH₂)** at the **para position** (carbon-4) relative to the carboxylic acid group. | Feature | Position on Benzene Ring | Description | |:---:|:---:|:---| | –COOH | C-1 | Carboxylic acid (reference carbon) | | –OH | C-2 | Hydroxyl group (ortho) | | –H | C-3 | Unsubstituted (meta) | | –NH₂ | C-4 | Primary aromatic amine (**para**) | | –H | C-5 | Unsubstituted (meta) | | –H | C-6 | Unsubstituted (ortho) | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Molecular Imposter" Imagine the tuberculosis bacterium as a small factory that requires a specific raw material called **PABA** (*p*-aminobenzoic acid) to manufacture its essential vitamin "**folic acid**." The bacterium uses an enzyme assembly line (dihydropteroate synthase) that recognises PABA by its shape and functional groups. Now, **PAS is a "look-alike imposter"** of PABA — its molecular structure is so similar (an amino group on a benzene ring with a carboxyl group) that the bacterial enzyme mistakenly accepts PAS instead of PABA. Once PAS is loaded onto the assembly line, the entire manufacturing process grinds to a halt because the product (folic acid) cannot be completed. Without folic acid, the bacterium **cannot synthesize DNA, RNA, and proteins**, and eventually stops multiplying. This is a classic example of **competitive enzyme inhibition**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuitive Summary:** PAS does **not** kill the TB bacterium outright (bacteriostatic, not bactericidal). It simply "starves" it of folic acid by impersonating PABA, holding the bacterial population in check while other drugs (like **isoniazid**, **rifampicin**, **ethambutol**) actively destroy the cells. ## 1.4 Physicochemical Properties at a Glance | Property | Value / Description | |:---|:---| | **Physical Appearance** | White to off-white crystalline powder | | **Stability** | Darkens upon prolonged exposure to light and air | | **Melting Point** | ~ **150–151 °C** (with decomposition) | | **Solubility in Water** | Slightly soluble (~ 1 g/L at 20 °C) | | **Solubility in Ethanol** | Freely soluble | | **Solubility in Ether** | Soluble | | **pKa₁ (carboxyl –COOH)** | ≈ **3.25** | | **pKa₂ (aromatic –OH)** | ≈ **9.78** | | **Partition Coefficient (log P)** | ≈ **0.89** (moderately hydrophilic) | > [!NOTE] > **Examination Tip:** The darkening of PAS upon light exposure is a frequently tested point because it indicates **photochemical oxidation** of the aromatic –NH₂ group, leading to the formation of coloured quinonoid polymers. Hence, PAS formulations are always stored in **amber-coloured, light-resistant containers**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework ### A. Mechanism of Action — Competitive Antagonism of PABA The anti-mycobacterial action of PAS is best understood through the **Wood-Field pathway** of bacterial folate biosynthesis: 1. **Normal Bacterial Pathway:** PABA + Dihydropteridine-PP → Dihydropteroate (catalysed by **dihydropteroate synthase**) → Dihydrofolate → Tetrahydrofolate (THF). 2. **THF** is essential for one-carbon transfers in **purine** and **thymidylate** biosynthesis. 3. **PAS as a Substrate Mimic:** Because PAS differs from PABA by only **one hydroxyl group at the ortho position**, it is recognised by **dihydropteroate synthase**. 4. **Failed Product:** The enzyme condenses PAS with dihydropteridine-PP to form a **pseudo-pteroate** that **cannot** be converted to dihydrofolate. 5. **Result:** THF pool collapses → DNA/RNA synthesis halts → **bacteriostasis**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Why" Matters:** Mammals **do not** synthesise folate (they absorb it from diet); hence, PAS is **selectively toxic** to bacteria — a beautiful example of the **biochemical selectivity principle** taught in pharmaceutical chemistry. ### B. Structure–Activity Relationship (SAR) — The KTU Favourite | Structural Variation | Effect on Anti-TB Activity | |:---|:---| | –NH₂ at **para** position (4-position) | **Maximum** activity (as in PAS) | | –NH₂ at ortho or meta position | Markedly **reduced** activity | | Replacement of –COOH with –SO₃H or –CONH₂ | Loss of activity | | Removal of –OH (i.e., *p*-aminobenzoic acid) | **No anti-TB activity** (used as growth factor by bacteria!) | | Replacement of aromatic ring with aliphatic chain | **Complete loss** of activity | | Esterification of –COOH (e.g., methyl ester) | **Inactive** as such; must hydrolyse in vivo to free acid | > [!NOTE] > **Critical SAR Insight:** Both the **–NH₂ (para)** and the **–OH (ortho)** groups are **obligatory pharmacophoric features**. The –OH group forms an **intramolecular hydrogen bond** with the adjacent –COOH, locking the carboxyl in a conformation that best mimics the transition state of the enzyme–substrate complex. ### C. Pharmacokinetic Profile | Parameter | Value | |:---|:---| | **Oral Bioavailability** | ~ **77–88 %** (rapidly absorbed in GI tract) | | **Peak Plasma Time (Tmax)** | **1–2 hours** | | **Plasma Protein Binding** | ~ **50–60 %** | | **Volume of Distribution** | ~ **0.5–0.9 L/kg** (good tissue penetration, including TB granulomas) | | **Metabolism** | Hepatic — **N-acetylation** (inactivated) and glucuronide conjugation | | **Elimination Half-life (t½)** | ~ **1 hour** (short — necessitates frequent dosing) | | **Excretion** | Renal (> 80 % as N-acetyl-PAS) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Frequently Asked:** The **N-acetylation polymorphism** (fast vs slow acetylators) is a key pharmacogenomic concept. *Slow acetylators* experience higher plasma concentrations of free PAS, leading to greater **gastrointestinal and hepatic toxicity**, whereas *fast acetylators* may have sub-therapeutic levels. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept / Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Molecular Weight of PAS** | $M = 7(12) + 7(1) + 14 + 3(16)$ | g/mol | $84 + 7 + 14 + 48 = 153.14$ | | **Molarity of stock solution** | $M = \dfrac{m_{\text{solute}} \times 1000}{M_w \times V_{\text{solution}}}$ | mol/L | Standard preparative formula | | **Henderson–Hasselbalch (acid form)** | $\text{pH} = pK_a + \log\dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$ | dimensionless | For –COOH group (pKa₁ ≈ 3.25) | | **Henderson–Hasselbalch (phenolic form)** | $\text{pH} = pK_a + \log\dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$ | dimensionless | For –OH group (pKa₂ ≈ 9.78) | | **Dose Calculation** | $D_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{D_{\text{desired}}}{S \times V}$ | mg/kg or mg | For experimental pharmacology | | **Percentage Yield (synthesis)** | $\%\text{ yield} = \dfrac{\text{Actual yield}}{\text{Theoretical yield}} \times 100$ | % | Critical for synthesis-based exam questions | | **Partition Coefficient** | $P = \dfrac{C_{\text{octanol}}}{C_{\text{water}}}$ ; $\log P = \log_{10}(P)$ | dimensionless | PAS log P ≈ 0.89 (hydrophilic) | | **Bioavailability (oral)** | $F = \dfrac{AUC_{\text{oral}}}{AUC_{\text{iv}}} \times 100$ | % | Used in pharmacokinetic tables | | **Clearance (renal)** | $Cl = k_e \times V_d$ | mL/min | $k_e$ = elimination rate constant | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world Engineering Utility:** The log P value of **0.89** for PAS informs pharmaceutical formulation scientists that the drug prefers aqueous environments — hence it is formulated as **sodium salt granules/solutions** (Na-PAS) to enhance aqueous solubility for oral and parenteral delivery. ## 2.3 Pharmaceutical Formulations Marketed | Brand / Form | Description | |:---|:---| | **Sodium PAS granules** | Water-soluble, sachet form for oral reconstitution | | **PAS Calcium tablets** | Less GI irritation, used historically | | **PAS Potassium tablets** | Modern alternative; better tolerability | | **Enteric-coated tablets** | To bypass gastric acid degradation | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Molecular Weight of PAS (C₇H₇NO₃) Step 1 — Identify the atomic composition from the molecular formula: - Carbon (C): **7 atoms** - Hydrogen (H): **7 atoms** - Nitrogen (N): **1 atom** - Oxygen (O): **3 atoms** Step 2 — Recall the standard atomic weights (IUPAC 2021): - C = **12.011 g/mol** - H = **1.008 g/mol** - N = **14.007 g/mol** - O = **15.999 g/mol** Step 3 — Compute the contribution of each element using the standard summation form: $$ M_{\text{PAS}} = (n_C \times A_C) + (n_H \times A_H) + (n_N \times A_N) + (n_O \times A_O) $$ Step 4 — Substitute the values into the expression: $$ M_{\text{PAS}} = (7 \times 12.011) + (7 \times 1.008) + (1 \times 14.007) + (3 \times 15.999) $$ Step 5 — Evaluate each multiplication term: $$ \begin{aligned} (7 \times 12.011) &= 84.077 \text{ g/mol} \quad \text{(Carbon contribution)} \\ (7 \times 1.008) &= 7.056 \text{ g/mol} \quad \text{(Hydrogen contribution)} \\ (1 \times 14.007) &= 14.007 \text{ g/mol} \quad \text{(Nitrogen contribution)} \\ (3 \times 15.999) &= 47.997 \text{ g/mol} \quad \text{(Oxygen contribution)} \end{aligned} $$ Step 6 — Sum the individual contributions to obtain the final molecular weight: $$ M_{\text{PAS}} = 84.077 + 7.056 + 14.007 + 47.997 = 153.137 \text{ g/mol} $$ Step 7 — Round to two decimal places as per pharmaceutical convention: $$ \boxed{M_{\text{PAS}} \approx 153.14 \text{ g/mol}} $$ > [!NOTE] > This value matches the official pharmacopoeial standard (Indian Pharmacopoeia, USP, BP) for para-amino salicylic acid. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Synthesis Route — Industrial Kolbe–Schmitt Pathway The industrial synthesis of PAS proceeds through the **Kolbe–Schmitt carboxylation reaction** applied to *m*-aminophenol: ### Reaction Scheme $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1 — Salt formation:} \quad & m\text{-aminophenol} + \text{KOH (or NaOH)} \rightarrow m\text{-aminophenolate salt} \\ \text{Step 2 — Carboxylation:} \quad & m\text{-aminophenolate} + \text{CO}_2 \; (\text{high pressure, } \sim 5 \text{–} 10 \text{ atm}) \xrightarrow{120{-}150\,^\circ\text{C}} \text{Potassium 4-amino-salicylate (dipotassium salt)} \\ \text{Step 3 — Acidification:} \quad & \text{Potassium salt} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{PAS (free acid)} \downarrow + \text{KCl} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mechanistic Note:** The Kolbe–Schmitt reaction is **regioselective** — the –O⁻ group is a strong **ortho/para director** (activator), and the bulky potassium ion forms a chelate intermediate that directs CO₂ to the position *ortho* to the –OH group (which becomes the 2-position in the final product) and *para* to the –NH₂ group (which is the 4-position in the final product). This regiochemistry is **mandatory** — the 4-amino-2-hydroxy isomer is the only therapeutically active form. ### Detailed Mechanistic Highlights (one-step carboxylation) $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{(a) } \text{ArO}^- + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{ArO–COO}^- \;\; (\text{chelate intermediate, } \pi\text{-complex with } K^+) \\ &\text{(b) Rearrangement of chelate to give } \sigma\text{-bonded carboxylate at the activated position} \\ &\text{(c) Proton transfer / acidification yields the free } \text{–COOH group of PAS} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Identification Tests for PAS | Test | Reagent | Observation | Inference | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Melting point** | Capillary tube / melting point apparatus | **150–151 °C** (with decomposition) | Confirms identity of PAS | | **Ferric chloride (FeCl₃) test** | Neutral FeCl₃ solution | **Violet to purple** colour | Phenolic –OH group present | | **Bromine water test** | Excess Br₂ / H₂O | **White precipitate** of tribromo-derivative | Activated aromatic ring (–NH₂ and –OH are both strong activators) | | **Diazotisation test** | NaNO₂ + HCl (0–5 °C) → couple with β-naphthol | **Red / orange azo dye** | Primary aromatic –NH₂ confirmed | | **Carbon dioxide evolution (effervescence)** | NaHCO₃ solution | **Brisk effervescence** (CO₂) | Free –COOH group present | | **UV max** | Spectrophotometer in 0.1 N HCl | **λmax ≈ 265 nm** and **300 nm** | Characteristic PAS spectrum | | **IR spectroscopy** | KBr pellet | Broad –OH/–NH₂ (~ 3300 cm⁻¹); C=O of –COOH (~ 1650 cm⁻¹); aromatic C=C (~ 1600 cm⁻¹) | All functional groups confirmed | | **Solubility in NaHCO₃** | Saturated NaHCO₃ | Soluble with effervescence | Confirms acid functionality | > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing the **ferric chloride** test of PAS (violet — due to phenolic –OH) with the ferric chloride test of **salicylic acid** (violet — same). The distinction is made by the **diazotisation test**, which is positive for PAS (–NH₂) but negative for salicylic acid. ## 3.4 Calculation: Theoretical Yield of PAS from *m*-Aminophenol Given: Starting material = **100 g** of *m*-aminophenol (M_w = 109.13 g/mol). Assuming **100 % stoichiometric efficiency**: Step 1 — Calculate moles of starting material: $$ n_{m\text{-aminophenol}} = \dfrac{100 \text{ g}}{109.13 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.9163 \text{ mol} $$ Step 2 — Apply the **1 : 1 stoichiometry** of the Kolbe–Schmitt carboxylation: $$ n_{\text{PAS, theoretical}} = 0.9163 \text{ mol} $$ Step 3 — Convert to mass using molecular weight of PAS: $$ \begin{aligned} m_{\text{PAS, theoretical}} &= n_{\text{PAS}} \times M_{\text{PAS}} \\ &= 0.9163 \text{ mol} \times 153.14 \text{ g/mol} \\ &= 140.30 \text{ g} \end{aligned} $$ Step 4 — If the **actual yield** obtained in the laboratory was **112.24 g**, calculate the **percentage yield**: $$ \% \text{ yield} = \dfrac{112.24}{140.30} \times 100 = 80.00 \, \% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This type of calculation is **extremely common** in KTU synthesis-based questions. Memorise the formula: % yield = (actual / theoretical) × 100. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Two-Dimensional Structural Diagram of PAS ```mermaid flowchart LR ring(["Benzene Ring
C1 - C6"]) C1["C1 - COOH
Carboxylic acid"] C2["C2 - OH
Hydroxyl ortho"] C3["C3 - H
Meta unsubstituted"] C4["C4 - NH2
Amino para"] C5["C5 - H
Meta unsubstituted"] C6["C6 - H
Ortho unsubstituted"] ring --- C1 ring --- C2 ring --- C3 ring --- C4 ring --- C5 ring --- C6 C1 --- note1["Reference carbon
anchors numbering"] C2 --- note2["Intramolecular H-bond
with COOH"] C4 --- note3["Mimics PABA
binds dihydropteroate
synthase"] ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Mechanism of Action ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Mycobacterium tuberculosis cell"] --> B["Requires PABA
for folate synthesis"] B --> C["Dihydropteroate synthase enzyme"] C --> D["PABA binds enzyme"] D --> E["Produces dihydropteroate"] E --> F["Produces dihydrofolate THF"] F --> G["DNA RNA Protein synthesis
Bacteria survive"] P1["PAS administered orally"] --> P2["Absorbed GI tract"] P2 --> P3["Enters bacterial cell"] P3 --> C C --> H["PAS binds enzyme
by mistake"] H --> I["Forms pseudo-pteroate
inactive product"] I --> J["THF pool depleted"] J --> K["Bacteriostasis
TB growth halted"] style C fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px style K fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px style P1 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Process Flow — Industrial Synthesis (Kolbe–Schmitt) ```mermaid flowchart LR R1["Raw material:
m-aminophenol"] --> R2["Dissolve in
KOH or NaOH"] R2 --> R3["Form m-aminophenolate
potassium salt"] R3 --> R4["High-pressure
carboxylation
120-150 deg C
5-10 atm CO2"] R4 --> R5["Dipotassium
4-aminosalicylate"] R5 --> R6["Acidification
with HCl"] R6 --> R7["PAS crystals
precipitate"] R7 --> R8["Filtration
Washing
Drying"] R8 --> R9["Final API
Bulk PAS powder"] style R4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px style R9 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Structural Block Diagram — PAS vs PABA ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PABA["PABA - p-aminobenzoic acid"] P1["C1 - COOH"] --- P2["C2 - H"] P2 --- P3["C3 - H"] P3 --- P4["C4 - NH2"] P4 --- P5["C5 - H"] P5 --- P6["C6 - H"] end subgraph PAS["PAS - 4-amino-2-hydroxybenzoic acid"] Q1["C1 - COOH"] --- Q2["C2 - OH"] Q2 --- Q3["C3 - H"] Q3 --- Q4["C4 - NH2"] Q4 --- Q5["C5 - H"] Q5 --- Q6["C6 - H"] end style PABA fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px style PAS fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Visual Takeaway:** The **only difference** between PABA and PAS is the **ortho-hydroxyl (–OH) at C-2** in PAS. This single atom of distinction converts a **bacterial growth factor (PABA)** into a **life-saving anti-tuberculosis drug (PAS)** — an elegant example of how subtle structural modifications alter biological function. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define Para-Amino Salicylic Acid. Write its IUPAC name, molecular formula, and molecular weight.** **Model Answer:** Para-Amino Salicylic Acid (PAS) is a **second-line anti-tuberculosis drug** belonging to the anti-folate class, used as an adjunct in the treatment of multi-drug resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB). - **IUPAC Name:** 4-Amino-2-hydroxybenzoic acid - **Molecular Formula:** C₇H₇NO₃ - **Molecular Weight:** 153.14 g/mol > [!TIP] > Examiners award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for IUPAC name + formula, 1 mark for molecular weight. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Explain the mechanism of anti-tuberculosis action of Para-Amino Salicylic Acid.** **Model Answer:** PAS acts as a **structural analogue of *p*-aminobenzoic acid (PABA)**. It competitively inhibits the bacterial enzyme **dihydropteroate synthase**, thereby blocking the condensation of PABA with dihydropteridine-PP to form dihydropteroate. This halts the biosynthesis of **folic acid (tetrahydrofolate, THF)** in *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, depriving the bacterium of an essential cofactor for **purine and thymidylate synthesis**, ultimately leading to **bacteriostasis**. PAS is **selectively toxic** to bacteria because mammals obtain folate from their diet and do not possess this pathway. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer **one full question** (with sub-parts a and b, 7 marks each). The two alternatives given below are mutually exclusive choices. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]* **(a) Discuss the synthesis of Para-Amino Salicylic Acid by the Kolbe–Schmitt reaction. Add a note on the regioselectivity of the reaction.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Salt formation:** *m*-Aminophenol is dissolved in aqueous KOH (or NaOH) to form the corresponding **potassium *m*-aminophenolate salt**. This deprotonation of the phenolic –OH generates the highly nucleophilic phenoxide ion. **Step 2 — Carboxylation (Kolbe–Schmitt):** The dry potassium salt is heated with **CO₂ under pressure (5–10 atm)** at **120–150 °C**. CO₂ attacks the activated aromatic ring, and the bulky **K⁺ ion forms a chelated transition state** that directs the incoming –COOH group to the position **ortho to the –O⁻ (C-2)** and **para to the –NH₂ (C-4)**, yielding **dipotassium 4-aminosalicylate**. **Step 3 — Acidification:** The dipotassium salt is acidified with dilute **HCl** to liberate the free acid form, which **precipitates** as white crystals of PAS. **Regioselectivity Note:** The –O⁻ group is a strong **ortho/para activator and director**. The combination of –O⁻ (at C-1) and –NH₂ (at C-3 of starting material) directs the electrophile to the only doubly-activated position, which becomes the 4-amino-2-hydroxy arrangement. **Any other regioisomer is therapeutically inactive.** **Step 4 — Purification:** Recrystallisation from hot water or aqueous ethanol. **(b) Write the structure–activity relationship (SAR) of PAS. Why is the para-amino and ortho-hydroxy arrangement critical for activity?** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** | SAR Feature | Effect | |:---|:---| | 1. **–NH₂ at para position** | **Essential** for binding to dihydropteroate synthase. Shifting –NH₂ to ortho/meta drastically reduces activity. | | 2. **–OH at ortho position** | Forms an **intramolecular hydrogen bond** with –COOH, locking the molecule in the bioactive conformation. Removal of –OH (i.e., PABA) eliminates anti-TB activity. | | 3. **–COOH (free acid)** | Required for ionic interaction with the enzyme's active site. **Esterification abolishes activity** unless hydrolysed in vivo. | | 4. **Aromatic benzene ring** | Provides the rigid scaffold; aliphatic analogues are inactive. | | 5. **Substituents on C-3, C-5, C-6** | Generally reduce activity due to steric hindrance. | **Why para + ortho arrangement is critical:** - The para-amino group mimics the PABA amine precisely. - The ortho-hydroxyl group forms a **pseudo-chelate** with –COOH, mimicking the geometry of the natural PABA–pteridine transition state. - This dual feature is recognised by the **active site topology** of *M. tuberculosis* dihydropteroate synthase, ensuring **selective toxicity**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]* **(a) Describe the physicochemical properties and identification tests for Para-Amino Salicylic Acid.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Physicochemical Properties:** - White to off-white crystalline powder - Darkens on exposure to light - Melting point: **150–151 °C** (with decomposition) - Slightly soluble in water, freely soluble in ethanol and ether - pKa₁ (–COOH) ≈ 3.25; pKa₂ (–OH) ≈ 9.78 - log P ≈ 0.89 (moderately hydrophilic) - Forms complexes with Fe³⁺ (violet), Cu²⁺ (green), and other transition metals **Identification Tests:** 1. **Melting point determination:** 150–151 °C (matches official monograph). 2. **Ferric chloride test:** Forms a **violet/purple complex** (phenolic –OH confirmed). 3. **Diazotisation test:** PAS + NaNO₂/HCl (0–5 °C) forms a diazonium salt, which couples with **β-naphthol** to yield a **red azo dye** (aromatic –NH₂ confirmed). 4. **Bromine water test:** Forms a **white precipitate** of tribromo-PAS (activated ring confirmed). 5. **Effervescence with NaHCO₃:** Liberates **CO₂** (free –COOH confirmed). 6. **UV spectroscopy:** λmax ≈ **265 nm** and **300 nm** in 0.1 N HCl. 7. **IR spectroscopy:** Broad –OH/–NH₂ (3300 cm⁻¹); C=O of –COOH (1650 cm⁻¹); aromatic C=C (1600 cm⁻¹); C–N stretch (1280 cm⁻¹). **(b) Explain the pharmacokinetics, therapeutic uses, and adverse effects of PAS.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Pharmacokinetics:** - **Absorption:** Rapidly absorbed orally; bioavailability ~ 77–88 %; Tmax = 1–2 h. - **Distribution:** Plasma protein binding ~ 50–60 %; V_d ≈ 0.5–0.9 L/kg; good penetration into TB granulomas. - **Metabolism:** Primarily hepatic via **N-acetylation** (subject to **acetylator polymorphism**). - **Excretion:** Renal; t½ ≈ 1 h; > 80 % as N-acetyl-PAS. **Therapeutic Uses:** 1. **Multi-drug resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB)** in combination with other anti-TB drugs. 2. Historically used as a first-line agent (now second-line due to GI intolerance). 3. As the **sodium or potassium salt** for better aqueous solubility in oral formulations. **Adverse Effects:** 1. **Gastrointestinal:** Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea (most common; cause of poor compliance). 2. **Hepatic:** Hepatotoxicity (elevated liver enzymes, jaundice). 3. **Hypersensitivity:** Rash, fever, eosinophilia. 4. **Haematological:** Rarely, agranulocytosis, leukopenia. 5. **Renal:** Crystalluria (rare with adequate hydration). 6. **Drug interaction:** Reduces **rifampicin** absorption (separate dosing by 8–12 h). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Many students write the **mechanism of action** of PAS as "kills bacteria by inhibiting cell wall synthesis" — this is **WRONG**. PAS inhibits **folic acid synthesis** (similar to sulfonamides). Confusing PAS with **isoniazid** (which inhibits mycolic acid synthesis) is a **fatal mark-loss error**. > - For SAR questions, students frequently forget to mention that **removal of –OH** converts PAS into **PABA**, which is a **bacterial growth factor** — a key board-exam pointer worth 1–2 marks. > - For synthesis questions, omitting the **regioselectivity explanation** (role of K⁺ chelate) typically costs **2 marks** out of the 7. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **IUPAC Name:** 4-Amino-2-hydroxybenzoic acid | **M.W.:** 153.14 g/mol | **Formula:** C₇H₇NO₃ - ✅ **Drug Class:** Anti-folate, bacteriostatic, **second-line anti-tuberculosis agent** - ✅ **Mechanism:** Competitive inhibitor of **dihydropteroate synthase** → blocks folate synthesis → bacteriostasis - ✅ **Key Pharmacophore:** **Para –NH₂** + **ortho –OH** + **free –COOH** (all three are obligatory) - ✅ **Synthesis:** **Kolbe–Schmitt carboxylation** of *m*-aminophenol with CO₂ under pressure (5–10 atm) at 120–150 °C - ✅ **SAR Critical Point:** Removal of –OH converts drug (PAS) into growth factor (PABA) — a board favourite - ✅ **Identification:** Melting point 150–151 °C; FeCl₃ = violet; diazotisation = red azo dye; effervescence with NaHCO₃ - ✅ **Adverse Effects:** GI upset (most common), hepatotoxicity, hypersensitivity, acetylator polymorphism - ✅ **Storage:** **Amber, light-resistant containers** (darkens on light exposure due to oxidation of –NH₂) - ✅ **Drug Interaction:** Reduces rifampicin absorption (separate dosing by 8–12 h) - ✅ **History:** Discovered by **Jörgen Lehmann in 1944** (first effective oral anti-TB drug) - ✅ **log P:** ≈ 0.89 (moderately hydrophilic — formulated as Na⁺/K⁺ salt for solubility) - ✅ **Memorise the % yield formula:** % yield = (actual / theoretical) × 100

topics.title: Isonicotinic Acid Hydrazide
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Isonicotinic Acid Hydrazide

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Isonicotinic Acid Hydrazide (INH)**, internationally recognized by its non-proprietary name **Isoniazid**, is a synthetic, first-line **antitubercular (anti-TB) chemotherapeutic agent** classified under the hydrazide class of pharmaceuticals. It is the hydrazide derivative of isonicotinic acid (pyridine-4-carboxylic acid), where the carboxylic acid group (–COOH) is condensed with hydrazine (H₂N–NH₂) to yield the –CO–NH–NH₂ functional group.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT751 – Module 1):** Study of important drug classes with respect to their *chemical name, structure, synthesis, SAR, mechanism of action, and therapeutic uses*. Isoniazid is a compulsory drug asked regularly under antitubercular agents.

**Chemical Identifiers (Board-Exam Relevant):**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| IUPAC Name | **Pyridine-4-carbohydrazide** |
| Synonyms | Isoniazid, INH, Rimifon® |
| Molecular Formula | **C₆H₇N₃O** |
| Molar Mass | **137.14 g/mol** |
| CAS Registry No. | **54-85-3** |
| ATC Code | J04AC01 |
| Melting Point | **171–173 °C** |
| pKa | 1.8 (pyridinium), 3.5 (hydrazide) |
| log P | –0.7 (highly polar) |
| BCS Class | Class I/III (high solubility) |
| Appearance | White, odourless crystalline powder |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Trojan Horse of TB Bacteria"**
> Imagine a heavily fortified castle (the *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* bacterium) protected by a thick, waxy wall made of bricks called **mycolic acids**. The castle cannot be destroyed from outside.
> Isoniazid is a small, harmless-looking key (the drug) that walks in disguised as a normal citizen (it mimics the natural substrate **nicotinamide**, which the bacterium recognizes as food). Once inside, the bacterium's own **enzymatic machinery (KatG – catalase/peroxidase)** activates the drug into a lethal weapon. The weapon jams the brick-making machine (**InhA enzyme – enoyl-ACP reductase**), so the wall stops growing, and the bacterium eventually collapses.
> *In short:* **INH = Trojan Horse + Saboteur**, activated by the bacterium's own enzyme to attack its cell wall.

### 1.3 Historical & Therapeutic Context

- Discovered in **1952** by three independent research groups (Bayer, Hoffmann-La Roche, and Squibb).
- Remains the **cornerstone of the DOTS regimen** (Directly Observed Treatment, Short-course) recommended by the WHO.
- Administered as a **prodrug** — pharmacologically inactive until bio-activated.
- Always used in **combination therapy** (with rifampicin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol) to prevent resistance.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of Isoniazid

Isoniazid is built from three pharmacophoric elements essential for antitubercular activity:

1. **Pyridine ring (heterocyclic nucleus)** — provides the *carrier specificity*; the bacterium's enzymes recognize the ring and pull the drug inside.
2. **Carbohydrazide linker (–CO–NH–NH₂)** — the *reactive warhead*; this hydrazide function undergoes KatG-mediated oxidation to the reactive acyl radical.
3. **Terminal –NH₂ group** — the *hydrogen-bond donor/acceptor*; critical for binding to the NAD⁺ cofactor pocket of InhA.

The intact **–CO–NH–NH₂** group is a **non-classical bioisostere of the pyridine-carboxamide (–CO–NH₂)** moiety found in nicotinamide (vitamin B₃) and in the coenzyme NAD⁺. This explains the **molecular mimicry** at the InhA active site.

### 2.2 Physicochemical Properties (Board-Exam Critical)

- **Solubility:** Freely soluble in water (≈ 140 mg/mL at 25 °C) and in boiling ethanol; sparingly soluble in cold ethanol; practically insoluble in ether and chloroform.
- **Stability:** Sensitive to heat, light, and moisture. Undergoes **hydrolytic degradation** to **isonicotinic acid + hydrazine** in acidic/alkaline media (important for formulation shelf-life).
- **Oxidation:** Reacts with mild oxidizing agents (e.g., Fehling's, Tollens', ammonium vanadate) → characteristic colour reactions used in **identification tests**.
- **Crystallinity:** Polymorphic — Form I (stable, m.p. 171.4 °C) and Form II (metastable, m.p. ~167 °C) are pharmaceutically important.
- **Polarity:** log P ≈ –0.7 → **very hydrophilic**, hence crosses inflamed meninges (used in TB-meningitis).

### 2.3 Identification Tests (Indian Pharmacopoeia / IP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These are high-frequency 3-mark questions in KTU exams. Memorize at least 3 tests.**

| S.No. | Test | Reagent | Observation | Inference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Tollens' test** | Ammoniacal AgNO₃ | Silver mirror on warm water bath | Reduces Ag⁺ → confirms hydrazide –CONHNH₂ group |
| 2 | **Fehling's test** | Fehling A + B (heated) | Red precipitate of Cu₂O | Reducing nature of hydrazide |
| 3 | **Ammonium vanadate test** | NH₄VO₃ in H₂SO₄ | Yellow → **green colour** | Specific for hydrazide –CO–NH–NH₂ |
| 4 | **2,4-DNP test** | Brady's reagent | Orange/yellow hydrazone precipitate | Carbonyl of amide |
| 5 | **Picrate test** | Saturated picric acid | **Yellow crystalline picrate**, m.p. 187 °C | Salt formation with basic pyridine N |
| 6 | **König reaction** | CNBr + barbituric acid | Yellow → red-violet colour | Confirms pyridine ring |
| 7 | **Fluorimetry (USP)** | Excitation λ ~ 300 nm; Emission ~ 410 nm | Intense fluorescence | Quantification in plasma |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Stoichiometric Reference

The relevant stoichiometric relationship for **assay by bromatometry (KBrO₃–KBr)** is:

$$ \text{INH} \;+\; 2\,\text{KBrO}_3 \;+\; 2\,\text{HBr} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{oxidation products} \;+\; \text{N}_2 \;+\; 2\,\text{KBr} \;+\; 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

- **1 mole of Isoniazid ≡ 2 moles of KBrO₃ ≡ 4 equivalents of oxidant**
- Equivalently: **1 g-eq. of INH = 137.14 / 4 = 34.285 g**

**Titrations to remember:**

| Method | Titrant | Indicator | 1 eq. mass |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bromatometry | KBrO₃ (0.1 N) | Methyl orange / disappearance of Br₂ | **34.285 g/eq** |
| Iodometry (back) | KIO₃ + HCl → ICl | — | 34.285 g/eq |
| Cerimetry | (NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆ | Ferroin | 17.14 g/eq (8e⁻) |
| UV-Spectro | λmax = **263 nm** in 0.1 N HCl | — | A = εbc |

### 2.5 KTU Numeric / Stoichiometry Cheat-Sheet Table

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of \vert in the cells below to preserve KTU markdown table integrity.

| Derivation Step | Mathematical Expression | Numerical / Limiting-Case Value |
|---|---|---|
| Equivalent mass of INH (4e⁻) | $E = M / 4 = 137.14 / 4$ | **34.285 g/eq** |
| Molar absorptivity in 0.1 N HCl | $\varepsilon_{263}$ | ≈ **3,700 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹** |
| Log P (octanol/water) | $\log P$ | **–0.7** (highly polar) |
| pH of 1 % aq. solution | — | **≈ 6.5 – 7.5** |
| Half-life (slow acetylators) | $t_{1/2}$ | **3 – 4 h** |
| Half-life (fast acetylators) | $t_{1/2}$ | **1 – 1.5 h** |
| Oral bioavailability | F | **≈ 90 %** |
| Plasma protein binding | — | **< 10 %** |
| Vd (volume of distribution) | $0.6\,\text{L/kg}$ | Wide distribution incl. CSF |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Synthesis & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Industrial Synthesis Route (KTU-Favourite 14-Mark Question)

**Method 1 – From Isonicotinic Acid (Most Common, GMP Route)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The industrial route proceeds by converting isonicotinic acid → its ethyl ester (or acid chloride) → condensation with hydrazine hydrate.

**Step-by-step reaction sequence:**

**Step 1 — Esterification of isonicotinic acid**

Isonicotinic acid is refluxed with **absolute ethanol** in the presence of catalytic **concentrated H₂SO₄** to give **ethyl isonicotinate**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pyridine-4-COOH} \;+\; \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} \;&\xrightleftharpoons[\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4]{\text{reflux, 4–6 h}} \\
\text{Pyridine-4-COOC}_2\text{H}_5 \;&+\; \text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

- The H₂SO₄ protonates the carbonyl oxygen, making the carbon more electrophilic.
- The reaction is **reversible**; water is removed (Dean-Stark) to drive equilibrium to the right.

**Step 2 — Hydrazinolysis of the ester**

Ethyl isonicotinate reacts with **hydrazine hydrate (80 % aq. N₂H₄·H₂O)** in ethanol to give isoniazid.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pyridine-4-COOC}_2\text{H}_5 \;&+\; \text{H}_2\text{N-NH}_2\!\cdot\!\text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\;&\xrightarrow{\text{EtOH, reflux, 3 h}} \\
\text{Pyridine-4-CO-NH-NH}_2 \;&+\; \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

- Mechanism: nucleophilic addition–elimination (tetrahedral intermediate).
- The hydrazine's terminal –NH₂ attacks the carbonyl; the ethoxy group leaves.
- Crude product is recrystallized from **aqueous ethanol** to obtain pure isoniazid (m.p. 171–173 °C).

**Overall stoichiometry (net):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NO}_2 \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{N-NH}_2 \;&\longrightarrow \\
\text{C}_6\text{H}_7\text{N}_3\text{O} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O} \;&
\end{aligned}
$$

**Method 2 – From 4-Cyanopyridine (One-Pot, Lab Scale)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{4-Cyanopyridine} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{N-NH}_2 \;&\xrightarrow{\text{EtOH / H}_2\text{SO}_4,\; \Delta} \\
\text{Isoniazid} \;&(\text{via amidrazone intermediate})
\end{aligned}
$$

The nitrile (–C≡N) is first attacked by hydrazine to form an **amidrazone** (–C(=NH)–NH–NH₂), which is then hydrolysed in situ to the hydrazide (–CO–NH–NH₂).

**Method 3 – From 4-Methylpyridine (γ-Picoline) via Oxidation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{4-Methylpyridine} \;&\xrightarrow{\text{KMnO}_4 / \text{OH}^-} \text{Isonicotinic acid} \\
\text{Isonicotinic acid} \;&\xrightarrow{\text{SOCl}_2} \text{Isonicotinoyl chloride} \\
\text{Isonicotinoyl chloride} \;&\xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{N-NH}_2} \text{Isoniazid}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Detailed Analytical Method (UV-Spectrophotometric Assay)

**Aim:** Determination of isoniazid in bulk and tablet dosage form.

**Principle:** Isoniazid shows a characteristic **λmax at 263 nm** in 0.1 N HCl due to π→π* transitions of the conjugated pyridine–carbonyl chromophore.

**Procedure (Beer-Lambert application):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \;&=\; \varepsilon \, b \, c \\
\text{where } A \;&=\; \text{Absorbance (no unit)} \\
\varepsilon \;&=\; \text{Molar absorptivity (L·mol}^{-1}\!\cdot\!\text{cm}^{-1}\text{)} \\
b \;&=\; \text{Path length (cm, usually 1 cm)} \\
c \;&=\; \text{Concentration (mol/L)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Working formula for tablet assay (% purity):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ purity} \;&=\; \frac{A_{\text{sample}}}{A_{\text{standard}}} \;\times\; \frac{W_{\text{std}}}{W_{\text{sample}}} \;\times\; \frac{P}{100} \;\times\; 100 \\
\text{where } W_{\text{std}} \;&=\; \text{weight of standard} \\
W_{\text{sample}} \;&=\; \text{weight of sample} \\
P \;&=\; \text{assay purity of standard (\%)} 
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Structure–Activity Relationship (SAR) – Board-Exam Gold

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically demands SAR of important drug molecules. INH is the most-asked antitubercular SAR.

| Position / Modification | Effect on Activity |
|---|---|
| **Removal of pyridine ring** | Loss of activity (specificity for mycobacterial enzymes lost) |
| **Change of N position to 2- or 3-** | Activity ↓↓ drastically (positional isomerism critical) |
| **–CO–NH–NH₂ → –CO–NH₂ (hydrazide → amide)** | **Activity lost** (cannot form acyl-NAD⁺ adduct) |
| **N-methylation of terminal NH₂ (–CO–NH–NH–CH₃)** | Activity retained, but **toxicity increased** (releases methylhydrazine) |
| **N,N-disubstitution (e.g., N,N-dimethyl)** | Activity ↓ (steric hindrance prevents enzyme binding) |
| **Replacement of pyridine with benzene or pyrimidine** | Activity ↓ by 10–100 × |
| **Introduction of –CH= (aldehyde) in place of –CO–** | Loss of activity |
| **Conversion of –CO–NH–NH₂ to thiosemicarbazone (–CH=N–NH–CS–NH₂)** | Activity retained, different spectrum (anti-leprosy) |
| **Halogenation on ring (Cl, Br)** | Marginal activity ↓ |

**Key SAR conclusions to write in exams:**

1. The **pyridine nitrogen must be at the 4-position** (para to the carbohydrazide) for maximum antimycobacterial activity.
2. The **hydrazide function (–CO–NH–NH₂)** is essential — both H atoms on terminal N contribute to H-bonding with the enzyme.
3. The **carbonyl (C=O)** is required to form the acyl-NAD⁺ adduct.
4. **Mono-substitution** at terminal N is tolerated; **di-substitution** abolishes activity.

### 3.4 Mechanism of Action (Prodrug Activation Pathway)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Isoniazid is a **prodrug** requiring bio-activation. This is a 5–7 mark KTU-favourite.

**Step-by-step intracellular events:**

1. **Entry into bacillus:** INH diffuses passively into the mycobacterial cell.
2. **Oxidative activation by KatG (catalase-peroxidase):** The enzyme oxidises INH using H₂O₂ to generate an **acyl radical (isonicotinoyl radical)** with release of N₂.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{INH} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \;&\xrightarrow{\text{KatG}} \text{ISONICOTINOYL-NADH adduct} \\
\;&+\; \text{NAD}^+ \;\longrightarrow\; \text{ISONICOTINOYL-NAD}^+ \;\text{(active species)}
\end{aligned}
$$

3. **Inhibition of InhA (enoyl-ACP reductase):** The acyl-NAD⁺ adduct binds tightly to InhA, blocking the reduction of **trans-2-enoyl-ACP** in the **FAS-II (Fatty Acid Synthase type II) system**.
4. **Consequence:** Cessation of **mycolic acid synthesis** → loss of cell-wall integrity → bacterial death.
5. **Secondary targets:** INH-NAD adduct also inhibits **NAD⁺-dependent DNA ligase** and **antioxidant enzymes (AhpC, KatG)**.

**Resistance mechanisms (frequently asked):**

- **katG mutations** → loss of activation.
- **inhA promoter mutations** → overexpression of InhA.
- **ndh mutations** → altered NAD⁺/NADH ratio.
- **ethA/ethR** → affect activation of ethionamide (a structural analogue of INH).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of INH Bio-Activation

> [!NOTE]
> Below is a process-flow diagram representing the *intracellular fate* of isoniazid — the sequential enzymatic transformation and final target inhibition. All node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid safety rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["ORAL ADMINISTRATION OF ISONIAZID TABLET 100 MG OR 300 MG"]
    A2["ABSORPTION IN GI TRACT BIOAVAILABILITY APPROX 90 PERCENT"]
    A3["PLASMA DISTRIBUTION T1 BY 2 EQUALS 1 TO 4 HOURS"]
    A4["DIFFUSION INTO MACROPHAGES AND GRANULOMA"]
    A5["ENTRY INTO MYCOBACTERIUM TUBERCULOSIS CELL"]

    B1["KATG ENZYME CATALASE PEROXIDASE"]
    B2["OXIDATIVE ACTIVATION USING H2O2"]
    B3["FORMATION OF ISONICOTINOYL RADICAL"]
    B4["RELEASE OF N2 GAS AND WATER"]

    C1["COUPLING WITH NAD PLUS COFACTOR"]
    C2["STABLE ISONICOTINOYL NAD ADDUCT"]

    D1["TARGET ENZYME INHA ENOYL ACP REDUCTASE"]
    D2["BLOCKING OF FAS II PATHWAY"]
    D3["INHIBITION OF MYCOLIC ACID SYNTHESIS"]
    D4["BACTERIAL CELL WALL DISRUPTION"]
    D5["BACTERICIDAL EFFECT ON M TUBERCULOSIS"]

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5
    A5 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> C1 --> C2 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4 --> D5
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Industrial Synthesis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["RAW MATERIAL ISONICOTINIC ACID"]

```
