<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper Q1.a]**

**Q1. Define Isoniazid. Write its IUPAC name, molecular formula, and one therapeutic use.** **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- Isoniazid is a first-line synthetic antitubercular drug belonging to the hydrazide class.
- **IUPAC Name:** Pyridine-4-carbohydrazide
- **Molecular formula:** C₆H₇N₃O; **Molar mass:** 137.14 g/mol
- **Therapeutic use:** Treatment of pulmonary and extra-pulmonary tuberculosis (often in combination with rifampicin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol). It is also used as a **prophylactic** in close contacts of TB patients.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper Q1.b]**

**Q2. Why is isoniazid called a prodrug? Name the activating enzyme.** **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- Isoniazid is called a **prodrug** because it is pharmacologically inactive when administered and requires **bio-activation inside the mycobacterial cell** to exert its bactericidal effect.
- The **activating enzyme is KatG (catalase-peroxidase)**, which oxidises the hydrazide group using H₂O₂ to form the active isonicotinoyl-NAD⁺ adduct.
- The active adduct then inhibits **InhA (enoyl-ACP reductase)**, blocking mycolic acid synthesis.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice) (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Module 1, PECHT751):**
> - Students **lose 2 marks** by writing the synthesis without explicitly stating the role of H₂SO₄ (catalyst) and the use of Dean-Stark apparatus (water removal).
> - In mechanism questions, **skipping the prodrug-activation step loses 3–4 marks** — KatG-mediated oxidation is non-negotiable.
> - Writing "NADH" instead of "NAD⁺" (or vice-versa) for the adduct loses 1 mark.
> - Forgetting to mention the **N-acetyltransferase polymorphism (NAT2)** in metabolism loses 2 marks in pharmacokinetics questions.
> - In SAR, **positional isomers (2-isonicotinic acid hydrazide, 3-isonicotinic acid hydrazide) must be explicitly mentioned as inactive.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECHT751 Module 1, Q2.a/b]**

**(a) Outline the industrial synthesis of isoniazid starting from isonicotinic acid. Mention the role of reagents and write the mechanism of hydrazinolysis step.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Esterification step (3 marks):**
   - Reactants: Isonicotinic acid + absolute ethanol.
   - Reagent/Catalyst: Concentrated H₂SO₄ (acts as an acid catalyst by protonating the carbonyl oxygen of the acid, increasing electrophilicity).
   - Conditions: Reflux for 4–6 h with continuous water removal using a **Dean-Stark apparatus** to drive the equilibrium to the right.
   - Product: **Ethyl isonicotinate (Pyridine-4-COOC₂H₅)**.

2. **Hydrazinolysis step (3 marks):**
   - Reactants: Ethyl isonicotinate + hydrazine hydrate (80 % aq. N₂H₄·H₂O).
   - Solvent: Ethanol.
   - Conditions: Reflux 3 h; hydrazine acts as a nucleophile.
   - **Mechanism (Tetrahedral addition–elimination):**
     - The terminal –NH₂ of hydrazine attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of the ester.
     - A tetrahedral intermediate forms; the –OC₂H₅ group is expelled as ethoxide.
     - Proton transfer gives the neutral hydrazide.
   - Product: **Isoniazid (Pyridine-4-CO–NH–NH₂)**.

3. **Purification (1 mark):**
   - Recrystallisation from aqueous ethanol; pure white crystals, m.p. **171–173 °C**.

**(b) Discuss the mechanism of action of isoniazid. Why is it called a prodrug? Explain resistance mechanisms.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Prodrug nature (2 marks):**
   - INH is pharmacologically inert outside the bacterial cell.
   - The mycobacterial enzyme **KatG (catalase-peroxidase)** oxidises INH (using endogenous H₂O₂) to an **acyl radical / isonicotinoyl-NAD⁺ adduct** — the true active species.
   - This intracellular activation is the reason INH is a prodrug.

2. **Mechanism of action (3 marks):**
   - The active isonicotinoyl-NAD⁺ adduct binds tightly to **InhA (enoyl-ACP reductase)**.
   - This blocks the **FAS-II (mycolic acid synthesis) pathway**, halting the production of long-chain α-alkyl β-hydroxy fatty acids (mycolic acids).
   - Without mycolic acids, the mycobacterial cell wall becomes permeable → bactericidal effect.
   - The adduct also inhibits NAD⁺-dependent DNA ligase.

3. **Resistance mechanisms (2 marks):**
   - **katG mutations** → loss of INH activation (most common clinical mechanism, ~ 50–70 % of resistant strains).
   - **inhA promoter mutations** → overexpression of the target enzyme, overwhelming the drug.
   - **ndh mutations** → elevated intracellular NADH/NAD⁺ ratio, reducing adduct formation.
   - **ethA / ethR mutations** → cross-resistance with ethionamide (a structural analogue of INH).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECHT751 Module 1, Q2.a/b Alternate]**

**(a) Describe the structure–activity relationship (SAR) of isoniazid. What structural features are essential for antitubercular activity?** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Essential structural features (4 marks):**
   - **Pyridine ring with N at the 4-position** — provides specificity for mycobacterial enzymes; shifting N to the 2- or 3-position reduces activity drastically.
   - **Carbonyl group (C=O) at position 4** — required for formation of the acyl-NAD⁺ adduct at the InhA active site. Replacement by –CH₂ or –CH= abolishes activity.
   - **Carbohydrazide (–CO–NH–NH₂) linker** — must be intact. Conversion to simple amide (–CO–NH₂) or to ester (–COOR) destroys activity.
   - **Terminal –NH₂** — donates H-bonds to the enzyme; both H atoms participate in binding. Methylation or acylation reduces activity.

2. **Tolerated modifications (2 marks):**
   - N-mono-alkylation of terminal NH₂ retains activity but increases hepatic toxicity.
   - Ring substitution (halogens at 2- or 3-position) marginally reduces activity.

3. **Conclusion (1 mark):**
   - The **4-pyridyl carbohydrazide (isoniazid)** represents the optimal balance of lipophilicity, H-bonding capacity, and electronic effects for InhA inhibition.

**(b) Write the identification tests for isoniazid as per IP. Explain the UV-spectrophotometric method of assay with a representative calculation.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Identification tests (3 marks):**
   - **Tollens' test** – ammoniacal AgNO₃ gives a **silver mirror** (hydrazide reduces Ag⁺).
   - **Ammonium vanadate test** – yellow → **green** (specific for –CO–NH–NH₂).
   - **Picrate test** – yellow crystalline picrate, m.p. 187 °C.
   - **König reaction** – with CNBr + barbituric acid gives red-violet colour (confirms pyridine).
   - **Melting point** – 171–173 °C.

2. **UV-Spectrophotometric method (3 marks):**
   - **λmax = 263 nm** in 0.1 N HCl.
   - Beer's law obeyed over **2–14 µg/mL**.
   - Procedure: weigh ~ 100 mg sample, dissolve in 0.1 N HCl, dilute to 100 mL; further dilute to working concentration.
   - Measure absorbance at 263 nm against 0.1 N HCl blank.

3. **Calculation (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{std}} \;&=\; 0.612 \\
A_{\text{sample}} \;&=\; 0.598 \\
\% \text{ purity} \;&=\; \frac{0.598}{0.612} \times 100 \;&=\; 97.71 \,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

- The sample complies with IP limits (98–101 %).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this box and stick it in your lab record. These are 100% exam-relevant facts.**

- **IUPAC:** Pyridine-4-carbohydrazide
- **Molecular formula:** C₆H₇N₃O; **Mol. wt.: 137.14 g/mol**
- **M.p.:** 171–173 °C; **λmax:** 263 nm (0.1 N HCl)
- **Drug class:** First-line **antitubercular** (anti-TB) — *Hydrazide* family
- **Mechanism:** **Prodrug** → activated by **KatG** → forms **isonicotinoyl-NAD⁺ adduct** → inhibits **InhA** (enoyl-ACP reductase) → blocks **mycolic acid synthesis** → bactericidal.
- **SAR essentials:** 4-Pyridyl ring + intact carbohydrazide (–CO–NH–NH₂) + free terminal –NH₂ → all three are **mandatory**.
- **Synthesis:** Isonicotinic acid → ester (EtOH/H₂SO₄) → hydrazinolysis (N₂H₄·H₂O) → Isoniazid.
- **Identification:** Tollens' (silver mirror), ammonium vanadate (green), picrate (yellow ppt, m.p. 187 °C), König (pyridine test).
- **Pharmacokinetics:** Rapidly absorbed, F ≈ 90 %, metabolised by **hepatic N-acetyltransferase (NAT2)** — **slow vs. fast acetylators** dictate dose and toxicity.
- **Adverse effects:** **Peripheral neuropathy** (prevented by pyridoxine/B6), **hepatotoxicity**, drug-induced lupus, CYP450 inhibition.
- **Resistance:** *katG* mutations (loss of activation), *inhA* promoter mutations (target over-expression), *ndh* mutations.
- **Formulations:** Tablets (100 mg, 300 mg), syrup (50 mg/5 mL), injectable solution, fixed-dose combinations (FDC) with rifampicin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol.
- **Storage:** Air-tight container, protected from light and moisture; below 25 °C.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aspirin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Aspirin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Aspirin** is the internationally accepted generic (non-proprietary) name for the chemical compound **Acetylsalicylic Acid (ASA)**, chemically designated as **2-acetoxybenzoic acid** with the molecular formula **C₉H₈O₄** and a molecular weight of **180.16 g/mol**. It belongs to the pharmacological class of **Non-Steroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs (NSAIDs)**, specifically the **salicylate family**, and functions as a **reversible/irreversible inhibitor of cyclooxygenase (COX-1 and COX-2) enzymes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Aspirin is the most commonly cited example of a *prodrug-like* synthetic pharmaceutical agent where the acetyl (–COCH₃) group on the aromatic ring confers superior analgesic, antipyretic, and antiplatelet properties compared to its parent molecule, **salicylic acid**, which is highly gastric-irritant.

### 1.1.1 IUPAC Nomenclature

$$
\text{2-(Acetyloxy)benzoic acid}
$$

### 1.1.2 Synonyms Encountered in Pharmaceutical Literature

| Synonym | Context of Use |
| :--- | :--- |
| Acetylsalicylic Acid (ASA) | Pharmacopoeial (IP, USP, BP, EP) |
| Aspirin | Generic/Trivial name |
| 2-Acetoxybenzoic acid | IUPAC substitute name |
| Acetophen | Older British pharmaceutical brand name |
| Acenterine | French commercial synonym |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition (Real-World Analogy)**

Imagine your body has a built-in **"alarm factory"** that produces chemical messengers called **prostaglandins**. Whenever you get a cut, have a fever, or feel pain, these prostaglandins are released and scream at your nerve endings: *"Something is wrong here!"* They also tell your blood platelets to clump together to stop bleeding, and they protect your stomach lining.

**Aspirin acts like a master locksmith who permanently jams the locks (COX enzymes) on this alarm factory's doors.** Once the locks are jammed, the factory cannot sound the alarm (pain), cannot raise the temperature gauge (fever), but it ALSO subtly stops the protective stomach-lining signal and blocks the blood-platelet "glue" (which is why low-dose aspirin prevents heart attacks!).

### Key Conceptual Takeaways
- **Pain relief (analgesic)** → Blocks prostaglandin alarm at injury site.
- **Fever reduction (antipyretic)** → Blocks prostaglandin alarm in the brain's hypothalamus.
- **Heart attack prevention (antiplatelet)** → Permanently acetylates platelet COX-1, making them less sticky for the platelet's ~7-day lifespan.
- **Anti-inflammatory (at high doses)** → Reduces prostaglandin-mediated swelling in joints.

### 1.3 Critical Pharmaceutical Constants (Bolded for Recall)

- **Melting Point:** **136–140 °C** (pharmacopoeial acceptance range).
- **pKa:** **3.5** at 25 °C (weakly acidic carboxylic acid).
- **Log P (octanol/water):** **1.19** (moderately lipophilic).
- **Aqueous Solubility:** **3 mg/mL** at 20 °C (poorly water-soluble; freely soluble in ethanol, chloroform, ether).
- **Standard Adult Analgesic Dose:** **325–650 mg every 4 hours**, not exceeding **4 g/day**.
- **Standard Antiplatelet (Cardioprotective) Dose:** **75–150 mg/day** (low-dose).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemical Architecture — The Salicylate Backbone

Aspirin is built on a **benzene ring** bearing two substituents in an *ortho* relationship:

1. A **carboxylic acid group (–COOH)** at the **C-1** position.
2. An **acetoxy (acetyl ester) group (–O–CO–CH₃)** at the **C-2** position.

The acetylation of the phenolic –OH of salicylic acid to form the ester linkage is the *single chemical transformation* that defines aspirin's pharmacological superiority. The free phenolic –OH in salicylic acid is responsible for:
- Gastric mucosal damage (direct acid irritation).
- Binding to plasma proteins and active transport interference.
- Bitter, unpleasant taste.

> [!NOTE]
> **Structure–Activity Relationship (SAR) Insight for KTU Board Exams**
> The esterification "masks" the offending phenolic –OH, allowing the molecule to survive gastric transit and reach systemic circulation, where esterases later hydrolyze it back to salicylic acid (the *active* moiety in tissues).

---

## 2.2 Physicochemical Properties — Bullet Logic Breakdown

- **Acidity:** As a carboxylic acid (pKa 3.5), aspirin is **~10,000 times more acidic than simple aliphatic alcohols** due to resonance stabilization of the conjugate base carboxylate.
- **Hydrolysis:** Aspirin is susceptible to **hydrolytic degradation** in moist/warm conditions, reverting to acetic acid + salicylic acid (the official pharmacopoeial impurity limit is monitored via salicylic acid content).
- **Crystallinity:** Forms **monoclinic prismatic crystals** or **white crystalline powder**.
- **Stability Window:** Optimum stability at **pH 2–3** in solid state; hydrolyzes rapidly in alkaline aqueous media.
- **UV Absorption:** λmax ≈ **265 nm in methanol** (used in pharmacopoeial assays).

---

## 2.3 Mechanism of Action — The COX Inhibition Story

Aspirin **irreversibly acetylates** a critical **serine residue (Ser-530 in COX-1; Ser-516 in COX-2)** located in the active-site channel of the cyclooxygenase enzyme. This covalent modification:

1. **Sterically blocks** arachidonic acid (the natural substrate) from entering the active site.
2. Prevents the formation of **Prostaglandin H₂ (PGH₂)** — the universal precursor to all prostaglandins and thromboxanes.
3. In platelets (anucleate cells), because they **cannot synthesize new COX**, the inhibition lasts the **entire ~7–10 day platelet lifespan** — the basis for once-daily cardioprotective dosing.

### Why Low-Dose Aspirin is Cardioprotective
At low doses (75–150 mg), aspirin preferentially inhibits **platelet COX-1** (in portal circulation) while sparing **endothelial COX-2** (which produces vasodilatory PGI₂). This shifts the **TXA₂/PGI₂ balance** toward antithrombotic net effect.

---

## 2.4 Pharmacokinetics — ADME Profile

| Phase | Event | Key Quantitative Detail |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Absorption** | Rapidly absorbed from stomach & small intestine | Peak plasma in **30–40 min** (regular tablets); **3–4 h** (enteric-coated) |
| **Distribution** | Widely distributed; crosses BBB & placenta | Plasma protein binding **80–90%** (mainly albumin) |
| **Metabolism** | Hydrolyzed by **esterases** in plasma & liver | t½ of salicylic acid = **2–3 h** (low dose) → **15–30 h** (high dose, zero-order kinetics) |
| **Excretion** | Renal as salicyluric acid, glucuronides, gentisic acid | Urinary pH affects excretion (alkaline urine ↑ clearance) |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key formulas, doses, and boundary conditions in one place. Memorize this table for KTU exams.**

| Parameter | Formula / Numerical Value | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Molecular Formula | $\text{C}_9\text{H}_8\text{O}_4$ | — |
| Molecular Weight | $M = 9(12) + 8(1) + 4(16) = 180.16$ | $\text{g/mol}$ |
| Degree of Unsaturation (DoU) | $\text{DoU} = \dfrac{2C + 2 - H}{2} = \dfrac{2(9)+2-8}{2} = 6$ | 4 from benzene + 2 from 2 C=O |
| pKa | $3.5$ at $25\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Weak acid |
| Henderson–Hasselbalch | $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}\right)$ | Predicts ionized fraction at gut pH |
| Dose (Analgesic) | $325 - 650\,\text{mg}$ per $4\,\text{h}$, max $4\,\text{g/day}$ | Adult oral |
| Dose (Antiplatelet) | $75 - 150\,\text{mg/day}$ | Adult oral, chronic |
| Dose (Pediatric — KAWASAKI) | $80 - 100\,\text{mg/kg/day}$ | Rare Kawasaki disease only |
| Onset of Action | $5 - 30\,\text{min}$ (chewed) | — |
| Hydrolysis Kinetics | Pseudo-first order in solution | Ester hydrolysis by H⁺/OH⁻ |
| Shelf-life Climatic Zone | $\text{Zone I/II: } \geq 5 \text{ yrs}; \text{ Zone IV: } \geq 2 \text{ yrs}$ | ICH Q1A stability |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Pharmaceutical Engineering Utility

- **Synthesis Reactor Design**: The industrial Kolbe–Schmitt + acetylation route informs reactor material selection (glass-lined to resist acetic anhydride corrosion).
- **Particle Engineering**: Micronized aspirin (D₉₀ < 50 µm) accelerates dissolution — applied in fast-onset formulations.
- **Polymorphism Control**: Aspirin exhibits *Form I* and *Form II* crystals; Form I is the thermodynamically stable pharmaceutical form.
- **Analytical Chemistry**: UV-spectrophotometric assay at 265 nm and HPLC assay at 237 nm are pharmacopoeial gold standards.
- **Packaging Science**: Aspirin tablets degrade in humid environments → **blister packs with desiccants** are engineering essentials.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Industrial Synthesis of Aspirin — The Acetic Anhydride Route

The commercial synthesis proceeds in **two major chemical steps** starting from **phenol**.

### Step 0: Starting Material — Phenol

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} \quad (\text{M.W.} = 94.11 \text{ g/mol})
$$

---

### Step 1: Kolbe–Schmitt Reaction → Sodium Salicylate

Phenol reacts with **sodium hydroxide** to form sodium phenoxide, which is then carboxylated under pressure using **carbon dioxide gas**.

#### Reaction 1 (Phenoxide Formation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} &+ \text{NaOH} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}^-\text{Na}^+ \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Reaction 2 (Kolbe–Schmitt Carboxylation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}^-\text{Na}^+ &+ \text{CO}_2 \;\xrightarrow[5\text{ atm},\; 125\,^\circ\text{C}]{}\; \text{HOOC-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-ONa}
\end{aligned}
$$

The carboxylation is **regioselective** for the *ortho* position because the sodium ion forms a **chelated six-membered transition state** that locks the electrophile in the ortho position. Acidification with sulfuric acid yields **salicylic acid**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HOOC-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-ONa} &+ \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{HOOC-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-OH} \;+\; \text{NaHSO}_4
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Note**: The Kolbe–Schmitt reaction is essentially an **electrophilic aromatic substitution** in which the phenoxide (a strong activator) directs the incoming electrophile *ortho/para*, but the Na⁺ chelation favors *ortho*.

---

### Step 2: Acetylation with Acetic Anhydride → Aspirin

Salicylic acid reacts with **acetic anhydride** in the presence of a **sulfuric acid (or phosphoric acid) catalyst**.

#### Balanced Reaction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_7\text{H}_6\text{O}_3 \;(\text{Salicylic acid}) &+ (\text{CH}_3\text{CO})_2\text{O} \;\xrightarrow[\text{or } \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4]{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4,\; 70\,^\circ\text{C}}\; \text{C}_9\text{H}_8\text{O}_4 \;(\text{Aspirin}) \;+\; \text{CH}_3\text{COOH}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Detailed Mechanism (Six Elementary Steps):

**Step A — Protonation of Acetic Anhydride Carbonyl:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\text{CH}_3\text{CO})_2\text{O} &+ \text{H}^+ \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CH}_3\text{-}\overset{+}{\text{C}}(\text{OH})\text{-O-COCH}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

The carbonyl oxygen of acetic anhydride is protonated by the acid catalyst, making the carbonyl carbon highly electrophilic.

**Step B — Nucleophilic Attack by Phenolic –OH:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{2-HO-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-COOH} &+ \text{CH}_3\text{-}\overset{+}{\text{C}}(\text{OH})\text{-O-COCH}_3 \\
&\longrightarrow \text{2-HO-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-COO-CH}_3\text{...} \\
&\quad\text{(tetrahedral intermediate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The phenolic oxygen of salicylic acid (more nucleophilic than the carboxylic –OH due to resonance with the ring) attacks the activated carbonyl carbon.

**Step C — Proton Transfer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tetrahedral intermediate} &\longrightarrow \text{Protonated ester intermediate}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Elimination of Acetate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Protonated ester intermediate} &\longrightarrow \text{Aspirin} + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH}
\end{aligned}
$$

The carboxylate (CH₃COO⁻) leaves, completing the acyl substitution.

**Step E — Deprotonation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Protonated aspirin} &\longrightarrow \text{Aspirin} + \text{H}^+
\end{aligned}
$$

The acid catalyst is regenerated (true **catalytic** behavior).

---

### Step 3: Stoichiometric Mass Balance & Theoretical Yield

**Given:** $50.0\,\text{g}$ salicylic acid reacted with excess acetic anhydride.
**Molar mass:** Salicylic acid $= 138.12\,\text{g/mol}$, Aspirin $= 180.16\,\text{g/mol}$.

**Moles of salicylic acid:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{SA}} &= \frac{50.0\,\text{g}}{138.12\,\text{g/mol}} = 0.3620\,\text{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stoichiometric ratio:** 1 mol salicylic acid → 1 mol aspirin.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{Aspirin, theoretical}} &= 0.3620\,\text{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Theoretical mass of aspirin:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{Aspirin, theoretical}} &= 0.3620\,\text{mol} \times 180.16\,\text{g/mol} = 65.21\,\text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Percent yield (if actual recovered = 52.0 g):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{Yield} &= \frac{52.0\,\text{g}}{65.21\,\text{g}} \times 100 = 79.74\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Trap**: Always use *moles* (not grams) for stoichiometric conversion. A common student error is multiplying 50 g × 180/138 directly without computing moles. The unit conversion is non-negotiable for full marks.

---

### 3.2 Recrystallization & Purification

Crude aspirin is purified by **recrystallization from hot ethanol–water mixture**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Crystalline Aspirin} &\xleftarrow[\text{cooling}]{\text{Ethanol + Water, hot}} \text{Crude Aspirin} + \text{Salicylic acid impurity}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why ethanol–water?**
- Ethanol dissolves aspirin well at high T (good solubility).
- Water prevents excessive solubility loss at low T (anti-solvent effect).
- Salicylic acid (impurity) stays in the mother liquor because of its higher polarity and lower solubility differential.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Quality Control — Salicylic Acid Limit Test

Pharmacopoeias mandate that **free salicylic acid impurity ≤ 0.1%** in pharmaceutical-grade aspirin. This is determined by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Complexation: } \text{Salicylic acid} + \text{Fe}^{3+} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Violet [Fe(salicylic)}_3\text{] complex}
\end{aligned}
$$

The intensity of violet color (measured at **530 nm**) is proportional to salicylic acid concentration. This is a classic **colorimetric limit test** in pharmaceutical analysis.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Define Aspirin. Write its IUPAC name, molecular formula, and molecular weight.

**Model Answer:**
Aspirin is the generic name for **Acetylsalicylic Acid (ASA)**, a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) belonging to the salicylate class, used as an analgesic, antipyretic, anti-inflammatory, and antiplatelet agent.
- **IUPAC Name:** 2-(Acetyloxy)benzoic acid
- **Molecular Formula:** C₉H₈O₄
- **Molecular Weight:** **180.16 g/mol**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**: Students often write "Acetylsalicylic acid" as IUPAC name. That is the *trivial* name. The exact IUPAC is *2-(Acetyloxy)benzoic acid* (or *2-Acetoxybenzoic acid*). Marks will be deducted for sloppy nomenclature.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Differentiate between salicylic acid and acetylsalicylic acid in terms of structure and gastric tolerance.

**Model Answer:**
| Feature | Salicylic Acid | Acetylsalicylic Acid (Aspirin) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Structure | –COOH and **free phenolic –OH** | –COOH and **acetylated phenolic –OH (–O–CO–CH₃)** |
| Gastric Tolerance | **Highly irritant** — free –OH is corrosive to mucosa | **Better tolerated** — esterification masks the phenolic –OH |
| Medicinal Use | Topical (keratolytic, wart remover); not used orally | Oral analgesic, antipyretic, antiplatelet |
| Taste | Intensely sweet-bitter | Slightly tart |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### ⭐ OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`**

**(a)** Describe the **industrial synthesis of Aspirin** from phenol, including the **Kolbe–Schmitt reaction** and the **acetylation step**. Discuss the role of the catalyst and write the mechanism of the acetylation reaction. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **mechanism of analgesic and antipyretic action** of Aspirin. Discuss why low-dose aspirin acts as an antiplatelet agent. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

The industrial synthesis of Aspirin from phenol proceeds through **two major stages**:

**Stage 1 — Kolbe–Schmitt Carboxylation to Salicylic Acid:**

Step 1: Phenol is treated with aqueous NaOH to form **sodium phenoxide**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{NaOH} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2: Sodium phenoxide reacts with **CO₂ at 125 °C and ~5 atm pressure**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{CO}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{HOOC-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-ONa}
\end{aligned}
$$

The sodium ion chelates the incoming CO₂ in a **six-membered transition state**, directing the carboxyl group to the *ortho* position (regioselective EAS).

Step 3: Acidification with H₂SO₄ yields **salicylic acid**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HOOC-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-ONa} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{HOOC-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-OH} + \text{NaHSO}_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stage 2 — Acetylation to Aspirin:**

Salicylic acid + **acetic anhydride** in the presence of catalytic **H₂SO₄ (or H₃PO₄)** at 70 °C:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HOOC-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-OH} + (\text{CH}_3\text{CO})_2\text{O} \;\xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4}\; \text{HOOC-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-O-COCH}_3 + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mechanism (catalytic role of H₂SO₄):**
The acid catalyst **protonates the carbonyl oxygen** of acetic anhydride, enhancing electrophilicity at the carbonyl carbon. The phenolic –OH of salicylic acid (more nucleophilic than –COOH due to resonance) attacks this carbon, forming a tetrahedral intermediate which then eliminates acetate (CH₃COO⁻). Finally, deprotonation regenerates the H⁺ catalyst.

**Role of catalyst:** H₂SO₄ lowers the activation energy by protonating the carbonyl and is regenerated at the end (true catalyst).

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Mechanism of Analgesic and Antipyretic Action:**

Aspirin **irreversibly acetylates** the serine residue (Ser-530 in COX-1, Ser-516 in COX-2) at the active site of the **cyclooxygenase (COX) enzyme**. This covalent modification blocks the conversion of **arachidonic acid → Prostaglandin H₂ (PGH₂)**, the universal precursor of:
- **PGE₂** (mediates pain at peripheral nerve endings and fever in hypothalamic thermoregulatory center).
- **PGI₂** (vasodilator, gastric mucosal protection).

Without PGE₂ synthesis, the body's perception of pain is blunted and the hypothalamic set-point returns to normal, reducing fever.

**Why Low-Dose Aspirin is Antiplatelet:**

At low doses (75–150 mg daily), aspirin preferentially inhibits **platelet COX-1** in the **portal circulation** before hepatic first-pass deacetylation. Because platelets are **anucleate** and cannot synthesize new COX enzymes, the inhibition persists for the **entire 7–10 day platelet lifespan**. This blocks **TXA₂** (a platelet aggregator and vasoconstrictor). Meanwhile, **endothelial COX-2** in the systemic circulation remains largely unacetylated, allowing continued **PGI₂** (vasodilator, anti-aggregator) production. The net **TXA₂ ↓ / PGI₂ ↑** balance produces a powerful antithrombotic effect — the rationale for cardioprotection in myocardial infarction and stroke prophylaxis.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning**: Many students forget to mention that *platelets cannot synthesize new COX*, which is the KEY reason for the long-lasting antiplatelet effect. Without this, the answer is incomplete — expect **1–2 marks deducted**.

---

### ⭐ OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`**

**(a)** Discuss the **physicochemical properties and stability profile** of Aspirin. What is the effect of moisture on Aspirin stability? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Outline the **pharmacokinetics (ADME)** and **major adverse effects** of Aspirin, including **Reye's syndrome**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Physicochemical Properties:**

| Property | Value / Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| Appearance | White crystalline powder or monoclinic prismatic crystals |
| Odor | Odorless; faintly acetic acid-like on storage |
| Melting Point | 136–140 °C |
| Solubility in water | 3 mg/mL at 20 °C (poor) |
| Solubility in organic solvents | Freely soluble in ethanol, chloroform, ether |
| pKa | 3.5 (weakly acidic) |
| Log P | 1.19 (moderately lipophilic) |
| UV λmax | 265 nm (methanol) |

**Stability Profile:**

- Aspirin is **stable in dry air** but hydrolyzes in **moisture** to **acetic acid + salicylic acid**.
- The hydrolysis follows **pseudo-first-order kinetics** in solution.
- It is **most stable in the pH range of 2–3**; hydrolysis accelerates in alkaline media.
- In solid dosage form, hydrolysis is negligible if protected from humidity, but **tablet formulations must contain < 0.1% free salicylic acid** as per pharmacopoeial limit.

**Effect of Moisture:**
- Hydrolytic degradation produces **salicylic acid** (impurity, gastric irritant) and **acetic acid** (vinegar-like odor).
- Moisture also accelerates crystal transformation between polymorphs.
- Therefore, aspirin tablets are packaged in **aluminum blister packs with desiccants** to minimize humidity exposure.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Pharmacokinetics (ADME):**

- **Absorption:** Orally, rapidly absorbed from the stomach and upper small intestine by passive diffusion. Peak plasma concentration in **30–40 min** (regular tablets) or **3–4 h** (enteric-coated). The acidic environment of the stomach keeps aspirin largely non-ionized, favoring absorption.
- **Distribution:** Widely distributed; **80–90% plasma protein bound** (mainly albumin). Crosses blood-brain barrier (small amount) and placenta (avoid in pregnancy).
- **Metabolism:** Rapidly hydrolyzed by **carboxylesterases** in plasma and liver to **salicylic acid**, the principal active metabolite. Salicylic acid is further metabolized to **salicyluric acid**, **glucuronide conjugates**, and **gentisic acid**.
- **Elimination:** Excreted renally. **Half-life** is dose-dependent (saturable kinetics): **2–3 h at low doses**, **15–30 h at high doses** (Michaelis–Menten / zero-order at overdose).

**Adverse Effects:**

1. **Gastric mucosal damage & ulceration** — due to direct acid irritation + inhibition of protective PGE₂.
2. **Salicylism** — tinnitus, dizziness, nausea at high doses.
3. **Reye's syndrome** — **acute encephalopathy + hepatic failure** in children recovering from viral infection (influenza, varicella) given aspirin; therefore aspirin is **contraindicated in children < 18 years** with viral illness.
4. **Salicylate overdose** — mixed respiratory alkalosis + metabolic acidosis; potentially fatal.
5. **Hypersensitivity** — bronchospasm in asthmatics (aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease).
6. **Bleeding tendency** — prolonged bleeding time due to irreversible platelet inhibition.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **IUPAC Nomenclature Confusion** — "Acetylsalicylic acid" is NOT the IUPAC name. The correct IUPAC is **2-(Acetyloxy)benzoic acid**.
> 2. **Skipping the catalyst regeneration step** — In the mechanism of acetylation, students forget to mention that H₂SO₄ is *regenerated* (catalytic role).
> 3. **Forgetting the irreversibility + anucleate platelet logic** — This is the heart of why aspirin's antiplatelet effect lasts 7–10 days. Skipping it costs 2 marks easily.
> 4. **Confusing Reye's syndrome with meningitis symptoms** — Reye's is *fatty degeneration of the liver + encephalopathy*, triggered by aspirin in viral-infected children.
> 5. **Using "grams" directly in yield calculations** — Always convert to moles first.
> 6. **Writing "COX inhibitor" without specifying "irreversible acetylation of Ser-530"** — Examiners want the *mechanistic* depth.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before KTU Exam**

- ✅ Aspirin = **Acetylsalicylic Acid (ASA)** = **2-(Acetyloxy)benzoic acid** = **C₉H₈O₄** (M.W. 180.16).
- ✅ Belongs to **NSAID / salicylate** class.
- ✅ **IUPAC Name:** 2-(Acetyloxy)benzoic acid.
- ✅ Melting Point: **136–140 °C**; pKa: **3.5**.
- ✅ **Industrial Synthesis**: Phenol → (Kolbe–Schmitt, NaOH + CO₂, 125 °C, 5 atm) → Salicylic Acid → (Acetylation with acetic anhydride + H₂SO₄) → Aspirin.
- ✅ Kolbe–Schmitt is **ortho-selective** due to **Na⁺ chelation transition state**.
- ✅ **Mechanism**: Irreversible acetylation of **Ser-530 (COX-1)** / **Ser-516 (COX-2)** → blocks PGH₂ formation → ↓ prostaglandins.
- ✅ Analgesic Dose: **325–650 mg q4h**; Antiplatelet Dose: **75–150 mg/day**; Anti-inflammatory: up to **5.4 g/day**.
- ✅ **Antiplatelet lasts 7–10 days** because platelets are anucleate (cannot synthesize new COX).
- ✅ **Low-dose aspirin** inhibits platelet COX-1 (TXA₂ ↓) but spares endothelial COX-2 (PGI₂ preserved) → net antithrombotic.
- ✅ Pharmacokinetics: Oral absorption 30–40 min; protein binding 80–90%; t½ dose-dependent (2–3 h low dose → 15–30 h high dose).
- ✅ **Adverse effects**: gastric ulcer, salicylism (tinnitus), **Reye's syndrome in children**, salicylate overdose (mixed acid-base disorder), hypersensitivity in asthmatics.
- ✅ **Contraindication in viral illness in children < 18 years** (Reye's syndrome prevention).
- ✅ Aspirin hydrolyzes to **acetic acid + salicylic acid** in moisture → must be stored dry.
- ✅ Pharmacopoeial impurity limit: **free salicylic acid ≤ 0.1%** (limit test with Fe³⁺ at 530 nm).
- ✅ **Polymorphism**: Form I (stable), Form II (metastable).
- ✅ **Drug-drug interaction**: Aspirin + warfarin → bleeding risk; Aspirin + ibuprofen → antagonism of antiplatelet effect.
- ✅ **Recrystallization solvent**: Ethanol–water mixture.
- ✅ Aspirin's discovery: **1897 by Felix Hoffmann at Bayer** (often cited but examiners rarely ask — for general awareness only).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Penicillin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Penicillin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Penicillin

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Penicillin** is a group of β-lactam antibiotics derived from or structurally related to the natural product *Penicillium notatum* / *Penicillium chrysogenum*, characterized by a fused bicyclic core consisting of a four-membered **β-lactam ring** and a five-membered **thiazolidine ring**, which collectively inhibit bacterial cell wall (peptidoglycan) biosynthesis by acylating the transpeptidase (penicillin-binding protein, PBP) enzyme.

Chemically, the pharmacophore of penicillin is **6-aminopenicillanic acid (6-APA)**, bearing a free amino group at C-6 to which various acyl side chains (R–CO–) are attached to yield the medically useful analogues.

- **IUPAC Nomenclature Style:** (2*S*,5*R*,6*R*)-6-{[(2*R*)-2-amino-2-phenylacetyl]amino}-3,3-dimethyl-7-oxo-4-thia-1-azabicyclo[3.2.0]heptane-2-carboxylic acid (for Ampicillin).
- **Discovery Year:** **1928** (Sir Alexander Fleming, St. Mary's Hospital, London).
- **First Clinical Use:** **1942** (Howard Florey, Ernst Chain, Norman Heatley).
- **Molecular Formula Range (Core 6-APA):** **C₈H₁₂N₂O₃S** (MW ≈ **216.26 g/mol**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 aligned syllabus for *Drugs and Pharmaceutical Technology*, Penicillin is treated as the **founding case study** of the entire chemotherapy branch — it establishes the template for understanding antibiotic discovery, fermentation technology, semi-synthesis, SAR, and β-lactam chemistry.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Jammed Lock" 🗝️

Imagine a factory (the bacterial cell) that builds its protective outer wall (peptidoglycan) using a special machine (the **transpeptidase enzyme**). The factory workers feed raw bricks (peptidoglycan precursors ending in **D-Ala-D-Ala**) into this machine, and it stitches the bricks together crosswise.

Now imagine a **molecular saboteur** that looks *almost* identical to the brick — so much so that the machine grabs it and tries to process it, but once the saboteur is in place, the machine gets **permanently stuck** (acylated at the active-site serine) and can no longer stitch real bricks.

**That saboteur is Penicillin.**

The β-lactam ring mimics the D-Ala-D-Ala dipeptide so well that the enzyme is fooled, but the strained 4-membered ring opens irreversibly and glues itself to the enzyme — a process called **suicide inhibition** (or mechanism-based irreversible inhibition).

### 1.3 Historical Timeline (Board-Favorite)

| Year | Milestone | Scientist |
|------|-----------|-----------|
| **1928** | Accidental discovery on contaminated *Staphylococcus* plate | Sir Alexander Fleming |
| **1939** | Isolation & therapeutic evaluation begun | Florey, Chain, Heatley (Oxford) |
| **1940** | Animal trial (mice infected with *Streptococcus*) — cured | Howard Florey |
| **1941** | First human trial — Albert Alexander | Charles Fletcher |
| **1942–45** | Industrial mass production via submerged fermentation | USA + UK war effort |
| **1959** | Isolation of **6-APA** nucleus → birth of semi-synthetic penicillins | Beecham group (Batchelor, Doyle, Nayler) |
| **1960–64** | Methicillin, Ampicillin, Amoxicillin marketed | Beecham, Beecham, Beecham |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Penicillin Pharmacophore — Anatomy of the Molecule

Every penicillin has three indispensable structural elements:

1. **β-Lactam Ring (4-membered)** — a strained cyclic amide. Its ring strain provides the thermodynamic driving force for acylation of PBPs.
2. **Thiazolidine Ring (5-membered)** — contains the sulfur atom and the gem-dimethyl group (3,3-dimethyl) and the C-3 carboxylic acid.
3. **Side Chain (R-CO-NH-)** — attached at C-6 nitrogen. This is the **"tunable handle"** that chemists modify to:
    - improve acid stability (gastric absorption),
    - resist β-lactamases,
    - broaden the spectrum to Gram-negative bacteria.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stereochemistry KTU Note:** Three chiral centres exist — C-2, C-5, C-6 — and the **natural 6S, 5R, 2R** configuration is **mandatory** for antibacterial activity. Epimerization destroys activity.

### 2.2 Classification of Penicillins (Board-Critical)

#### (a) Natural Penicillins
- **Penicillin G (Benzylpenicillin):** R = benzyl; acid-labile, injectable only.
- **Penicillin V (Phenoxymethylpenicillin):** R = phenoxymethyl; acid-stable, oral.

#### (b) Semi-Synthetic Penicillins
- **Acid-Resistant / Oral:** Penicillin V, Ampicillin, Amoxicillin.
- **β-Lactamase Resistant:** Methicillin, Oxacillin, Cloxacillin, Flucloxacillin, Nafcillin.
- **Extended Spectrum (Gram-negative):** Ampicillin, Amoxicillin, Amoxicillin + Clavulanic acid (Augmentin).
- **Anti-Pseudomonal:** Carbenicillin, Ticarcillin, Piperacillin, Mezlocillin, Azlocillin.

### 2.3 Structure–Activity Relationship (SAR) — The Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **SAR of Penicillins** is a guaranteed 7-mark question under *Module 1* in the KTU 2024 scheme. Memorize the following:

| Position | Modification | Effect on Activity |
|----------|--------------|--------------------|
| C-6 acyl side chain (R) | α-Amino group addition (e.g., Ampicillin) | Broadens spectrum to Gram-negative |
| C-6 acyl side chain (R) | 2,6-Dimethoxybenzoyl (Methicillin) | β-lactamase resistance |
| C-6 acyl side chain (R) | 2,6-Dichlorophenyl + 5-methylisoxazole (Dicloxacillin) | β-lactamase resistance + oral absorption |
| C-3 carboxyl (COOH) | Esterification (e.g., Pivampicillin) | Oral bioavailability as prodrug |
| C-3 dimethyl | Replacement by other groups | Discovered Cephalosporins |
| β-lactam ring cleavage | Loss of ring | **Complete loss of activity** |
| Stereochemistry inversion at C-6 | Epimerization | **Loss of activity** |

### 2.4 Mechanism of Action — Step-by-Step

1. Penicillin diffuses through the bacterial **outer membrane** (Gram-negatives) and **peptidoglycan layer**.
2. It binds the **Penicillin-Binding Protein (PBP / transpeptidase)** active site via the D-Ala-D-Ala terminus of the peptidoglycan precursor.
3. The **carbonyl carbon** of the β-lactam is attacked by the **active-site serine –OH** of the PBP.
4. The β-lactam ring opens (C–N bond cleavage) — the enzyme becomes **acylated**.
5. The acyl-enzyme is **hydrolytically stable** → enzyme is **permanently inactivated**.
6. Peptidoglycan cross-linking halts → autolysins trigger → **bacterial lysis**.

### 2.5 Resistance Mechanisms

- **β-Lactamase production** (e.g., *Staphylococcus aureus* penicillinase) — cleaves the β-lactam ring.
- **Altered PBPs** (e.g., MRSA's PBP2a).
- **Reduced porin permeability** (Gram-negative outer membrane).
- **Efflux pumps**.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet Table

| Item | Expression / Value | Units / Notes |
|------|---------------------|---------------|
| 6-APA molecular weight | $\text{MW} = 216.26$ | g/mol |
| β-Lactam ring strain energy | $\Delta E \approx 26$ | kcal/mol (drives acylation) |
| Dimensional purity (HPLC) | $\text{Purity} = \dfrac{A_{\text{sample}}}{A_{\text{standard}}} \times 100$ | percent (A = peak area) |
| Fermentation yield (Pen G) | $Y \approx 50\,000$ | units/mL (modern industrial) |
| Half-life (Pen G, IV) | $t_{1/2} \approx 0.5$ | hour |
| Assay (iodometric) | $1\ \text{unit} \equiv 0.6\ \mu\text{g}$ pure benzylpenicillin sodium | USP/FDA |
| MIC range (susceptible) | $\text{MIC} \leq 0.12$ | $\mu$g/mL (CLSI) |
| Acid hydrolysis rate constant | $k \approx 10^{-4}$ | s$^{-1}$ at pH 1, 37°C |
| Henderson–Hasselbalch (absorption) | $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical rule for the table:** All symbols must be readable; use $\vert x \vert$ style for absolute values when needed, e.g. $\vert C-6 \vert$ configuration, by writing `\vert C-6 \vert` in the underlying source — but avoid `|` directly in markdown cells.

### 2.7 Real-World Utility

Penicillin chemistry led to:
- The discovery of **Cephalosporins**, **Carbapenems**, and **Monobactams** — all β-lactam families.
- **Mechanism-based drug design** (rational drug design based on enzyme-substrate mimics).
- **Biocatalytic 6-APA production** by *Pseudomonas* or *Aspergillus* amidases — a green-chemistry milestone.
- **Clavulanic acid** co-administration to inhibit β-lactamase — a Nobel-prize-winning concept.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why the β-Lactam Ring Reacts (Ring Strain Argument)

The β-lactam (4-membered) ring is highly reactive because of **angle strain**, **torsional strain**, and **loss of amide resonance** — the nitrogen lone pair is forced out of conjugation with the carbonyl. We can compute the strain contribution qualitatively:

$$
E_{\text{strain}} = E_{\text{angle}} + E_{\text{torsional}} + E_{\text{non-resonance}}
$$

For a 4-membered ring, the ideal internal angle is 109.5° but it is forced to ~90°:

$$
E_{\text{angle}} = \frac{1}{2}\,k_\theta\,(\theta_{\text{ideal}} - \theta_{\text{actual}})^2
$$

where $k_\theta$ is the angle-bending force constant (~ 60 kcal·mol$^{-1}$·rad$^{-2}$ for C–C). Substituting $\theta_{\text{ideal}} = 109.5^\circ = 1.911$ rad and $\theta_{\text{actual}} = 90^\circ = 1.571$ rad:

$$
E_{\text{angle}} = \frac{1}{2}\,(60)\,(1.911 - 1.571)^2
$$

$$
= 30 \times (0.340)^2
$$

$$
= 30 \times 0.1156 = 3.47\ \text{kcal/mol}
$$

**Reasoning:** This angle-strain energy per bond, summed with torsional strain and the absence of amide resonance (~ 20 kcal/mol in normal amides), totals roughly **26 kcal/mol** — enough thermodynamically to make the C–N bond of the β-lactam **ring-open** when attacked by a nucleophile like serine.

### 3.2 Derivative: Quantifying PBP Inactivation Kinetics

Inactivation of PBP follows a **pseudo-first-order suicide kinetics** model:

$$
\frac{d[E]}{dt} = -k_{\text{inact}}\,[E]\,\frac{[I]}{K_I + [I]}
$$

Under saturating inhibitor (Penicillin, $[I] \gg K_I$):

$$
[E](t) = [E]_0\,e^{-k_{\text{inact}}\,t}
$$

Integrating to find the time for 99 % inactivation ($[E]/[E]_0 = 0.01$):

$$
\ln(0.01) = -k_{\text{inact}}\,t_{99\%}
$$

$$
t_{99\%} = \frac{\ln(100)}{k_{\text{inact}}} = \frac{4.605}{k_{\text{inact}}}
$$

For Pen G vs *E. coli* PBP, $k_{\text{inact}} \approx 1.0\ \text{s}^{-1}$:

$$
t_{99\%} = \frac{4.605}{1.0} = 4.6\ \text{seconds}
$$

**Reasoning:** Hence, a single bound penicillin molecule inactivates the transpeptidase within seconds — a remarkable kinetic efficiency.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Semi-Synthesis of Ampicillin from 6-APA

1. **Cultivate** *Penicillium chrysogenum* in a stirred-tank bioreactor (submerged fermentation) → obtain Penicillin G (R = benzyl) in fermentation broth.
2. **Isolate** Pen G via solvent extraction (butyl acetate) at pH 2–3.
3. **Cleave the side chain enzymatically** using *Escherichia coli* **penicillin acylase (PA)** at pH 8.0, 37 °C, releasing **6-APA** + phenylacetic acid.
4. **Couple 6-APA with D-phenylglycine** (or its activated ester, DANE - Dane salt, mixed anhydride) via **Schotten–Baumann acylation** in aqueous acetone with NaOH at 0–5 °C.
5. **Acidify** to pH 2 → Ampicillin trihydrate precipitates.
6. **Recrystallize** from water/acetone; **dry** under vacuum.
7. **Quality control:** HPLC purity $\geq 99\%$, assay 96–100.5 % of label, water content (KF), specific rotation $[\alpha]_D^{25} = +280^\circ$ to $+305^\circ$ (c=1, water).

Overall reaction:

$$
\text{Pen G} \xrightarrow{\text{PA, pH 8.0}} \text{6-APA} + \text{Phenylacetic acid}
$$

$$
\text{6-APA} + \text{D-Phenylglycine} \xrightarrow{\text{Schotten-Baumann}} \text{Ampicillin}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The phrase **"Schotten–Baumann acylation of 6-APA with an activated amino acid"** is the most-expected wording in 14-mark questions on semi-synthesis.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Assay Methods

| Method | Principle | Quantity Measured | Typical KTU Use |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Iodometric titration** | β-lactam ring + iodine | Penicillin content | Total potency |
| **HPLC (UV 254 nm)** | Reverse-phase C-18 column | Purity / individual components | Dispersal test |
| **Microbiological assay** | Zone of inhibition (*Sarcina lutea*) | Potency vs reference | Bioassay |
| **FT-IR** | β-lactam C=O stretch ~ 1770 cm⁻¹ | Identification | Fingerprint |
| **NMR (¹H, ¹³C)** | Chemical shifts at 4.1 (H-3), 5.4 (H-5), 5.6 (H-6) ppm | Structure confirmation | Quality control |
| **Polarimetry** | Specific rotation | Optical purity | Chiral purity |
| **Karl Fischer** | Water reacts with I₂ / SO₂ | Water content | Hydration state |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Penicillin Pharmacophore — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CORE["BICYCLIC CORE 6 APA"]
        A["Beta lactam ring 4 membered"] --> B["Thiazolidine ring 5 membered"]
        B --> C["3 3 gem dimethyl group"]
        B --> D["C 3 carboxyl COOH"]
    end
    CORE --> E["C 6 amide NH COR"]
    E --> F["Side chain R GROUP"]
    F --> G1["Natural R benzyl Pen G"]
    F --> G2["Phenoxymethyl Pen V"]
    F --> G3["Alpha amino benzyl Ampicillin"]
    F --> G4["Hydroxy para amino Amoxicillin"]
    F --> G5["2 6 dimethoxybenzoyl Methicillin"]
    F --> G6["3 phenyl 5 methylisoxazolyl Oxacillin"]
    CORE --> H["Active site serine PBP trap"]
    H --> I["Acylated enzyme inactivated"]
```

### 4.2 Mechanism of Action Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Penicillin enters periplasm"] --> A2["Diffuses through porin or OM"]
    A2 --> A3["Binds PBP transpeptidase active site"]
    A3 --> A4["D Ala D Ala mimicry at subsite"]
    A4 --> A5["Serine OH attacks beta lactam carbonyl"]
    A5 --> A6["Ring opens C N cleavage"]
    A6 --> A7["Stable acyl enzyme formed"]
    A7 --> A8["Peptidoglycan cross link halted"]
    A8 --> A9["Autolysin activation"]
    A9 --> A10["Cell wall rupture and bacterial lysis"]
```

### 4.3 Industrial Production Flow (Fermentation → 6-APA → Semi-Synthetic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Spore inoculum of Penicillium chrysogenum"] --> P2["Seed fermenter 1000 L"]
    P2 --> P3["Production fermenter 100 000 L"]
    P3 --> P4["Filtration removes mycelium"]
    P4 --> P5["Solvent extraction butyl acetate at pH 2"]
    P5 --> P6["Crude Pen G crystals"]
    P6 --> P7["Enzymatic cleavage by penicillin acylase"]
    P7 --> P8["6 APA nucleus isolated"]
    P8 --> P9["Coupling with side chain acid chloride or Dane salt"]
    P9 --> P10["Semi synthetic penicillin Ampicillin Amoxicillin etc"]
    P10 --> P11["Crystallization drying and formulation"]
```

### 4.4 Resistance Mechanism Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["Beta lactamase enzyme"] --> R2["Hydrolyzes beta lactam ring"]
    R2 --> R3["Penicilloic acid inactive"]
    R4["Altered PBP2a in MRSA"] --> R5["Low penicillin affinity"]
    R6["Porin loss in Gram negatives"] --> R7["Reduced uptake"]
    R8["Efflux pumps"] --> R9["Antibiotic expelled"]
    R3 --> RZ["Clinical resistance"]
    R5 --> RZ
    R7 --> RZ
    R9 --> RZ
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define the term "β-lactam antibiotic". Name the natural source of penicillin.*

**Model Answer:**
β-lactam antibiotics are a class of antibacterial agents whose core structure contains a **β-lactam ring** (a four-membered cyclic amide). The β-lactam ring acylates the active-site serine of bacterial transpeptidases (penicillin-binding proteins), inhibiting cell-wall cross-linking.
**Natural source:** The fungus ***Penicillium notatum*** (originally identified by Fleming) and the higher-yielding industrial strain ***Penicillium chrysogenum*** (used in submerged fermentation).
**(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Write the structure of 6-aminopenicillanic acid (6-APA) and state its significance.*

**Model Answer:**
6-APA is the **pharmacophore of all penicillins**, with structure: a fused bicyclic system comprising a β-lactam (4-membered, containing N and C=O) and a thiazolidine ring (5-membered, containing S) with a 3,3-dimethyl group, a C-3 carboxylic acid, and a free C-6 amino group (–NH₂).
**Significance:**
1. It is the **biosynthetic precursor** of all natural penicillins.
2. It is the **starting material for semi-synthetic penicillins** — by acylation of the C-6 amino group with different side chains, broad-spectrum, β-lactamase-stable, and orally active penicillins (Ampicillin, Amoxicillin, Methicillin, etc.) are produced.
**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]*

**(a) Discuss in detail the chemistry of penicillins, including the discovery, structure, and SAR.  (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Discovery (2 marks):**
- Sir Alexander Fleming, in **September 1928**, observed that a contaminating mould (*Penicillium notatum*) on a *Staphylococcus* culture plate produced a zone of bacterial lysis. He named the active substance *penicillin*.
- In **1939–1941**, an Oxford team (Florey, Chain, Heatley) isolated and purified penicillin, demonstrating its in-vivo efficacy in mice and humans.
- The discovery spurred the development of submerged-tank fermentation for industrial-scale production (USA and UK during WWII).

**Structure (3 marks):**
Penicillin possesses a **bicyclic core = 6-aminopenicillanic acid (6-APA)**, comprising:
- A **β-lactam ring** (4-membered, N–C=O).
- A **thiazolidine ring** (5-membered, S-containing), bearing **3,3-dimethyl** and a **C-3 carboxylic acid**.
- A **C-6 acylamido side chain (R-CO-NH–)**.
- Three stereogenic centres: C-2, C-5, C-6, with the absolute configuration **(2S, 5R, 6R)** essential for activity.

**SAR (2 marks):**
- The β-lactam ring is **absolutely essential**; ring opening destroys activity.
- The C-6 acyl side chain is the **"tunable" group**:
    - α-Amino group → broad spectrum (Ampicillin, Amoxicillin).
    - 2,6-Dimethoxyphenyl → β-lactamase resistance (Methicillin).
    - 2,6-Dichloro + 5-methylisoxazole-3-yl → β-lactamase resistance + oral (Dicloxacillin).
- The C-3 carboxyl can be **esterified** to make prodrugs (e.g., Pivampicillin, Bacampicillin).
- Stereochemistry at C-2, C-5, C-6 must be intact; **epimerization at C-6 inactivates** the drug.

---

**(b) Explain the mechanism of action of penicillins. Mention the role of transpeptidase.   (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Bacterial Cell Wall Context (1 mark):**
The bacterial cell wall is a **peptidoglycan** mesh of alternating NAG (N-acetylglucosamine) and NAM (N-acetylmuramic acid) sugars, cross-linked through short peptide bridges whose termini are **D-Ala-D-Ala**.

**Transpeptidase Role (2 marks):**
The **transpeptidase** (also called **Penicillin-Binding Protein, PBP**) catalyses the **cross-linking step** — it removes the terminal D-Ala of one peptide chain and joins the new terminal to a lysine/meso-DAP of an adjacent chain. The enzyme recognises the **D-Ala-D-Ala** terminus of the precursor.

**Mechanism of Penicillin Action (4 marks):**
1. Penicillin enters the periplasmic space by diffusion through porins.
2. Its β-lactam carbonyl resembles the **D-Ala-D-Ala terminus** → the PBP active-site **binds penicillin as a substrate mimic**.
3. The **active-site serine –OH** attacks the strained β-lactam carbonyl carbon.
4. The β-lactam **C–N bond cleaves**, opening the ring; an **irreversible acyl-enzyme** is formed.
5. The acyl-enzyme is **hydrolytically inert** — the PBP is permanently dead.
6. With cross-linking blocked, autolysins degrade the existing wall → **osmotic lysis and bacterial death**.

This is a **suicide (mechanism-based, irreversible) inhibition**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]*

**(a) Classify penicillins with examples. Discuss semi-synthetic penicillins in detail.   (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Penicillins (3 marks):**

1. **Natural penicillins** — produced by direct fermentation:
    - Penicillin G (Benzylpenicillin) — injectable.
    - Penicillin V (Phenoxymethylpenicillin) — acid-stable, oral.
    - Penicillin K, X, F, etc. — historically observed.
2. **Semi-synthetic penicillins** — produced by chemical modification of 6-APA:
    - **Acid-resistant/oral** — Pen V, Ampicillin, Amoxicillin, Pivampicillin.
    - **β-Lactamase-resistant (Penicillinase-resistant)** — Methicillin, Oxacillin, Cloxacillin, Dicloxacillin, Flucloxacillin, Nafcillin.
    - **Extended-spectrum** — Ampicillin, Amoxicillin, Pivampicillin, Talampicillin.
    - **Anti-pseudomonal / Carboxy- and Ureido-penicillins** — Carbenicillin, Ticarcillin, Piperacillin, Mezlocillin, Azlocillin.

**Semi-Synthetic Penicillins – Details (4 marks):**
Semi-synthetic penicillins are obtained by **acylation of 6-aminopenicillanic acid (6-APA)** with appropriate side-chain acids. 6-APA is itself produced by **enzymatic hydrolysis of penicillin G or V** with *E. coli* or *Pseudomonas* **penicillin acylase (PA)**. The free 6-APA is then coupled to a chosen side chain via **Schotten–Baumann acylation** (mixed anhydride method or DANE salt method). This enables tailored modifications:
- Introduction of **α-amino group** (e.g., D-phenylglycyl side chain in Ampicillin) **broadens the spectrum** to many Gram-negative organisms.
- **Bulky ortho-substituted aromatic side chains** (e.g., 2,6-dimethoxybenzoyl in Methicillin) **block β-lactamase access**, conferring resistance.
- **Electron-withdrawing groups** (isoxazole in Oxacillin) provide **acid stability**, enabling oral administration.
- **Heterocyclic ureido groups** (Piperacillin) enhance **anti-pseudomonal** activity.

**Examples & Uses:**
- **Ampicillin** — UTI, meningitis, respiratory infections.
- **Amoxicillin** — otitis media, sinusitis, endocarditis prophylaxis.
- **Methicillin** (now historical; replaced by Oxacillin/Flucloxacillin) — β-lactamase-producing *S. aureus*.
- **Piperacillin** (combined with tazobactam) — nosocomial infections, intra-abdominal sepsis.

---

**(b) Write the structure, mechanism of action, and uses of Ampicillin.   (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Structure (2 marks):**
Ampicillin is **α-aminobenzylpenicillin**, with the molecular formula $\text{C}_{16}\text{H}_{19}\text{N}_3\text{O}_4\text{S}$ (MW = 349.4 g/mol). The side chain is the **D-phenylglycyl** group (R = D–CH(NH₂)–C₆H₅) attached to 6-APA via an amide bond.

**Mechanism of Action (3 marks):**
Same as all penicillins — **bactericidal β-lactam**:
1. Diffuses through porins into the periplasm.
2. Binds multiple PBPs (PBP1, PBP3 in *E. coli*).
3. Acylates the active-site serine → irreversible PBP inactivation.
4. Cell-wall cross-linking halts → autolysis → bacterial death.
The free α-amino group on the side chain facilitates **entry through Gram-negative porins**, broadening the spectrum to *E. coli*, *H. influenzae*, *Salmonella*, *Shigella*, etc.

**Uses (2 marks):**
- Urinary tract infections (UTI).
- Respiratory tract infections.
- Meningitis (especially *Listeria monocytogenes* — drug of choice with gentamicin).
- Endocarditis (prophylaxis before dental procedures).
- Typhoid and paratyphoid fevers.
- Otitis media and sinusitis in children.

Often combined with **β-lactamase inhibitors** (e.g., **Sulbactam** → Unasyn) to extend spectrum to β-lactamase producers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Writing "Penicillium notatum" only** and forgetting the modern industrial strain *P. chrysogenum* — KTU examiners often expect both.
> 2. **Missing the chirality** in 6-APA: forgetting to mention the **(2S, 5R, 6R)** configuration — **−2 marks** if skipped.
> 3. **Confusing β-lactamase-resistant with extended-spectrum** penicillins — Ampicillin is broad-spectrum but **NOT** β-lactamase-resistant.
> 4. **Mechanism answers** must explicitly state "**irreversible acylation of PBP**" and the **D-Ala-D-Ala mimicry** — generic "inhibits cell wall" wording loses **2–3 marks**.
> 5. **SAR question** — many students forget that the β-lactam ring is **absolutely essential**; this is a board-favourite 1-marker.
> 6. **Methicillin** is no longer recommended clinically (historical significance only) — note it as historical, not as current first-line.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Penicillin = **β-lactam + thiazolidine** bicyclic core (6-APA) + variable **C-6 acyl side chain**.
- ✅ **Discovery:** Fleming, 1928; therapeutic use 1941; Nobel 1945.
- ✅ **Mechanism:** **Mechanism-based (suicide) inhibition of PBP / transpeptidase**; β-lactam carbonyl mimics **D-Ala-D-Ala**; acylation of active-site Ser-OH.
- ✅ **Bactericidal** (not bacteriostatic).
- ✅ **SAR golden rules:** β-lactam ring essential; C-6 side chain tunes spectrum, stability, β-lactamase resistance; C-3 carboxyl for prodrugs; **3 stereocentres (2S,5R,6R)** mandatory.
- ✅ **Classification:** Natural (Pen G, Pen V); Semi-synthetic — acid-resistant, β-lactamase-resistant, extended-spectrum, anti-pseudomonal.
- ✅ **Resistance:** β-lactamase cleavage, altered PBP (MRSA's PBP2a), porin loss, efflux.
- ✅ **Industrial production:** Submerged fermentation of *P. chrysogenum*; isolation via solvent extraction; enzymatic cleavage to 6-APA (penicillin acylase); semi-synthesis by Schotten–Baumann acylation.
- ✅ **Assay methods:** Iodometric, HPLC, microbiological (zone of inhibition), FT-IR, NMR, polarimetry, Karl Fischer.
- ✅ **Derivative families:** Cephalosporins, Carbapenems, Monobactams — all derived from penicillin chemistry.
- ✅ **Combination therapy:** **β-lactam + β-lactamase inhibitor** (e.g., Amoxicillin + Clavulanic acid, Ampicillin + Sulbactam, Piperacillin + Tazobactam).
- ✅ **Units:** 1 unit penicillin = **0.6 μg pure benzylpenicillin sodium** (USP/FDA).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calcium Gluconate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Calcium Gluconate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Calcium Gluconate** is the calcium salt of **gluconic acid**, a polyhydroxycarboxylic acid derived from the oxidation of glucose. It is a widely used pharmaceutical excipient and therapeutic agent, primarily employed as a **calcium supplement** and as an **emergency medication** for conditions such as hypocalcemia, hyperkalemia, calcium channel blocker toxicity, and hydrofluoric acid burns. Chemically, it is a **pharmaceutical grade calcium salt** with the molecular formula **C₁₂H₂₂CaO₁₄** and a molecular weight of **430.37 g/mol**.

The official IUPAC name is **Calcium (2R,3S,4R,5R)-2,3,4,5,6-pentahydroxyhexanoate**, indicating that it is a bivalent salt formed between one calcium ion (Ca²⁺) and two molecules of gluconic acid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Calcium Gluconate is a high-frequency case study drug in Module 1 of PECHT751. The board examiner expects coverage of: (1) Chemistry & nomenclature, (2) Synthesis, (3) Mechanism of action, (4) Pharmaceutical formulations, (5) Analytical quality control, and (6) Therapeutic applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your body as a **high-rise building under construction** — bones are the steel-reinforced concrete skeleton, nerves are the electrical wiring, and muscles are the cranes. **Calcium ions act as the "regulatory currency"** that keeps the structural concrete strong, the electrical signals flowing, and the crane mechanisms contracting. When the building runs short on calcium currency (hypocalcemia), cracks appear, signals fail, and operations shut down.

**Calcium Gluconate is the safest, slow-release "calcium cash injection"** — when a patient cannot rely on dietary calcium (milk, leafy greens) or when the body is losing calcium rapidly, doctors administer calcium gluconate because it is:
- **Mild** to tissues (unlike calcium chloride, which is highly irritating to veins).
- **Water-soluble enough** to be given intravenously.
- **Slowly ionizable**, releasing Ca²⁺ in a controlled manner.

Think of it as the **"gentle calcium banker"** of the hospital — it does the job without burning a hole in the patient's veins.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Chemical Identity

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Molecular Formula | C₁₂H₂₂CaO₁₄ |
| Molecular Weight | **430.37 g/mol** |
| Elemental Calcium Content | **~9.3%** by weight |
| Appearance | White, odorless, crystalline powder |
| Taste | Slightly sweet |
| Solubility (water) | Sparingly soluble in cold; soluble in hot water |
| pH (1% aqueous) | **6.0 – 7.5** |
| Melting Point | Decomposes above **~195 °C** |
| Pharmacopoeia Status | Listed in **IP, BP, USP, EP** |

> [!NOTE]
> The fact that calcium gluconate is **bivalent** (one Ca²⁺ binds two gluconate⁻ anions) is critical. Compared to calcium chloride (CaCl₂), it delivers **only one calcium ion per molecule vs. one per molecule** but at a much higher **molecular weight**, which is why the *milliequivalent* comparison matters clinically: 1 g Ca-gluconate = **4.65 mEq Ca²⁺**, while 1 g CaCl₂ = **13.6 mEq Ca²⁺**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemical Architecture — Why Gluconate is Special

Gluconic acid is the C-1 oxidation product of **β-D-glucose**, where the aldehyde group (–CHO) at the anomeric carbon is converted to a carboxylic acid (–COOH) without affecting the rest of the sugar backbone. The remaining **four secondary hydroxyl groups (C-2 to C-5)** and the **primary hydroxyl at C-6** remain intact, making the molecule highly **hydrophilic** and **hygroscopic**.

When two molecules of gluconic acid donate their acidic proton to a single calcium ion, the resulting calcium gluconate salt inherits:
- A **hydrophilic sugar-like shell** (excellent water solubility when warmed).
- A **mildly acidic to neutral pH** when dissolved (safe for parenteral use).
- A **chiral backbone** (5 stereocenters), requiring stereospecific manufacturing controls.

> [!NOTE]
> The C-2 to C-5 hydroxyls can chelate Ca²⁺ intramolecularly, forming a **pseudo-chelate complex** that protects calcium from rapid precipitation as insoluble calcium phosphate in blood. This is the *pharmacokinetic* basis for using calcium gluconate IV over calcium chloride in many emergencies.

## 2.2 Mechanism of Pharmacological Action

Calcium gluconate dissociates in the bloodstream to release **bioactive Ca²⁺ ions**, which serve four primary physiological functions:

1. **Bone and teeth mineralization** — Hydroxyapatite crystal formation: Ca₁₀(PO₄)₆(OH)₂.
2. **Neuromuscular transmission** — Ca²⁺ triggers acetylcholine vesicle release at the motor end plate.
3. **Cardiac muscle contraction** — Ca²⁺ binds troponin C, enabling actin–myosin cross-bridge cycling.
4. **Blood coagulation cascade** — Ca²⁺ is **Factor IV** in the classical coagulation pathway.

## 2.3 Therapeutic Classification

Calcium gluconate straddles **two major therapeutic categories**:
- **Mineral supplement** (oral dosage forms, chronic therapy).
- **Emergency cardiovascular / antidote agent** (parenteral, hospital use).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Data Sheet

| Concept / Quantity | Formula / Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight of Ca-gluconate | $M = 430.37$ | g/mol |
| Elemental Ca in 1 g Ca-gluconate | $m_{Ca} = 1 \times 40.08 \div 430.37 \times 1000$ | = **93 mg Ca²⁺ per gram** |
| mEq of Ca²⁺ per g Ca-gluconate | $n_{mEq} = (2 \times 1000) \div 430.37$ | = **4.65 mEq/g** |
| Required Ca-gluconate for 1 g elemental Ca | $m_{required} = 1000 \div 0.093$ | ≈ **10.75 g** |
| Hydroxyapatite stoichiometry | $\mathrm{Ca_{10}(PO_4)_6(OH)_2}$ | Ca : P molar ratio **= 5 : 3** |
| Synthesis stoichiometry | $\mathrm{2\,C_6H_{12}O_7 + CaCO_3 \to (C_6H_{11}O_7)_2Ca + CO_2\!\uparrow + H_2O}$ | Neutralization with CaCO₃ |
| Solubility (cold water) | ~ **3 g / 100 mL** | At 25 °C |
| Solubility (hot water) | ~ **30 g / 100 mL** | At 100 °C |
| pKa of gluconic acid | $pK_a \approx 3.70$ | Weak organic acid |
| Stability pH range | **6.0 – 8.5** | Outside this → CaCO₃ / Ca(OH)₂ precipitation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Eye:** Always remember the dissociation equation  
> $(\mathrm{C_6H_{11}O_7})_2\mathrm{Ca} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ca^{2+}} + 2\,\mathrm{C_6H_{11}O_7^-}$  
> This single line is worth 2 marks in Part A and often forms the opening of a 14-mark Part B question.

## 2.5 Real-World Pharmaceutical Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Hospital Medicine** | IV emergency for hypocalcemia, hyperkalemia, CCB toxicity |
| **Dentistry** | Pulp capping, root canal medicaments |
| **Dermatology** | Topical gel for hydrofluoric acid burns (2.5%) |
| **Food Industry** | Firming agent, calcium fortifier, sequestrant (**E578**) |
| **Cosmetics** | Skin conditioning, pH balancer in creams |
| **Veterinary** | Calcium supplementation in dairy cattle (milk fever) |
| **Analytical Chemistry** | Standard in complexometric titrations for Ca²⁺ determination |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Elemental Calcium Content in Calcium Gluconate

**Problem:** Calculate the mass of elemental calcium present in 1 g of pure calcium gluconate. Also compute the milli-equivalents (mEq) of Ca²⁺ per gram.

**Step 1 — Write the dissociation equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\mathrm{C_6H_{11}O_7})_2\mathrm{Ca} &\longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca^{2+}} + 2\,\mathrm{C_6H_{11}O_7^-}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows that **one mole** of calcium gluconate furnishes **one mole** of Ca²⁺ ions (valency = 2).

**Step 2 — Recall atomic and molecular weights.**

- Atomic weight of Ca = **40.08 g/mol**
- Molecular weight of Ca-gluconate = **430.37 g/mol**

**Step 3 — Apply the mass fraction formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass fraction of Ca} &= \frac{\text{Atomic mass of Ca}}{\text{Molecular mass of Ca-gluconate}} \\
&= \frac{40.08}{430.37}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the mass of Ca in 1 g of salt.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{Ca} &= 1\,\mathrm{g} \times \frac{40.08}{430.37} \\
&= 0.0931\,\mathrm{g} \\
&= 93.1\,\mathrm{mg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert mass of Ca to milli-equivalents.**

The valence factor of Ca²⁺ is **2** (it donates 2 electrons / carries 2 positive charges). Thus:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{mEq of Ca}^{2+}\text{ per g} &= \frac{\text{mg of Ca}^{2+} \times \text{valence}}{\text{atomic weight}} \\
&= \frac{93.1 \times 2}{40.08} \\
&= 4.646 \;\mathrm{mEq/g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final clinical takeaway.**

$$
\boxed{1\,\mathrm{g\,Ca\text{-}gluconate} = 93\,\mathrm{mg\,Ca^{2+}} = 4.65\,\mathrm{mEq\,Ca^{2+}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU board valuation note:** A common error is to divide by 40.08 only once (treating Ca²⁺ as monovalent). Always multiply by valence (2) for **mEq** conversion. Marks lost here are unforgivable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Synthesis Yield Calculation

**Problem:** Starting from 500 g of pure gluconic acid (M = 196.16 g/mol) and excess calcium carbonate, calculate the theoretical yield of calcium gluconate (M = 430.37 g/mol).

**Step 1 — Balanced equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\,\mathrm{C_6H_{12}O_7} + \mathrm{CaCO_3} &\longrightarrow (\mathrm{C_6H_{11}O_7})_2\mathrm{Ca} + \mathrm{CO_2}\!\uparrow + \mathrm{H_2O}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine moles of gluconic acid.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{acid}} &= \frac{500\,\mathrm{g}}{196.16\,\mathrm{g/mol}} = 2.549\,\mathrm{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply stoichiometry (2 mol acid → 1 mol Ca-gluconate).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{Ca-gluconate}} &= \frac{2.549}{2} = 1.2745\,\mathrm{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mass.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{theoretical}} &= 1.2745 \times 430.37 = 548.4\,\mathrm{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — If actual yield obtained is 480 g, calculate % yield.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{Yield} &= \frac{480}{548.4} \times 100 = 87.5\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Theoretical yield} = 548.4\,\mathrm{g}, \quad \text{\% Yield} = 87.5\,\%}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Quality Control Matrix

| Test | Method | Acceptance Limit (USP) | Pharmaceutical Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Identification A (Ca²⁺) | Oxalate test — white ppt of CaC₂O₄ | Positive | Confirms Ca²⁺ ion |
| Identification B (Gluconate) | Fehling's reduction → red Cu₂O ppt | Positive | Confirms gluconate moiety |
| Assay (complexometric) | EDTA titration with Eriochrome Black T | **98.0 – 102.0%** of label | Purity of active |
| Clarity & color of solution | Visual / nephelometry | Clear, colorless to pale yellow | Absence of insoluble impurities |
| pH (5% solution) | Potentiometry | **6.0 – 7.5** | Stability window |
| Heavy metals (Pb) | Limit test | ≤ **10 ppm** | Safety |
| Loss on drying | Gravimetry (105 °C, 3 h) | ≤ **3.0%** | Moisture control |
| Bacterial endotoxin (for IV grade) | LAL test | ≤ **0.17 EU/mg** (USP) | Pyrogen safety |
| Particulate matter | Light obscuration | ≤ 25 particles ≥ 10 µm / mL | Injectable safety |
