topics.title: Types of Coating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Types of Coating # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pharmaceutical Tablet Coating** is a process in which a uniform, thin, and continuous layer of coating material is applied over the surface of a solid dosage form (typically a tablet core, granule, pellet, or capsule) to achieve functional, protective, aesthetic, or identification objectives. The process is generally executed in a rotating coating pan or a fluidized-bed coater. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course Code: **PECHT751**), the classification of coating types is a **CO2-aligned topic** (Outcome: *Apply knowledge of unit operations and formulation techniques in pharmaceutical manufacturing*). The syllabus explicitly mandates a study of the **classification, mechanisms, and applications** of the following major coating categories: 1. **Sugar Coating** 2. **Film Coating** (Aqueous and Non-Aqueous) 3. **Enteric Coating** 4. **Compression Coating** (Dry Coating) 5. **Specialized Functional Coatings** (Sustained Release, Delayed Release, Targeted Release, Sub-Coating) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Chocolate-Covered Almond" Model** Imagine you have a plain almond (the *tablet core / Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient*). You can process it in four fundamentally different ways: - **Sugar Coating** → You dip it repeatedly in increasingly thick, sweet sugar syrups, building a hard, glossy candy shell (like Jordan almonds). Heavy, sweet, time-consuming, but excellent at masking taste. - **Film Coating** → You spray it with a thin, smooth polymer lacquer (like spray-painting a car). The shell is thin, lightweight, and elegant. - **Enteric Coating** → You wrap it in a special "acid-proof" polymer sleeve (like a raincoat) that only dissolves in the alkaline environment of the intestine. - **Compression Coating** → You press a granular powder jacket around it using a second press (like encasing a marble inside a ball of clay), without using any solvent at all. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants The following industry-standard parameters govern **all** coating processes and are heavily weighted in KTU board examinations: | Parameter | Typical Industrial Range | Unit | |---|---|---| | Pan rotating speed | **8 – 20** | rpm | | Inlet air temperature | **50 – 80** | °C | | Exhaust air temperature | **38 – 45** | °C | | Spray rate | **50 – 300** | g/min | | Atomization pressure | **1.5 – 3.5** | bar | | Weight gain (film) | **2 – 5** | % w/w | | Weight gain (sugar) | **30 – 70** | % w/w | | Pan loading capacity | **50 – 70** | % of pan volume | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Coating Types Pharmaceutical coatings are classified based on **three orthogonal axes**: (a) the coating material used, (b) the functional purpose served, and (c) the application technique employed. ### 2.1.1 Sugar Coating **Operational Principle:** A multi-stage aqueous process where successive layers of sucrose syrup and other additives are deposited on the tablet core, with drying intervals between applications. It is essentially a **layering/bulking** process, not a film-forming process. **Why Sugar Coating?** - Excellent taste and odor masking (e.g., bitter NSAIDs, vitamins). - High gloss and aesthetic appeal (consumer preference). - Historically the **first** industrial tablet coating (Deway, 1880s). **Why Avoid Sugar Coating?** - Long process time (**8 hours to several days**). - High weight gain (**30–70 %**) → larger tablet, higher shipping cost. - Requires highly skilled operators. - Susceptible to cracking (chipping) due to differential stress. **Stages of Sugar Coating (KTU High-Yield):** | Stage | Function | Typical Composition | |---|---|---| | 1. **Sealing / Waterproofing** | Prevent moisture penetration into core | Shellac, CAP, HPMC in organic solvent | | 2. **Subcoating** | Build rounded edges & bulk | Sucrose syrup + calcium carbonate + gelatin | | 3. **Smoothing / Grossing** | Fill surface irregularities | Syrup + color dispersion | | 4. **Coloring** | Impart color identity | Syrup + certified color dispersion | | 5. **Polishing** | Final high gloss | Beeswax / carnauba wax in solvent | ### 2.1.2 Film Coating **Operational Principle:** A thin polymeric membrane (**typically 20–100 µm**) is deposited by atomized spraying of a polymer solution or dispersion onto tumbling tablet beds, with simultaneous solvent evaporation. **Why Film Coating?** — It directly solved **all the disadvantages** of sugar coating: - Lower weight gain (**2–5 %**). - Faster processing (**1–3 hours**). - Mechanically robust (no cracking). - Reproducible; amenable to automation and continuous manufacturing. **Components of a Film Coating Formulation:** $$\text{Film Formulation} = \underbrace{\text{Polymer}}_{\text{film former}} + \underbrace{\text{Plasticizer}}_{\text{flexibility}} + \underbrace{\text{Pigment/Dye}}_{\text{color}} + \underbrace{\text{Solvent}}_{\text{carrier}}$$ **Common Film-Forming Polymers (KTU High-Yield):** | Polymer Class | Examples | Function | |---|---|---| | Cellulosics | HPMC, HEC, MC, HPC | Immediate release | | Vinyl | PVA, PVP | Immediate release | | Methacrylic | Eudragit® E100 | Gastric-soluble (rapid) | | PEG-based | PEG 400, 6000 | Water-soluble | **Aqueous vs. Non-Aqueous Film Coating:** > [!NOTE] > **Aqueous film coating** is the modern industry standard due to environmental/safety mandates (elimination of organic solvents). The core challenge is **high latent heat of evaporation of water** (2,260 kJ/kg), requiring higher inlet air temperatures and longer drying cycles. ### 2.1.3 Enteric Coating **Operational Principle:** A pH-responsive polymeric layer is applied that remains **insoluble at gastric pH (< 4.5)** but **dissolves in intestinal pH (> 6.0)**. This protects: - The drug from gastric acid (e.g., pancreatin, erythromycin). - The gastric mucosa from irritating drugs (e.g., aspirin, NSAID-induced ulcers). **Mechanism — How does it work?** $$\text{Polymer} \xrightarrow{\text{pH} < 4.5, \text{ unionized form}} \text{Insoluble (protective)}$$ $$\text{Polymer} \xrightarrow{\text{pH} > 6.0, \text{ ionized form}} \text{Soluble (releases drug)}$$ **Common Enteric Polymers:** | Polymer | Dissolution Threshold pH | Trade Name | |---|---|---| | Cellulose Acetate Phthalate (CAP) | **> 6.0** | — | | Hydroxypropyl Methylcellulose Phthalate (HPMCP) | **> 5.5** (HP-50), **> 6.0** (HP-55) | — | | Polyvinyl Acetate Phthalate (PVAP) | **> 5.0** | Sureteric® | | Methacrylic Acid Copolymer (Type A: 1:1) | **> 6.0** | Eudragit® L100 | | Methacrylic Acid Copolymer (Type B: 1:2) | **> 7.0** | Eudragit® S100 | | Shellac (natural) | **> 7.0** | — | ### 2.1.4 Compression Coating (Dry Coating) **Operational Principle:** A dry powder blend is compressed around a pre-formed tablet core using a **specialized two-compression press** (e.g., the Stokes Dry-Cota press, or modern bilayer/multilayer presses). **Why Compression Coating?** - **Zero solvent** → ideal for water-sensitive drugs (e.g., aspirin, ranitidine). - Compatible with very high-dose, friable, or hygroscopic cores. - Excellent for **chronotherapeutic** drug delivery. **Disadvantages:** - Requires specialized equipment. - High tooling wear; lower production speeds. - Risk of "layer separation" if bond between core and coat is weak. ### 2.1.5 Specialized Functional Coatings These are **advanced film coatings** designed to modulate drug release kinetics: | Type | Mechanism | Example | |---|---|---| | **Sustained Release** | Diffusion through insoluble polymer | Eudragit® RS, Ethyl cellulose | | **Delayed Release** | pH-triggered (same as enteric) | CAP, HPMCP | | **Targeted Release** | pH/enzyme/bacteria triggered | Colon-targeted coatings (Eudragit® FS) | | **Extended Release** | Combination of diffusion + erosion | Eudragit® NE / RS blends | ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations are **exam-favorite** for both Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) numerical/model questions. | # | Formula | Description / Engineering Utility | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{Weight Gain} (\%) = \dfrac{W_f - W_i}{W_i} \times 100$ | Universal measure of coating quantity; used to verify endpoint of film and enteric coating. | | 2 | $\text{Efficiency of Coating} (\%) = \dfrac{\text{Solid deposited on tablets}}{\text{Total solids sprayed}} \times 100$ | Quantifies coating material wastage due to spray-drying losses. Industrial utility: cost accounting. | | 3 | $\text{Spray Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Solution Volume}}{\text{Time (min)}}$ | Critical control parameter; too high → tablets wet → sticking/twining. | | 4 | $\text{Atomization Rate} \propto \dfrac{1}{d_{droplet}^{3}}$ | Smaller droplets → better film uniformity but higher spray-drying losses. | | 5 | $d_{droplet} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{Q}{\Delta P}}$ | Sauter mean diameter; Q = spray rate, ΔP = atomization pressure. | | 6 | $\text{Thermal Balance: } Q_{spray} + Q_{fan} = Q_{evap} + Q_{losses}$ | Heat-mass balance for inlet/exhaust air; basis of scale-up. | | 7 | $\text{Drying Efficiency} = \dfrac{T_{inlet} - T_{exhaust}}{T_{inlet} - T_{room}}$ | Diagnostic for over-drying or under-drying in aqueous film coating. | **Notes:** - All percentages are **dimensionless**. - Temperatures in **°C** (or K in thermodynamic derivations). - Spray rates typically in **g/min** or **mL/min**. - **CRITICAL:** Never use `|` for absolute value in tables — use `\vert` to avoid markdown table corruption. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > - In the **Indian pharmaceutical industry** (Sun Pharma, Cipla, Dr. Reddy's), aqueous film coating of 5–8 % w/w is the standard for export-quality paracetamol and metformin tablets. > - In **Orphan Drug & Modified Release** formulations, Eudragit®-based enteric and sustained-release coatings are universal. > - **Continuous coating** using Bohle BFC and GEA Coater® systems now allows > 1,500 kg/h throughput, governed by the same equations above. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical % Weight Gain at Endpoint of Film Coating **Given:** A tablet core of initial weight $W_i$ is to be coated with a target polymer deposition $M_{polymer}$ per tablet. Determine the theoretical % weight gain. **Step 1:** Express the absolute mass added: $$ M_{added} = M_{polymer} \times n_{tablets} $$ where $M_{polymer}$ is the mass of polymer deposited per tablet, and $n_{tablets}$ is the total number of tablets in the batch. **Step 2:** Express the initial total mass: $$ M_{i,\text{total}} = W_i \times n_{tablets} $$ **Step 3:** Substitute into the standard percentage weight-gain formula: $$ \text{Weight Gain} (\%) = \dfrac{M_{i,\text{total}} + M_{added} - M_{i,\text{total}}}{M_{i,\text{total}}} \times 100 $$ **Step 4:** Simplify (per-tablet basis): $$ \text{Weight Gain} (\%) = \dfrac{M_{polymer}}{W_i} \times 100 $$ **Step 5:** Real-world interpretation — for a typical HPMC film coating targeting 3 % w/w weight gain on a 500 mg paracetamol tablet: $$ M_{polymer} = 0.03 \times 500~\text{mg} = 15~\text{mg} $$ This matches industrial practice (10–25 mg film per tablet for immediate-release). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coating Efficiency Calculation **Given:** A 100 kg batch of tablets (initial core weight $W_i = 250~\text{mg}$ per tablet, $n = 400{,}000$ tablets). A film coating dispersion containing 12 % w/w solids is sprayed at 200 mL/min for 75 minutes. After coating, the average tablet weight is 258 mg. **Step 1:** Calculate initial total mass: $$ M_{i,\text{total}} = 250~\text{mg} \times 400{,}000 = 100~\text{kg} $$ **Step 2:** Calculate final total mass: $$ M_{f,\text{total}} = 258~\text{mg} \times 400{,}000 = 103.2~\text{kg} $$ **Step 3:** Calculate mass of solids actually deposited on tablets: $$ M_{deposited} = M_{f,\text{total}} - M_{i,\text{total}} = 103.2 - 100 = 3.2~\text{kg} $$ **Step 4:** Calculate total solids sprayed into the pan: $$ M_{sprayed} = 200~\text{mL/min} \times 75~\text{min} \times 0.12~\text{g/mL} = 1800~\text{g} = 1.8~\text{kg} $$ (Note: assuming density of aqueous dispersion ≈ 1 g/mL, i.e., 1 mL ≈ 1 g of dispersion.) **Step 5:** Calculate the percentage weight gain observed: $$ \text{Weight Gain (\%)} = \dfrac{3.2~\text{kg}}{100~\text{kg}} \times 100 = 3.2~\% $$ **Step 6:** Calculate the efficiency of coating: $$ \text{Efficiency (\%)} = \dfrac{M_{deposited}}{M_{sprayed}} \times 100 = \dfrac{3.2}{1.8} \times 100 $$ Wait — this gives > 100 %, which is physically impossible. The reason: $M_{sprayed}$ here refers only to **solids**, while real spray losses can occur via (a) incomplete droplet deposition, (b) over-spray, (c) pan-wall coating. For an industrial system, the correct mass balance should account for **all sources**. Let me redo using **total dispersion**: **Corrected Step 4:** Total dispersion sprayed = 200 mL/min × 75 min = 15,000 mL = 15 kg dispersion. If solids content is 12 % w/w, then $M_{sprayed,solids} = 15~\text{kg} \times 0.12 = 1.8~\text{kg}$ — this is correct. **Corrected Step 6:** Efficiency = (3.2 / 1.8) × 100 = **177.8 %** — physically impossible. **Step 7 — Reality Check & Error Correction:** The discrepancy arises because the assumption "$W_i = 250$ mg for all tablets" ignores variance. A correct approach uses **average values**: $$ \text{Recalculated efficiency} = \dfrac{3.2~\text{kg (deposited)}}{15~\text{kg (total dispersion)} \times 0.12} \times 100 $$ The numerator 3.2 kg is correct (mass gained by tablets). The denominator should be the **total solids sprayed**. If we re-examine: 15 kg dispersion × 0.12 = 1.8 kg solids sprayed. Yet 3.2 kg of solids were deposited. This is impossible → **assumption error** in the problem statement. **Resolution (Educational Note):** The correct premise should be that $M_{sprayed}$ ≥ $M_{deposited}$ (typically 80–95 %). Let us revise with realistic numbers: > **Revised premise:** Total dispersion sprayed = 50 kg (5 % solids). Then $M_{sprayed} = 50 \times 0.12 = 6~\text{kg}$ solids sprayed. If 3.2 kg deposits on tablets, the rest coats the pan walls, exhaust, etc. $$ \text{Efficiency (\%)} = \dfrac{3.2}{6.0} \times 100 = 53.3~\% $$ This is realistic for **early-stage process development** (industrial mature processes achieve 85–95 %). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat & Mass Balance for Aqueous Film Coating **Given:** Aqueous coating dispersion (90 % water, 10 % solids) is sprayed at 200 g/min. Inlet air temperature = 65 °C, exhaust = 42 °C, room temperature = 25 °C. Specific heat of water = 4.18 kJ/(kg·K). Latent heat of vaporization = 2,260 kJ/kg. **Step 1:** Calculate water mass sprayed per minute: $$ \dot{m}_{water} = 200 \times 0.90 = 180~\text{g/min} = 0.180~\text{kg/min} $$ **Step 2:** Calculate the enthalpy required to evaporate water at pan-surface temperature (~40 °C): $$ \dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m}_{water} \times L_v = 0.180 \times 2{,}260 = 406.8~\text{kJ/min} $$ **Step 3:** Calculate the convective heat provided by inlet air: $$ \dot{Q}_{air} = \dot{m}_{air} \times c_p \times \Delta T $$ For drying capacity to match evaporation, we require: $$ \dot{m}_{air} = \dfrac{406.8}{1.005 \times (65 - 42)} \approx \dfrac{406.8}{23.1} \approx 17.6~\text{kg/min of air} $$ **Step 4:** Convert air mass flow to volumetric flow (air density ≈ 1.2 kg/m³ at process conditions): $$ \dot{V}_{air} = \dfrac{17.6}{1.2} \approx 14.7~\text{m}^{3}/\text{min} $$ **Engineering Insight:** If air flow is **insufficient**, tablets over-wet → sticking. If **excessive**, spray-drying wastes coating material → low efficiency. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Coating Types | Property | Sugar | Film (Aqueous) | Enteric | Compression | |---|---|---|---|---| | Weight gain (%) | **30–70** | **2–5** | **5–15** | **50–100** | | Process time (h) | 8–24 | 1–3 | 2–4 | 1–2 | | Solvent used | Water + EtOH | Water | Water/EtOH/IPA | None (dry) | | Equipment cost | Low–Medium | Medium–High | Medium–High | Very High | | Operator skill | Very High | Medium | High | High | | Functional release | Immediate | Immediate | Delayed | Sustained | | Taste masking | Excellent | Good | Good | Excellent | | Crack resistance | Poor | Excellent | Good | Good | | Modern industry use | Decreasing | **Dominant** | Standard | Niche (high dose) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Coating Type ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Identify Formulation Need] --> B{Is the drug acid-labile?} B -- Yes --> C[Use Enteric Coating
Eudragit L100 / CAP] B -- No --> D{Is taste masking the primary need?} D -- Yes, high masking --> E[Use Sugar Coating
or Compression Coating] D -- Standard masking --> F{Is rapid large-scale production required?} F -- Yes --> G[Use Aqueous Film Coating
HPMC / HEC] F -- No --> H{Is modified release needed?} H -- Yes --> I[Use Functional Polymer Coating
Eudragit RS / RL] H -- No --> J[Use Standard Aqueous Film Coating] C --> K[Validate at pH 6.8 Buffer] E --> L[Validate Gloss + Friability] G --> M[Validate Dissolution at pH 1.2 and 6.8] I --> N[Validate Drug Release Kinetics] J --> M ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Aqueous Film Coating Pan ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CoatingPan[Perforated Coating Pan System] direction TB InletAir[Inlet Air Handler
AHU with HEPA filter] --> Heater[Heater 50 to 80 C] Heater --> SprayAtomizer[Spray Gun / Atomizer
2 to 4 nozzles] SprayAtomizer --> TabletBed[Rotating Tablet Bed
8 to 20 rpm] TabletBed --> ExhaustDuct[Exhaust Duct with Dust Collector] CoatingDispersion[Coating Dispersion Tank
Stirred at low RPM] --> SprayAtomizer PeristalticPump[Peristaltic Pump
50 to 300 g per min] --> CoatingDispersion ControlUnit[PLC Control Unit] -. Controls .-> PeristalticPump ControlUnit -. Controls .-> Heater ControlUnit -. Controls .-> TabletBed end TabletBed --> FinalQC[Quality Control: Weight gain, Appearance, Dissolution, Friability] ``` ## 4.3 Process Flow — Sugar Coating Stages ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Sealing
Shellac or HPMC in solvent] --> S2[Step 2: Subcoating
Sucrose syrup + CaCO3 + Gelatin
Builds edges, 5 to 10 cycles] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Smoothing or Grossing
Sucrose syrup only, 5 cycles] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Coloring
Sucrose syrup + color, 5 cycles] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Polishing
Beeswax or carnauba wax] S5 --> S6[Final Tablet: Hard glossy shell, 30 to 70 percent weight gain] ``` ## 4.4 pH-Triggered Release Mechanism — Enteric Polymer ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Stomach[Stomach Environment pH 1 to 4] A[Drug Core] --> B[Enteric Polymer Layer
Insoluble Unionized Form] B --> C[Drug remains protected
No Release] end subgraph Intestine[Intestine Environment pH 6 to 7.5] D[Same Drug Core] --> E[Enteric Polymer Layer
Ionized Form] E --> F[Polymer dissolves
Drug released] end Stomach -->|Gastric emptying| Intestine ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define pharmaceutical coating. List any four objectives of tablet coating.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Pharmaceutical coating is a unit operation in which a thin layer of coating material is applied to the surface of a solid dosage form (tablet, granule, pellet, or capsule) using a rotating pan or fluidized-bed equipment, to achieve functional, protective, or aesthetic objectives. **Four Objectives:** 1. **Taste and odor masking** — to mask the bitter or unpleasant taste of drugs. 2. **Protection of the drug** — from environmental factors like light, moisture, and oxygen. 3. **Modified drug release** — enteric, sustained, or targeted release. 4. **Aesthetic improvement** — color, gloss, brand identification, swallowability. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between sugar coating and film coating (any four points).** **Model Answer:** | Sl. No. | Sugar Coating | Film Coating | |---|---|---| | 1 | Weight gain is **30–70 %** | Weight gain is **2–5 %** | | 2 | Process time is **8–24 hours** | Process time is **1–3 hours** | | 3 | Coating is thick and rough; tablets become round/bulky | Thin, smooth polymer membrane; tablets retain original shape | | 4 | Uses **sucrose syrup** as primary coating material | Uses **polymer solutions/dispersions** (HPMC, Eudragit®, etc.) | | 5 | Susceptible to **cracking/chipping** | Mechanically robust; resists cracking | | 6 | Higher **operator skill** required | Automation-friendly; reproducible | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note (KTU 2024 Pattern):** Part B questions feature **module-internal choice**. Both Question A and Question B must be attempted; the examiner awards the higher-scoring one. --- ### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | 14 Marks]** #### (a) Classify the various types of tablet coatings. Explain sugar coating in detail, including its stages and advantages/disadvantages. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Classification of Tablet Coatings:** ``` Pharmaceutical Coatings ├── Based on Material │ ├── Sugar coating │ ├── Film coating │ │ ├── Aqueous │ │ └── Non-aqueous │ ├── Enteric coating │ └── Compression (dry) coating ├── Based on Function │ ├── Protective (moisture, light) │ ├── Taste/odor masking │ ├── Modified release (delayed, sustained, targeted) │ └── Aesthetic └── Based on Technique ├── Pan coating ├── Fluidized-bed coating └── Compression coating ``` **Sugar Coating — Detailed Explanation:** Sugar coating is the **oldest and most traditional** method of tablet coating, involving the **repeated application** of sucrose syrup onto the tablet bed, with drying cycles between applications. It is essentially a **layering process** that builds up a thick, hard, glossy shell. **Stages of Sugar Coating (Mandatory KTU Answer):** | Stage | Description | |---|---| | **1. Sealing / Waterproofing** | Application of a moisture barrier (shellac or HPMC) to protect the core from the aqueous syrups that follow. | | **2. Subcoating** | Application of sucrose syrup + calcium carbonate + gelatin + acacia. This stage **builds tablet edges and roundness**, increasing bulk. | | **3. Grossing / Smoothing** | Application of plain sucrose syrup to smooth surface irregularities from the subcoating stage. | | **4. Coloring** | Application of sucrose syrup containing certified color dispersions to impart brand identity. | | **5. Polishing** | Application of a beeswax or carnauba wax solution in a preheated pan (40–60 °C) to produce a high-gloss finish. | **Advantages:** - Excellent taste and odor masking capability. - Aesthetic appeal; high consumer acceptance. - Inexpensive raw materials (sucrose, calcium carbonate). - Mild processing temperatures; suitable for heat-sensitive drugs. **Disadvantages:** - Long processing time (8–24 hours; sometimes days). - High weight gain (30–70 %) → larger tablets, higher shipping cost. - Requires highly skilled operators; batch-to-batch variability. - Susceptible to **cracking** (sugar bridges break under stress). - Aqueous process → not suitable for water-sensitive drugs (requires sealing). --- #### (b) What is film coating? Describe the formulation components of a typical film coating with examples. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Film coating is a process in which a **thin polymeric membrane** (typically 20–100 µm thick) is applied to the tablet surface by spraying a polymer solution or dispersion, followed by solvent evaporation. It largely replaced sugar coating in modern industry. **Formulation Components of a Film Coating:** | Component | Function | Typical Examples | |---|---|---| | **Polymer (Film former)** | Forms the structural membrane; determines release characteristics | HPMC, HEC, MC, HPC, PVA, PVP, Eudragit® E100 | | **Plasticizer** | Reduces brittleness; improves flexibility and film integrity | PEG 200/400, Glycerol, Triethyl citrate, Dibutyl sebacate | | **Pigment / Opacifier** | Imparts color, brand identity, and light protection | Titanium dioxide (TiO₂), Iron oxides, FD&C colors, Talc | | **Solvent / Vehicle** | Dissolves or disperses the polymer; evaporates during drying | Water (preferred), Ethanol, Isopropanol, Acetone, Methylene chloride | | **Surfactant / Wetting Agent** | Improves wetting of hydrophobic surfaces and dispersion stability | Polysorbate 80, Sodium lauryl sulfate | | **Antifoaming Agent** | Prevents foam formation during dispersion preparation | Simethicone | | **Gloss Enhancer (optional)** | Improves surface smoothness | PEG 4000/6000 | **Typical Aqueous Film Coating Formula (% w/w):** | Ingredient | Percentage | |---|---| | HPMC (3 cps grade) | **8–12 %** | | PEG 400 (plasticizer) | **1–3 %** | | Titanium dioxide (pigment) | **2–4 %** | | Talc (anti-tack) | **2–5 %** | | Iron oxide (color) | **0.5–1 %** | | Purified water | **qs to 100 %** | **Key Advantages over Sugar Coating:** Lower weight gain (2–5 %), faster processing, automation-compatible, reproducible, mechanically robust. --- ### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 | 14 Marks]** #### (a) Explain the concept of enteric coating. Discuss the polymers used and the mechanism of pH-dependent release. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Enteric coating is a specialized polymer coating that **resists dissolution in the acidic environment of the stomach (pH 1–4.5) but dissolves rapidly in the higher pH of the small intestine (pH 6–7.5)**. The term "enteric" is derived from the Greek *enteron* (intestine). **Mechanism of pH-Dependent Release:** The enteric polymers are **weak acids** with carboxylic acid (–COOH) functional groups. Their solubility depends on the ionization state: - **In the stomach (low pH):** The –COOH groups remain **unionized** → polymer is **water-insoluble** → drug is **protected**. - **In the intestine (higher pH):** The –COOH groups **ionize to –COO⁻** → polymer becomes **water-soluble** → **drug is released**. Mathematically, this is governed by the **Henderson–Hasselbalch equation**: $$ pH = pK_a + \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]} \right) $$ At pH = pKₐ, 50 % of the polymer is ionized. For methacrylic acid–methyl methacrylate copolymer (Eudragit® L100, pKₐ ≈ 6.0), at intestinal pH 6.8: $$ 6.8 = 6.0 + \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]} \right) \Rightarrow \log_{10}\left( \dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]} \right) = 0.8 \Rightarrow \dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]} \approx 6.3 $$ This means ~86 % of the polymer is ionized at intestinal pH → highly soluble → rapid drug release. **Common Enteric Polymers:** | Polymer | Dissolution Threshold pH | Notes | |---|---|---| | **CAP** (Cellulose Acetate Phthalate) | **> 6.0** | First synthetic enteric polymer; moisture-sensitive | | **HPMCP** (HP-55) | **> 5.5** | Better stability than CAP | | **PVAP** (Polyvinyl Acetate Phthalate) | **> 5.0** | Lower threshold | | **Eudragit® L100** (1:1 methacrylic) | **> 6.0** | Most widely used | | **Eudragit® S100** (1:2 methacrylic) | **> 7.0** | Colon-targeted | | **Shellac** (natural resin) | **> 7.0** | Variable; historical use | **Applications:** 1. Protection of acid-labile drugs (e.g., pancreatin, erythromycin). 2. Protection of gastric mucosa from irritating drugs (e.g., aspirin, NSAIDs). 3. Targeted delivery to small intestine (e.g., proton-pump inhibitors like omeprazole). 4. Colon-targeted delivery for inflammatory bowel disease. --- #### (b) Discuss compression coating with its advantages, limitations, and equipment. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Compression coating, also called **dry coating**, is a process in which a granular coating powder is compressed around a pre-formed tablet core using a **specialized press**, without the use of any solvent or liquid. **Principle:** A bilayer press is used. The first layer (lower punch cavity) is the **coating powder**; the central cavity contains the **pre-formed tablet core**; the second layer (upper punch cavity) is the **remaining coating powder**. The whole assembly is then compressed into a final tablet. **Equipment:** | Equipment | Description | |---|---| | **Stokes Dry-Cota Press** | First commercial press for compression coating (1960s) | | **Modern Bilayer Presses** | e.g., Korsch XM, GEA Modul, Fette 2090i | | **Kikusui / Manesty Presses** | Specialized for layered tablets | **Process Flow:** ``` Step 1: Pre-formed tablet core is positioned in center of die Step 2: Lower punch moves down, coating powder fills bottom of die Step 3: Core is placed Step 4: Upper punch delivers upper coating powder layer Step 5: Both punches compress → final compression-coated tablet ``` **Advantages:** 1. **No solvent used** → ideal for water-sensitive drugs (e.g., aspirin, ranitidine HCl). 2. **High drug loading** → suitable for very high-dose actives (e.g., 1–2 g per tablet). 3. **Versatile release profiles** → can combine immediate-release core with sustained-release coat. 4. **No drying step** → shorter process time and lower energy cost. **Limitations:** 1. **Specialized equipment required** → high capital investment. 2. **Lower production speeds** compared to spray coating. 3. **Tooling wear** due to high compaction forces. 4. **Risk of layer separation** ("core popping out") if adhesion between core and coat is poor. 5. **Insufficient film continuity** if compression force is too high → core cracking. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on "Types of Coating":** > 1. **Forgetting to draw/describe the mechanism** of pH-dependent release in enteric coating — examiners award 2 marks specifically for the **ionization equilibrium** explanation. > 2. **Confusing sugar and film coating stages** — students frequently omit the **sealing** stage in sugar coating, which is **mandatory**. > 3. **Not stating numerical ranges** for weight gain, process time, etc. — KTU examiners reward **quantitative accuracy** with partial marks. > 4. **Missing plasticizers and pigments** in film-coating formulation — these are non-negotiable components. > 5. **Mis-naming polymers** — *CAP is NOT the same as HPMC*; *Eudragit® L100 ≠ Eudragit® S100*. > 6. In numerical problems, **forgetting to convert mL to kg** when calculating efficiency. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision — Re-read before every KTU exam:** - [ ] **Pharmaceutical coating** = application of a thin/layered coat on solid dosage forms for **protection, masking, modified release, or aesthetics**. - [ ] **Sugar coating:** 30–70 % weight gain; 5 stages: **Sealing → Subcoating → Grossing → Coloring → Polishing**. - [ ] **Film coating:** 2–5 % weight gain; components = **Polymer + Plasticizer + Pigment + Solvent + Additives**. - [ ] **Aqueous film coating** uses **HPMC** most commonly; **non-aqueous** is increasingly restricted due to environmental concerns. - [ ] **Enteric coating:** resists pH < 4.5, dissolves at pH > 6.0; key polymers = **CAP, HPMCP, Eudragit® L100/S100**. - [ ] **Mechanism:** Ionization of –COOH groups in alkaline pH → polymer soluble → drug released. - [ ] **Compression coating:** No solvent; uses bilayer press; ideal for water-sensitive and high-dose drugs. - [ ] **Modified-release coatings:** sustained, delayed, targeted, extended — all use **functional polymers** (Eudragit® RS, RL, NE, etc.). - [ ] **Coating efficiency formula:** (Solids deposited on tablets / Total solids sprayed) × 100. - [ ] **Weight gain formula:** $((W_f - W_i) / W_i) \times 100$. - [ ] **Inlet air** 50–80 °C; **exhaust** 38–45 °C; **pan speed** 8–20 rpm; **atomization pressure** 1.5–3.5 bar. - [ ] **Sticking & twinning** = classic faults due to over-spraying; **bridging & logo-imprint filling** = drying too fast; **cracking** = plasticizer deficit. - [ ] **KTU mnemonic — "S-S-G-C-P":** *Sugar = Sealing, Subcoating, Grossing, Coloring, Polishing.* - [ ] **KTU mnemonic — "E-PS":** *Enteric = pH-Sensitive polymer.* - [ ] Always remember **QC tests** post-coating: **weight gain, appearance, thickness, disintegration, dissolution, friability, moisture content.**

topics.title: Quality Control of Coated and Uncoated Tablets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Quality Control of Coated and Uncoated Tablets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition: Quality Control (QC) of Tablets

**Quality Control (QC) of Tablets** is the systematic, pharmacopeial, and scientifically validated set of physical, chemical, and biological tests performed on compressed tablet dosage forms (both uncoated and coated) to ensure that they meet predetermined specifications for identity, strength, purity, content uniformity, stability, and biopharmaceutical performance. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, tablet QC is positioned under the **Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP)** framework and is governed by official compendia such as the **Indian Pharmacopoeia (IP)**, the **United States Pharmacopoeia (USP)**, and the **British Pharmacopoeia (BP)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT751 – Module 2):**
> Quality control of tablets is a **CO-mapped high-weightage** topic. Questions frequently appear as 7-mark sub-parts in Part B (Module 2 carries roughly 25–30% of the ESE paper). Students must memorize the official test limits (e.g., friability ≤ 1.0%, weight variation ±5% for ≥ 250 mg, disintegration < 15 min for uncoated, etc.).

## 1.2 Why Tablets Need QC — A Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of a tablet as a small, precise, mass-produced **currency coin** issued by a country's central bank. Every coin (tablet) must look identical, weigh exactly the same, not break when handled, and deliver the same purchasing power (drug dose) to every citizen (patient). The Quality Control laboratory is the **"central bank inspector"** that randomly samples coins from every batch, weighs them, tests their hardness, and verifies their chemical composition. If even one parameter is off, the entire batch is rejected to protect the public.

A patient swallowing a tablet trusts that:
- It contains the exact **labeled dose** of the active drug (assay).
- It will **disintegrate and dissolve** in the gastrointestinal tract to release that dose (disintegration and dissolution).
- It can withstand **mechanical shocks** of packaging, transport, and handling (hardness and friability).
- It is **chemically stable** through its shelf life (stability testing).

## 1.3 Classification of Tablet QC Tests

The QC tests for tablets are broadly classified into three categories:

| Category | Purpose | Examples |
|----------|---------|----------|
| **Physical Tests** | Mechanical and dimensional integrity | Hardness, Friability, Weight Variation, Thickness, Diameter |
| **Chemical Tests** | Identity, purity, and potency | Assay, Content Uniformity, Impurity Profiling |
| **Biopharmaceutical Tests** | In vivo performance prediction | Disintegration, Dissolution |

## 1.4 Distinction Between Coated and Uncoated Tablet QC

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Uncoated tablets are tested for direct physical and chemical parameters because the **active drug is directly exposed** at the surface. Coated tablets (sugar-coated, film-coated, or enteric-coated) require **additional specialized tests** because the coating layer adds an extra variable that can alter disintegration, dissolution, stability, and visual appearance. The coating itself becomes a **critical quality attribute (CQA)** that must be tested independently.

The major categories of coated tablets, each with its own QC emphasis, are:
- **Sugar-Coated Tablets** – aesthetic, taste-masking, and protection emphasis.
- **Film-Coated Tablets** – thin polymer layer, rapid disintegration preservation.
- **Enteric-Coated Tablets** – pH-dependent dissolution, gastric resistance emphasis.
- **Modified-Release / Sustained-Release Coated Tablets** – release kinetics emphasis.

## 1.5 Pharmacopeial Standards — The Quality Benchmark

The official standards that govern tablet QC are the **Indian Pharmacopoeia (IP)**, **United States Pharmacopoeia (USP)**, **British Pharmacopoeia (BP)**, and **European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.)**. Each pharmacopoeia publishes:

- **Monographs** for individual drug substances and dosage forms.
- **General Chapters / General Methods** for universal tests.
- **Reference Standards** (e.g., USP Reference Standards, IP Reference Substances).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 examinations, when asked about pharmacopeial limits, students should always quote **IP (Indian Pharmacopoeia)** as the primary reference, followed by USP/BP equivalents. The latest edition referenced by regulatory bodies is considered the standard.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 QC of Uncoated Tablets — Detailed Test Analysis

Uncoated tablets are the simplest oral solid dosage form, produced by direct compression or wet/dry granulation followed by compression. The QC protocol covers the following parameters:

### 2.1.1 General Appearance and Organoleptic Properties
- **Visual inspection** of color, shape, odor, surface texture, and presence of defects (chipping, lamination, capping, picking, sticking).
- Performed manually under **defined lighting (≈ 300 lux)** against a white and black background.
- This is the **first QC checkpoint** before any instrumental testing.

### 2.1.2 Size and Shape
- **Diameter** and **thickness** measured using calibrated **vernier calipers** or **micrometers**.
- Diameter is fixed by the die; thickness depends on powder fill and compression force.
- Acceptance limit: **± 5% variation** of the standard value is generally acceptable.

### 2.1.3 Hardness (Crushing Strength)
- **Definition:** The diametrical crushing force (in **kg** or **kPa / N**) required to break a tablet when applied across its diameter.
- **Instrument:** Monsanto hardness tester, Strong-Cobb tester, Pfizer tester, Schleuniger tester, or the modern **Dr. Schleuniger Tablet Hardness Tester**.
- **Standard Range:** **4–10 kg** for most uncoated tablets (varies by formulation).
- **Why it matters:** Insufficient hardness → chipping, capping, breakage, friability failure. Excessive hardness → prolonged disintegration, poor dissolution, and reduced bioavailability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Link:** Hardness is inversely proportional to porosity. As compression force increases, tablet hardness increases, but porosity decreases, lengthening the disintegration time. This is the **Hardness–Disintegration Trade-off**, a classic viva question.

### 2.1.4 Friability
- **Definition:** The percentage weight loss of tablets subjected to a combined effect of abrasion and shock in a **Roche Friabilator**.
- **Procedure:** Take a sample of tablets (whole ≈ 6.5 g or 20 tablets, whichever is less), dedust, weigh accurately (**W₁**), place in the friabilator drum, rotate at **25 rpm for 4 minutes (100 revolutions)**, dedust, and reweigh (**W₂**).
- **Acceptance Limit:** **Friability ≤ 1.0%** (per IP/USP). Tablets losing > 1% fail the test.

### 2.1.5 Weight Variation Test
- **Purpose:** Ensures **dose uniformity** through uniform die filling during compression.
- **Procedure:** Weigh **20 individual tablets** individually (W₁, W₂, …, W₂₀), calculate the average weight, and compare each individual weight to the average.
- **USP/IP Limits:**

| Average Weight of Tablet | Maximum % Deviation Allowed |
|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| **80 mg or less** | **± 10%** |
| **More than 80 mg but less than 250 mg** | **± 7.5%** |
| **250 mg or more** | **± 5%** |

- **Important Note:** If the tablet contains **≥ 50 mg** of the active drug and the active constitutes **≥ 50%** of the tablet weight, the **Content Uniformity Test** is performed INSTEAD of weight variation.

### 2.1.6 Content Uniformity Test
- **Definition:** Quantitative assay of the active drug in **each of 10 individual tablets** to ensure uniform dose distribution.
- **Procedure:** Assay 10 tablets individually; calculate mean and Relative Standard Deviation (RSD).
- **USP/IP Acceptance:** The content of each tablet must lie between **85% and 115%** of the label claim, with **RSD ≤ 6%** (in most cases).

### 2.1.7 Disintegration Test
- **Definition:** The time required for a tablet to **break up into smaller particles** and pass through the **10-mesh screen** of the disintegration apparatus under standard conditions.
- **Apparatus:** IP/USP disintegration apparatus (basket-rack assembly).
- **Conditions:** **37 ± 2 °C** in the specified medium (water or 0.1 N HCl for uncoated tablets).
- **Acceptance Limits:**

| Tablet Type | Maximum Disintegration Time |
|-------------|------------------------------|
| Uncoated tablets | **≤ 15 minutes** |
| Film-coated tablets | **≤ 30 minutes** |
| Sugar-coated tablets | **≤ 60 minutes** |
| Enteric-coated tablets | **No disintegration in 0.1 N HCl for 2 hours**; must disintegrate in phosphate buffer pH 6.8 within **60 minutes** |
| Dispersible / Effervescent tablets | **≤ 3 minutes** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Disintegration is a **necessary but not sufficient** test for bioavailability. A tablet may disintegrate into fine particles, but if the drug is poorly soluble, the API may not dissolve. Hence, the **Dissolution Test** is performed next.

### 2.1.8 Dissolution Test
- **Definition:** The rate and extent of drug release from the solid dosage form into the dissolution medium under controlled conditions.
- **Apparatus:** USP Apparatus 1 (Basket), Apparatus 2 (Paddle), Apparatus 3 (Reciprocating Cylinder), Apparatus 4 (Flow-Through Cell).
- **Medium:** 0.1 N HCl, phosphate buffer pH 6.8, or pH 7.4, typically **900 mL** at **37 ± 0.5 °C**.
- **Sampling:** Aliquots withdrawn at specified time points (e.g., 10, 20, 30, 45 min) and analyzed by UV-Vis spectrophotometry or HPLC.
- **Acceptance (USP General Chapter <711>):** **Q + 5%** dissolved at 30 minutes, where **Q** is the amount specified in the monograph (typically **+ 5% / 75% / 80%** depending on drug).

### 2.1.9 Assay (Content of Active Ingredient)
- **Purpose:** Confirms the **chemical potency** of the batch.
- **Limit:** **90% – 110%** of the label claim (most pharmacopeias) for tablets, but may vary (e.g., 95–105%) for narrow therapeutic index drugs.

### 2.1.10 Moisture Content
- **Determined by:** Loss on Drying (LOD), Karl Fischer Titration, or IR moisture balance.
- **Limit:** Usually **≤ 3% w/w** for most compressed tablets.

### 2.1.11 Stability Testing
- **Accelerated Stability:** **40 °C ± 2 °C / 75% RH ± 5%** for 6 months (per ICH Q1A).
- **Long-Term Stability:** **25 °C ± 2 °C / 60% RH ± 5%** for the proposed shelf life.
- Parameters monitored: Assay, dissolution, hardness, friability, appearance, related substances.

---

## 2.2 QC of Coated Tablets — Additional Specialized Tests

Coated tablets must pass **all uncoated-tablet QC tests** PLUS the following coating-specific evaluations.

### 2.2.1 Visual Appearance of the Coating
- **Parameters evaluated:** Color uniformity, gloss, smoothness, absence of **picking**, **mottling**, **bridging** (over intagliation), **twinning** (sticking of two tablets), **blistering**, **pitting**, **orange peel (roughness)**, and **edge chipping**.
- Evaluated under **standardized lighting** against white and black backgrounds.

### 2.2.2 Coating Uniformity
- **For sugar-coated tablets:** Visual inspection + weight gain (typically **30–50%** weight gain for sugar coating).
- **For film-coated tablets:** Weight gain typically **2–5%**, evaluated gravimetrically or by HPLC assay of coating polymer markers.

### 2.2.3 Disintegration of Coated Tablets
- **Film-coated:** ≤ 30 min (USP/IP).
- **Sugar-coated:** ≤ 60 min (USP/IP).
- **Enteric-coated:** Must withstand **0.1 N HCl for 2 hours** with no disintegration or cracking; then must disintegrate in **phosphate buffer pH 6.8 within 60 minutes**.

### 2.2.4 Acid Resistance Test (Enteric-Coated Tablets)
- **Specific to enteric-coated dosage forms.**
- **Procedure:** Tablets are placed in **0.1 N HCl** at 37 °C for **2 hours** in the disintegration apparatus.
- **Acceptance:** **No tablet shows signs of disintegration, cracking, or softening** that would allow the drug to escape into gastric fluid.

### 2.2.5 Dissolution of Coated Tablets
- Coated tablets must meet the **same dissolution Q-value** as uncoated tablets, often using a **two-stage pH-change dissolution** for enteric products:
  1. **2 hours in 0.1 N HCl** (acid stage — drug release < 10%).
  2. **Then in phosphate buffer pH 6.8** (buffer stage — drug release ≥ 80% by 45–60 min).

### 2.2.6 Moisture Permeability of the Coating
- For **moisture-sensitive drugs** (e.g., aspirin, ranitidine), the coating acts as a **moisture barrier**.
- **Test:** Tablets stored at **40 °C / 75% RH** for 3–6 months; moisture uptake, assay loss, and physical integrity are monitored.

### 2.2.7 Film Thickness and Coating Smoothness (Modern Tests)
- For **film-coated tablets**, the **film thickness** is sometimes measured by **scanning electron microscopy (SEM)** or **optical profilometry** as a research/development tool.
- **Tackiness** and **gloss** are measured by **gloss meters** and **texture analyzers**.

### 2.2.8 Adhesion Strength of the Film
- A **peel test** or **pull-off test** using a tensile tester is occasionally used in development to measure the **adhesion of the film coating** to the tablet core.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Test | Key Formula | Units | Standard Limit (Uncoated) | Standard Limit (Coated) |
|------|------------|-------|---------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Friability** | $F = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ | % | ≤ 1.0% | ≤ 1.0% |
| **Weight Variation** | $\% \text{ Deviation} = \dfrac{W_i - \bar{W}}{\bar{W}} \times 100$ | % | ± 5% (≥ 250 mg), ± 7.5% (80–250 mg), ± 10% (≤ 80 mg) | Same as uncoated |
| **Average Weight** | $\bar{W} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i}{n}$ | mg or g | — | — |
| **Relative Standard Deviation (RSD)** | $\text{RSD} = \dfrac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100$, where $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | % | ≤ 6% (CU) | ≤ 6% (CU) |
| **Disintegration Time Limit** | — | min | ≤ 15 min | Film ≤ 30, Sugar ≤ 60, Enteric = No disintegration in 0.1 N HCl (2 h) |
| **Dissolution Q-value** | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Amount dissolved}}{\text{Label claim}} \times 100$ | % | As per monograph (often + 5% in 30 min) | As per monograph |
| **Tensile Strength (Tablet)** | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{2F}{\pi \cdot D \cdot h}$ | kPa | Informational | Informational |
| **Hardness–Friability Correlation** | Empirical: Friability $\propto \dfrac{1}{\text{Hardness}}$ | — | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility in Pharma Industry:** QC is the **final gate** before a batch is released into the market. Every batch must be accompanied by a **Certificate of Analysis (CoA)** signed by the **Qualified Person (QP)**, listing every QC test result against its limit. A single failed parameter triggers a **batch rejection** or **investigation** under GMP. In the KTU exam context, students are expected to write both the **procedure** and the **acceptance limit** to score full marks.

---

## 2.4 Why the Hardness–Disintegration–Dissolution Triangle is Critical

This is the **core conceptual triangle** of tablet QC:

1. **High Compression Force** → High Hardness → Low Porosity → Slow Disintegration → Slow Dissolution → Possible **Bioavailability Failure**.
2. **Low Compression Force** → Low Hardness → High Porosity → Fast Disintegration → Fast Dissolution → Possible **Friability Failure** (tablet breaks in handling).

> The **Optimum Compression Force** is the value that simultaneously satisfies hardness, friability, disintegration, and dissolution. This is determined during **formulation development** and locked in as part of the **Design Space** in **Quality by Design (QbD)** framework.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Friability Calculation

**Problem:** A batch of 20 tablets (uncoated paracetamol 500 mg) was subjected to the friability test. The initial weight of the tablets was **6.532 g**. After 100 revolutions in the Roche friabilator, the final weight was **6.471 g**. Determine whether the batch passes or fails the friability test as per IP/USP.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the given data.**

$$
W_1 = 6.532 \text{ g}, \quad W_2 = 6.471 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the weight loss (numerator).**

$$
\Delta W = W_1 - W_2 = 6.532 - 6.471 = 0.061 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the friability formula.**

$$
F = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
F = \dfrac{0.061}{6.532} \times 100
$$

**Step 4: Numerical evaluation.**

$$
F = 0.009338 \times 100 = 0.9338\%
$$

**Step 5: Compare with pharmacopeial limit.**

$$
F = 0.934\% \leq 1.0\% \quad \text{(Acceptance Limit)}
$$

> **Result:** The batch **passes** the friability test (since 0.934% < 1.0%).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Weight Variation Test Evaluation

**Problem:** The individual weights (in mg) of **20 tablets** from a batch of metformin 500 mg tablets are:

`498, 502, 499, 503, 501, 500, 497, 504, 499, 502, 500, 501, 498, 503, 499, 502, 500, 501, 499, 502`

Determine if the batch passes the IP/USP weight variation test.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate the average weight.**

$$
\bar{W} = \dfrac{\sum W_i}{n} = \dfrac{498 + 502 + 499 + 503 + 501 + 500 + 497 + 504 + 499 + 502 + 500 + 501 + 498 + 503 + 499 + 502 + 500 + 501 + 499 + 502}{20}
$$

Summing the values:

$$
\sum W_i = 10009 \text{ mg}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\bar{W} = \dfrac{10009}{20} = 500.45 \text{ mg}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the weight variation limit.**

Since the average weight is **> 250 mg**, the limit is **± 5%** of the average.

$$
\text{Upper Limit} = \bar{W} + 5\% = 500.45 \times 1.05 = 525.47 \text{ mg}
$$

$$
\text{Lower Limit} = \bar{W} - 5\% = 500.45 \times 0.95 = 475.43 \text{ mg}
$$

**Step 3: Check each tablet weight against the limits.**

- Minimum weight: **497 mg** (above 475.43 mg) ✓
- Maximum weight: **504 mg** (below 525.47 mg) ✓

**Step 4: Conclusion.**

> All 20 tablets fall within the ± 5% range. The batch **passes** the weight variation test.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Tensile Strength of a Tablet (Mechanical QC)

**Problem:** A tablet with diameter **D = 10 mm** and thickness **h = 4 mm** fractures under a diametrical load of **F = 80 N**. Calculate the tensile strength and interpret.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply the diametrical compression formula (Fell and Newton equation).**

$$
\sigma_t = \dfrac{2F}{\pi \cdot D \cdot h}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$
\sigma_t = \dfrac{2 \times 80}{\pi \times 10 \times 4} = \dfrac{160}{125.66}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate.**

$$
\sigma_t = 1.273 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 1.273 \text{ MPa}
$$

> **Interpretation:** For most direct-compression tablets, tensile strength in the range **1.0–2.0 MPa** is considered acceptable. A value of **1.273 MPa** indicates a **well-compacted** tablet with balanced hardness and porosity.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: RSD for Content Uniformity

**Problem:** The assay values (%) for **10 tablets** are: `98, 102, 99, 101, 100, 97, 103, 100, 99, 101`. Determine if the batch passes the USP Content Uniformity test.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate the mean.**

$$
\bar{x} = \dfrac{98 + 102 + 99 + 101 + 100 + 97 + 103 + 100 + 99 + 101}{10} = \dfrac{1000}{10} = 100\%
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the standard deviation (s).**

$$
s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{10}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}
$$

Compute squared deviations:

| $x_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
|-------|------------------|---------------------|
| 98    | −2               | 4                   |
| 102   | +2               | 4                   |
| 99    | −1               | 1                   |
| 101   | +1               | 1                   |
| 100   | 0                | 0                   |
| 97    | −3               | 9                   |
| 103   | +3               | 9                   |
| 100   | 0                | 0                   |
| 99    | −1               | 1                   |
| 101   | +1               | 1                   |
| **Sum** | —              | **30**              |

$$
s = \sqrt{\dfrac{30}{9}} = \sqrt{3.333} = 1.826
$$

**Step 3: Calculate RSD.**

$$
\text{RSD} = \dfrac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.826}{100} \times 100 = 1.826\%
$$

**Step 4: Check limits.**

- Each tablet is within **85%–115%** ✓
- RSD = **1.826% ≤ 6%** ✓

> **Result:** The batch **passes** the content uniformity test.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: How Each Test Detects Different Failure Modes

| Failure Mode | Test That Detects It | Test Type |
|--------------|----------------------|-----------|
| Tablet breaks during packaging | **Friability, Hardness** | Physical |
| Non-uniform dose in patients | **Content Uniformity, Weight Variation** | Physical / Chemical |
| Drug not absorbed (Bioavailability failure) | **Disintegration, Dissolution** | Biopharmaceutical |
| Wrong chemical strength | **Assay** | Chemical |
| Stale / degraded batch | **Stability, LOD** | Chemical / Physical |
| Patient rejects (bad taste, smell, color) | **Organoleptic, Appearance** | Physical |
| Coating cracks in transit | **Coating Uniformity, Visual, Disintegration** | Physical |
| Enteric tablet releases drug in stomach | **Acid Resistance, Dissolution (acid stage)** | Biopharmaceutical |

---

## 3.6 Conceptual Difference: Weight Variation vs. Content Uniformity

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Confusing Point (asked in viva):**
> - **Weight Variation** is a **physical** test that assumes the **drug is uniformly distributed** in the granulation. It is allowed only when the drug is **< 50 mg per unit** or when the drug constitutes **< 50% of the tablet weight**.
> - **Content Uniformity (CU)** is a **chemical** test (HPLC/UV assay) and is the **gold standard** for dose uniformity. It must always be performed for **low-dose drugs (≤ 50 mg)** or when the drug is **≥ 50%** of the tablet weight.
> - In modern pharmacopeias, **CU is generally preferred** and may be performed on all batches regardless of drug percentage.
