# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define friability. Write the pharmacopeial limit and the procedure briefly.**

> **Model Answer:**
> **Friability** is defined as the percentage weight loss of tablets due to combined abrasion and shock when rotated in a **Roche Friabilator** at **25 rpm for 4 minutes (100 revolutions)**. The formula is:
> $$F = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100$$
> where **W₁** is the initial weight and **W₂** is the final weight. The **pharmacopeial limit is ≤ 1.0%** (IP/USP). Tablets with friability > 1.0% fail the test and require reformulation. Friability is critical because tablets must withstand mechanical shocks during handling, packaging, and transport.

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**List any six quality control tests performed on uncoated tablets.**

> **Model Answer:**
> The six QC tests for uncoated tablets are:
> 1. **General Appearance** – color, shape, odor, surface defects.
> 2. **Hardness (Crushing Strength)** – measured using Monsanto or Pfizer tester; standard range 4–10 kg.
> 3. **Friability** – Roche friabilator, limit ≤ 1.0%.
> 4. **Weight Variation** – 20 tablets, ± 5% (≥ 250 mg), ± 7.5% (80–250 mg), ± 10% (≤ 80 mg).
> 5. **Disintegration** – IP/USP apparatus, ≤ 15 minutes in water at 37 °C.
> 6. **Dissolution** – USP Apparatus 1 or 2, Q + 5% dissolved at 30 min (per monograph).
> *(Other acceptable answers: Assay, Content Uniformity, Moisture Content, Thickness/Diameter.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam – Module 2 ESE 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **weight variation test** in detail. What are the IP/USP acceptance limits? Under what conditions is the **content uniformity test** performed instead of weight variation?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **quality control tests that are specifically required for coated tablets**, with emphasis on enteric-coated tablets. Mention the acid resistance test and its pharmacopeial requirement.

---

#### (a) Model Solution — Weight Variation & Content Uniformity

**Step 1: Definition.**
The **weight variation test** is a physical QC test designed to verify the **uniformity of tablet weight**, which indirectly confirms the **uniformity of the active drug dose**, assuming the drug is homogeneously distributed in the granulation or blend.

**Step 2: Procedure.**
- Take **20 tablets** randomly from the batch.
- Weigh each tablet **individually** on an **analytical balance** (sensitivity 0.1 mg).
- Calculate the **average weight** $\bar{W}$ using the formula:
$$\bar{W} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{20} W_i}{20}$$
- Calculate the **% deviation** of each tablet from the average:
$$\%\text{Deviation}_i = \dfrac{W_i - \bar{W}}{\bar{W}} \times 100$$
- Compare each deviation with the pharmacopeial limit.

**Step 3: IP/USP Acceptance Limits.**

| Average Tablet Weight | Maximum Permitted % Deviation |
|------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **≤ 80 mg**            | **± 10%**                     |
| **> 80 mg and < 250 mg** | **± 7.5%**                  |
| **≥ 250 mg**           | **± 5%**                      |

- Not more than **2 tablets** may deviate beyond the limit, and **no tablet** may deviate by more than **twice the limit**.

**Step 4: When is Content Uniformity Performed Instead?**
The **Content Uniformity (CU) test** is performed in the following cases:
1. When the **active drug per tablet is ≤ 50 mg**, regardless of tablet weight.
2. When the active drug constitutes **≥ 50%** of the tablet weight (e.g., in tablets of metformin 500 mg or paracetamol 500 mg).
3. When mandated by a **specific pharmacopeial monograph**.

**Step 5: Brief on Content Uniformity Procedure.**
- **10 tablets** are assayed individually by HPLC or UV-Vis spectroscopy.
- **Mean content** and **RSD** are calculated.
- Each tablet must be between **85% and 115%** of the label claim.
- **RSD** must be **≤ 6%**.

> **Conclusion:** Weight variation is a rapid physical screen for dose uniformity, but for **low-dose, potent, or high-drug-load tablets**, content uniformity is the **gold-standard chemical test**.

---

#### (b) Model Solution — QC Tests for Coated Tablets

**Step 1: Why Coated Tablets Need Special QC.**
A coating (sugar, film, or enteric polymer) adds an extra **manufacturing variable** that affects **drug release, stability, appearance, and patient compliance**. Therefore, coated tablets must pass all uncoated-tablet QC tests **PLUS** coating-specific tests.

**Step 2: Coating-Specific Tests.**

1. **Visual Appearance of the Coating**
   - Inspected for **color uniformity, gloss, smoothness**.
   - Defects like **picking, mottling, bridging, twinning, blistering, and orange-peel** roughness are checked.
   - Performed under **standardized light** (~300 lux) against white and black backgrounds.

2. **Coating Uniformity**
   - Measured as **weight gain** (typically 30–50% for sugar coating, 2–5% for film coating).
   - For polymeric films, assessed by **HPLC quantification of coating polymer markers**.

3. **Disintegration of Coated Tablets**

| Coating Type | Disintegration Time Limit |
|--------------|----------------------------|
| **Film-coated** | ≤ **30 minutes** |
| **Sugar-coated** | ≤ **60 minutes** |
| **Enteric-coated** | **No disintegration in 0.1 N HCl for 2 hours**; **≤ 60 minutes in pH 6.8 buffer** |

4. **Acid Resistance Test (Enteric-Coated Tablets)**
   - **Definition:** A specialized test confirming that the enteric coating resists dissolution in **gastric acid (pH ~1.2)**.
   - **Procedure:**
     - Place **6 tablets** in the basket-rack assembly.
     - Immerse in **0.1 N HCl at 37 ± 2 °C** for **2 hours**.
     - Observe for any signs of **disintegration, cracking, softening, or drug leakage**.
   - **Acceptance:** **No tablet** should show disintegration or drug release in the acid phase. The tablets are then transferred to **phosphate buffer pH 6.8**, where they must disintegrate within **60 minutes**.

5. **Dissolution of Coated Tablets (Two-Stage Test)**
   - **Stage 1 (Acid):** 0.1 N HCl for 2 hours — **drug release < 10%** (for enteric).
   - **Stage 2 (Buffer):** Phosphate buffer pH 6.8 — **≥ 75% (Q + 5%)** of drug must be released in 45 minutes.

6. **Moisture Permeability / Moisture Barrier Test**
   - For **moisture-sensitive drugs** (e.g., aspirin, ranitidine HCl).
   - Tablets stored at **40 °C / 75% RH** for 3 months; monitored for moisture uptake, assay loss, and physical defects.

7. **Adhesion Strength of Film (Development Test)**
   - Measured by **peel test** or **tensile pull-off** using a universal testing machine.

8. **Stability Under Coating-Specific Conditions**
   - Coated tablets are also subjected to **accelerated stability** (40 °C / 75% RH) and **long-term stability** (25 °C / 60% RH) per ICH Q1A.

> **Conclusion:** Coated tablets require an **expanded QC protocol** that evaluates not only the drug but also the **integrity, uniformity, and performance of the coating layer**, particularly for **enteric-coated** products where gastric resistance is critical for therapeutic efficacy.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam – Module 2 ESE 2023 – Alternative Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **disintegration and dissolution tests** for tablets. Discuss the apparatus, procedure, and pharmacopeial limits for both uncoated and coated (including enteric) tablets.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define **hardness and friability**. Describe the **instruments** used and the **pharmacopeial limits**. Explain the **hardness–disintegration trade-off** in tablet formulation.

---

#### (a) Model Solution — Disintegration and Dissolution Tests

**Step 1: Disintegration Test — Definition.**
Disintegration is the **breakdown of a tablet into smaller particles** and its passage through a **10-mesh screen** of the disintegration apparatus under prescribed conditions of temperature and immersion fluid.

**Step 2: Apparatus (USP/IP).**
- **Basket-rack assembly** with **6 glass tubes**, each with a **10-mesh screen at the bottom**.
- A **cylindrical disc** (plastic) may be placed on top of each tablet to prevent floating.
- Immersion fluid: **0.1 N HCl, simulated gastric fluid, simulated intestinal fluid, phosphate buffer, or water** (depending on the tablet).
- Temperature: **37 ± 2 °C**.
- Motor-driven vertical movement: **28–32 cycles per minute**, amplitude **50–60 mm**.

**Step 3: Procedure and Limits.**

| Tablet Type | Medium | Time Limit |
|-------------|--------|------------|
| Uncoated | Water / 0.1 N HCl | **≤ 15 min** |
| Film-coated | Water | **≤ 30 min** |
| Sugar-coated | Water | **≤ 60 min** |
| Enteric-coated | 0.1 N HCl (2 h) → Buffer pH 6.8 | **No disintegration in acid (2 h)**; **≤ 60 min in buffer** |
| Dispersible / Effervescent | Water | **≤ 3 min** |

**Step 4: Dissolution Test — Definition.**
Dissolution is the **rate and extent of drug release from the solid dosage form into the dissolution medium** under controlled temperature, agitation, and medium pH.

**Step 5: Apparatus (USP <711>/ <712>).**
- **Apparatus 1 (Basket):** Tablet in a rotating basket, 50–100 rpm.
- **Apparatus 2 (Paddle):** Tablet at the bottom of a vessel, paddle rotates at 50–75 rpm. **Most commonly used.**
- **Apparatus 3 (Reciprocating Cylinder):** For beaded and modified-release products.
- **Apparatus 4 (Flow-Through Cell):** For poorly soluble drugs.

**Step 6: Standard Conditions.**
- **Medium volume:** 900 mL (or 1000 mL for some drugs).
- **Temperature:** **37 ± 0.5 °C**.
- **Sampling times:** Typically 10, 20, 30, and 45 minutes.
- **Analysis:** UV-Vis spectrophotometry or HPLC.

**Step 7: Acceptance (USP).**
At the specified sampling time (commonly 30 min), the **amount dissolved** must be at least **Q + 5%**, where **Q** is specified in the individual monograph (often 75% or 80%).

**Step 8: Significance.**
Disintegration is a **necessary** but **not sufficient** test for bioavailability. Dissolution is the **more direct predictor** of in vivo drug release and is mandatory for **BCS Class II and IV drugs**.

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Hardness, Friability, and the Trade-off

**Step 1: Definition of Hardness.**
**Hardness (Crushing Strength)** is the **diametrical compressive force** (in kg, kPa, or N) required to **break a tablet** when applied across its diameter.

**Step 2: Instruments.**
- **Monsanto Hardness Tester** – manual, spring-loaded, units in kg/cm².
- **Strong-Cobb Tester** – measures in arbitrary Strong-Cobb units.
- **Pfizer Tester** – manual, measures in kg.
- **Dr. Schleuniger Tester** – modern, electronic, measures in N or kPa.
- **Key Test:** The **tensile strength** can be calculated from hardness using the **Fell-Newton equation**:
$$\sigma_t = \dfrac{2F}{\pi \cdot D \cdot h}$$

**Step 3: Standard Limits for Hardness.**
- **General range:** **4–10 kg** (≈ 40–100 N) for most uncoated compressed tablets.
- **Soft tablets** (e.g., chewable): 2–4 kg.
- **Hard tablets** (e.g., sustained-release): 10–20 kg.

**Step 4: Definition of Friability.**
**Friability** is the **percentage weight loss** of tablets due to the combined effects of **abrasion and shock** when rotated in a Roche Friabilator.

**Step 5: Instrument and Procedure.**
- **Roche Friabilator**: A plastic drum that rotates at **25 rpm for 4 minutes (100 revolutions)**.
- **Sample:** ≈ **6.5 g** of tablets (or 20 tablets).
- **Procedure:** Weigh → Rotate → Dedust → Reweigh.
- **Formula:**
$$F = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100$$
- **Limit:** **F ≤ 1.0%** (IP/USP).

**Step 6: The Hardness–Disintegration Trade-off (Conceptual Core).**
- **High Compression Force** → **High Hardness** → **Low Porosity** → **Slow Penetration of Fluid** → **Slow Disintegration** → **Slow Dissolution** → **Possible Bioavailability Failure**.
- **Low Compression Force** → **Low Hardness** → **High Porosity** → **Fast Disintegration** → **Fast Dissolution** → **But Tablet Chips or Breaks During Handling (Friability Failure)**.

> **Optimum Compression Force:** The value where hardness, friability, disintegration, and dissolution **all pass** simultaneously. This is the **central challenge** in tablet formulation.

**Step 7: Graphical Interpretation.**

```
Hardness   ↑
  |        ●●●
  |      ●
  |    ●
  |   ●
  |  ●
  | ●
  |●
  +————————————————→ Compression Force
    Low          High

Disintegration ↑
  |●
  | ●
  |  ●
  |    ●
  |      ●●●
  |         (Disintegration time DROPS
  |          as compression rises)
  +————————————————→ Compression Force
```

> **Conclusion:** The formulator must balance hardness (mechanical strength) with disintegration/disintegration (biopharmaceutical performance). This is achieved through systematic **pre-compression and main-compression force optimization** during formulation development.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Writing the formula but forgetting the LIMIT.** Marks are awarded separately for the formula AND the pharmacopeial limit. Example: Friability formula gives 1 mark; stating "limit ≤ 1.0%" gives another 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing the weight variation limits.** Students often write ± 5% for all tablets, but the limit is **± 10% for ≤ 80 mg, ± 7.5% for 80–250 mg, and ± 5% for ≥ 250 mg**. This is a frequent **2-mark loss**.
> 3. **Writing disintegration time as 15 min for coated tablets.** Film-coated = **30 min**, Sugar-coated = **60 min**, Enteric = **no disintegration in acid for 2 h**.
> 4. **Confusing Content Uniformity with Weight Variation.** CU is a **chemical assay of 10 individual tablets**; Weight Variation is a **physical weighing of 20 tablets**.
> 5. **Omitting apparatus details in dissolution/disintegration answers.** Always mention the **USP Apparatus 1 (Basket)** or **Apparatus 2 (Paddle)**, **900 mL medium**, **37 ± 0.5 °C**, and **rpm**.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention friability apparatus conditions:** 25 rpm × 4 min = 100 revolutions.
> 7. **Not discussing the Hardness–Disintegration trade-off** in tablet QC questions — this is a **favourite 2-3 mark sub-question** across ESE papers.
> 8. **Skipping the acid resistance test** when the question is on enteric-coated tablets — this is a **guaranteed 3-mark sub-question**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Topper's Quick-Recall Box for Last-Minute Revision:**

- [x] **Friability Formula:** $F = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100$, **Limit ≤ 1.0%**, Roche Friabilator, **25 rpm × 4 min = 100 revolutions**.
- [x] **Weight Variation Limits:** **± 10%** (≤ 80 mg), **± 7.5%** (80–250 mg), **± 5%** (≥ 250 mg).
- [x] **Hardness Range:** **4–10 kg** (uncoated tablets); instrument examples: Monsanto, Pfizer, Strong-Cobb, Schleuniger.
- [x] **Disintegration Time Limits:** Uncoated **≤ 15 min**; Film-coated **≤ 30 min**; Sugar-coated **≤ 60 min**; Enteric **No disintegration in 0.1 N HCl for 2 h, then ≤ 60 min in pH 6.8 buffer**.
- [x] **Dissolution:** USP Apparatus 1 (Basket) or 2 (Paddle), **900 mL**, **37 ± 0.5 °C**, **Q + 5%** in 30 min.
- [x] **Content Uniformity:** 10 tablets assayed; **85–115%** of label claim; **RSD ≤ 6%**; mandatory for ≤ 50 mg dose or ≥ 50% drug load.
- [x] **Assay Limit:** **90–110%** of label claim (general); **95–105%** for narrow therapeutic index drugs.
- [x] **Acid Resistance Test:** Enteric-coated tablets in **0.1 N HCl at 37 °C for 2 h**, no disintegration; then in **pH 6.8 buffer, ≤ 60 min**.
- [x] **Coating Weight Gain:** Sugar = **30–50%**; Film = **2–5%**.
- [x] **Pharmacopeias to Remember:** **IP, USP, BP, Ph. Eur.** (primary = IP for Indian context).
- [x] **Stability Conditions:** Accelerated **40 °C / 75% RH, 6 months**; Long-term **25 °C / 60% RH** per ICH Q1A.
- [x] **Hardness–Disintegration Trade-off:** **Inverse relationship** — high hardness slows disintegration; optimal balance is the formulator's goal.
- [x] **Key Coated Tablet Defects:** Picking, Mottling, Bridging, Twinning, Blistering, Orange-Peel, Chipping, Capping.
- [x] **Tensile Strength Formula (Fell-Newton):** $\sigma_t = \dfrac{2F}{\pi \cdot D \cdot h}$.
- [x] **Roche Friabilator** is the standard friability instrument; **USP disintegration apparatus** is the standard disintegration instrument.

> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Students:** In Part B, always structure your answer as **(i) Definition → (ii) Procedure → (iii) Formula (if any) → (iv) Acceptance Limit → (v) Pharmaceutical Significance**. This **5-point structure** is the same pattern KTU examiners use in their **valuation key** and guarantees full marks when each section is addressed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Capsule Dosage Form: Rationale, Advantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Capsule Dosage Form: Rationale, Advantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Capsule Dosage Form (KTU 2024 Official Definition):**
> A capsule is a solid dosage form in which **one or more medicinal agents** and/or inert excipients are enclosed within a small, sealed, water-soluble shell (typically made of **gelatin**). The shell can be of two principal types: **hard gelatin capsules (two-piece)** and **soft gelatin capsules (one-piece)**, with the design governing the method of manufacture, filling, and the nature of the fill formulation (powder, granule, pellet, semi-solid, or liquid).

In simpler KTU Board Examination language: *A capsule is a tasteless, odourless, and elegant unit dosage container made from gelatin, used to deliver an accurate dose of active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) to the patient in a single, easy-to-swallow form.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a capsule as a "tiny, edible medicine cup."**
> Imagine you need to drink a very bitter Ayurvedic tonic. Instead of sipping it directly (terrible taste), you pour it into a clean paper cup, seal the top, and drink it whole. The paper protects your tongue from the bitterness, but your stomach still receives the full dose. A capsule does exactly this — the **gelatin shell** acts like that cup, masking the unpleasant taste, odour, and sight of the drug, while dissolving quickly in the stomach to release the active ingredient at the right place and time.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terminology

- **Standard capsule sizes (for hard gelatin):** Range from size **5 (smallest, ~0.13 mL)** → size **000 (largest, ~1.37 mL)**.
- **Typical fill weight range:** **25 mg to 1.5 g** depending on powder bulk density and capsule size.
- **Industry standard machine speed:** Modern automatic capsule-filling machines operate at **30,000 to 150,000 capsules/hour**.
- **Shell composition benchmark:** Hard gelatin shell typically contains **~70% water (during manufacture), 30% gelatin, plus colourants/opacifiers**.
- **Disintegration time (USP standard):** Capsules must disintegrate within **≤ 15 minutes** in 0.1 N HCl at 37 °C.

### 1.4 Classification Snapshot (for Module 2 context)

> [!NOTE]
> According to KTU 2024 syllabus, the two principal categories are:
> 1. **Hard Gelatin Capsules (HGC)** — two-piece (body + cap), used for **dry solids** (powders, granules, pellets, tablets).
> 2. **Soft Gelatin Capsules (SGC)** — one-piece hermetically sealed, used for **liquids, suspensions, pastes, oils**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Capsules Exist? — The Pharmaceutical Rationale

The development of capsules was driven by a specific set of **pharmaceutical, pharmacological, and patient-compliance needs** that tablets and powders could not adequately meet. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly asks for the *rationale* behind capsule formulation:

**a. Masking of Unpleasant Organoleptic Properties**
- Many APIs (e.g., **paracetamol, chloramphenicol, NSAIDs, vitamins of B-complex**) possess intensely **bitter, pungent, or fishy taste** when exposed to the oral cavity.
- Powders and uncoated tablets release the drug immediately upon contact with saliva → poor patient compliance, especially in **pediatric and geriatric populations**.
- The sealed gelatin shell physically separates the drug from taste receptors until the capsule reaches the stomach.

**b. Accurate Unit Dose Delivery**
- Each capsule delivers a **pre-measured, uniform dose** (unit-dose concept).
- Eliminates the patient-error of measuring powders or liquid spoonfuls.

**c. Protection of Light- and Oxygen-Sensitive Drugs**
- The opaque, hermetically sealed gelatin shell shields the drug from **photolytic degradation** and **oxidative deterioration**.
- Important for drugs like **vitamin A, fish oils, and certain antibiotics**.

**d. Delivery of Liquid and Semi-Solid Formulations**
- Tablets cannot hold liquids — but **soft gelatin capsules** are ideal for **oils (e.g., evening primrose oil, omega-3), lipophilic vitamins, and suspensions**.
- This is one of the **major rationales** highlighted in the KTU module.

**e. Improved Bioavailability and Rapid Onset**
- Hard gelatin shells rupture quickly in gastric fluid (≤ 5 minutes), dispersing the powder into fine particles → **faster dissolution** than compressed tablets in many cases.
- Useful for **analgesics, antipyretics, and antihistamines** where fast onset is clinically desired.

**f. Elegance and Patient Acceptance**
- Capsules are **smooth, glossy, easy to swallow, and aesthetically pleasing** → high patient compliance.
- Available in multiple colours and printed identification marks → **brand recognition and dose-identification safety**.

### 2.2 Advantages of Capsules Over Other Solid Dosage Forms

The following matrix is **KTU high-yield** — direct questions appear in 2-mark and 14-mark sections:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Advantages of Capsules (Board Favourite):**

| # | Advantage | Engineering / Pharmaceutical Reason |
|---|-----------|--------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Taste and odour masking** | Hermetically sealed shell isolates drug from oral sensory receptors. |
| 2 | **Higher bioavailability (in many cases)** | Rapid shell rupture + fine drug dispersion → faster dissolution than tablets. |
| 3 | **Versatile fill formulation** | Accepts powders, granules, pellets, tablets, oils, suspensions, pastes. |
| 4 | **Protection of sensitive APIs** | Shell acts as barrier to O₂, moisture (in some cases), and UV light. |
| 5 | **Elegant, attractive appearance** | Smooth, glossy, multiple colour/print options → brand identity. |
| 6 | **Easy swallowing** | Smooth, lubricated, smaller effective volume than equivalent tablet. |
| 7 | **Accurate unit dosing** | Pre-fabricated precise volume of fill → less dose variation. |
| 8 | **Fewer excipients required** | No need for binders, disintegrants, granulation steps like tablets. |
| 9 | **No dust contamination in manufacturing** | Drug is enclosed, reducing airborne cross-contamination in production halls. |
| 10 | **Tamper-evidence visible** | Sealed shell visibly damaged if opened, providing visual tamper cue. |
| 11 | **Rapid product development** | Formulation is faster than tablet development (no compression studies). |
| 12 | **Efficient for clinical trial supplies** | Easy to fill small batches of varied doses (clinical flexibility). |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Quantitative Relationships in Capsule Technology**

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit / Boundary | Engineering Use |
|-------------------|------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Capsule fill volume capacity** | $V_{c} = \rho_{b} \times m$ | mL (where $\rho_{b}$ = bulk density, $m$ = fill mass) | Size selection during preformulation |
| **Dose uniformity (USP)** | $\text{Acceptance Value (AV)} = \vert M - \bar{X} \vert + k \cdot s$ | $M$ = reference value, $\bar{X}$ = mean, $k$ = 2.4 (n=10) | Capsules must pass AV ≤ 15 (L1) |
| **Disintegration test (Capsule)** | Time $\leq 15$ min in 0.1 N HCl at 37°C | Pharmacopoeial standard | Quality control release |
| **Content uniformity** | $\frac{\text{Actual content}}{\text{Label claim}} \times 100$ | % (USP: 85–115%) | Batch quality attribute |
| **Hard capsule sizes (volume)** | $V_{000} = 1.37$ mL, $V_{00} = 0.95$ mL, $V_{0} = 0.68$ mL, $V_{1} = 0.50$ mL, $V_{2} = 0.37$ mL, $V_{3} = 0.30$ mL, $V_{4} = 0.21$ mL, $V_{5} = 0.13$ mL | mL (industry reference) | Selecting capsule for dose |
| **Hygroscopic drug consideration** | Water vapour transmission $\propto \dfrac{A \cdot \Delta P}{l}$ | where A = area, l = wall thickness | Shell permeability evaluation |

**CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value and vertical-bar notation is rendered using `\vert` to maintain markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Capsules

In the pharmaceutical industry, capsules are the **dosage form of choice for**:
- **OTC analgesics and antipyretics** (paracetamol, ibuprofen) — high-volume, fast-dissolving.
- **Antibiotics** (amoxicillin, cephalexin) — for unpleasant taste masking.
- **Vitamins and nutraceuticals** (omega-3 fish oil, vitamin E, vitamin A) — delivered as soft gel.
- **Anti-emetics, anti-histamines** (ondansetron, cetirizine) — for elegant small-dose delivery.
- **Herbal and Ayurvedic extracts** — for consumer acceptance.

The capsule industry is a **multi-billion-dollar global market**, with India being a major manufacturing and export hub, particularly in Bangalore, Hyderabad, and Sikkim pharmaceutical zones.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Capsule Size Selection from Dose and Bulk Density

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU Board Style):**
> A formulator needs to deliver **250 mg of an antibiotic** with a tapped bulk density of **0.65 g/mL**. Select an appropriate hard gelatin capsule size and justify.

**Step 1 — Identify the data given**
- Dose required: $m = 250$ mg
- Bulk density: $\rho_{b} = 0.65 \text{ g/mL}$

**Step 2 — Convert dose to grams for unit consistency**
$$m = 250 \text{ mg} = 0.25 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the minimum fill volume required**
$$V_{\text{required}} = \frac{m}{\rho_{b}} = \frac{0.25 \text{ g}}{0.65 \text{ g/mL}}$$

**Step 4 — Perform the division step-by-step**
$$V_{\text{required}} = 0.3846 \text{ mL (rounded to 4 significant figures)}$$

**Step 5 — Refer to capsule volume table and apply the safety factor (industry rule: 10–15% head-space)**

The industry standard practice is to leave ~**10% head-space** for proper cap closing:
$$V_{\text{target}} = V_{\text{required}} \times 1.10 = 0.3846 \times 1.10 = 0.4230 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with capsule volume table**
$$V_{0} = 0.68 \text{ mL} \geq 0.4230 \text{ mL} \geq V_{1} = 0.50 \text{ mL} \geq V_{2} = 0.37 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 7 — Select the smallest capsule that comfortably holds the dose**
Since $V_{\text{target}} = 0.4230$ mL, the dose fits within a **Size "1" capsule (0.50 mL)** but is too large for Size "2" (0.37 mL).

$$\boxed{\text{Selected Capsule Size} = \textbf{Size 1 (Hard Gelatin Capsule, 0.50 mL)}}$$

**Step 8 — Engineering justification**
The selected size provides a margin of $\frac{0.50 - 0.4230}{0.50} \times 100 \approx 15.4\%$ free space, ensuring reliable machine-filling and proper cap-seal closure.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Acceptance Value (AV) for Content Uniformity

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Ten capsules sampled from a batch of paracetamol 500 mg have the following assay values (mg): 502, 498, 505, 495, 499, 503, 496, 500, 501, 504. Determine if the batch passes USP Content Uniformity Stage 1 (L1) using the Acceptance Value method.

**Step 1 — List the data**
$$X_i = \{502, 498, 505, 495, 499, 503, 496, 500, 501, 504\}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the mean (X̄)**
$$\bar{X} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{10} X_i}{10} = \frac{502 + 498 + 505 + 495 + 499 + 503 + 496 + 500 + 501 + 504}{10}$$

$$\bar{X} = \frac{5003}{10} = 500.3 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the standard deviation (s)**
$$s = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}{n - 1}}$$

Calculating the squared deviations:
- $(502 - 500.3)^2 = 2.89$
- $(498 - 500.3)^2 = 5.29$
- $(505 - 500.3)^2 = 22.09$
- $(495 - 500.3)^2 = 28.09$
- $(499 - 500.3)^2 = 1.69$
- $(503 - 500.3)^2 = 7.29$
- $(496 - 500.3)^2 = 18.49$
- $(500 - 500.3)^2 = 0.09$
- $(501 - 500.3)^2 = 0.49$
- $(504 - 500.3)^2 = 13.69$

$$\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2 = 100.10$$

$$s = \sqrt{\frac{100.10}{9}} = \sqrt{11.122} = 3.335$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the reference value M**
For a target of 500 mg with no overage, $M = 500$ mg.

**Step 5 — Compute the Acceptance Value (AV) using L1 constant k = 2.4 for n = 10**
$$AV = \vert M - \bar{X} \vert + k \cdot s$$
$$AV = \vert 500 - 500.3 \vert + 2.4 \times 3.335$$
$$AV = 0.3 + 8.004$$
$$AV = 8.304$$

**Step 6 — Compare with USP L1 acceptance limit (AV ≤ 15.0)**
$$AV = 8.304 \leq 15.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{BATCH PASSES Stage 1 (L1)}$$

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation**
Since the AV is well below 15.0, the batch exhibits excellent dose uniformity — typical of a **properly calibrated automatic capsule-filling machine** operating with good powder-flow characteristics.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Capsules vs. Tablets vs. Powders

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is high-yield for 14-mark KTU questions on dosage-form comparison.**

| Parameter | **Hard Gelatin Capsule** | **Compressed Tablet** | **Bulk Powder** |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------|------------------|
| Taste masking | Excellent | Requires film/sugar coating | Poor (exposed to taste) |
| Dose accuracy | High | High | Low (patient-dependent) |
| Manufacturing complexity | Moderate (filling) | High (granulation, compression) | Low |
| Cost per dose | Moderate | Low (high volume) | Lowest |
| Bioavailability (typical) | Good to excellent | Moderate to good | Variable |
| Suitable for liquids | No (HGC), Yes (SGC) | No | No |
| Patient compliance | High | Moderate | Low (taste, measurement) |
| Stability vs. humidity | Moderate | Good | High |
| Suitable for hygroscopic drugs | Limited (with sealing) | Yes (with blister) | Yes |
| Speed of development | Fast | Slow | Fast |
| Overage for wastage | ~5% | ~2–3% | Negligible |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Anatomy of a Hard Gelatin Capsule (Two-Piece)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block describes the structural anatomy and functional architecture of a hard gelatin capsule system. Due to Mermaid rendering limitations with curved 3D physical objects, the diagram is presented as a functional block architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Hard Gelatin Capsule Two-Piece System"]
    node1 --> nodeA["CAP Upper Piece Pre-Locked"]
    node1 --> nodeB["BODY Lower Piece Filled"]
    nodeA --> nodeA1["Outer Surface Glossy Coloured"]
    nodeA --> nodeA2["Tamper Seal Groove at Rim"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["Inner Volume 0.13 to 1.37 mL"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["HOLD Powder Granule Pellet Mini Tablet"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["Rim Bead for Cap Locking"]
    node2["Gelatin Shell Composition"] --> node2A["Bovine or Porcine Gelatin Type A or B"]
    node2 --> node2B["Plasticiser Glycerin or Sorbitol"]
    node2 --> node2C["Colourant FD and C or Lake Pigment"]
    node2 --> node2D["Opacifier Titanium Dioxide"]
    node2 --> node2E["Water 30 to 40 percent at Manufacture"]
    node3["Functional Lifecycle Stages"] --> node3A["1 Shell Manufacture Dip Pin Moulding"]
    node3 --> node3B["2 Filling Powder Dispense into Body"]
    node3 --> node3C["3 Joining Cap onto Body by Snap Fit"]
    node3 --> node3D["4 Polishing and Inspection"]
    node3 --> node3E["5 Blister or Bottle Packaging"]
    node4["Route of Administration"] --> node4A["Oral Swallow with Water"]
    node4 --> node4B["Gastric Rupture within 5 min"]
    node4 --> node4C["Drug Release and Absorption"]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: From API to Finished Capsule

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["API Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient"] --> stepB["Sieving to Uniform Particle Size"]
    stepB --> stepC["Blending with Excipients Lubricant Glidant"]
    stepC --> stepD["Capsule Filling Machine Dosing Disc or Auger Type"]
    stepD --> stepE["Capsule Body Filled with Powder"]
    stepE --> stepF["Cap Joining and Tamper Sealing"]
    stepF --> stepG["Polishing to Remove Surface Dust"]
    stepG --> stepH["Quality Control Weight Variation Disintegration Assay"]
    stepH --> stepI["Packaging Blister Strip or HDPE Bottle"]
    stepI --> stepJ["Finished Capsule for Patient Dispensing"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Map: Decision Tree for Choosing Capsule vs. Tablet

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    decision1{"API is a liquid or semi solid"}
    decision2{"API has intensely bitter taste or odour"}
    decision3{"Dose is very high above 1000 mg per unit"}
    decision4{"Drug is highly moisture sensitive"}
    decision5{"Rapid product launch is required"}
    pathA["Choose SOFT GELATIN CAPSULE"]
    pathB["Choose HARD GELATIN CAPSULE"]
    pathC["Choose COATED TABLET"]
    pathD["Choose EFFERVESCENT or SACHET"]
    pathE["Use SPECIALTY CAPSULE Sealed in Blister"]
    pathF["Choose HGC with PEG sealing or ampoule in bottle"]
    pathG["Choose HGC for speed advantage"]
    decision1 -- YES --> pathA
    decision1 -- NO --> decision2
    decision2 -- YES --> pathB
    decision2 -- NO --> decision3
    decision3 -- YES --> pathD
    decision3 -- NO --> decision4
    decision4 -- YES --> pathF
    decision4 -- NO --> decision5
    decision5 -- YES --> pathG
    decision5 -- NO --> pathC
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question 1]**
> **Q1. Define a capsule. List four advantages of capsules over compressed tablets.**

**Model Answer:**
A capsule is a solid dosage form in which one or more medicaments and inert excipients are enclosed within a small, sealed, water-soluble shell made of gelatin.

**Four advantages over compressed tablets:**
1. **Excellent taste and odour masking** — the sealed shell hides bitter APIs.
2. **Faster drug release and higher bioavailability** — rapid rupture in stomach disperses fine particles.
3. **Fewer excipients required** — no need for binders, disintegrants, or granulation.
4. **Rapid product development** — simpler formulation workflow accelerates clinical and commercial timelines.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question 2]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between hard gelatin capsules (HGC) and soft gelatin capsules (SGC) in four points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hard Gelatin Capsule (HGC) | Soft Gelatin Capsule (SGC) |
|-----------|----------------------------|----------------------------|
| Number of pieces | Two (body + cap) | One-piece hermetically sealed |
| Shell composition | Mainly gelatin + water | Gelatin + plasticiser (glycerol/sorbitol) |
| Fill material | Powders, granules, pellets, mini-tablets | Liquids, oils, suspensions, pastes |
| Manufacturing | Shell made separately, then filled | Shell formed and filled simultaneously (rotary die process) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (ESE Module Internal Choice) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Long-Answer Choice 1]**
> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the pharmaceutical **rationale** for developing capsule dosage forms. Why is a capsule preferred for bitter, photosensitive, and oil-based drugs?

**(b) [7 Marks)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of hard gelatin capsules as a modern oral solid dosage form.

---

#### **Model Answer for Question A**

**(a) Pharmaceutical Rationale for Capsules (7 Marks):**

The rationale for capsule formulation addresses three key patient- and product-driven concerns:

**1. Organoleptic Property Masking (2 marks)**
Many drugs like **chloramphenicol, paracetamol, quinine, NSAIDs, and B-complex vitamins** possess intensely **bitter, astringent, or pungent taste** and unpleasant odour. The gelatin shell, being **sealed, tasteless, and odourless**, prevents the drug from contacting taste buds until the capsule reaches the stomach, dramatically improving **patient compliance — especially in paediatric and geriatric cohorts**.

**2. Protection from Environmental Degradation (2 marks)**
The opaque, hermetically sealed shell shields **light-sensitive** APIs (e.g., vitamin A, riboflavin, furosemide) from photolytic degradation and **oxygen-sensitive** APIs from oxidative deterioration. This is a significant advantage over bulk powders, which are fully exposed to ambient air and UV.

**3. Delivery of Oils and Lipophilic Actives (1.5 marks)**
Soft gelatin capsules uniquely enable the oral delivery of **fish oils, evening primrose oil, vitamin E, lecithin, and lipophilic drugs** in a unit-dose, leak-proof, and elegant form — something that tablets physically cannot do.

**4. Improved Bioavailability and Faster Onset (1.5 marks)**
The shell ruptures rapidly in gastric fluid (typically within 3–5 min), dispersing the fill into fine particles with high surface area → **rapid dissolution and absorption** compared to compressed tablets in many cases. This makes capsules the **dosage form of choice for antipyretics, analgesics, and anti-allergic drugs** where fast action is therapeutic.

---

**(b) Advantages and Limitations of HGC (7 Marks):**

**Advantages (4 marks):**
- Excellent **taste/odour masking** of bitter APIs.
- **Accurate unit-dose** delivery with low dose variation.
- **Simple, fast formulation** — no granulation, no compression, no coating required.
- **High patient acceptance** — easy to swallow, elegant appearance, multiple colour options for brand identity.
- Suitable for **clinical trial flexibility** — small batches of varied strengths can be prepared quickly.

**Limitations (3 marks):**
- **Hygroscopic and moisture-sensitive drugs** are difficult to encapsulate in HGC because the gelatin shell is itself hydrophilic — water exchange can soften or damage the shell.
- **Strongly **reactive drugs** (e.g., aldehydes, strong acids/bases) can chemically interact with gelatin and **cause shell brittleness or cross-linking**.
- **Highly concentrated, potent drugs** (dose < 25 mg) face **content uniformity challenges** due to small mass of fill.
- **Volatile oils and liquids** cannot be filled into standard HGC — require soft gelatin (SGC) instead.
- Not suitable for **chewing or crushing** by patients (lose dose-protective function).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Long-Answer Choice 2]**
> ### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a **hard gelatin capsule**? Describe its **shell composition and manufacturing** in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Enumerate the **advantages** of capsules as a dosage form. Briefly explain the **KTU classification** of capsule dosage forms.

---

#### **Model Answer for Question B**

**(a) Hard Gelatin Capsule — Definition, Shell Composition and Manufacturing (7 Marks):**

A **hard gelatin capsule (HGC)** is a two-piece, telescoping, cylindrical solid dosage form in which the **body** is filled with dry powder/granules and the **cap** is then pushed over the body to form a sealed unit.

**Shell Composition (3 marks):**
- **Gelatin** (Type A from porcine skin or Type B from bovine skin) — primary film-forming polymer, **~70% w/w** of dry shell.
- **Water** — **30–40% w/w** during manufacture, retained as plasticising moisture.
- **Plasticiser** — **glycerol or sorbitol (0–10%)** for shell flexibility.
- **Colourant** — **FD&C dyes or lakes** for appearance and brand identity.
- **Opacifier** — **titanium dioxide (1–5%)** to protect light-sensitive fills.
- **Preservatives** — **parabens or SO₂** in some formulations to prevent microbial growth.
- **Flavouring agent** — occasionally added (e.g., peppermint) to improve mouth-feel.

**Manufacturing — Dip-Pin Method (4 marks):**
1. **Preparation of gelatin solution** — gelatin is dissolved in demineralised water at **60–70°C** under vacuum to remove entrapped air bubbles.
2. **Dipping** — Stainless steel moulding pins (pins) are dipped into the warm gelatin solution. The thickness of the film on the pin is controlled by **dwell time** (~12–15 s) and **gelatin viscosity**.
3. **Gelling** — The pins are rotated to a position where the gelatin film sets by cooling at controlled temperature (~22°C, 50% RH).
4. **Drying** — The set gelatin films are dried in a series of drying tunnels with stepwise temperature and humidity control (final RH < 20%).
5. **Stripping** — Dried shells are stripped from the pins as a single cap or body piece.
6. **Trimming and joining** — Caps and bodies are cut to precise length and pre-locked for use in capsule-filling machines.
7. **Printing** — Optional ink printing of identification on cap or body.

---

**(b) Advantages of Capsules and Classification (7 Marks):**

**Advantages of Capsules (4 marks):**
1. **Excellent taste and odour masking** — sealed shell isolates drug.
2. **Faster onset of action** — rapid shell rupture in stomach.
3. **High patient compliance** — elegant, easy-to-swallow, available in multiple colours.
4. **Fewer excipients** — no binder or disintegrant required (unlike tablets).
5. **Versatile fill** — powders, granules, pellets, mini-tablets (HGC) or oils, suspensions, pastes (SGC).
6. **Tamper-evident** — visible shell damage upon opening.
7. **Rapid formulation development** — ideal for clinical trial supplies and new launches.

**Classification of Capsule Dosage Forms (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Classification (Module 2 syllabus):**
>
> **A. Based on Shell Construction:**
> 1. **Hard Gelatin Capsules (HGC)** — two-piece, used for dry solids.
> 2. **Soft Gelatin Capsules (SGC)** — one-piece sealed, used for liquids and semi-solids.
>
> **B. Based on Release Pattern:**
> 1. **Immediate-release capsules** — standard, rapid rupture.
> 2. **Modified-release capsules** — enteric-coated, sustained-release, delayed-release.
> 3. **Extended-release capsules** — coated pellets/beads inside HGC.
>
> **C. Based on Shell Material:**
> 1. **Gelatin capsules** — traditional (bovine/porcine).
> 2. **HPMC (vegetable) capsules** — for vegetarian/religious-restricted markets.
> 3. **Starch capsules** — emerging alternative.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Capsule Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing rationale with advantages** — *Rationale* answers *WHY capsules were developed*; *Advantages* answers *what benefits they offer over other forms*. Examiners deduct marks when these are mixed up.
>
> 2. **Not knowing capsule size numbers** — Writing "size 0" without knowing its volume (0.68 mL) loses you a mark. Memorise: **5 = 0.13 mL (smallest), 000 = 1.37 mL (largest)**.
>
> 3. **Skipping shell composition percentages** — Writing only "gelatin and water" without quantifying (~70% gelatin, 30–40% water at manufacture) loses a mark.
>
> 4. **Forgetting limitations** — Many students write only advantages. Always pair the answer with at least **3–4 limitations** for full 14-mark question coverage.
>
> 5. **Misnaming the dip-pin method** — It's not "dipping method" or "pin dipping" alone; the correct term is **"dipping-pin (or mould-pin) method"** of capsule shell manufacture.
>
> 6. **Wrong unit for bulk density** — Always write g/mL, not g/cm³ (cm³ is acceptable but g/mL is the KTU convention).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Capsule Introduction):**
>
> ✅ Capsule = solid unit dosage form with **medicament enclosed in a sealed, water-soluble gelatin shell**.
>
> ✅ Two principal types: **HGC (two-piece, dry fill)** and **SGC (one-piece, liquid/semi-solid fill)**.
>
> ✅ **Rationale** = *Why* capsules were developed → taste masking, protection of light/oxygen-sensitive drugs, delivery of oils, faster onset, accurate dosing.
>
> ✅ **Advantages** = *What* capsules offer → fewer excipients, high compliance, versatile fill, tamper-evidence, elegant appearance, faster development.
>
> ✅ **Capsule sizes** increase from 5 (smallest, 0.13 mL) → 000 (largest, 1.37 mL). Size 0 and 00 are most common in industry.
>
> ✅ Shell composition: **gelatin + water + plasticiser + colourant + opacifier** (with optional preservative).
>
> ✅ Manufacturing method for shells: **dipping-pin (mould-pin) method** with controlled temperature and humidity drying.
>
> ✅ USP standards: **disintegration ≤ 15 min in 0.1 N HCl at 37°C**; **content uniformity AV ≤ 15.0 (L1)**; **weight variation within pharmacopoeial limits**.
>
> ✅ Limitations to remember: **not for hygroscopic drugs, not for reactive APIs (with gelatin), not for very small doses, not for liquids in HGC** (use SGC).
>
> ✅ Always pair your answer with **limitations** to score full marks in 14-mark questions.
>
> ✅ Mention **HPMC (vegetarian) capsules** as the modern alternative to satisfy global vegetarian, halal, and kosher markets.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preformulation Considerations for Capsule Dosage Form
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Preformulation Considerations for Capsule Dosage Form

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Preformulation** is the stage of pharmaceutical development between drug discovery/discovery of a new chemical entity (NCE) and the final dosage form formulation, wherein the physicochemical, mechanical, and biopharmaceutical properties of the drug substance are thoroughly characterized. For **capsule dosage forms**, preformulation specifically refers to the systematic investigation of those drug and excipient characteristics that govern the design, manufacture, stability, and in-vivo performance of a hard or soft gelatin capsule product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight (Board Exam Standard):**
> *"Preformulation is the science of developing a dosage form by studying the physical, chemical, and mechanical properties of a new drug entity, with the ultimate goal of designing a drug delivery system that is safe, efficacious, stable, and patient-compliant."*

### Scope in Capsule Dosage Form Development

For capsule development, preformulation encompasses study of:

- The **Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient (API)** — solid-state properties, solubility, stability.
- The **Excipients** — diluents, lubricants, glidants, disintegrants, wetting agents, and release modifiers.
- The **Capsule Shell** — gelatin or HPMC composition, moisture permeability, cross-linking potential, and compatibility with fill materials.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Pillars of Capsule Preformulation:**
> 1. **Drug Substance Characterization** — establishes the intrinsic properties of the API.
> 2. **Drug-Excipient Compatibility** — establishes how the API behaves with added excipients and the shell.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **master tailor** who has been handed a beautiful, expensive fabric (the API) and asked to stitch it into a formal suit (the capsule). Before you cut a single piece, you must:

- **Feel the fabric** — Is it silk, cotton, or wool? (corresponds to **crystallinity, polymorphism** of the drug).
- **Test how it reacts to water/heat** — Will it shrink? Fade? Bleed color? (corresponds to **hygroscopicity, thermal stability, photostability**).
- **Choose the right lining, buttons, and thread** — Will the metal buttons rust and stain the fabric? (corresponds to **drug–excipient compatibility**, e.g., iron oxide excipients accelerating drug degradation).
- **Decide the size of the suit** — Will a Large fit the customer, or do you need a tailor-made cut? (corresponds to **capsule size selection** based on **bulk density** and **dose**).
- **Ensure the suit remains crisp in a humid wardrobe** — (corresponds to **packaging and moisture protection**).

In the same way, the pharmaceutical formulator must understand every intrinsic property of the drug and excipients *before* a single capsule is filled on a machine. **Preformulation is the "tailoring phase" of pharmaceutical development.**

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exams:** "**PC C S T F R**"
> **P**article size, **C**ompatibility, **C**ompaction, **S**olubility, **T**apped/True density, **F**low, **R**elease/Hygroscopicity.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants / Reference Metrics

- **Standard Tap Density (USP):** Initial volume → 10 taps → 500 taps → 1250 taps; reported when difference between two consecutive volumes ≤ **2 % mL**.
- **Standard Moisture Content for Hard Gelatin Shells:** **13 % w/w ± 1 %** (gelatin) or **4–6 % w/w** (HPMC).
- **Storage Condition (ICH Q1A):** Long-term testing at **25 °C / 60 % RH** for **12 months** minimum.
- **Carr's Index Classification:** 5–15 % = excellent, 12–18 % = good, 18–21 % = fair-passable, 21–25 % = poor, >25 % = very poor.
- **Hausner Ratio:** 1.00–1.11 = excellent, 1.12–1.18 = good, 1.19–1.25 = fair, 1.26–1.34 = poor, >1.34 = very poor.
- **Capsule Body Capacity (size 0 hard gelatin):** ≈ **0.68 mL** (the universal benchmark size for dose-volume calculations).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Capsule Dosage Forms (Operational Background)

Before diving into preformulation, the KTU syllabus requires a sharp understanding of the two parent types:

### A. Hard Gelatin Capsules (HGC)
- Two-piece (body + cap) telescoping design.
- Used for **powders, granules, pellets, beads, mini-tablets, oily liquids, and semisolids**.
- Manufactured by **dipping steel moulds (pins)** into a gelatin solution maintained at ~**50 °C** with viscosity 300–500 cP.
- Moisture content of shell: **12–15 % w/w**.

### B. Soft Gelatin Capsules (SGC)
- One-piece hermetically sealed unit.
- Used for **oils, suspensions, and emulsions**.
- Manufactured by the **rotary die process (R.P. Scherer technology)** or **Accogel bubble method**.
- Shell plasticized with **glycerin, sorbitol, or propylene glycol**.

---

## 2.2 Preformulation Parameters — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.2.1 Organoleptic / Description Properties

| Parameter | Why it Matters in Capsules |
|---|---|
| **Color** | Affects patient perception; impurities may darken. |
| **Odor / Taste** | Critical for ODT capsules; requires taste masking. |
| **Crystal Form** | Determines solubility and flow. |

---

### 2.2.2 Particle Size and Surface Area

- **Smaller particles** → ↑ surface area → ↑ dissolution rate.
- **But** too small → ↑ cohesion → ↓ flow → capsule fill-weight variation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** For capsule fills, the **ideal particle size is 60–200 µm** (60-mesh to 200-mesh range). Fines (<50 µm) cause segregation and flow issues; large particles (>500 µm) cause inconsistent fills and poor content uniformity.

**Specific Surface Area (SSA):**

$$\text{SSA} = \dfrac{6}{\rho_p \cdot d_{vs}}$$

where $\rho_p$ = particle density, $d_{vs}$ = volume-surface mean diameter.

---

### 2.2.3 Bulk Density, Tapped Density, and Derived Flow Indices

**Bulk Density ($\rho_b$):**

$$\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V_b}$$

**Tapped Density ($\rho_t$):**

$$\rho_t = \dfrac{m}{V_t}$$

**Carr's Compressibility Index (CI):**

$$\text{CI} = \dfrac{\rho_t - \rho_b}{\rho_t} \times 100$$

**Hausner Ratio (HR):**

$$\text{Hausner Ratio} = \dfrac{\rho_t}{\rho_b}$$

**Angle of Repose ($\theta$):**

$$\tan(\theta) = \dfrac{h}{r}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These three indices together form the *Flow Triad* — they predict the behavior of a powder on the **dosing disc** or **auger filler** of a capsule-filling machine. Poor flow → weight variation → batch failure in uniformity of dosage units (USP <905>).

---

### 2.2.4 Powder Compaction and Compactibility

- Compaction is the **ability of a powder to form a coherent mass** under pressure.
- Important in capsules filled by **direct compression into pellets** or when **mini-tablets** are filled.
- Evaluated by measuring **tensile strength** of compacts at different compression forces using the **Heckel equation**:

$$\ln\left[\dfrac{1}{1 - D}\right] = K \cdot P + A$$

where $D$ = relative density, $P$ = applied pressure, $K$ = slope, $A$ = constant related to initial packing.

---

### 2.2.5 Hygroscopicity and Moisture Sorption

- **Hygroscopic drugs** absorb water from the capsule shell (which is 13–15 % water for HGC).
- This causes:
  - Shell brittleness loss.
  - Drug hydrolysis.
  - **Migration of gelatin components into the fill** (e.g., aldehydes from capsule cross-linking).
- Classified per Callahan et al. (1982) on a 5-tier scale from **Non-hygroscopic** to **Very Hygroscopic**.

**Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC):**

$$\text{EMC} = \dfrac{W_e - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$$

where $W_e$ = weight at equilibrium, $W_d$ = dry weight.

---

### 2.2.6 Drug-Excipient Compatibility (DEC)

Conducted via:
1. **Binary mixtures (1:1 w/w)** of drug + excipient, stressed at **40 °C / 75 % RH for 4 weeks**.
2. **DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry)** — peak shift/disappearance indicates interaction.
3. **TGA (Thermogravimetric Analysis)** — weight loss changes.
4. **FT-IR / XRD** — new chemical bonds or polymorphic transitions.
5. **HPLC** — assay loss >1 % indicates incompatibility.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Trap:** A **physical mixture** in DSC may show peak shifts due to **simple physical dilution**, not chemical interaction. Always confirm DEC by **HPLC assay** at stressed conditions.

---

### 2.2.7 Compatibility with Capsule Shell

Special tests for capsule dosage forms:

| Drug Property | Shell Interaction | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Hygroscopic** | Water migration from shell → shell brittleness | Use HPMC shell, add desiccant |
| **Aldehyde-releasing** | Maillard reaction with gelatin | Add antioxidant, use low aldehyde shell |
| **Hygroscopic alkaline** | Shell softening | Buffer, drying, use HPMC |
| **Eutectic mixtures** | Shell liquefaction at low T | Microencapsulate the drug |

---

### 2.2.8 Capsule Size Selection (Dose-Volume Calculation)

The **fundamental equation of capsule preformulation** (frequently asked in KTU boards):

$$V_c = \dfrac{m}{\rho_t}$$

where $V_c$ = capsule fill volume, $m$ = dose mass, $\rho_t$ = tapped density.

**Practical table — Capsule Body Capacity (mL):**

| Size | 000 | 00 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vol (mL) | 1.37 | 0.95 | 0.68 | 0.50 | 0.37 | 0.30 | 0.21 | 0.13 |

---

### 2.2.9 Stability Studies

Conducted as per **ICH Q1A(R2)** — Zones I–IVb based on long-term storage conditions. Capsule products specifically require:

- **Bracketing design** (e.g., smallest and largest size tested).
- **Water content monitoring** of shell.
- **Disintegration test (USP <701>)** as a stability-indicating parameter.
- **Dissolution test (USP <711>)** at 0.1 N HCl and pH 6.8 buffer.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Variables & Units | Boundary / Reference Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_b = m / V_b$ | Bulk density (g/mL) | $0.3 \le \rho_b \le 0.8$ typical for fills |
| 2 | $\rho_t = m / V_t$ | Tapped density (g/mL) | USP method: 10, 500, 1250 taps |
| 3 | $\text{CI} = (\rho_t - \rho_b)/\rho_t \times 100$ | Carr's Index (%) | Excellent: $\le 15$, Poor: $> 25$ |
| 4 | $\text{HR} = \rho_t / \rho_b$ | Hausner Ratio (unitless) | Excellent: $1.00-1.11$ |
| 5 | $\tan(\theta) = h / r$ | Angle of repose (degrees) | Excellent: $25°-30°$, Poor: $>40°$ |
| 6 | $\text{SSA} = 6/(\rho_p \cdot d_{vs})$ | Specific surface area (m²/g) | Higher SSA → faster dissolution |
| 7 | $V_c = m / \rho_t$ | Capsule volume (mL) | Compare to body capacity table |
| 8 | $\ln[1/(1-D)] = K \cdot P + A$ | Heckel equation (compaction) | $D$ = relative density |
| 9 | $\text{EMC} = (W_e - W_d)/W_d \times 100$ | Equilibrium moisture content (%) | Shell gelatin: 12-15 % |
| 10 | $\text{Ratio (fill:shuttle)} = \rho_b / \rho_t$ | Volumetric fill ratio | $0.85-0.95$ ideal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in **QbD (Quality by Design)** frameworks, **Process Analytical Technology (PAT)**, and **continuous manufacturing** of capsules. They form the foundation of **RTRT (Real-Time Release Testing)** as per FDA guidance.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Pharmaceutical Industry

- **Powder Flow Engineering:** Triadic indices (CI, HR, $\theta$) are used to design **hopper geometry** in capsule-filling machines (e.g., Bosch GKF, MG2, IMA Zanasi).
- **Process Engineering:** Tapped density is the key input for **dosing disc volume settings**.
- **Packaging Engineering:** Hygroscopicity data drives the choice of **cold-form Alu-Alu** vs. **PVC/PVDC blisters** vs. **HDPE bottles with desiccant**.
- **Regulatory Science:** ICH Q8 (R2) Pharmaceutical Development mandates all these preformulation data in the **CTD Module 3.2.P.2** submission dossier.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Selection of Capsule Size

### Problem Statement
A new drug **Acyclovir-mock** has a single dose of **500 mg**. Preformulation reveals the following powder properties:

- Bulk density, $\rho_b = 0.42 \text{ g/mL}$
- Tapped density, $\rho_t = 0.55 \text{ g/mL}$
- Carr's Index = **24 %** (Poor flow)
- Hausner Ratio = **1.31** (Poor flow)
- Angle of repose = **38°** (Passable)
- Hygroscopicity class: **Class III (Moderately hygroscopic)**
- Particle size: 80 % between **75–180 µm** (acceptable)
- Compatibility: **Incompatible with lactose (browning under stress)**

**Determine:** (a) Required capsule size, (b) Whether glidant is needed, (c) Suggested shell type.

---

### Step 1 — Calculate the Required Fill Volume

The standard preformulation design assumption is that the powder will be filled to a **tapped density state** inside the capsule (since the machine tamps the powder).

$$V_c = \dfrac{m}{\rho_t}$$

Substitute $m = 500 \text{ mg} = 0.500 \text{ g}$ and $\rho_t = 0.55 \text{ g/mL}$:

$$V_c = \dfrac{0.500 \text{ g}}{0.55 \text{ g/mL}}$$

Perform the division:

$$V_c = 0.9091 \text{ mL}$$

**Interpretation:** The capsule must hold at least **0.91 mL** of powder.

---

### Step 2 — Compare with Capsule Body Capacity Table

| Capsule Size | Body Capacity (mL) | Sufficient? |
|---|---|---|
| 00 | 0.95 | **Yes — marginal (0.04 mL safety margin)** |
| 000 | 1.37 | Yes, but oversized |
| 0 | 0.68 | **No — underfills by 0.23 mL** |

**Decision:** **Size 00** is the smallest capsule that holds the dose. However, the safety margin is only 0.04 mL (≈ 4 %).

---

### Step 3 — Check for Practical Safety Factor

Apply a **15 % safety margin** to account for:
- Overfill of granules (bulking by granulation).
- Tamping excess.
- Bridge-formation in the dosing disc.

$$V_{\text{required, safe}} = V_c \times 1.15$$

$$V_{\text{required, safe}} = 0.9091 \times 1.15 = 1.0455 \text{ mL}$$

**Revised Decision:** **Size 000 (1.37 mL)** is selected for robust production.

---

### Step 4 — Evaluate Flow Properties (Glidant Decision)

Given:
- CI = 24 % → **Poor flow** (>$25$ is very poor, so this is borderline).
- HR = 1.31 → **Poor flow**.
- $\theta = 38°$ → **Passable**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Threshold Rule:** For high-speed capsule filling (≥ 60,000 capsules/hour), the **target CI is ≤ 18 %**. Since 24 % > 18 %, **glidant addition is mandatory**.

**Typical Glidants:**

| Glidant | Usual Concentration (% w/w) | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Colloidal Silicon Dioxide (Aerosil 200) | 0.1–1.0 % | Spacer particles, reduces interparticulate friction |
| Talc | 0.5–3.0 % | Surfaces as a lubricant/glidant |
| Magnesium Stearate | 0.25–1.0 % | Boundary lubrication (also works as glidant) |

**Suggested:** Add **0.5 % w/w colloidal silicon dioxide** to reduce CI to ~16 %.

---

### Step 5 — Evaluate Shell Compatibility

- Drug is **moderately hygroscopic** (Class III) — gelatin shell may dry out and become brittle.
- **Decision:** Use **HPMC capsule shell** (e.g., Vcaps® by Lonza) with moisture content **4–6 % w/w**.
- Alternative: Use **Alu-Alu blister** packaging with desiccant if gelatin is mandated.

---

### Step 6 — Final Formulation Strategy

| Component | Role | Amount per Capsule |
|---|---|---|
| Acyclovir-mock (API) | Active | 500 mg |
| Microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) | Diluent (replacing lactose) | ~190 mg |
| Colloidal silicon dioxide | Glidant | 3.5 mg (0.5 %) |
| Magnesium stearate | Lubricant | 3.5 mg (0.5 %) |
| **Total fill weight** | | **~700 mg** |

**Selected Capsule:** **Size 000, HPMC shell, sealed by point-banding or HPMC banding**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Compatibility Decision Table

| Excipient Type | Common Example | Mechanism of Interaction with Drug | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reducing sugars** | Lactose | Maillard reaction (Amadori product) | Replace with MCC, mannitol |
| **Aldehyde-containing** | Povidone (high peroxide) | Oxidative degradation | Use low-peroxide grade (e.g., Kollidon VA 64) |
| **Hygroscopic** | Sorbitol, mannitol | Water migration into drug | Use HPMC shell, dry blends |
| **Ionic** | Calcium carbonate, MgO | pH shift, ionization | Buffered diluent |
| **Adsorbent** | Bentonite, kaolin | Reduced bioavailability | Use soluble diluent |
| **Oxidizing** | Ferric oxide | Oxidation of API | Antioxidant (BHT, BHA, ascorbic acid) |
| **Alkaline** | Sodium bicarbonate | Shell softening | HPMC shell, encapsulated granules |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Mnemonic — "RAMBO"** for excipient incompatibility types:
> **R**educing, **A**lkaline, **M**oisture-migrating, **B**ound/adsorbed, **O**xidizing.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Heckel Equation Interpretation

For a drug with measured compact relative densities $D_1, D_2, D_3$ at pressures $P_1, P_2, P_3$:

| $P$ (MPa) | $D$ (relative density) | $\ln[1/(1-D)]$ |
|---|---|---|
| 50 | 0.78 | $\ln(1/0.22) = 1.514$ |
| 100 | 0.86 | $\ln(1/0.14) = 1.966$ |
| 150 | 0.91 | $\ln(1/0.09) = 2.408$ |
| 200 | 0.94 | $\ln(1/0.06) = 2.813$ |

**Linear Regression:** $\ln[1/(1-D)] = K \cdot P + A$

Using the two end points (50, 1.514) and (200, 2.813):

$$K = \dfrac{2.813 - 1.514}{200 - 50} = \dfrac{1.299}{150} = 0.00866 \text{ MPa}^{-1}$$

Mean Yield Pressure:

$$P_y = \dfrac{1}{K} = \dfrac{1}{0.00866} = 115.5 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A low $P_y$ (~115 MPa) indicates a **plastic-deforming drug** — good for direct compression of pellets inside capsules. A high $P_y$ (>300 MPa) suggests **brittle fragmentation**, requiring granulation.

---

## 3.4 KTU-Style Numerical Problem with Full Solution

**Problem (14-Mark KTU Pattern):**

> *"A new anti-hypertensive drug has a dose of 250 mg. Preformulation studies show: bulk density 0.40 g/mL, tapped density 0.50 g/mL. The drug is moderately hygroscopic and incompatible with lactose. Determine (a) Carr's Index and Hausner Ratio, comment on flow; (b) required capsule size; (c) excipient strategy."*

### Solution

**(a) Carr's Index and Hausner Ratio**

$$\text{CI} = \dfrac{\rho_t - \rho_b}{\rho_t} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.50 - 0.40}{0.50} \times 100 = 20\,\%$$

$$\text{HR} = \dfrac{\rho_t}{\rho_b} = \dfrac{0.50}{0.40} = 1.25$$

**Comment:** CI = 20 % → **Fair-passable flow**; HR = 1.25 → **Fair flow**. Borderline — needs glidant for high-speed filling.

**(b) Required Capsule Size**

$$V_c = \dfrac{m}{\rho_t} = \dfrac{0.250 \text{ g}}{0.50 \text{ g/mL}} = 0.50 \text{ mL}$$

From body capacity table: **Size 1 (0.50 mL)** is exact. With 15 % safety margin:
$V_{\text{safe}} = 0.50 \times 1.15 = 0.575 \text{ mL}$ → **Size 0 (0.68 mL)** preferred.

**(c) Excipient Strategy**

- Replace lactose with **microcrystalline cellulose (MCC PH 102)** — 100 mg.
- Add **0.5 % colloidal silicon dioxide** (~2 mg) as glidant.
- Add **0.5 % magnesium stearate** (~2 mg) as lubricant.
- Use **HPMC capsule (Size 0)** for moderate hygroscopicity.
- Package in **Alu-Alu blister** with silica desiccant.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

    E --> F

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Drug-Excipient Compatibility Workflow

```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Capsule Size Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Required Dose in mg] --> B[Calculate Vc = Dose / Tapped Density]
    B --> C[Apply 15 percent Safety Factor]
    C --> D{Vc less than 0.13 mL?}

    D -- No --> F{Vc less than 0.21 mL?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Size 4 Capsule]
    F -- No --> H{Vc less than 0.30 mL?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Size 3 Capsule]
    H -- No --> J{Vc less than 0.37 mL?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Size 2 Capsule]
    J -- No --> L{Vc less than 0.50 mL?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Size 1 Capsule]
    L -- No --> N{Vc less than 0.68 mL?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Size 0 Capsule]
    N -- No --> P{Vc less than 0.95 mL?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Size 00 Capsule]
    P -- No --> R[Size 000 Capsule or Divide Dose]
```
