# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define preformulation. List any four preformulation considerations for capsule dosage form.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Preformulation is the phase of pharmaceutical research and development where the physicochemical and biopharmaceutical properties of a drug substance are characterized to design a stable, efficacious, and safe dosage form.

**Four preformulation considerations for capsule dosage form:**

1. **Particle size and distribution** — affects flow, fill-weight uniformity, and dissolution.
2. **Bulk and tapped density** — determines capsule size selection via $V_c = m/\rho_t$.
3. **Drug–excipient compatibility** — ensures chemical and physical stability in the presence of diluents, glidants, and lubricants.
4. **Hygroscopicity** — moisture sorption affects shell integrity and drug stability.

> [!TIP]
> **Supplementary points** (for depth): Polymorphism, compactibility, compatibility with capsule shell, stability as per ICH Q1A.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is Carr's Compressibility Index? How is it used to assess flow properties of capsule fill powders?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Carr's Compressibility Index (CI) is a numerical measure of powder compressibility that quantifies flow characteristics of a powder, calculated as:

$$\text{CI} = \dfrac{\rho_t - \rho_b}{\rho_t} \times 100$$

where $\rho_b$ = bulk density, $\rho_t$ = tapped density.

**Use in Capsule Fill Assessment:**

| CI (%) | Flow Description |
|---|---|
| 5–15 | Excellent |
| 12–18 | Good |
| 18–21 | Fair-Passable |
| 21–25 | Poor |
| >25 | Very Poor |

**Application:** A capsule fill with CI > 21 % requires glidant addition (e.g., colloidal silicon dioxide 0.1–1 % w/w) or granulation to improve flow on dosing disc.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, Q12]**

> *(a)* Discuss in detail the **preformulation considerations for hard gelatin capsule dosage form** with emphasis on drug–excipient compatibility and hygroscopicity. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> *(b)* A new drug has a single dose of **400 mg**, with $\rho_b = 0.45 \text{ g/mL}$ and $\rho_t = 0.60 \text{ g/mL}$. Calculate Carr's Index, Hausner Ratio, and recommend the appropriate capsule size. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Detailed Model Answer (7 Marks)

**1. Preformulation for Hard Gelatin Capsules — Overview**

Hard gelatin capsule (HGC) is a two-piece telescoping unit. Preformulation ensures the drug-excipient blend has:
- Adequate flow for the dosing disc.
- Compatible with the gelatin shell.
- Stable during storage at ICH conditions.

**2. Drug–Excipient Compatibility (DEC)**

- **Definition:** Study of physical, chemical, and biological interactions between drug and excipients.
- **Methods:** Binary mixtures (1:1), stressed at 40 °C / 75 % RH for 4 weeks, analyzed by DSC, TGA, FT-IR, HPLC.
- **Common incompatibilities:**
  - Lactose (reducing sugar) with primary/secondary amines → **Maillard browning**.
  - Povidone (peroxides) with oxidation-sensitive drugs.
  - Calcium carbonate (alkaline) with acid-labile drugs.
- **Mitigation:** Replace lactose with MCC; use low-peroxide excipients; add antioxidants (BHT, BHA).

**3. Hygroscopicity**

- **Definition:** Ability of a substance to absorb moisture from the environment.
- **Classes (Callahan et al.):** Non-hygroscopic to Very Hygroscopic.
- **Impact on HGC:**
  - Water migration from shell to drug → shell becomes brittle.
  - Drug hydrolysis and degradation.
  - Aldehyde formation in gelatin → **cross-linking**, slow dissolution.
- **Mitigation:**
  - Use **HPMC shell** (lower moisture content, 4–6 %).
  - Add **desiccant** (silica gel) in HDPE bottle.
  - **Alu-Alu blister** packaging.
  - Microencapsulate the hygroscopic drug.

**4. Other Critical Preformulation Parameters**

- Particle size: 60–200 µm ideal.
- Bulk/tapped density → capsule size.
- Compactibility → important for mini-tablet in capsule.
- Stability as per ICH Q1A.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Dose $m = 400 \text{ mg} = 0.400 \text{ g}$
- $\rho_b = 0.45 \text{ g/mL}$
- $\rho_t = 0.60 \text{ g/mL}$

**Step 1 — Carr's Index:**

$$\text{CI} = \dfrac{\rho_t - \rho_b}{\rho_t} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.60 - 0.45}{0.60} \times 100 = 25\,\%$$

**Step 2 — Hausner Ratio:**

$$\text{HR} = \dfrac{\rho_t}{\rho_b} = \dfrac{0.60}{0.45} = 1.33$$

**Step 3 — Flow Comment:**

- CI = 25 % → **Poor flow**.
- HR = 1.33 → **Very poor flow**.
- **Glidant mandatory**: Add **0.5 % colloidal silicon dioxide**; re-evaluate to CI ≤ 18 %.

**Step 4 — Capsule Size Calculation:**

$$V_c = \dfrac{m}{\rho_t} = \dfrac{0.400 \text{ g}}{0.60 \text{ g/mL}} = 0.667 \text{ mL}$$

With 15 % safety margin:
$$V_{\text{safe}} = 0.667 \times 1.15 = 0.767 \text{ mL}$$

**Selected Capsule Size:** Size 00 (0.95 mL capacity) — adequate safety margin.

**Final Recommendation:** **Size 00 HPMC capsule**, fill with 400 mg drug + MCC (q.s. to 0.95 mL) + 0.5 % colloidal SiO₂ + 0.5 % Mg stearate; pack in HDPE bottle with silica gel desiccant.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q11]**

> *(a)* Explain the **importance of bulk density, tapped density, and angle of repose** in preformulation of capsule dosage form with formulas. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> *(b)* Discuss **compatibility of drugs with capsule shells** and methods to overcome shell-related stability issues. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Detailed Model Answer (7 Marks)

**1. Bulk Density ($\rho_b$)**

- **Definition:** Mass of powder divided by the apparent volume including inter-particulate voids.
- **Formula:** $\rho_b = m / V_b$
- **Importance:** Determines the **loose-packed volume** in the capsule body. Critical for **dosing disc settings** on capsule-filling machines.

**2. Tapped Density ($\rho_t$)**

- **Definition:** Mass divided by volume after controlled tapping (USP 616) until volume change ≤ 2 %.
- **Formula:** $\rho_t = m / V_t$
- **Importance:** Reflects **packed state** of powder inside capsule. Used to calculate the **required capsule size** via $V_c = m/\rho_t$.

**3. Derived Indices**

- **Carr's Index:** $\text{CI} = (\rho_t - \rho_b)/\rho_t \times 100$ → flow quality.
- **Hausner Ratio:** $\text{HR} = \rho_t/\rho_b$ → packing efficiency.

**4. Angle of Repose ($\theta$)**

- **Definition:** Maximum angle of a stable powder heap relative to horizontal plane.
- **Formula:** $\tan(\theta) = h/r$ (h = height, r = radius of heap base).
- **Importance:** Independent measure of flowability using **fixed-funnel or rotating-cylinder methods**.

| $\theta$ (degrees) | Flow |
|---|---|
| 25–30 | Excellent |
| 31–35 | Good |
| 36–40 | Fair-Passable |
| 41–45 | Poor |
| >45 | Very Poor |

**5. Engineering Significance**

These three properties (the **"Flow Triad"**) together predict capsule-filling performance, hopper design, and weight uniformity compliance per USP <905>.

---

#### Part (b) — Detailed Model Answer (7 Marks)

**1. Drug–Shell Compatibility — Problem Statement**

The capsule shell (gelatin or HPMC) is not inert. Drugs and fill materials can interact with the shell, leading to:
- **Shell brittleness** (loss of water to hygroscopic fill).
- **Shell softening** (alkaline drugs absorbing water from shell).
- **Maillard cross-linking** (aldehydes from gelatin + amine drugs → slow dissolution).
- **Eutectic liquefaction** (low-melting drugs dissolving shell).

**2. Common Interactions & Examples**

| Drug Character | Interaction | Example Drug |
|---|---|---|
| Hygroscopic | Water migration; shell brittleness | Ranitidine HCl |
| Alkaline | Shell softening, brittle | Sodium salts |
| Eutectic-forming | Shell dissolution at low T | Eugenol, menthol |
| Carbonyl-reactive | Cross-linking, slow dissolution | Amlodipine, some amines |
| High moisture | Sorption; drug hydrolysis | Effervescent ingredients |

**3. Methods to Overcome Shell-Related Issues**

1. **Use HPMC shells** — moisture content 4–6 % (vs. 12–15 % gelatin), robust against low-water-fill drugs.
2. **Use Alu-Alu blister packaging** — moisture barrier, oxygen barrier.
3. **Add desiccants** in HDPE bottle packs (silica gel, molecular sieves).
4. **Microencapsulate the drug** — prevents direct contact with shell.
5. **Use anhydrous formulations** — minimises water migration.
6. **Adjust fill volume** — leave adequate headspace to reduce mechanical stress.
7. **Add drying agents in fill** — fumed silica, magnesium oxide.
8. **Use band-sealing or point-sealing** for HGC to prevent cap separation.
9. **Banded capsules** — additional gelatin band increases seal integrity.
10. **Storage conditions** — control temperature and humidity per ICH Q1A.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Emphasis:** A complete answer must cover **at least 3 interaction mechanisms** AND **at least 3 mitigation strategies**. A table-based answer scores higher marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Capsule Preformulation Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the 15 % safety margin** in capsule size selection — costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing bulk density with tapped density** for fill volume — always use $\rho_t$.
> 3. **Skipping the glidant recommendation** when CI > 18 % — costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Omitting drug–shell compatibility discussion** when the drug is hygroscopic.
> 5. **Not naming the regulatory guideline** (ICH Q1A) for stability — examiners expect this.
> 6. **Failing to write the units** (g/mL, mL, %) in numerical problems — strict penalty.
> 7. **Mixing up Carr's Index and Hausner Ratio formulas** — they look similar.
> 8. **Ignoring polymorphism** — polymorphic transitions can change capsule fill volume by up to 5 %; always include.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print-and-Use Revision Bullets for Last-Minute Exam Prep:**

- ✅ Preformulation bridges **drug discovery** and **dosage form design**.
- ✅ For capsules, the **flow triad** = Carr's Index + Hausner Ratio + Angle of Repose.
- ✅ Use **tapped density** for capsule size calculation: $V_c = m/\rho_t$.
- ✅ Apply a **15 % safety margin** for production robustness.
- ✅ Standard capsule body capacities: Size 000 = 1.37 mL → Size 5 = 0.13 mL.
- ✅ **Particle size 60–200 µm** is ideal for capsule fills.
- ✅ **Hygroscopic drugs** require HPMC shell, Alu-Alu pack, or desiccant.
- ✅ **Drug–excipient compatibility** is tested by 1:1 binary mixtures at 40 °C / 75 % RH × 4 weeks; analyzed by DSC, HPLC, FT-IR.
- ✅ **Lactose** is incompatible with primary/secondary amine drugs (Maillard reaction); use MCC as alternative.
- ✅ **Glidants** (0.1–1 % colloidal SiO₂) improve flow; **lubricants** (0.25–1 % Mg stearate) reduce die-wall friction.
- ✅ **CI > 18 %** triggers glidant addition; **CI > 25 %** requires granulation.
- ✅ **Hausner Ratio > 1.34** = very poor flow.
- ✅ **Angle of repose > 40°** = poor flow; need glidant.
- ✅ **Compaction studies** use the **Heckel equation** for plastic/brittle/elastic classification.
- ✅ **Stability studies** per **ICH Q1A(R2)** — 25 °C/60 % RH long-term, 40 °C/75 % RH accelerated.
- ✅ **Capsule shell moisture** = 12–15 % w/w (gelatin), 4–6 % w/w (HPMC).
- ✅ **EMC** = $(W_e - W_d)/W_d \times 100$.
- ✅ **Specific surface area** = $6/(\rho_p \cdot d_{vs})$.
- ✅ **Soft gelatin capsules** are made by **rotary die process**; for oily and suspension fills.
- ✅ **Hard gelatin capsules** are made by **dipping-pin process**; for powders, granules, pellets.
- ✅ A successful preformulation program reduces **scale-up failures by 60–70 %** in industrial practice.
- ✅ Always include **polymorphism, hygroscopicity, and DEC** in any capsule preformulation answer.
- ✅ Cite **ICH, USP, and QbD** guidelines wherever applicable in answers for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hard Gelatin Capsules: Formulation Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hard Gelatin Capsules: Formulation Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Gelatin Capsules (HGCs)** are solid, two-piece, cylindrical dosage forms consisting of a **body** (longer, wider piece) and a **cap** (shorter, narrower piece) manufactured primarily from **gelatin** — a heterogeneous mixture of purified protein fractions obtained by irreversible hydrolytic extraction of animal skin, bone, and connective tissue (Type A, acid-processed) or animal bones and hide (Type B, alkali-processed). The capsule shell encapsulates dry, semi-solid, or liquid **fill formulations** containing the active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) and pharmaceutical excipients.

A formulation scientist designing a hard gelatin capsule product is fundamentally responsible for converting a **drug substance** (API) into a **drug product** (capsule fill) that:
1. Delivers the correct **dose** uniformly into the gastrointestinal tract,
2. Remains **chemically and physically stable** throughout the shelf life,
3. Exhibits acceptable **powder flow**, **compactibility**, and **disintegration** characteristics, and
4. Complies with pharmacopoeial standards (IP, USP, BP).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Snap Pillbox" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Snap-Lock Container**
> Think of a hard gelatin capsule exactly like a **tiny cylindrical snap-lock plastic pillbox** (the kind used for travel pills). It has two halves — a shorter "cap" that snaps over a longer "body." The gelatin shell is **water-soluble**, **biodegradable**, and **flavourless**, which is why capsules dissolve in the stomach within minutes.

Imagine you are a chef preparing a sandwich inside a sealed container:
- The **bread slices (gelatin shell)** protect the ingredients from light, oxygen, and moisture.
- The **filling (powder, granules, pellets, or paste)** is the actual "meal" (drug).
- The **chef's recipe decisions** (particle size, diluent choice, lubricant concentration) determine whether the sandwich is palatable, easy to swallow, and well-absorbed.

Just as a sandwich chef worries about ingredient moisture, sogginess, and texture, a formulation scientist worries about **hygroscopicity**, **flowability**, **uniformity**, and **compatibility** of the capsule fill.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics in Hard Gelatin Capsules

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|-----------|------------------------|
| Shell moisture content | **12–16 % w/w** (optimum flexibility) |
| Standard capsule sizes | **000, 00, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5** (8 sizes) |
| Largest fill volume (Size 000) | **~1.37 mL** |
| Smallest fill volume (Size 5) | **~0.13 mL** |
| Shell thickness | **~0.1–0.2 mm** |
| Operating temperature for filling | **21–25 °C**; RH **30–45 %** |
| Standard tamping pin pin-bar pressure | **50–200 N** |
| Type A gelatin (isoelectric point) | **pH 7.0–9.0** |
| Type B gelatin (isoelectric point) | **pH 4.7–5.3** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The hard gelatin capsule shell is **NOT** a formulation variable; it is a **standardised pharmaceutical packaging component**. The *real* formulation science lies in optimising the **fill composition**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of a Hard Gelatin Capsule

A hard gelatin capsule is composed of two pre-formed, interlocking cylindrical shells:

1. **Cap (Smaller Diameter)** — fits over the body; outer closure.
2. **Body (Larger Diameter)** — holds the formulation fill; open end receives cap.
3. **Locking Groove** — pre-formed circumferential indentations that ensure **tamper evidence** and a secure pre-closing fit.
4. **Air Vent (Engagement Groove)** — air escape channel during cap-body engagement to prevent over-pressurisation of fill.

The formulation is filled into the **body**, and the **cap is engaged** to form a sealed unit that is then ejected from the dosing machine.

### 2.2 Critical Formulation Considerations — Structured Analytical Breakdown

The art of formulating a hard gelatin capsule fill is governed by **seven** core physicochemical and pharmaceutical principles. Each must be evaluated *before* selecting excipients and *during* process development.

#### A. Powder Flow and Rheology

- The fill formulation must flow uniformly under gravity into the **dosing disc/chamber** of the capsule-filling machine.
- **Carr's Index (Compressibility Index)** and **Hausner Ratio** are the two most critical rheological descriptors.
- Poor flow → high weight variation, poor content uniformity, machine stoppages.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does flow matter?** Capsule-filling machines (e.g., Zanasi, Bosch, CapPlus, MG2) depend on the powder forming a uniform **plug** or bed in the dosing chamber. A cohesive powder will "bridge" (arch), while an over-fluid powder will "rat-hole" (channel). Neither delivers a uniform dose.

#### B. Particle Size and Distribution

- The ideal particle size for capsule fills is **typically 60–200 mesh (75–250 µm)**.
- **Too fine (< 50 µm):** Cohesive, poor flow, electrostatic charge, can leak between cap and body.
- **Too coarse (> 500 µm):** Segregation by size, mottling, dose inaccuracy, gritty mouthfeel.
- **Polydisperse (broad distribution):** Best for content uniformity and reproducibility.

#### C. Bulk Density and Tapped Density

- The **fill weight (W)** is calculated from the desired **fill volume (V)** and the formulation's **bulk density (ρ_bulk)**.
- Knowing density allows accurate **dose** calculation: a 200 mg API dose is meaningless without knowing the **fill volume** the API alone occupies.

#### D. Hygroscopicity and Moisture Sensitivity

- Hard gelatin shells contain **12–16 % w/w water** of equilibrium moisture content.
- Hygroscopic fills can transfer moisture → shell becomes **brittle, cracked, or over-softened** (tackiness).
- **Anhydrous fills or desiccant-based formulations** are required for moisture-sensitive APIs.

#### E. Chemical Compatibility (Drug–Excipient & Drug–Shell)

- **Aldehydes** (e.g., from lactose-lubricant degradation) react with gelatin → **Maillard browning**.
- **Strong acids/alkalis** hydrolyse the gelatin protein backbone.
- **Reducing sugars + primary amines (gelatin)** → crosslinking (soluble → insoluble shell — slow disintegration).

#### F. Compactibility and Plug Formation

- For **direct powder-filling machines** (e.g., Bosch GKF, Zanasi LZ-64), the powder must form a compressible **plug** under tamping-pin pressure.
- For **dosator-type machines** (e.g., Zanasi, MG2, Macofar), the powder must **retain shape** in the dosing chamber.

#### G. Dose Uniformity and Content Uniformity (CU)

- Regulated by **USP <905> Content Uniformity** for low-dose APIs (< 25 mg or < 25 % of fill weight).
- Requires either:
  - **Trituration** (geometric dilution of API with diluent), or
  - **Granulation** (wet or dry) to ensure uniform distribution.

### 2.3 The Excipient Toolkit for Capsule Fill Formulation

| Excipient Class | Functional Purpose | Typical Examples | Typical Range (% w/w) |
|-----------------|---------------------|------------------|----------------------|
| **Diluents (Fillers)** | Bulk up the fill to a workable volume | Lactose monohydrate, Microcrystalline cellulose (MCC), Dibasic calcium phosphate, Starch, Mannitol, Sorbitol | 25–90 % |
| **Lubricants** | Reduce friction between powder and metal surfaces (dosing disc, tamping pins) | Magnesium stearate, Stearic acid, Sodium stearyl fumarate (SSF) | 0.25–2.0 % |
| **Glidants** | Improve powder flow by reducing interparticulate friction | Colloidal silicon dioxide (Aerosil®), Talc | 0.1–1.0 % |
| **Wetting Agents / Disintegrants** | Aid in capsule rupture and API release in GI tract | Sodium starch glycolate, Croscarmellose sodium, Crospovidone, Polysorbate 80 | 1.0–5.0 % |
| **Binders** (for granulation) | Provide cohesiveness in granulated fills | Hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC), Polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP), Starch paste | 2.0–10.0 % |
| **Colouring Agents** | Identification, brand recognition, stability (light protection) | FD&C dyes, Iron oxides, Titanium dioxide | 0.001–0.1 % |
| **Antimicrobial Preservatives** | Prevent microbial growth in the fill (especially for hygroscopic/herbal formulations) | Methyl paraben, Propyl paraben | 0.05–0.2 % |
| **Adsorbents** | Convert liquid/lipophilic APIs into free-flowing powder | Colloidal silicon dioxide, Magnesium oxide, Kaolin | 1.0–10.0 % |
| **Surfactants** | Improve wettability of poorly water-soluble APIs | Sodium lauryl sulphate (SLS), Polysorbate 80, Poloxamer | 0.05–2.0 % |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Meaning | Unit | Application |
|--------------------|---------|------|-------------|
| $\text{Fill Weight (W)} = V \times \rho_{\text{bulk}}$ | W = fill weight, V = capsule volume, ρ = bulk density | mg, mL, g/mL | Determines mg of formulation needed per capsule |
| $\text{Carr's Index (CI)} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{tapped}} - \rho_{\text{bulk}}}{\rho_{\text{tapped}}} \times 100$ | Compressibility index | % | Measures powder flowability |
| $\text{Hausner Ratio (HR)} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{tapped}}}{\rho_{\text{bulk}}}$ | Flow indicator | dimensionless | Tapped ÷ Bulk density |
| $\text{Angle of Repose} = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{h}{r}\right)$ | θ = angle, h = heap height, r = heap radius | degrees | Flowability measure |
| $\text{Capsule Size Volume} \approx \dfrac{\pi D^{2} L}{4}$ | V = volume, D = body diameter, L = body length | mL | Approximate fill volume per capsule |
| $\text{Dose of API per capsule} = W \times \dfrac{\% \text{API in fill}}{100}$ | W = fill weight | mg | Single-dose content calculation |
| $\text{Content Uniformity AV} = \vert M - \overline{X} \vert + ks$ | Acceptance Value (USP <905>) | dimensionless | QC release criterion |
| $\text{Bulk Density} = \dfrac{M}{V_{\text{bulk}}}$ | Mass per unsettled volume | g/mL | Powder rheology |
| $\text{Tapped Density} = \dfrac{M}{V_{\text{tapped}}}$ | Mass per tapped (settled) volume | g/mL | Powder rheology |
| $\text{Disintegration Time} \leq 15 \text{ min}$ (HGC, USP) | Pharmacopoeial limit | minutes | QC release criterion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Coding Note:** All absolute-value and dividing-bar symbols in the table above use the LaTeX `\vert` and `\dfrac` commands to prevent markdown parsing errors. **Never** use raw `|` in markdown tables.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of the Formulation Decision Matrix

In industrial pharmaceutical practice, formulation considerations directly determine:
1. **Machine selection** — dosator vs. tamping-pin vs. dosing-disc machines require different flow and compactibility profiles.
2. **Throughput** — fast-filling machines (e.g., 200,000 capsules/hour on Bosch GKF 3000) demand near-perfect flow.
3. **Cost-of-goods** — high diluent fraction, low lubricant concentration, and minimal processing (direct fill > granulation) reduce manufacturing cost.
4. **Regulatory dossier strength** — excipient selection is defended by **Excipient Selection Justification Reports (ESJRs)** in regulatory submissions (ANDA, DMF).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Carr's Index and Hausner Ratio Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A pharmaceutical scientist blends a capsule fill formulation. The bulk density of the blend is measured as **0.420 g/mL** and the tapped density (after 1,250 taps in a graduated cylinder) is **0.560 g/mL**. Determine:
(a) Carr's Compressibility Index
(b) Hausner Ratio
(c) Comment on the powder flowability.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Identify the given data.**
> $\rho_{\text{bulk}} = 0.420 \text{ g/mL}$ and $\rho_{\text{tapped}} = 0.560 \text{ g/mL}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 — Apply the Carr's Index formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CI} &= \frac{\rho_{\text{tapped}} - \rho_{\text{bulk}}}{\rho_{\text{tapped}}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.560 - 0.420}{0.560} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.140}{0.560} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 0.250 \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 25.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 — Apply the Hausner Ratio formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HR} &= \frac{\rho_{\text{tapped}}}{\rho_{\text{bulk}}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.560}{0.420} \\[4pt]
&= 1.333
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 4 — Interpretation using the standard reference table.**

| Carr's Index (%) | Flow Character | Hausner Ratio |
|------------------|----------------|---------------|
| $\leq 10$ | Excellent | 1.00–1.11 |
| 11–15 | Good | 1.12–1.18 |
| 16–20 | Fair | 1.19–1.25 |
| 21–25 | Passable | 1.26–1.34 |
| 26–31 | Poor | 1.35–1.45 |
| 32–37 | Very poor | 1.46–1.59 |
| $> 38$ | Very, very poor | $> 1.60$ |

**Inference:** CI = 25.0 % and HR = 1.333 indicate **passable flow** — borderline acceptable for direct powder filling. The formulator should add a glidant (e.g., 0.5 % colloidal silicon dioxide) to improve flowability before scale-up.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Fill Weight and Diluent Quantity Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
You are tasked with formulating 200 mg of **Drug X** per capsule in a **Size #1 hard gelatin capsule** with a fill volume of **0.50 mL**. The bulk density of the pure API is **0.320 g/mL**. The formulation uses lactose monohydrate as the diluent with a bulk density of **0.750 g/mL**. Assume additive volume blending.
Determine:
(a) Fill weight of pure API per capsule
(b) Volume occupied by the API
(c) Volume of diluent required
(d) Fill weight of diluent per capsule
(e) Total fill weight per capsule.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Fill weight of pure API per capsule (W_API).**
> Given dose = 200 mg API per capsule. Therefore, W_API = 200 mg = 0.200 g.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 — Volume occupied by the API (V_API).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{API}} &= \frac{W_{\text{API}}}{\rho_{\text{API}}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.200 \text{ g}}{0.320 \text{ g/mL}} \\[4pt]
&= 0.625 \text{ mL}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 — Determine volume available for diluent (V_diluent).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{diluent}} &= V_{\text{total}} - V_{\text{API}} \\[4pt]
&= 0.50 \text{ mL} - 0.625 \text{ mL} \\[4pt]
&= -0.125 \text{ mL}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 4 — Critical problem identification.**
> The volume of pure API **exceeds** the capsule fill volume! This means the formulation **cannot** be filled as a direct powder blend. The scientist must switch to one of the following strategies:
> 1. **Use a larger capsule** (e.g., Size #000 with 1.37 mL fill volume), OR
> 2. **Granulate the API with the diluent** to increase bulk density, OR
> 3. **Add a denser diluent** to compact the formulation, OR
> 4. **Reduce the dose** to 100 mg (re-evaluate dose–response).

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 5 — Re-design using Size #000 (V = 1.37 mL) with same formulation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{diluent}} &= 1.37 - 0.625 = 0.745 \text{ mL} \\[4pt]
W_{\text{diluent}} &= V_{\text{diluent}} \times \rho_{\text{diluent}} \\[4pt]
&= 0.745 \text{ mL} \times 0.750 \text{ g/mL} \\[4pt]
&= 0.55875 \text{ g} = 558.75 \text{ mg} \\[4pt]
W_{\text{total}} &= W_{\text{API}} + W_{\text{diluent}} = 200 + 558.75 \\[4pt]
&= 758.75 \text{ mg per capsule}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 6 — Verify final mass-balance check.**

$$
V_{\text{check}} = \frac{0.200}{0.320} + \frac{0.559}{0.750} = 0.625 + 0.745 = 1.370 \text{ mL} \quad \checkmark
$$

The mass and volume balance close perfectly. The formulation is **manufacturable** in a Size #000 capsule.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Disintegration Failure Root Cause Analysis

**Problem Statement:**
A capsule formulation passes all QC tests initially, but on **3-month accelerated stability (40 °C / 75 % RH)** the capsules fail the **disintegration time test** (DT = 28 min, limit ≤ 15 min). The formulation contains: **Paracetamol**, **Lactose monohydrate**, **Magnesium stearate (1.0 %)**, and **Croscarmellose sodium (3.0 %)**. The capsule is a standard clear bovine gelatin shell. Identify the most likely root cause and the corrective action.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — List the likely physicochemical causes of disintegration failure.**

| Cause | Mechanism | Likelihood |
|-------|-----------|-----------|
| Gelatin crosslinking | Aldehyde + amine (Maillard) | **HIGH** |
| API crystallisation | Recrystallisation plugging pores | LOW |
| Lubricant overage | Hydrophobic film on particles | MEDIUM |
| Moisture migration | Plasticisation of gelatin | LOW (DT not usually affected) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 — Apply mechanistic reasoning.**
> Paracetamol is a **primary amine-containing** drug. Lactose is a **reducing disaccharide** that, under high humidity and temperature, hydrolyses and oxidises to **5-hydroxymethylfurfural (5-HMF)**, a reactive aldehyde. The 5-HMF reacts with free **ε-amino groups of gelatin lysine residues** to form **Schiff bases**, crosslinking the gelatin shell. The crosslinked gelatin becomes **insoluble** in gastric fluid → slow disintegration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 — Confirm the hypothesis with literature evidence.**
> This is a **well-documented** incompatibility, especially in paracetamol–lactose formulations stored under accelerated stability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 4 — Define the corrective action.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Corrective Action} = \; & \text{(i) Replace lactose with non-reducing diluent} \\
& \text{(e.g., mannitol, microcrystalline cellulose, dicalcium phosphate)} \\
& \text{(ii) Use low-moisture grade lactose} \\
& \text{(iii) Add desiccant (silica gel) in HDPE bottle} \\
& \text{(iv) Add antioxidant (BHT 0.02\%) to prevent aldehyde formation}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 5 — Re-validate by re-running accelerated stability.**
> If DT ≤ 15 min at 3 months / 40 °C / 75 % RH, the corrective action is **validated**.

---

### 3.4 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — Formulation Considerations × Excipient Strategy

| Formulation Challenge | Diagnostic Test | Recommended Excipient Strategy | Common Excipients |
|-----------------------|-----------------|--------------------------------|-------------------|
| Poor flow | Carr's Index > 25 | Glidant + Spheronization | Aerosil 0.1–1 %, Spheronized granules |
| Sticking to tamping pins | Visual inspection of pins | Reduce Mg stearate blend time, use SSF | 0.25 % Mg stearate blended for 2 min |
| Dose segregation (low dose API) | Content uniformity AV > 15 | Granulation, ordered mixing | PVP-K30, MCC, Aerosil |
| Hygroscopic API | LOD, Karl Fischer | Anhydrous diluent, desiccant | DCP anhydrous, mannitol |
| Lipophilic/oily API | API state of matter | Adsorption on porous carrier | Aerosil 200, Neusilin |
| Chemical incompatibility | Stress studies (40/75) | Replace reactive excipient | Lactose → mannitol |
| Disintegration failure | DT > 15 min | Add superdisintegrant, reduce lubricant | Crospovidone 2–5 %, SSF 0.5 % |
| Moisture loss/shell cracking | Moisture content of shell | Hydrophobic barrier coating on shell | HPMC-coating on shell |
| Light-sensitive API | UV/Vis degradation | Add UV-absorber in fill + opaque shell | Titanium dioxide, iron oxide, Opaspray |
