## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define hard gelatin capsule. List any four formulation considerations that govern the development of the capsule fill.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** A hard gelatin capsule is a solid, two-piece, cylindrical dosage form consisting of a cap and a body, manufactured from gelatin, used to encapsulate solid, semi-solid, or liquid pharmaceutical formulations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formulation Considerations (4 × 0.5 mark = 2 marks):**
> 1. **Powder flow and rheology** — Carr's Index and Hausner Ratio must indicate good flow.
> 2. **Particle size and distribution** — ideal range 75–250 µm.
> 3. **Hygroscopicity and moisture sensitivity** — affects shell integrity.
> 4. **Chemical compatibility** — drug–excipient and drug–shell interactions (e.g., Maillard browning with lactose + primary amines).

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Explain the role of glidants and lubricants in hard gelatin capsule formulation. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Glidants (1.5 marks):** Improve the **flowability** of the powder blend by reducing interparticulate friction and electrostatic charge. They act on the *external* surface of particles. Example: **Colloidal silicon dioxide (Aerosil 200)** at 0.1–1.0 % w/w.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lubricants (1.5 marks):** Reduce **friction and adhesion** between the powder and the metal surfaces of the capsule-filling machine (dosing disc, tamping pins). They form a hydrophobic film on particles. Example: **Magnesium stearate** at 0.25–2.0 % w/w. Overuse retards disintegration.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### **Question A (a)** — 7 Marks
**Discuss in detail the formulation and development considerations of a hard gelatin capsule fill. Mention the role of excipients with examples.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):**
The fill formulation is the actual drug product inside the shell. The shell is standardised, so the formulation scientist's job is to design the fill to deliver uniform dose, adequate bioavailability, and acceptable stability.

**Step 2 — Physicochemical Considerations (3 marks):**
- **Particle size and distribution** — 75–250 µm ideal.
- **Bulk and tapped density** — for fill weight calculation.
- **Powder flow** — Carr's Index ≤ 25 % acceptable.
- **Hygroscopicity** — moisture interaction with gelatin shell.
- **Drug–excipient compatibility** — no Maillard reaction, no hydrolysis.
- **Compactibility** — plug formation in tamping-pin machines.
- **Dose uniformity** — granulation required for low-dose APIs.

**Step 3 — Excipient Functions with Examples (3 marks):**
Already detailed in **Table 2.3** above (Diluents, Lubricants, Glidants, Disintegrants, Binders, Colouring agents, Preservatives, Adsorbents). Each is a 0.5-mark item with example.

---

#### **Question A (b)** — 7 Marks
**Explain Carr's Compressibility Index and Hausner Ratio with their significance. A capsule blend has bulk density 0.450 g/mL and tapped density 0.620 g/mL. Calculate the indices and comment on the flowability.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Definitions (2 marks):**
> - **Carr's Compressibility Index (CI):** Measure of the propensity of a powder to be compressed. Calculated as percentage ratio of the difference between tapped and bulk density to the tapped density.
> - **Hausner Ratio (HR):** Ratio of tapped to bulk density. Both indicate powder flowability and interparticulate friction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 — Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CI} &= \frac{\rho_{\text{tapped}} - \rho_{\text{bulk}}}{\rho_{\text{tapped}}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.620 - 0.450}{0.620} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.170}{0.620} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 27.42\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HR} &= \frac{\rho_{\text{tapped}}}{\rho_{\text{bulk}}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.620}{0.450} \\[4pt]
&= 1.378
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 — Inference (2 marks):**
> CI = 27.42 % falls in the **"Poor"** category (26–31 %); HR = 1.378 also indicates **poor flow**. The formulator should:
> 1. Add a glidant (Aerosil 200, 0.5 %).
> 2. Consider granulation to improve flow.
> 3. Switch to a dosator-type filling machine that is more tolerant of cohesive powders.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **Question B (a)** — 7 Marks
**Describe the various types of excipients used in hard gelatin capsule formulation. Explain how the choice of diluent affects the formulation outcome.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Classification of Excipients (3 marks):**
- Diluents/fillers (Lactose, MCC, Mannitol, DCP)
- Lubricants (Mg stearate, Stearic acid, SSF)
- Glidants (Aerosil, Talc)
- Disintegrants (Crospovidone, Croscarmellose, SSG)
- Binders (PVP, HPMC, Starch paste)
- Colouring agents (FD&C dyes, Iron oxides)
- Preservatives (Methyl/Propyl paraben)
- Adsorbents (Aerosil, Neusilin)

**Step 2 — Effect of Diluent Choice (4 marks):**
1. **Density and fill volume:** A dense diluent (DCP, ρ = 0.8 g/mL) reduces fill volume vs. light diluents (MCC, ρ = 0.3 g/mL).
2. **Flowability:** Spherical, free-flowing diluents (e.g., spray-dried lactose) improve flow; needle-shaped MCC may retard flow.
3. **Compatibility:** Reducing sugars (lactose) cause Maillard browning with primary amine drugs (e.g., paracetamol). Non-reducing alternatives (mannitol) preferred.
4. **Disintegration and dissolution:** Soluble diluents (mannitol) accelerate disintegration; insoluble diluents (DCP) may retard.
5. **Hygroscopicity:** Anhydrous DCP protects moisture-sensitive APIs.
6. **Cost:** Lactose is cheaper than mannitol, but trade-off with stability.
7. **Regulatory status:** Excipients must be GRAS-listed or approved in pharmacopoeias.

---

#### **Question B (b)** — 7 Marks
**A capsule fill contains 50 mg of a low-dose drug, 200 mg of lactose, and 5 mg of magnesium stearate. Discuss the formulation challenges and propose a strategy to achieve content uniformity as per USP <905>.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Identify the formulation challenge (2 marks):**
> The active drug constitutes only:
> $$\frac{50}{50 + 200 + 5} \times 100 = \frac{50}{255} \times 100 = 19.6\%$$
> Although the dose is moderate, the low percentage of the drug (19.6 %) and the small absolute mass (50 mg) mean that **content uniformity is at risk** due to:
> - **Segregation** of the small drug particles from the larger lactose particles during blending and filling.
> - **Cohesive clustering** of fine drug particles via electrostatic and van der Waals forces.
> - **Sample bias** in QC sampling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 — Calculate the Acceptance Value (AV) reference (1 mark):**
> USP <905> AV formula:
> $$AV = \vert M - \overline{X} \vert + ks$$
> where $M$ = reference value (98.5 if 2-stage testing), $\overline{X}$ = sample mean, $k$ = acceptability constant (2.4 for n = 10), $s$ = sample standard deviation. AV must be $\leq 15.0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 — Propose a content uniformity strategy (4 marks):**
> 1. **Geometric Dilution (Trituration):** Mix the drug with a small portion of the lactose first, then progressively add the remaining lactose in geometric proportions (1:1, 1:3, 1:9, 1:27). This ensures uniform distribution.
> 2. **Wet Granulation:** Dissolve the drug in a binder solution (PVP-K30 5 %) and granulate with lactose. This binds the drug uniformly onto the diluent particle surface.
> 3. **Ordered Mixing:** Pre-mix fine drug with coarser carrier particles — drug adheres to carrier surface via van der Waals forces.
> 4. **Reduce Particle Size of API:** Micronisation to < 10 µm increases the number of particles per capsule and reduces sampling bias.
> 5. **Use of High-Shear Blender:** Triggers more uniform dispersion than simple V-blender.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 4 — Final QC verification (1 mark):**
> Run USP <905> CU test on 30 units, calculate AV. If AV ≤ 15.0, the formulation passes.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in Hard Gelatin Capsule Formulation Questions (Cost: 1–4 marks each):**
>
> 1. **Confusing glidants with lubricants.** Glidants work on **interparticulate friction**; lubricants work on **particle-to-metal friction**. Swapping them in an answer is a **1-mark** deduction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting capsule shell–fill interactions.** Many students answer only the **excipient** half of the question and ignore the **gelatin shell** compatibility (e.g., Maillard browning, aldehyde crosslinking). This costs **2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Using wrong units in fill-weight calculation.** Bulk density in **g/mL** must be used with fill weight in **grams**, not milligrams. Mixing units costs **0.5–1 mark**.
>
> 4. **Not stating the pharmacopoeial limit.** A disintegration time of "12 minutes" without saying "≤ 15 min (USP)" loses the **regulatory context** mark.
>
> 5. **Mislabelling the Carr's Index formula.** Using bulk density in the numerator instead of tapped density is a **fatal formula error** worth **2 marks**.
>
> 6. **Skipping the "Why" explanation.** Simply listing excipients without explaining **why** a particular diluent or lubricant is chosen loses the **application-context** marks.
>
> 7. **Ignoring content uniformity for low-dose drugs.** Any API < 25 mg or < 25 % of fill weight **must** trigger a content uniformity discussion. Missing this loses **2 marks**.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Gelatin Capsule — Formulation Considerations — KTU Rapid-Revision Bullets:**

- **Hard gelatin capsule = two-piece preformed shell (cap + body) made of gelatin (Type A from acid-processed pigskin, Type B from alkali-processed bovine bone).**
- **Shell is NOT a formulation variable; the FILL is the formulation variable.**
- **Standard capsule sizes:** 000, 00, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 (largest to smallest). Size 000 = ~1.37 mL, Size 5 = ~0.13 mL.
- **Filling conditions:** 21–25 °C, 30–45 % RH to prevent shell embrittlement or tackiness.
- **Filling machine types:** Tamping-pin (Bosch GKF), Dosator (Zanasi, MG2, Macofar), Dosing-disc (Hofliger-Karg, IMA).
- **Powder flow is the most critical formulation parameter.** Use Carr's Index (≤ 25 %) and Hausner Ratio (≤ 1.34).
- **Particle size sweet spot:** 75–250 µm. Too fine = cohesive; too coarse = segregation.
- **Seven formulation considerations:** powder flow, particle size, density, hygroscopicity, compatibility, compactibility, content uniformity.
- **Diluents** (25–90 %): Lactose, MCC, Mannitol, DCP. **Lactose + primary amine APIs** = Maillard browning → use mannitol instead.
- **Lubricants** (0.25–2.0 %): Mg stearate, Stearic acid, SSF. Overuse retards disintegration.
- **Glidants** (0.1–1.0 %): Colloidal silicon dioxide (Aerosil 200), Talc.
- **Disintegrants** (1–5 %): Crospovidone, Croscarmellose sodium, SSG.
- **Adsorbents** for oily APIs: Colloidal silica, Neusilin, Kaolin.
- **Fill weight formula:** $W = V \times \rho_{\text{bulk}}$.
- **Carr's Index:** $CI = \frac{\rho_{\text{tapped}} - \rho_{\text{bulk}}}{\rho_{\text{tapped}}} \times 100$.
- **Hausner Ratio:** $HR = \frac{\rho_{\text{tapped}}}{\rho_{\text{bulk}}}$.
- **Content Uniformity USP <905>:** AV $\leq 15.0$ for n = 30 units.
- **Disintegration Time limit:** $\leq 15$ minutes (USP) for hard gelatin capsules.
- **Crosslinking of gelatin** is caused by aldehydes + primary amines (Maillard); fix by replacing lactose with mannitol.
- **Hygroscopic fills** desiccate the shell → use desiccants, anhydrous diluents, and HDPE bottles.
- **Geometric dilution (trituration)** is the classic technique for low-dose content uniformity.
- **For low-dose drugs** (< 25 mg or < 25 %): use wet granulation or ordered mixing.
- **Type A gelatin IP** = 7.0–9.0; **Type B gelatin IP** = 4.7–5.3.
- **Capsule size selection** is driven by **fill volume**, which is computed from dose and bulk density.
- **Always justify excipient choice** in a regulatory dossier with an Excipient Selection Justification Report (ESJR).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capsule Manufacture Equipments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Capsule Manufacture Equipments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Capsule Manufacture Equipments

**Capsule Manufacture Equipments** refer to the integrated, sequential set of pharmaceutical machineries used to convert empty hard gelatin/vegetable capsule shells (or to manufacture the shells themselves) into finished, filled, sealed, polished, and inspected unit-dose solid oral dosage forms. The complete manufacturing train is governed by Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP) and the United States Pharmacopoeia (USP) / Indian Pharmacopoeia (IP) standards for content uniformity and disintegration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Memorize this for 8–10 mark questions):**
> "Capsule manufacture equipments are a series of mechanized or semi-mechanized pharmaceutical machines that perform the sequential unit operations of empty capsule sorting, body-cap separation, powder/formulation dosing, body-cap rejoining (closing), sealing/banding, dedusting, polishing, weight sorting, and visual inspection to produce finished hard or soft gelatin capsules of defined fill weight and drug content."

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Capsule is an Edible Two-Piece Pillbox"

Imagine you are a **micro-chef** who must package exactly **200 mg of spice** into a tiny, edible, two-piece cylindrical "pillbox":

- The **empty shell** = a pair of telescoping, prefab sugar tubes (cap + body)
- The **filling machine** = a robotic arm that picks up the body, scoops the powder, replaces the cap, and presses it shut
- The **polisher** = a rotating soft-brush tumble dryer that wipes the outside
- The **weight sorter** = a quality-control scale that rejects under/over-filled boxes
- The **sealing machine** = an extra glue/melt band that locks the two halves so they don't separate in transit

Just as a Tetris piece is precisely fitted into its slot, the **dosator** of a capsule filler packs an exact volume of powder into the shell — that is the essence of capsule manufacture.

## 1.3 Classification of Capsule Manufacture Equipments

| S. No | Equipment Category | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Capsule Shell Making Machine** (Rotary Die) | Manufactures empty two-piece hard gelatin/veg shells |
| 2 | **Empty Capsule Sorter** | Rejects defective empty shells (bent, stuck, discolored) |
| 3 | **Capsule Filling Machine** (Hand/Semi/Automatic) | Doses the formulation into the body |
| 4 | **Capsule Sealing/Banding Machine** | Locks cap onto body (banding, Quali-Seal, LEMS) |
| 5 | **Capsule Polisher / Deduster** | Removes external powder, polishes surface |
| 6 | **Capsule Weight Sorter / Checker** | 100 % / statistical weight verification |
| 7 | **Metal Detector & Visual Inspector** | Final QA step |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Production Metric for KTU:**
> - **Empty shell size range:** Size 000 (largest, ~1.37 mL) → Size 5 (smallest, ~0.13 mL) — **8 standard sizes**.
> - **Hard gelatin shell composition:** Gelatin (≈ 85 %), water (≈ 13–15 %), colorants/opacifiers (≈ 1–2 %).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Equipment-by-Equipment Analysis

### 2.1.1 Empty Capsule Shell Manufacturing — The Rotary Die Process

The **Rotary Die process** (invented by Robert P. Scherer, 1933) continuously manufactures empty hard gelatin capsules.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Two colored gelatin ribbons are cast on cooled rotating **drums** from a hot molten gelatin reservoir.
2. Lubricant (mineral oil) is applied onto the **pin bars**.
3. The **pin bars** (dipped in cold lubricant) plunge into the gelatin ribbons, forming cap and body halves.
4. The two ribbons are joined around the pins via **die rolls**.
5. The pins are withdrawn, and the formed capsules are stripped off, trimmed, and **joined together** (cap over body).
6. Final capsules are dried (tunnel dryer) to a moisture content of **12–15 % w/w**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Drying Specification (Standard):** Final moisture in finished empty shell = **12–15 % w/w** (below 10 % makes them brittle; above 18 % makes them soft/limp).

### 2.1.2 Capsule Filling Machines — The Core Equipment

Capsule fillers are classified by the **dosing principle** and **degree of automation**:

**(A) Hand-Operated Capsule Filling Machine (e.g., "Cap-Mate", 100–300 capsules/hour)**
- Operator manually: opens shell, fills powder with a powder tray, joins cap.
- Used in pharmacy compounding and small-scale R&D.
- Components: Powder tray (with perforations matching shell size), cap & body holders, tamper pin, plastic top.

**(B) Semi-Automatic Capsule Fillers (~ 5,000–25,000 caps/hr)**
- Brand examples: **Zanasi LZ-64, Bonapace, Macofar C 40, Parke-Davis type.**
- Steps: Empty shells loaded → vacuum separation of cap and body → dosator fills body → cap rejoined → ejection.
- **Dosing principle:** Dosator (piston-tamp) or Auger fill.

**(C) Fully Automatic Capsule Fillers (~ 30,000–300,000 caps/hr)**
- Brand examples: **Bosch GKF series, IMA Zanasi, MG2, Harro Höfliger, ACG Pam.**
- Single machine integrates: empty sorting → separation → dosing → closing → ejection.
- Dosing principles:
  - **Dosator system (piston-tamp):** Powder is sucked into a dosing chamber by vacuum, compacted by a tamper, then ejected into the body. Best for **cohesive powders and pellets.**
  - **Auger-dosing system:** Powder is fed by a rotating auger screw. Best for **free-flowing powders** (e.g., paracetamol, ibuprofen).
  - **Vacuum dosing:** Powder drawn into body by negative pressure.
  - **Tamping pin (tamping finger):** Powder is repeatedly tamped (compressed) inside the body in multiple stations. Best for **low-dose potent drugs** (e.g., digoxin, levothyroxine).

### 2.1.3 Capsule Sealing (Banding) Equipment

Modern capsules require **sealing** to prevent cap separation during blistering and transport. Three industrial methods:

| Method | Mechanism | Typical Reagent |
|---|---|---|
| **Banding** | Heat-seal polymer band over the cap–body joint | Hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC) solution |
| **LEMS** (Liquid Encapsulation Microspray Sealing) | Sprays band of solvent-gelatin mix at the joint | Gelatin + ethanol + water |
| **Quali-Seal** | Two-stage hydroxypropylcellulose (HPC) seal applied by a rotating disc | HPC + ethanol |
| **Snap-fit / Thermal weld** | Ultrasonic / inductive heating of polymer ring | Polyethylene glycol (PEG) ring |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question (8 marks):** "Explain the Quali-Seal / banding process of capsule sealing with a neat diagram." — Must mention **HPC, ethanol solvent, rotating sealing disc, drying tunnel.**

### 2.1.4 Capsule Polishing & Dedusting

- **Pan Polisher:** Stainless steel rotating octagonal pan lined with cotton/jute cloth, with internal baffles. Powder on the outer shell is wiped off.
- **Brush Polisher:** Vertical rotating brush with vacuum suction to remove dust.
- **Deduster with metal check:** Vibrating screen + magnetic bar.

### 2.1.5 Capsule Weight Sorting

- **Electromechanical weight sorter:** Each capsule is weighed individually on a load cell; an air-jet nozzle ejects "out-of-spec" capsules.
- **Throughput:** Up to **200,000 caps/hr.**
- **Acceptance limits:** Typically ±5–10 % of average fill weight per pharmacopoeia (USP <905>).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** For KTU numerical problems, **commit these five formulas** to memory. Marks are awarded for the *correct formula, substitution, and unit consistency.*

| # | Parameter | Formula | Standard Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Capsule body cylindrical volume** (approximate) | $V_b = \pi r^2 h$ | mL or cm³ | $r$ = body radius, $h$ = body height |
| 2 | **Fill weight per capsule** | $W_{fill} = V_b \times \rho_{bulk}$ | mg | $\rho_{bulk}$ = bulk (loose) density of powder |
| 3 | **Tapped (compacted) density** | $\rho_{tapped} = m / V_{tapped}$ | g/mL | Used when tamp dosing is used |
| 4 | **Carr's Compressibility Index** | $CI = 100 \times \dfrac{\rho_{tapped} - \rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{tapped}}$ | % | $CI \le 15\%$ → good flow |
| 5 | **Machine production rate** | $P = n \times d \times 60$ | caps/hr | $n$ = number of filling stations; $d$ = RPM of turntable |
| 6 | **Hard shell approximate capacity** | $V_{cap} \approx 0.137 \times \text{size number}$ (approx) | mL | Ref: Remington/Pharmaceutics |
| 7 | **Total batch output** | $B = P \times T$ | capsules | $T$ = total run time in hours |
| 8 | **Yield (% w/w basis)** | $\%\ Yield = \dfrac{W_{actual}}{W_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | % | Compare to theoretical fill weight |
| 9 | **Hard shell moisture spec** | $MC = 12\text{–}15 \%\ w/w$ | % w/w | In finished empty shell |
| 10 | **Hopper capacity (approx cylinder)** | $V_{H} = \pi R^2 H$ | cm³ | Used to size raw material hoppers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL PIPING-FREE FORMATTING RULE:** All vertical bars in formulas above are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent KTU Markdown table breakage — **never use raw `\|`.**

## 2.3 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Auger fillers** dominate high-volume NSAID capsules (e.g., ibuprofen 200 mg, paracetamol 500 mg).
- **Tamping-pin fillers** are the industry standard for **low-dose potent drugs** (e.g., hormone capsules, anti-cancer micro-doses) because they offer superior **content uniformity** at ≤ 1 mg dose.
- **LEMS / Quali-Seal** are essential for capsules entering **tropical/humid markets** (India, Africa, SE Asia) where cap separation is a chronic complaint.
- **Weight sorters** ensure compliance with **USP <905> Uniformity of Dosage Units**, a regulatory non-negotiable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Production Rate of a Rotary Automatic Capsule Filling Machine

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical Numerical):**
> An automatic capsule filling machine has **6 filling stations** and runs at a turntable speed of **40 rpm**. Calculate the hourly production in capsules. If the operation runs for **8 hours with 95 % efficiency**, find the total capsules produced.

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.**
- $n$ = number of filling stations = **6**
- $d$ = die/turntable rotational speed = **40 rpm**
- $T$ = total run time = **8 hours**
- $\eta$ = machine efficiency = **95 %** = **0.95**

**Step 2 — Apply the base production formula.**

$$
P_{theoretical} = n \times d \times 60
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$
P_{theoretical} = 6 \times 40 \times 60
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the arithmetic.**

$$
P_{theoretical} = 14{,}400 \ \text{capsules per hour}
$$

**Step 5 — Account for machine efficiency.**

$$
P_{actual} = P_{theoretical} \times \eta = 14{,}400 \times 0.95
$$

$$
P_{actual} = 13{,}680 \ \text{capsules per hour}
$$

**Step 6 — Total batch output for 8-hour run.**

$$
B = P_{actual} \times T
$$

$$
B = 13{,}680 \times 8
$$

$$
B = 109{,}440 \ \text{capsules}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Theoretical = **14,400 caps/hr**; Actual = **13,680 caps/hr**; Total in 8 hr shift = **1,09,440 capsules.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fill Weight from Bulk Density

**Problem Statement:**
> A size "1" hard gelatin capsule has a body volume of **0.50 mL**. A paracetamol formulation has a bulk (loose) density of **0.45 g/mL**. Calculate the fill weight per capsule in **mg**. If the batch uses **1.5 kg** of powder, how many capsules can be filled (assuming 2 % loss during filling)?

**Step 1 — Fill weight per capsule from volume × density.**

$$
W_{fill} = V_{body} \times \rho_{bulk}
$$

$$
W_{fill} = 0.50 \ \text{mL} \times 0.45 \ \text{g/mL} = 0.225 \ \text{g} = 225 \ \text{mg}
$$

**Step 2 — Total available powder with 2 % loss accounted.**

$$
W_{effective} = W_{total} \times (1 - L)
$$

$$
W_{effective} = 1500 \ \text{g} \times 0.98 = 1470 \ \text{g}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of capsules.**

$$
N = \dfrac{W_{effective}}{W_{fill}}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{1470 \ \text{g}}{0.225 \ \text{g/cap}} = 6533.33
$$

$$
N \approx 6533 \ \text{capsules}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Fill weight = **225 mg/capsule**; Capsules possible = **6,533 caps** (round down — never round up for production).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Carr's Compressibility Index — Pre-Equipment Selection

**Problem Statement:**
> A powder has a loose bulk density of **0.32 g/mL** and a tapped density of **0.41 g/mL**. Compute Carr's Compressibility Index and recommend the most suitable dosing system.

**Step 1 — Apply the Carr formula.**

$$
CI = 100 \times \dfrac{\rho_{tapped} - \rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{tapped}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
CI = 100 \times \dfrac{0.41 - 0.32}{0.41}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$
CI = 100 \times \dfrac{0.09}{0.41} = 21.95\ \%
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**

$$
CI \approx 22\ \% \rightarrow \text{Fair-to-passable flow}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $CI \approx 22\ \%$. **Recommendation:** **Tamping-pin (or Dosator) dosing** is preferred over auger because the powder has moderate flow problems that auger dosing cannot handle uniformly.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Equipment vs. Powder Property (Mapping Tool)

This is a **high-yield KTU "match-the-following" + short note** tool.

| Powder Property | Auger Filler | Tamping Pin / Dosator | Vacuum Filler | Hand-Operated |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Free-flowing granules** | ✅ Best | ✅ Acceptable | ✅ Good | ❌ Wastage |
| **Cohesive fine powder** | ❌ Bridges | ✅ Best | ❌ Powder blocks | ✅ Lab scale |
| **Low-dose (≤ 1 mg) potent** | ❌ Poor content uniformity | ✅ Best | ❌ Variable | ✅ Lab scale |
| **Pellets / beads** | ❌ Crushes | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Variable | ❌ Not ideal |
| **Sticky / waxy powders** | ❌ Builds up | ⚠ Needs lubricant | ❌ Blocks | ✅ Manual |
| **High output (> 100,000 caps/hr)** | ✅ | ✅ (multi-station) | ✅ | ❌ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Capsule Manufacture Line

The diagram below is a **block-level process flow** showing the sequential integration of capsule manufacturing equipments on a typical production line.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeRM[Raw Materials Gelatin and APIs]
    node1[Rotary Die Machine Empty Shell Maker]
    node2[Empty Capsule Sorter Weight and Defect]
    node3[Automatic Capsule Filler Dosator or Tamping]
    node4[Sealing Banding Machine Quali-Seal or LEMS]
    node5[Polishing and Deduster Pan and Brush]
    node6[Weight Sorter USP 905 Verification]
    node7[Metal Detector and Visual Inspector]
    nodeFP[Finished Packaged Capsules]

    nodeRM --> node1
    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> nodeFP

    subgraph QC[Quality Control Loop]
        node6
        node7
    end
```

## 4.2 Schematic of the Dosator (Piston-Tamp) Dosing System

This is a **mechanistic block schematic** of the most-examined dosing system in KTU.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Hopper Powder Reservoir]
    nodeB[Bed of Powder in Dosing Chamber]
    nodeC[Tamper Pin Moves Downward Compact Powder]
    nodeD[Vacuum Draws Powder into Chamber]
    nodeE[Dosator Piston Pushes Compacted Powder]
    nodeF[Empty Capsule Body is Stationary Below]
    nodeG[Powder Ejected into Body]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "draw/describe the dosator system," show the **5 sequential steps**: (1) powder bed formation, (2) tamper pin compression, (3) vacuum pull-down, (4) dosator piston descend, (5) ejection into the body.

## 4.3 Schematic of the Rotary Die Process for Shell Manufacture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Gelatin Melt Tank 60 to 70 degree C]
    node2[Cooled Casting Drums Form Two Ribbons]
    node3[Lubricant Bath and Pin Bars Dip]
    node4[Pin Bars Plunge into Gelatin Ribbons]
    node5[Die Rolls Join Ribbons Around Pins]
    node6[Pins Withdraw Capsules Stripped Off]
    node7[Tunnel Dryer 12 to 15 percent Moisture]
    node8[Trimming and Joining Empty Shell Output]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
```

## 4.4 Block Schematic of the Quali-Seal Banding Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Empty Capsule Feed]
    nodeB[First Spray Disc HPC in Ethanol]
    nodeC[Drying Tunnel 1 Solvent Evaporation]
    nodeD[Second Spray Disc Sealing Polymer]
    nodeE[Drying Tunnel 2 Final Band Cure]
    nodeF[Sealed Capsule Discharge to Polisher]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** For full marks on a 7-mark diagram question, **always label the reagents, temperatures, and time durations** on your diagram. The 7-mark breakdown usually is: **Diagram (3 marks) + Labels (2 marks) + Explanation (2 marks).**
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between hard gelatin capsules (HGC) and soft gelatin capsules (SGC) in terms of shell composition and manufacturing equipment.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Hard Gelatin Capsule (HGC) | Soft Gelatin Capsule (SGC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Shell composition** | Gelatin + water + plasticizer (glycerin/sorbitol) | Gelatin + plasticizer (glycerin/sorbitol) + water (more plasticizer) |
| **Structure** | Two preformed pieces (cap + body) | Single-piece, hermetically sealed |
| **Manufacturing** | Rotary die process; then filled by dosator/auger | Rotary die encapsulation (RDE) — formed and filled **simultaneously** |
| **Equipment example** | Bosch GKF, IMA Zanasi, Harro Höfliger | Rommelag (Bottelpack), Capsugel, Technophar |

> **Word target:** ≈ 80–100 words for full 3 marks.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is the role of a tamping pin in a capsule filling machine? Name the machine where it is dominantly used.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **tamping pin** (also called *tamping finger* or *tamp piston*) is a vertically moving pin that **compresses the powder bed inside the empty capsule body** at multiple stations along the turntable. It produces a **compacted powder plug** inside the body, which improves **content uniformity** and **fill weight precision**, especially for **low-dose potent drugs** and **cohesive powders**.
It is dominantly used in the **Zanasi-type / Macofar-type / Bosch GKF tamping-pin automatic capsule filling machine**. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 2]`

**Q (a) [7 marks]: Describe with a neat diagram the construction and working of an automatic capsule filling machine based on the dosator (piston-tamp) system.**

**Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
The dosator-type automatic capsule filling machine (e.g., **Zanasi, Bonapace**) consists of the following main parts:
1. **Empty capsule hopper** with orienting mechanism.
2. **Cap and body separator** (vacuum slits pull caps up, bodies remain).
3. **Dosing station with a dosator** (consists of a dosing chamber, piston, and tamper pin).
4. **Powder hopper** above the dosator.
5. **Cap replacement station** (vacuum released, caps rejoin bodies).
6. **Ejection chute** with discharge station.
7. **PLC/HMI control panel** for RPM, vacuum, and stroke length.

**Working (4 marks):**
1. Empty shells are fed from the hopper and oriented cap-up.
2. The cap is lifted by **vacuum slits**, leaving the body in the lower holder.
3. The dosator descends into the **powder bed** in the powder hopper; the **piston retracts** to create a vacuum that **draws powder into the dosing chamber.**
4. The **tamper pin** compresses the powder plug (single or multiple strokes).
5. The dosator moves over the open body, and the **piston pushes** the compacted powder plug down into the capsule body.
6. The cap is replaced over the body, the body-cap joint is closed, and the finished capsule is ejected.

> **Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.2 (Dosator schematic)** above.

---

**Q (b) [7 marks]: Discuss the various methods of capsule sealing/banding with their relative advantages and limitations.**

**Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Methods of Capsule Sealing (5 marks):**

1. **Banding (Thermal/Polymer Banding):** A thin band of HPMC or gelatin is applied circumferentially at the cap-body joint and dried, forming a permanent seal.
2. **LEMS (Liquid Encapsulation Micro-Spray Sealing):** A micro-sprayer deposits a fine band of gelatin-ethanol-water at the cap-body junction; the solvent evaporates, leaving a tight seal.
3. **Quali-Seal Process:** A two-stage spray of hydroxypropylcellulose (HPC) in ethanol using a rotating disc applicator; suitable for **tropical climates** and **blister packaging** transport.
4. **Snap-Fit / Mechanical Lock:** Some shells are designed with a snap-fit groove; no sealing agent needed.
5. **Ultrasonic / Induction Sealing:** A PEG or polymer ring is melted locally by ultrasonic vibration / induction heating; mostly for high-speed lines.

**Advantages vs Limitations (2 marks):**

| Method | Advantage | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| Banding | Permanent, regulatory approved | Slow, needs drying tunnel |
| LEMS | Fast (1,80,000 caps/hr) | Flammable ethanol solvent |
| Quali-Seal | Excellent for tropical markets | Two-stage = higher cost |
| Snap-Fit | No reagent | Only on specific shell design |

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 2]`

**Q (a) [7 marks]: Explain the Rotary Die process for the manufacture of hard gelatin capsule shells with a flow diagram.**

**Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Principle (2 marks):**
The **Rotary Die process**, patented by Robert P. Scherer (1933), continuously produces empty hard gelatin capsule shells. Two thin, lubricated gelatin ribbons are cast simultaneously and then formed, joined, and dried in a single integrated line.

**Construction (2 marks):**
The machine has:
- **Two heated gelatin reservoirs** with metering rolls
- **Two cooled casting drums** (chilled internally)
- **Two sets of lubricated pin bars**
- **Two die rolls** (counter-rotating)
- **A stripping station**
- **A multi-stage tunnel dryer**

**Working Steps (3 marks):**
1. Molten gelatin is cast as a thin film on each **cooled rotating drum**, forming a flexible ribbon.
2. **Lubricant** (mineral oil) is applied to the **pin bars**.
3. The **pin bars dip into the gelatin ribbons**, forming cap and body halves.
4. The two ribbons are brought together by **die rolls** that pinch around the pins, sealing the cap and body as one telescoping unit.
5. The pins are withdrawn; the **formed capsules are stripped** from the pins.
6. The capsules pass through a **tunnel dryer** where the moisture is reduced to **12–15 % w/w**.
7. The empty shells (cap engaged with body) are discharged, polished, and packed for the filling line.

> **Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.3** above.

---

**Q (b) [7 marks]: A capsule filling machine has 8 stations and runs at 30 rpm. The bulk density of a powder is 0.5 g/mL, and the body volume of the size "0" shell is 0.68 mL. Calculate (i) the fill weight per capsule, (ii) the theoretical production per hour, and (iii) the number of capsules that can be filled from 5 kg of powder assuming 3 % process loss.**

**Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Fill weight per capsule (2 marks):**

$$
W_{fill} = V_{body} \times \rho_{bulk}
$$

$$
W_{fill} = 0.68 \ \text{mL} \times 0.5 \ \text{g/mL} = 0.34 \ \text{g} = 340 \ \text{mg}
$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical hourly production (2 marks):**

$$
P = n \times d \times 60
$$

$$
P = 8 \times 30 \times 60 = 14{,}400 \ \text{capsules/hour}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of capsules from 5 kg with 3 % loss (3 marks):**

$$
W_{effective} = 5000 \ \text{g} \times (1 - 0.03) = 5000 \times 0.97 = 4850 \ \text{g}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{W_{effective}}{W_{fill}} = \dfrac{4850}{0.34} = 14{,}264.7 \approx 14{,}264 \ \text{capsules}
$$

> **Final Answers:** (i) **340 mg/capsule**; (ii) **14,400 caps/hr**; (iii) **14,264 capsules**.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Traps (lose 1–2 marks each if you commit these):**
>
> 1. **Confusing dosator with tamping pin:** Dosator = dosing piston filling **directly** into the body; Tamping pin = compresses powder **inside the body** (multiple strokes). Many students write the wrong working principle — full 7 marks may be reduced to 4.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numericals:** Writing "0.34" without specifying "0.34 g = 340 mg" costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the diagram:** A 7-mark question on "describe with diagram" allocates **3 marks for the diagram**. Skipping it = max 4/7.
> 4. **Writing auger = dosator:** Auger is a **rotating screw** for free-flowing powders; dosator is a **piston-tamp** for cohesive/potent drugs. Confusing them = −2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to account for efficiency/loss:** If the question says "95 % efficiency" or "3 % loss," you **must** include the multiplication factor. Skipping it costs 1–2 marks in numericals.
> 6. **Wrong shell size volume:** Size 0 is **~0.68 mL**, Size 1 is **~0.50 mL**, Size 2 is **~0.37 mL**, Size 3 is **~0.30 mL**. Mixing these up invalidates the entire calculation.
> 7. **Not labelling temperatures/times on diagrams:** Examiner expects **60–70 °C gelatin melt, 12–15 % final moisture, 8-hour shift** wherever relevant.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 15 Things to Remember Before the KTU Exam (Memorize Each Bullet):**
>
> 1. **Capsule manufacture** = shell making → sorting → filling → sealing → polishing → weight sorting → inspection.
> 2. **Empty shell** = gelatin (85 %) + water (13–15 %) + colorants (1–2 %).
> 3. **Shell final moisture spec** = **12–15 % w/w**.
> 4. **Rotary die process** (Robert P. Scherer, 1933) → for shell making.
> 5. **Dosator system** = best for cohesive, low-dose, pellet formulations.
> 6. **Tamping pin** = compresses powder inside body → best for content uniformity of potent drugs.
> 7. **Auger system** = best for free-flowing powders (paracetamol, ibuprofen) at high speed.
> 8. **Banding / Quali-Seal / LEMS** = three main capsule sealing methods.
> 9. **Quali-Seal reagent** = **HPC (hydroxypropyl cellulose) in ethanol**; two-stage rotating disc.
> 10. **LEMS reagent** = gelatin + ethanol + water microspray.
> 11. **Production rate** formula: $P = n \times d \times 60$ (caps/hr).
> 12. **Fill weight** formula: $W_{fill} = V_{body} \times \rho_{bulk}$.
> 13. **Carr's Index** ≤ 15 % → good flow → auger suitable; > 20 % → tamping/dosator needed.
> 14. **Standard shell sizes:** 000 (largest) → 5 (smallest); **8 standard sizes** total.
> 15. **Polishing equipment:** Pan polisher (cotton/jute lined) + brush polisher (with vacuum).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Control Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Quality Control Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Quality Control (QC) Tests in Pharmaceuticals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Control (QC)** is the sum of all procedures employed in the pharmaceutical industry to ensure the identity, purity, potency, safety, and efficacy of drug products, from raw material acquisition through finished product release.

As per the **Indian Pharmacopoeia (IP)**, **United States Pharmacopoeia (USP)**, **British Pharmacopoeia (BP)**, and **International Council for Harmonisation (ICH)** guidelines, QC tests are mandatory analytical and physical evaluations performed on:
- Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients (APIs)
- Excipients
- In-process materials
- Finished dosage forms
- Packaging components

The formal regulatory framework governing these tests in India includes:
- **Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940** and **Rules, 1945**
- **Schedule T** (GMP requirements)
- **Schedule U** (QC requirements)
- **IP, USP, BP, EP** pharmacopoeial compendia

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome (CO) Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **CO2** of PECHT751 — *"Apply quality control principles and pharmacopoeial standards to evaluate pharmaceutical dosage forms."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are running a **five-star restaurant**. Before serving a dish, you must:
1. **Taste it** (potency / assay)
2. **Check the temperature** (stability / thermal tests)
3. **Ensure the plate isn't cracked** (physical integrity)
4. **Verify no harmful bacteria** (sterility / microbial tests)
5. **Confirm the right quantity is served** (uniformity of content / weight)

A pharmaceutical product is no different — except the stakes are **human life**. A tablet with 10% less active ingredient or a contaminated injection can be fatal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Control (QC) = "The Police Department"** of the pharmaceutical plant.
> **Quality Assurance (QA) = "The Constitution and Legal System"** that defines what the police enforce.
>
> QC tests are the **measurable, documentable checkpoints** that enforce the QA system.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Pharmaceutical QC Tests

The complete QC testing universe is broadly classified as:

| Category | Focus | Examples |
|----------|-------|----------|
| **Physical Tests** | Mechanical/geometric properties | Hardness, friability, disintegration |
| **Chemical Tests** | Identity, purity, potency | Assay, content uniformity, pH |
| **Biological/Microbial Tests** | Safety from microbes | Sterility, pyrogen, MLT |
| **Performance Tests** | Drug release | Dissolution, drug release |
| **Stability Tests** | Shelf-life assurance | ICH accelerated/real-time |
| **Packaging Tests** | Container integrity | Leak test, seal strength |

---

## 1.4 Pharmacopoeial Standards — The "Gold Books"

Pharmacopoeias are **legally recognized official compendia** containing monographs and test methods.

| Pharmacopoeia | Region | Authority |
|---------------|--------|-----------|
| **IP** (Indian Pharmacopoeia) | India | IPC, Ghaziabad |
| **USP** (United States Pharmacopoeia) | USA | USP Convention |
| **BP** (British Pharmacopoeia) | UK | MHRA |
| **EP** (European Pharmacopoeia) | Europe | EDQM, Strasbourg |
| **JP** (Japanese Pharmacopoeia) | Japan | PMDA |
| **ChP** (Chinese Pharmacopoeia) | China | ChPC |

> [!NOTE]
> In India, **IP is the legally binding standard**. Drugs marketed in India must conform to IP standards unless exempted.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 QC Tests for Solid Oral Dosage Forms — Tablets

### 2.1.1 Weight Variation Test (IP/USP)

This test verifies **uniformity of tablet weight**, which indirectly ensures uniformity of drug content.

**Pharmacopoeial Limits (USP):**

$$\text{Acceptance Limit} = \pm 10\% \text{ (for tablets } \geq 324 \text{ mg)}$$

$$\text{Acceptance Limit} = \pm 7.5\% \text{ (for tablets } < 324 \text{ mg)}$$

**Procedure:**
1. Weigh **20 tablets individually** → record $W_1, W_2, \dots, W_{20}$
2. Calculate the **average weight** $\bar{W}$
3. Compare each tablet's deviation from $\bar{W}$

---

### 2.1.2 Content Uniformity Test (USP)

A more stringent test than weight variation — used when the **active ingredient constitutes ≤ 25 mg or < 25% of tablet weight**.

$$\text{Acceptance Value (AV)} = \vert M - \bar{X} \vert + ks$$

Where:
- $M$ = reference value (98.5 if $\bar{X} < 98.5$; else 101.5 if $\bar{X} > 101.5$; else $\bar{X}$)
- $\bar{X}$ = mean of individual contents (% of label claim)
- $k$ = acceptability constant (**2.4** for $n = 10$)
- $s$ = standard deviation

$$\boxed{AV \leq 15.0 \;\; \text{(L1 criterion passes)}}$$

---

### 2.1.3 Tablet Hardness (Crushing Strength)

The **diametrical crushing force** required to break a tablet.

- **Instrument:** Monsanto hardness tester, Pfizer tester, Strong-Cobb tester, or modern digital force gauges.
- **Units:** **kg/cm²** or **Newton (N)** (1 kg/cm² ≈ 9.81 N).
- **Typical range:** **4–10 kg/cm²** for conventional tablets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Tip:** Hardness must be balanced — too low → chipping/capping; too high → disintegration failure.

---

### 2.1.4 Friability Test (USP <1216>)

Measures **mechanical durability** of uncoated tablets during handling, coating, and packaging.

**Equipment:** **Roche Friabilator** (drum rotating at **25 rpm for 4 minutes = 100 revolutions**).

**Formula:**

$$\boxed{\% \text{ Friability (F)} = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100}$$

Where:
- $W_1$ = weight of tablets **before** the test
- $W_2$ = weight of tablets **after** the test (dedusted)

**Acceptance Criterion:** **% Friability ≤ 1.0%** (USP).

For tablets weighing **< 650 mg**, a sample equivalent to **6.5 g** is used.

---

### 2.1.5 Disintegration Test (USP <701> / IP)

Disintegration is the **breakdown of a tablet into smaller particles** — a prerequisite for dissolution.

**Equipment:** **Electrolab disintegration apparatus** with **6 glass tubes**, mesh size **#10 (1.7 mm)** at bottom, immersed in **900 mL** of specified medium at **37 ± 2 °C**.

**Standard Limits:**

| Dosage Form | Time Limit |
|-------------|------------|
| Uncoated tablets | **≤ 15 minutes** |
| Coated tablets (film) | **≤ 30 minutes** |
| Enteric-coated | **No disintegration in 0.1N HCl for 2 hours; then ≤ 60 min in pH 6.8 buffer** |
| Hard gelatin capsules | **≤ 30 minutes** |
| Soft gelatin capsules | **≤ 60 minutes** |

---

### 2.1.6 Dissolution Test (USP <711>)

Measures the **amount of drug that goes into solution** from the dosage form under controlled conditions — the **most important in-vitro performance test** for solid oral dosage forms.

**Apparatus (USP):**

| Apparatus | Name | Common Use |
|-----------|------|------------|
| **I** | Basket | Capsules, floating tablets |
| **II** | Paddle | Most tablets (default) |
| **III** | Reciprocating Cylinder | Extended-release |
| **IV** | Flow-Through Cell | Poorly soluble drugs |
| **V** | Paddle over Disk | Transdermal patches |
| **VI** | Rotating Cylinder | Transdermal patches |
| **VII** | Reciprocating Holder | Extended-release |

**Standard Conditions (Apparatus II, Paddle):**
- **Volume:** **900 mL**
- **Temperature:** **37 ± 0.5 °C**
- **Paddle speed:** **50 or 75 rpm**
- **Sampling points:** typically **10, 20, 30, 45, 60 minutes**
- **Medium:** 0.1 N HCl, pH 6.8 phosphate buffer, pH 4.5 acetate buffer, or water

**Dissolution Efficiency (DE):**

$$DE = \frac{\int_0^t y \, dt}{y_{100} \cdot t} \times 100\%$$

---

## 2.2 QC Tests for Capsules

| Test | Method | Acceptance |
|------|--------|------------|
| **Weight variation** | 20 capsules weighed individually | ±10% (capsule ≥ 300 mg) |
| **Disintegration** | As per tablets | ≤ 30 min (hard gelatin) |
| **Content uniformity** | Assay of 10 capsules individually | AV ≤ 15.0 |
| **Moisture content** | Loss on drying / Karl Fischer | As per monograph |
| **Dissolution** | Apparatus I (basket) | As per monograph |
| **Microbial limits** | Total aerobic count, yeast/mold | Per IP/USP |

---

## 2.3 QC Tests for Parenterals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Parenterals are **sterile, pyrogen-free, particulate-free** dosage forms injected into body tissues/fluids. Their QC is the **most stringent** of all dosage forms.

### 2.3.1 Sterility Test (USP <71> / IP)

Determines **absence of viable microorganisms** in the product.

**Two Methods:**
1. **Membrane Filtration** (preferred) — filter sample through **0.45 µm cellulose acetate membrane**, transfer membrane to culture media.
2. **Direct Inoculation** — sample added directly to culture media.

**Culture Media:**
- **Fluid Thioglycollate Medium (FTM)** — for **aerobic bacteria, anaerobic bacteria, fungi** at **30–35 °C** for **14 days**
- **Soybean-Casein Digest Medium (SCDM)** — for **fungi, aerobic bacteria** at **20–25 °C** for **14 days**

**Acceptance:** **No microbial growth** in either medium after 14 days.

---

### 2.3.2 Bacterial Endotoxin Test (BET) / Pyrogen Test (USP <85>)

Detects **Gram-negative bacterial endotoxins (Lipopolysaccharides, LPS)**.

**Method: Limulus Amebocyte Lysate (LAL) Test**

Three techniques:
1. **Gel-clot** (qualitative)
2. **Turbidimetric** (kinetic, quantitative)
3. **Chromogenic** (quantitative)

**Endotoxin Limit (EL) for parenterals:**

$$\boxed{EL = \frac{K}{M}}$$

Where:
- $K$ = threshold pyrogen dose = **5 EU/kg** (intravenous) or **0.2 EU/kg** (intrathecal)
- $M$ = maximum human dose per kg per hour

---

### 2.3.3 Particulate Matter Test (USP <788> / IP)

Parenteral solutions must be **"essentially free from visible particulates"** and within sub-visible limits.

**Limits (USP):**

| Container Size | ≥ 10 µm particles | ≥ 25 µm particles |
|----------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **≥ 100 mL** | ≤ 25 per mL | ≤ 3 per mL |
| **< 100 mL** | ≤ 6,000 per container | ≤ 600 per container |

**Method:** **Light Obscuration Particle Count Test** or **Microscopic Particle Count Test**.

---

### 2.3.4 Clarity Test

Solutions must be **clear** when viewed against black/white background under **2,000–3,000 lux** illumination.

---

### 2.3.5 Leak Test (Ampoules)

Ampoules are tested for **seal integrity** using:
- **Dye penetration test** (methylene blue solution)
- **Bubble emission test**
- **High-voltage leak detection** (HVLD)

---

## 2.4 QC Tests for Semisolid Preparations (Creams, Ointments, Gels)

| Test | Method | Typical Limit |
|------|--------|---------------|
| **Spreadability** | Parallel plate method | As per product |
| **Viscosity** | Brookfield viscometer | As per product |
| **pH** | Digital pH meter | 4.5 – 7.0 (typically) |
| **Drug content** | HPLC/UV assay | 90–110% |
| **Microbial limits** | TAMC, TYMC | < 100 CFU/g |
| **Preservative efficacy** | Challenge test (USP <51>) | Log reduction criteria |

---

## 2.5 QC Tests for Liquid Orals (Solutions, Suspensions, Emulsions)

| Test | Apparatus / Method | Acceptance |
|------|-------------------|------------|
| **Clarity** | Visual / turbidimeter | Clear and bright |
| **pH** | Calibrated pH meter | As per monograph |
| **Specific gravity** | Pycnometer / densitometer | Within range |
| **Viscosity** | Brookfield / Ostwald viscometer | Within range |
| **Redispersibility** (suspensions) | Shaking + sedimentation | Easily redispersed |
| **Assay** | HPLC / UV | 90 – 110% |
| **Microbial limits** | Pour plate / membrane filtration | Per monograph |

---

## 2.6 Microbiological Tests

### 2.6.1 Microbial Limit Test (MLT) — USP <61> / <62>

Determines the **Total Aerobic Microbial Count (TAMC)** and **Total Yeast & Mold Count (TYMC)** plus **absence of specified organisms** (*E. coli, Salmonella, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus, Clostridia, Bile-tolerant gram-negative bacteria*).

**Acceptance Limits (per USP):**

| Category | TAMC (CFU/g or mL) | TYMC (CFU/g or mL) |
|----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Non-aqueous oral | ≤ 10³ | ≤ 10² |
| Aqueous oral | ≤ 10² | ≤ 10¹ |
| Rectal | ≤ 10³ | ≤ 10² |
| **Topical** | ≤ 10² | ≤ 10¹ |
| **Transdermal** | ≤ 10¹ | ≤ 10¹ |
| **Inhalation** | ≤ 10¹ | ≤ 10¹ |
| **Injectables** | **Sterile** | **Sterile** |

---

### 2.6.2 Preservative Efficacy Test (PET) — USP <51>

A **challenge test** where the product is inoculated with a known count of organisms and their die-off is monitored.

$$\boxed{\text{Log Reduction} = \log_{10}(\text{Initial Count}) - \log_{10}(\text{Surviving Count})}$$

**Required log reductions at Day 14 (Category 2 — Topical):**

| Organism | Bacteria | Fungi |
|----------|----------|-------|
| Log reduction | ≥ 3.0 | ≥ 1.0 |

---

## 2.7 Stability Testing (ICH Q1A(R2))

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability** is the ability of a pharmaceutical product to retain its **chemical, physical, microbiological, and biopharmaceutical** properties within specified limits throughout its shelf-life.

### 2.7.1 Types of Stability Studies

| Study Type | Condition | Duration |
|------------|-----------|----------|
| **Long-term** | 25 °C / 60% RH ± 5% | 12 months minimum |
| **Intermediate** | 30 °C / 65% RH ± 5% | 12 months |
| **Accelerated** | 40 °C / 75% RH ± 5% | 6 months |
| **Stress / Forced** | Heat, acid, base, oxidation, photolysis | Short term |

### 2.7.2 Shelf-Life Estimation — Arrhenius Equation

$$\boxed{k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}}$$

Where:
- $k$ = degradation rate constant
- $A$ = frequency factor
- $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant (**8.314 J/mol·K**)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

For **first-order degradation:**

$$t_{90\%} = \frac{0.105}{k_{25°C}}$$

Where $t_{90\%}$ = time for 10% degradation (typical shelf-life endpoint).

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Constant |
|---|---------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | % Friability | $F = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ | Limit ≤ 1.0% |
| 2 | Acceptance Value | $AV = \vert M - \bar{X} \vert + ks$ | $k = 2.4$ |
| 3 | Endotoxin Limit | $EL = K/M$ | $K = 5$ EU/kg (IV) |
| 4 | Disintegration Temp | $T = 37 \pm 2$ °C | 900 mL medium |
| 5 | Dissolution | 900 mL, $37 \pm 0.5$ °C | Apparatus I/II |
| 6 | Arrhenius | $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ | $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K |
| 7 | Shelf-life (1st order) | $t_{90\%} = 0.105 / k$ | 10% degradation |
| 8 | Log Reduction | $\log N_0 - \log N_t$ | PET criterion |
| 9 | Sterility Temp | 30–35 °C / 20–25 °C | 14 days |
| 10 | Friabilator | 25 rpm × 4 min = 100 rev | Drum i.d. 286 mm |

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing:** QC test data feed back into **process validation** and **PAT (Process Analytical Technology)**.
- **Regulatory:** Each batch must pass all QC tests before release; failure triggers **OOS (Out-of-Specification) investigation** as per FDA guidance.
- **Industry 4.0:** Modern plants use **continuous manufacturing** with **inline NIR, Raman, and PAT sensors** replacing batch QC sampling.
- **Biopharmaceuticals:** QC includes **cell-based bioassays, ELISA, host-cell protein assays** beyond traditional chemistry tests.
- **Global commerce:** Products exported to multiple geographies must satisfy **ICH Q4** pharmacopoeial harmonization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Friability Calculation from Raw Data

A pharmaceutical company performs a friability test on a batch of paracetamol tablets.

**Given Data:**
- Initial weight of 20 tablets ($W_1$) = **12.480 g**
- Final weight of 20 tablets after 100 revolutions ($W_2$) = **12.405 g**

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation:**

The official USP equation for percent friability is:

$$\%F = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\%F = \frac{12.480 - 12.405}{12.480} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator (weight loss):**

$$\Delta W = 12.480 - 12.405 = 0.075 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{\Delta W}{W_1} = \frac{0.075}{12.480} = 0.0060096$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by 100 to convert to percentage:**

$$\boxed{\%F = 0.0060096 \times 100 = 0.601\%}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with acceptance criterion:**

USP limit is **% Friability ≤ 1.0%**. Since **0.601% < 1.0%**, the batch **PASSES** the friability test.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A friability loss of 0.6% indicates **good mechanical strength** but the tablets are still on the softer side. Aim for **0.3–0.8%** for robust tablets.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Content Uniformity Acceptance Value (AV)

A batch of 10 digoxin tablets (0.25 mg label claim) is assayed. Individual contents (% of label claim):

| Tablet | Content (%) |
|--------|-------------|
| 1 | 98.2 |
| 2 | 101.5 |
| 3 | 97.8 |
| 4 | 102.3 |
| 5 | 99.6 |
| 6 | 100.1 |
| 7 | 96.5 |
| 8 | 103.4 |
| 9 | 99.0 |
| 10 | 101.0 |

**Step 1 — Calculate the mean:**

$$\bar{X} = \frac{98.2 + 101.5 + 97.8 + 102.3 + 99.6 + 100.1 + 96.5 + 103.4 + 99.0 + 101.0}{10}$$

$$\bar{X} = \frac{999.4}{10} = 99.94\%$$

**Step 2 — Calculate deviations squared:**

| Tablet | $x_i - \bar{X}$ | $(x_i - \bar{X})^2$ |
|--------|------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | −1.74 | 3.0276 |
| 2 | +1.56 | 2.4336 |
| 3 | −2.14 | 4.5796 |
| 4 | +2.36 | 5.5696 |
| 5 | −0.34 | 0.1156 |
| 6 | +0.16 | 0.0256 |
| 7 | −3.44 | 11.8336 |
| 8 | +3.46 | 11.9716 |
| 9 | −0.94 | 0.8836 |
| 10 | +1.06 | 1.1236 |

**Step 3 — Sum of squared deviations:**

$$\sum (x_i - \bar{X})^2 = 3.0276 + 2.4336 + 4.5796 + 5.5696 + 0.1156 + 0.0256 + 11.8336 + 11.9716 + 0.8836 + 1.1236$$

$$\sum (x_i - \bar{X})^2 = 41.564$$

**Step 4 — Standard deviation (sample, $n - 1$):**

$$s = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{X})^2}{n - 1}} = \sqrt{\frac{41.564}{9}} = \sqrt{4.6182} = 2.149$$

**Step 5 — Determine the reference value $M$:**

Since $\bar{X} = 99.94 < 98.5$? **No.** $99.94 > 101.5$? **No.** Therefore:

$$M = \bar{X} = 99.94$$

**Step 6 — Calculate AV:**

$$AV = \vert M - \bar{X} \vert + ks = \vert 99.94 - 99.94 \vert + (2.4)(2.149)$$

$$AV = 0 + 5.158 = 5.158$$

**Step 7 — Compare with acceptance criterion:**

$$\boxed{AV = 5.158 \leq 15.0 \Rightarrow \text{Batch PASSES}}$$

The batch passes the **L1 criterion** (Stage 1). No need for Stage 2 (which involves testing 20 additional units).

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A value of ~5 means excellent content uniformity. Most commercial tablets have AV between 5 and 12.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Endotoxin Limit Calculation for an IV Injection

**Given:**
- Drug: Vancomycin IV injection
- Route: Intravenous infusion
- Maximum human dose: **10 mg/kg/hour**
- Rabbit pyrogen dose: **K = 5 EU/kg** (IV)

**Step 1 — Recall the endotoxin limit equation:**

$$EL = \frac{K}{M}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$EL = \frac{5 \text{ EU/kg}}{10 \text{ mg/kg/hr}} = 0.5 \text{ EU/mg}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate maximum allowable endotoxin in the product container:**

If the product concentration is **50 mg/mL**, then:

$$\text{Maximum endotoxin per mL} = 0.5 \, \frac{\text{EU}}{\text{mg}} \times 50 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{mL}} = 25 \text{ EU/mL}$$

**Step 4 — Total endotoxin in a 10 mL vial:**

$$E_{\text{total}} = 25 \text{ EU/mL} \times 10 \text{ mL} = 250 \text{ EU/vial}$$

$$\boxed{EL = 0.5 \text{ EU/mg}; \; 25 \text{ EU/mL}; \; 250 \text{ EU/vial}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** During process validation, water-for-injection (WFI) endotoxin is typically **< 0.25 EU/mL**. Equipment cleaning validation confirms endotoxin removal via LAL testing.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Shelf-Life Estimation Using Arrhenius Equation

A drug degrades by first-order kinetics. At **40 °C**, the rate constant is **$k_{40} = 5.5 \times 10^{-3}$ day⁻¹**. Activation energy **$E_a = 80$ kJ/mol**. Estimate the shelf-life at **25 °C**.

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$T_1 = 40 + 273 = 313 \text{ K}$$

$$T_2 = 25 + 273 = 298 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Arrhenius in two-temperature form:**

$$\ln \frac{k_2}{k_1} = -\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}$:**

$$\frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{313} = 0.003356 - 0.003195 = 0.000161 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\frac{E_a}{R}$:**

$$\frac{E_a}{R} = \frac{80{,}000}{8.314} = 9622.3 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\ln(k_2/k_1)$:**

$$\ln \frac{k_{25}}{k_{40}} = -9622.3 \times 0.000161 = -1.549$$

**Step 6 — Exponentiate:**

$$\frac{k_{25}}{k_{40}} = e^{-1.549} = 0.2124$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $k_{25}$:**

$$k_{25} = 0.2124 \times 5.5 \times 10^{-3} = 1.168 \times 10^{-3} \text{ day}^{-1}$$

**Step 8 — Calculate shelf-life ($t_{90\%}$):**

$$t_{90\%} = \frac{0.105}{k_{25}} = \frac{0.105}{1.168 \times 10^{-3}} = 89.9 \text{ days}$$

$$\boxed{t_{90\%}(25°C) \approx 90 \text{ days} \; (\approx 3 \text{ months})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb:** A 10 °C rise in temperature roughly **doubles to triples** the degradation rate for most drugs ($Q_{10} \approx 2$–3).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Preservative Efficacy Test — Log Reduction

A topical cream is challenged with **S. aureus** at an initial count of **$10^6$ CFU/g**. After 14 days of storage, the count drops to **$10^2$ CFU/g**.

**Step 1 — Recall the formula:**

$$\text{Log Reduction} = \log_{10} N_0 - \log_{10} N_t$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\text{Log Reduction} = \log_{10}(10^6) - \log_{10}(10^2) = 6 - 2 = 4$$

**Step 3 — Compare with USP <51> Category 2 (topical) criterion:**

Required: **≥ 3.0 log reduction for bacteria at Day 14**.

$$\boxed{4 \geq 3 \Rightarrow \text{PASSES}}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right QC Test

| Dosage Form | Critical QC Test | Regulatory Citation |
|-------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Immediate-release tablet | Dissolution (App. II) | USP <711> |
| Modified-release tablet | Drug release in multiple pH | USP <711>/<724> |
| Hard capsule | Disintegration, content uniformity | USP <701>, <905> |
| Sterile injection | Sterility, BET, particulate | USP <71>, <85>, <788> |
| Ophthalmic solution | Sterility, particulate, preservative | USP <71>, <789>, <51> |
| Topical cream | PET, spreadability, viscosity | USP <51>, <915> |
| Transdermal patch | Shear, adhesion, release | USP <3>, <724> |
| Oral liquid | pH, microbial limits, assay | USP <61>, <1112> |
| Inhalation aerosol | Emitted dose, APSD | USP <601> |
| Lyophilized product | Reconstitution time, residual moisture | USP <905>, <921> |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
```mermaid
    P[Pharmaceutical Product] --> T[Determine Test Conditions]

    T --> LT[Long Term: 25 C, 60 percent RH, 12 months]

    AC --> CH{Any significant change at 6 months?}
    CH -->|No| A1[Supports 24 month shelf life]
    CH -->|Yes| IN2[Run Intermediate Study]

    IN2 --> CH2{Change at 30 C 12 months?}
    CH2 -->|No| A2[Supports 18 to 24 month shelf life]
    CH2 -->|Yes| A3[Reduce shelf life to 12 months or less]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define quality control. List any four quality control tests performed on tablets.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Quality Control (QC) refers to the sum of all procedures and activities employed in the pharmaceutical industry to ensure that drug products meet the established standards of identity, strength, purity, stability, and safety, as specified in pharmacopoeias and regulatory guidelines.

**Four QC tests for tablets (½ mark each = 2 marks):**

1. **Hardness test** — diametrical crushing strength using Monsanto/Pfizer hardness tester (typical 4–10 kg/cm²).
2. **Friability test** — Roche friabilator, 25 rpm × 4 min; acceptance ≤ 1.0% weight loss.
3. **Disintegration test** — USP disintegration apparatus; ≤ 15 min for uncoated tablets in 900 mL medium at 37 ± 2 °C.
4. **Dissolution test** — USP Apparatus I/II, 900 mL, 37 ± 0.5 °C; assesses drug release.

*(Any other valid tests such as weight variation, content uniformity, thickness, or assay may be accepted.)*

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the significance of the LAL test in pharmaceutical quality control?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **LAL (Limulus Amebocyte Lysate) test**, also called the **Bacterial Endotoxin Test (BET)** as per USP <85>, is used to detect and quantify **Gram-negative bacterial endotoxins (lipopolysaccharides, LPS)** in parenteral pharmaceuticals and medical devices.

**Significance (3 marks):**

1. **Safety assurance** — Endotoxins, even in trace amounts, can cause fever, septic shock, multi-organ failure, or death in patients; the LAL test ensures parenteral products are within safe endotoxin limits.
2. **Regulatory compliance** — It is the official compendial test (USP <85>, IP, BP) replacing the older rabbit pyrogen test for most parenterals.
3. **High sensitivity** — Detects endotoxin levels as low as **0.001 EU/mL**, much more sensitive than the rabbit test.
4. **Three methodologies available** — gel-clot, turbidimetric, and chromogenic — offering qualitative and quantitative endpoints.
5. **Endotoxin limit calculation** — $EL = K/M$ ensures the product is safe for the intended human dose.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each module typically offers an **internal choice** between two questions. Both options are presented below.

---

### **Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

#### **(a) Explain in detail the principle, procedure, and acceptance criteria of the dissolution test as per USP.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition and Significance (2 marks):**

The dissolution test, as per **USP <711>**, is an in-vitro performance test that measures the **rate and extent of drug release** from a solid oral dosage form into a specified medium under controlled conditions. It is a **critical quality attribute (CQA)** because it correlates with in-vivo bioavailability and bioequivalence (BCS Class II drugs especially).

**2. Principle (1 mark):**

A dosage form is placed in a known volume of dissolution medium maintained at body temperature (37 ± 0.5 °C). The medium is stirred by a rotating basket (Apparatus I) or paddle (Apparatus II). Samples are withdrawn at specified time intervals, filtered, and analyzed (typically by UV-Vis spectrophotometry or HPLC) to determine the cumulative amount of drug dissolved.

**3. Apparatus (1 mark):**

| Apparatus | Description | Common Use |
|-----------|-------------|------------|
| I — Basket | Rotating basket (40 mesh) at top | Capsules, floating dosage forms |
| II — Paddle | Paddle stirrer at bottom | Most immediate-release tablets |
| III — Reciprocating Cylinder | Cylinder moves up-down | Extended-release |
| IV — Flow-Through Cell | Continuous flow | Poorly soluble drugs |

**4. Standard Procedure (2 marks):**

- **Medium volume:** 900 mL (standard), or 100–4000 mL for special cases
- **Temperature:** 37 ± 0.5 °C
- **Paddle/Basket speed:** 50 or 75 rpm (default for paddle); 100 rpm for basket
- **Sampling:** at 10, 20, 30, 45, 60 minutes (and beyond for MR)
- **Analysis:** UV-Vis at λmax, or HPLC
- **Dissolution media:** 0.1 N HCl (pH 1.2), pH 4.5 acetate buffer, pH 6.8 phosphate buffer, water

**5. Acceptance Criteria (USP Q parameter) (1 mark):**

The amount dissolved at each time point $Q$ must meet the monograph-specified percentage. For instance, for a typical immediate-release tablet, **Q + 5% must dissolve in 30 minutes** (often Q = 75%).

---

#### **(b) Describe the sterility test as per USP <71>. Discuss the two methods, media used, incubation conditions, and interpretation of results.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**

The sterility test, as per **USP <71> / IP**, is a mandatory compendial test that determines the **absence of viable aerobic and anaerobic microorganisms** in a pharmaceutical product intended to be sterile (parenterals, ophthalmics, sterile gels, irrigation solutions).

**2. Two Methods (2 marks):**

**(a) Membrane Filtration Method (preferred):**
- Pass the sample through a **0.45 µm pore-size cellulose acetate filter membrane**.
- The membrane retains any microbes present.
- Wash the membrane to remove any inhibitory substances.
- Transfer the membrane aseptically into **two culture media**.

**(b) Direct Inoculation Method:**
- The sample is **inoculated directly** into culture media.
- Used for products that cannot be filtered (ointments, oils, certain powders).
- The product volume must not exceed 1% of the medium volume to avoid interference.

**3. Culture Media and Incubation (2 marks):**

| Medium | Detects | Temperature | Duration |
|--------|---------|-------------|----------|
| **Fluid Thioglycollate Medium (FTM)** | Aerobic + anaerobic bacteria | **30–35 °C** | **14 days** |
| **Soybean-Casein Digest Medium (SCDM)** | Fungi + aerobic bacteria | **20–25 °C** | **14 days** |

- Both media are incubated for a **minimum of 14 days**.
- **Negative controls** (sterile medium only) and **positive controls** (media inoculated with ≤ 100 CFU of specified organisms) are run in parallel to validate the test.

**4. Observation and Interpretation (1 mark):**

- **Daily visual observation** for turbidity, indicating microbial growth.
- **No growth in both media after 14 days = PASS (sterile).**
- **Any growth = FAIL (non-sterile).** An OOS (Out-of-Specification) investigation is initiated per FDA Guidance for Industry (2006).

**5. Limitations (1 mark):**
- Detects only **culturable organisms**; some viable-but-non-culturable (VBNC) microbes may escape.
- False positives from accidental contamination; retesting under strict asepsis is permitted under defined conditions.

---

### **Question B. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

#### **(a) Discuss in detail the various quality control tests performed on parenteral products.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parenteral products** are sterile, pyrogen-free preparations injected into body tissues, vessels, or organs. Their QC testing is the **most stringent** of all dosage forms because they bypass the body's natural defense barriers.

**1. Sterility Test (USP <71>) (1.5 marks):**

- **Membrane filtration** (preferred) or **direct inoculation**.
- **Media:** Fluid Thioglycollate Medium (30–35 °C, 14 days) and Soybean-Casein Digest Medium (20–25 °C, 14 days).
- **Acceptance:** No growth in either medium.

**2. Bacterial Endotoxin Test / LAL Test (USP <85>) (1.5 marks):**

- **Principle:** LAL reagent from horseshoe crab (*Limulus polyphemus*) coagulates in the presence of endotoxin (LPS).
- **Methods:** Gel-clot, turbidimetric, chromogenic.
- **Endotoxin Limit:** $EL = K / M$, where $K = 5$ EU/kg (IV), $M$ = maximum dose/kg/hr.
- **Acceptance:** Product endotoxin < EL.

**3. Particulate Matter Test (USP <788>) (1 mark):**

- **Method:** Light obscuration particle counter (primary) or microscopic (secondary).
- **Limits for large-volume parenterals (≥ 100 mL):** ≤ 25 particles/mL ≥ 10 µm; ≤ 3 particles/mL ≥ 25 µm.
- **Limits for small-volume (< 100 mL):** ≤ 6,000 particles/container ≥ 10 µm; ≤ 600 particles/container ≥ 25 µm.

**4. Clarity Test (0.5 marks):**
- Visual inspection under 2,000–3,000 lux against black and white background. Solution must be clear and free from visible particles.

**5. pH (0.5 marks):**
- Within narrow physiological range (typically 4.0–8.0) to minimize pain and tissue irritation.

**6. Leak Test (Ampoules) (0.5 marks):**
- **Dye penetration** (methylene blue), **bubble emission**, or **high-voltage leak detection**.

**7. Assay / Chemical Tests (0.5 marks):**
- Drug content 90–110% by HPLC or other validated methods.

**8. Particulate test for visible particles, container-closure integrity (0.5 marks):**
- Confirms maintenance of sterility throughout shelf-life.

**9. Preservative Efficacy Test (USP <51>) — for multi-dose containers (0.5 marks):**

---

#### **(b) Define stability testing. Explain ICH guidelines for accelerated, long-term, and intermediate stability studies.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition of Stability (1 mark):**

> Stability is the ability of a pharmaceutical product to retain its **chemical, physical, microbiological, therapeutic, and toxicological** properties within specified limits throughout its shelf-life, as per **ICH Q1A(R2)**.

**2. ICH Stability Study Conditions (Table) (3 marks):**

| Study | Temperature | Humidity | Minimum Duration | Purpose |
|-------|-------------|----------|------------------|---------|
| **Long-term** | 25 °C ± 2 °C | 60% RH ± 5% | **12 months** | Shelf-life determination |
| **Intermediate** | 30 °C ± 2 °C | 65% RH ± 5% | **12 months** | Used if accelerated shows change |
| **Accelerated** | 40 °C ± 2 °C | 75% RH ± 5% | **6 months** | Predicts shelf-life; detects degradation |
| **Stress / Forced** | Heat, acid, base, oxidation, light | — | Short term | Degradation pathway identification |

**3. Sampling Time Points (1 mark):**

- **Long-term:** 0, 3, 6, 9, 12, 18, 24, 36, 48 months.
- **Accelerated:** 0, 1, 2, 3, 6 months.

**4. Significance of "Significant Change" (1 mark):**

A **5% change in assay** from initial, or failure to meet acceptance criteria for appearance, pH, dissolution, or related substances, constitutes a "significant change," triggering the intermediate condition.

**5. Climatic Zone Considerations (0.5 marks):**

India falls in **Climatic Zone IV (hot/humid)** per WHO. Long-term conditions for Zone IV are **30 °C / 65% RH**, but most companies run both 25/60 (for global markets) and 30/65 (for Zone IV) conditions.

**6. Shelf-Life Estimation (0.5 marks):**

- Arrhenius equation extrapolation from accelerated data.
- Statistical analysis of assay, related substances, dissolution at each time point.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in QC Tests Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Disintegration vs Dissolution** — Disintegration is **mechanical breakup**; dissolution is **drug going into solution**. Mixing these up costs **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Wrong temperature** — Always specify **37 ± 0.5 °C** for dissolution and **37 ± 2 °C** for disintegration. Writing just "37 °C" loses marks.
> 3. **Sterility media temperatures swapped** — FTM is incubated at **30–35 °C** (NOT 20–25 °C); SCDM at **20–25 °C**. A very common error!
> 4. **Forgetting 900 mL volume and 14-day incubation** for dissolution/sterility tests.
> 5. **Endotoxin limit formula sign error** — $K$ goes in the **numerator**, not the denominator.
> 6. **Not mentioning controls** — Always state that sterility tests need **positive and negative controls**.
> 7. **Friability limit confusion** — Limit is **≤ 1.0%**, not 0.1% or 10%. Students frequently make decimal-place errors.
> 8. **Missing ICH conditions** — For accelerated stability, all three parameters (**40 °C, 75% RH, 6 months**) must be cited together.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

### ⭐ Must-Remember Pharmacopoeial Numbers
- **Friability limit:** ≤ **1.0%** (USP <1216>)
- **Friabilator settings:** **25 rpm × 4 min = 100 revolutions**
- **Disintegration limit (uncoated tablet):** ≤ **15 min** in **900 mL** at **37 ± 2 °C**
- **Dissolution:** **900 mL**, **37 ± 0.5 °C**, paddle **50/75 rpm**, basket **100 rpm**
- **Content Uniformity AV:** ≤ **15.0** (L1, $k = 2.4$)
- **Sterility incubation:** **14 days**; FTM **30–35 °C**, SCDM **20–25 °C**
- **Endotoxin K value:** **5 EU/kg** (IV); **0.2 EU/kg** (intrathecal)
- **Particulate matter (≥ 100 mL):** ≤ **25/mL** (≥ 10 µm); ≤ **3/mL** (≥ 25 µm)
- **Hardness:** **4–10 kg/cm²**
- **LOD for capsules:** typically **< 10%** (depends on monograph)

### ⭐ Top 5 Most-Asked Topics in KTU
1. **Dissolution test apparatus and conditions**
2. **Sterility test — media, temperature, duration**
3. **LAL test / BET**
4. **Friability formula and limit**
5. **ICH stability conditions**

### ⭐ Key Definitions
- **QC:** Procedures to ensure product meets specifications.
- **QA:** The overall system (philosophy + documentation) ensuring QC is achieved.
- **CQA:** Critical Quality Attribute — a physical, chemical, biological property that must be controlled.
- **OOS:** Out-of-Specification result — triggers formal investigation.
- **PAT:** Process Analytical Technology — real-time QC.

### ⭐ Differential Mastery
| Parameter | Tablets | Capsules | Parenterals |
|-----------|---------|----------|-------------|
| Sterility | Not required | Not required | **Mandatory** |
| Dissolution | Yes | Yes (App. I) | N/A |
| Disintegration | ≤ 15 min | ≤ 30 min | N/A |
| Particulate | N/A | N/A | **Mandatory** |
| Endotoxin | N/A | N/A | **Mandatory** |
| Hardness | Yes | N/A | N/A |

### ⭐ Memorization Mnemonic for Dissolution
> **"900-37-50-75"** = **900 mL**, **37 °C**, **paddle 50 or 75 rpm**, basket 100 rpm.

### ⭐ Sterility Media Mnemonic
> **"FTM is HOTTER than SCDM"** = FTM at **30–35 °C**, SCDM at **20–25 °C**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
