# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**"Define syrup. Mention the IP standard concentration of sucrose and explain how it acts as a self-preserving agent."**

**Model Answer (3 marks allocation):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Syrup is a concentrated or nearly saturated aqueous solution of sucrose, used as a vehicle for medicinal agents. Per IP, Simple Syrup contains **66.7% w/v** of sucrose in purified water.
- **Sucrose concentration (1 mark):** 66.7% w/v yields specific gravity 1.31–1.33.
- **Self-preservation mechanism (1 mark):** At this high concentration, the **osmotic pressure** is high enough to **plasmolyze microbial cells** and inhibit the growth of bacteria, yeasts, and molds. Hence, additional preservatives are not mandatory.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**"Differentiate between an elixir and a syrup. Give one example of each."**

**Model Answer:**
- **Elixir (1 mark):** A clear, sweetened, **hydroalcoholic** oral solution. Contains ethanol (5–40% v/v). Used for poorly water-soluble drugs. Example: **Phenobarbital Elixir**.
- **Syrup (1 mark):** A sweetened, viscous, **aqueous** solution. High sucrose content (66.7% w/v). No alcohol. Example: **Paracetamol Syrup**.
- **Key difference (1 mark):** Vehicle type — elixirs use alcohol + water; syrups use water + sugar only. Elixirs are less viscous and often have lower sugar content.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
**(a)** Classify monophasic dosage forms with suitable examples. Discuss the formulation components of any two oral monophasics in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are ear drops? Describe the formulation considerations and vehicles used, with examples. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question A (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Classification (3 marks)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification of Monophasic Dosage Forms:**

**A. Oral Monophasics:**
1. **Solutions** — e.g., Potassium Citrate Solution IP (aqueous).
2. **Syrups** — e.g., Paracetamol Syrup (66.7% w/v sucrose base).
3. **Elixirs** — e.g., Phenobarbital Elixir (hydroalcoholic).
4. **Linctus** — e.g., Simple Linctus BP (viscous, demulcent).

**B. Topical Monophasics:**
5. **Nasal Drops** — e.g., Xylometazoline 0.1% Nasal Drops.
6. **Ear Drops** — e.g., Chloramphenicol Ear Drops.

**Step 2: Formulation of Syrup (2 marks)**
- API + 66.7% w/v sucrose + purified water + preservative (if needed) + flavor + color.
- Example: Paracetamol Syrup IP contains 2.4% w/v paracetamol, 50% w/v sucrose, sorbitol solution, strawberry flavor, methyl paraben, sunset yellow FCF, purified water.

**Step 3: Formulation of Elixir (2 marks)**
- API + ethanol (5–40%) + purified water + glycerin (co-solvent) + sucrose/sorbitol (sweetener) + flavor + color.
- Example: Phenobarbital Elixir BP contains 0.4% w/v phenobarbital, 14% v/v ethanol, glycerin, syrup, orange oil, purified water.

---

### Solution to Question A (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Definition (1 mark)**
- Ear drops are sterile solutions (or suspensions) of medicaments in aqueous, oily, or glycerin-based vehicles, intended for instillation into the external ear canal.

**Step 2: Formulation Considerations (3 marks)**

| Consideration | Requirement |
|---|---|
| **Sterility** | Mandatory; filtered through 0.22 µm |
| **pH** | 5.0–7.0 (acidic to match ear canal & inhibit pathogens) |
| **Tonicity** | Isotonic with ear fluids |
| **Viscosity** | Moderate — must flow but coat canal |
| **Ototoxicity** | Avoid aminoglycosides if tympanic membrane perforated |
| **Preservation** | Multi-dose bottles need preservatives |

**Step 3: Vehicles (2 marks)**
- **Glycerin:** Hygroscopic, viscous, draws out fluid — used for otitis externa.
- **Olive oil / Arachis oil:** Lubricant, body-temperature compatible — used for cerumen softening.
- **Aqueous (buffered):** Miscible with ear secretions — used for antibiotic drops.
- **Example:** Chloramphenicol Ear Drops (aqueous), Sodium Bicarbonate Ear Drops (glycerin-based).

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
**(a)** Define linctus. Why is it not diluted before administration? Explain its formulation components with an example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are nasal drops? Explain the formulation, pH, and tonicity requirements with a worked example on sodium chloride equivalent calculation. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question B (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Definition (1 mark)**
- Linctus is a viscous, sweetened, aqueous liquid preparation containing demulcent, expectorant, or antitussive agents intended for **slow sipping** to provide a **local soothing effect** on the throat mucosa.

**Step 2: Why Not Diluted? (2 marks)**
- Linctus is formulated for **high viscosity and high sugar content** to ensure prolonged contact with the pharyngeal mucosa.
- Dilution would:
  - Reduce viscosity → faster swallowing → reduced local action.
  - Reduce sugar content → reduced demulcent coating.
  - Reduce drug concentration at the site of action.
- Hence, linctus is **sipped undiluted** in small doses (typically 5 mL).

**Step 3: Formulation Components (2 marks)**
- API (e.g., codeine phosphate, pholcodine).
- Vehicle: purified water.
- Viscosity-imparting agents: glycerin, syrup, honey.
- Sweeteners: sucrose, sorbitol.
- Flavoring: lemon, aniseed, tolu tincture.
- Preservative: benzoic acid.

**Step 4: Example (2 marks)**
- **Codeine Linctus BP:** Codeine phosphate (0.3% w/v) + sucrose + glycerin + benzoic acid + lemon flavor + purified water. Indicated for dry, irritating cough.

---

### Solution to Question B (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Definition (1 mark)**
- Nasal drops are sterile aqueous, hydroalcoholic, or oleaginous solutions intended for instillation into the nasal cavity by means of a calibrated dropper.

**Step 2: Formulation Requirements (2 marks)**
- Sterility (aseptic filling, 0.22 µm filtration).
- pH 4.5–7.5 (buffered with phosphate/acetate).
- Isotonic (~285–310 mOsm/kg).
- Viscosity modifier (HPMC, carbomer) for prolonged contact.
- Preservative (benzalkonium chloride 0.02%) for multi-dose.

**Step 3: pH & Tonicity (2 marks)**
- Acidic pH prevents microbial growth and matches nasal mucosa.
- Tonicity prevents ciliary damage (hypotonic → cell swelling; hypertonic → cell shrinkage, pain).

**Step 4: Worked Example (2 marks)**
- Calculate NaCl equivalent for a 0.1% w/v nasal solution of drug X (MW = 200, i = 1.8).
- Using $E = \dfrac{58.5 \times 1.8}{200 \times 1.86} = \dfrac{105.3}{372} \approx 0.283$
- For 0.1 g drug per 100 mL → NaCl equivalent = $0.1 \times 0.283 = 0.0283$ g per 100 mL.
- NaCl to be added = $0.9 - 0.0283 = 0.8717$ g per 100 mL.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Most common mistake (2 marks lost):** Students write "syrup" when they mean "elixir" or vice versa — examiners **strictly** penalize this. Remember: **Elixirs contain ethanol; Syrups do NOT.**
> - **Second most common mistake (1 mark lost):** Failing to mention **sterility** for nasal/ear drops — these are TOPICAL STERILE preparations.
> - **Third mistake:** Omitting the **osmotic adjustment calculation** (NaCl equivalent or E-value) in tonicity-related questions — examiners expect a numerical demonstration.
> - **Fourth mistake:** Confusing **linctus** (sipped, not diluted) with **cough syrup** (measured and may be diluted) — this is a favorite KTU examiner trap.
> - **Mark distribution hint:** For 7-mark sub-questions, expect ~3 definition/component marks + ~2 application marks + ~2 example marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Module 3 Monophasics:**

- ⭐ **Monophasic = Single Phase** (no physical boundary between dispersed and continuous phase).
- ⭐ **Solutions:** Aqueous or non-aqueous vehicles; no alcohol/sugar mandatory.
- ⭐ **Syrup IP:** **66.7% w/v sucrose** in water → specific gravity **1.31–1.33** → self-preserving.
- ⭐ **Elixir:** **Hydroalcoholic** (water + ethanol) → for poorly water-soluble drugs → contains **5–40% v/v ethanol**.
- ⭐ **Linctus:** **Viscous**, sweetened, aqueous → **sipped slowly** → **NOT diluted** → antitussive/demulcent.
- ⭐ **Nasal Drops:** Sterile, **pH 4.5–7.5**, isotonic (~285–310 mOsm/kg), 0.22 µm filtered.
- ⭐ **Ear Drops:** Sterile, **pH 5.0–7.0**, vehicles: **glycerin / oil / aqueous**.
- ⭐ **Acid mantle of ear canal:** pH 5.0–5.5 — acidic preparations inhibit *Pseudomonas*.
- ⭐ **Aminoglycosides (gentamicin, neomycin) are OTOTOXIC** — contraindicated if tympanic membrane is perforated.
- ⭐ **Tonicity formula:** $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$, NaCl equivalent: $E = \dfrac{58.5 \cdot i}{MW \cdot 1.86}$.
- ⭐ **Sucrose role in syrup:** Sweetener + viscosity builder + osmotic preservative.
- ⭐ **Ethanol role in elixir:** Co-solvent + preservative + clarity enhancer.
- ⭐ **Viscosity in linctus:** >30 cP — prolonged mucosal contact.
- ⭐ **Stock dilution formula:** $C_1 V_1 = C_2 V_2$ — used for alcohol dilution and drug concentration adjustments.
- ⭐ **Always add alcohol to water** (not water to alcohol) — safety + clarity.
- ⭐ **Preservatives in monophasics:** Methyl/propyl paraben (oral), benzalkonium chloride (nasal).
- ⭐ **Sucrose inversion risk:** Prolonged heating of syrup → hydrolysis to glucose + fructose → darkening → avoid.
- ⭐ **Self-preservation of syrup** depends on sucrose ≥ 66.7% w/v → lower concentrations require added preservatives.
- ⭐ **Pediatric formulations** often avoid ethanol → hence syrups are preferred over elixirs for children.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preformulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Preformulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧪 PREFORMULATION — The Heart of Rational Drug Design

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preformulation** is defined as the *systematic, multidisciplinary investigation of the physical, chemical, and physicochemical properties of a drug substance in isolation and in combination with excipients*, conducted **prior to the development of an effective, stable, safe, and bioavailable dosage form**.

In KTU parlance, preformulation is the **"scientific bridge"** between *Drug Discovery* (medicinal chemistry) and *Dosage Form Development* (pharmaceutics). It generates the foundational **database of physicochemical information** that drives every rational formulation decision.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus Areas:**
> 1. Goals & Objectives of Preformulation
> 2. Physicochemical Properties of Drug Substances
> 3. Stability & Compatibility Studies
> 4. Solid-State Properties (Polymorphism, Crystallinity)
> 5. Biopharmaceutical Classification System (BCS)
> 6. Salt Selection & pH-Solubility Profile

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Pre-Construction Soil Test"

> [!NOTE]
> **🏗️ Real-World Analogy:** *Building a House on Untested Soil*
>
> Imagine you want to build a **15-storey apartment** in Kerala. Before laying a single brick, a civil engineer performs a **soil test** (bearing capacity, water table, pH, salinity, settlement). This is **preformulation** in construction.
>
> - If the **soil is salty (high pKa issue)** → engineers change cement type (salt selection).
> - If the **soil has clay (polymorph issue)** → they compact differently or replace it.
> - If the **soil shifts (instability)** → they use deep pile foundations (stability enhancement).
>
> **Without the soil test → the building cracks, sinks, or collapses.**
> **Without preformulation → the tablet won't dissolve, degrades, or causes toxicity.**

The drug substance is the **"soil"**, and the dosage form is the **"building"**. Preformulation tells the formulator *what kind of building can safely stand* on this soil.

---

## 1.3 Why Preformulation? — The "Why" Behind Every Test

| KTU Examiner Trigger | Practical Engineering Reasoning |
|---|---|
| *Why study preformulation at all?* | Saves **~40–60%** of formulation development time by eliminating bad leads early |
| *Why is it called "Pre"?* | It is performed **before** the actual dosage form is finalized, on the *pure drug* and *drug + excipient* mixtures |
| *What is the ultimate output?* | A **"Formulation Decision Matrix"** — selecting salt form, polymorph, excipients, and dosage form type |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Pareto Principle" of Pharmaceutics:** *80% of a drug's final quality is determined by the preformulation phase; only 20% comes from manufacturing optimization.*

---

## 1.4 Objectives of Preformulation Studies (KTU Board Point of View)

1. **Establish the physicochemical "fingerprint"** of the drug substance.
2. **Identify degradation pathways** (hydrolysis, oxidation, photolysis, thermolysis).
3. **Determine the most stable solid form** (polymorph, solvate, salt, co-crystal).
4. **Predict bioavailability** using *in vitro* surrogates (solubility, partition coefficient).
5. **Establish drug–excipient compatibility** to avoid failed formulations.
6. **Provide a regulatory data package** (CTD Module 3.2.S — Drug Substance) for FDA/DCGI filing.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two Pillars of Preformulation:**
> - **Pillar 1: "What is the drug?"** → Identity, Purity, Solid-State Form
> - **Pillar 2: "How does the drug behave?"** → Solubility, Stability, Compatibility

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 The Preformulation "Five-Stage" Logic Flow

### **Stage 1: Bulk Characterization (Purity & Identity)**
The pure drug (API — Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient) is characterized using:
- **Melting Point / Range** (capillary method, DSC)
- **UV-Visible Spectroscopy** (λ_max, molar absorptivity $\varepsilon$)
- **IR / FTIR Spectroscopy** (functional group fingerprint)
- **NMR & Mass Spectrometry** (structural confirmation)
- **HPLC Purity Assay** (≥ 99% is desirable for preformulation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A narrow melting range (≤ 1°C) indicates high purity.** A broad range or low melting point is a **red flag** for contamination or polymorphism.

---

### **Stage 2: Solubility Analysis (The Most Tested KTU Topic)**

#### 2.2.1 Intrinsic Solubility ($S_0$)
The aqueous solubility of the **unionized** (free acid or free base) form of the drug.

$$S_{total} = S_0 \left[ 1 + 10^{(pK_a - pH)} \right] \quad \text{for weak acids}$$

$$S_{total} = S_0 \left[ 1 + 10^{(pH - pK_a)} \right] \quad \text{for weak bases}$$

Where:
- $S_{total}$ = Total solubility at given pH
- $S_0$ = Intrinsic solubility (of unionized form)
- $pK_a$ = Acid dissociation constant
- $pH$ = Hydrogen ion concentration of medium

#### 2.2.2 pH-Solubility Profile
- For **weak acids**: solubility **increases** as pH increases (above $pK_a$).
- For **weak bases**: solubility **increases** as pH decreases (below $pK_a$).
- This is the basis for **salt selection** and **enteric coating**.

#### 2.2.3 Solubility Classification (USP Pharmacopeia)

| Descriptive Term | Solubility (mg/mL) | Practical Example |
|---|---|---|
| Very Soluble | $\geq 1000$ | Glucose, Paracetamol |
| Freely Soluble | $100$–$1000$ | Aspirin |
| Soluble | $33$–$100$ | Atropine sulfate |
| Sparingly Soluble | $10$–$33$ | Theophylline |
| Slightly Soluble | $1$–$10$ | Griseofulvin |
| Very Slightly Soluble | $0.1$–$1$ | Riboflavin |
| Practically Insoluble | $< 0.1$ | Nifedipine, Celecoxib |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** The **Biopharmaceutics Classification System (BCS)** categorizes drugs by solubility and permeability. **Class II drugs** (low solubility, high permeability) are the most challenging — and most common — in modern drug development.

---

### **Stage 3: Lipophilicity & Partition Coefficient**

The **partition coefficient ($P$)** is the ratio of a drug's concentration in an organic phase (typically *n*-octanol) to its concentration in an aqueous phase, at equilibrium.

$$P = \frac{C_{octanol}}{C_{water}}$$

The **logarithm** of $P$ is universally used (Lipinski's Rule of 5):

$$\log P = \log_{10} \left( \frac{C_{octanol}}{C_{water}} \right)$$

**Significance:**
- $\log P < 0$ → Hydrophilic (poor membrane permeability)
- $0 < \log P < 3$ → Optimal for oral absorption
- $\log P > 5$ → Lipophilic (poor aqueous solubility, may be trapped in membranes)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lipinski's Rule of 5 (1997) — "Drug-likeness" filter:**
> 1. Molecular weight $\leq 500$ Da
> 2. $\log P \leq 5$
> 3. $\leq 5$ Hydrogen bond donors
> 4. $\leq 10$ Hydrogen bond acceptors
>
> A drug violating **>2 rules** is likely to have poor oral bioavailability.

---

### **Stage 4: Dissociation Constant ($pK_a$)**

The $pK_a$ is the pH at which the drug is **50% ionized** and **50% unionized**.

$$\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^-$$

$$pK_a = pH + \log \frac{[HA]}{[A^-]}$$

**Henderson-Hasselbalch Equation:**

For **weak acids**:
$$pH = pK_a + \log \frac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$$

For **weak bases**:
$$pH = pK_a + \log \frac{[BH^+]}{[B]}$$

**Importance in Preformulation:**
- Determines **pH for maximum stability** (unionized form is often more stable).
- Determines **pH for maximum solubility** (ionized form is more soluble).
- Guides **salt selection** and **buffer choice**.

---

### **Stage 5: Solid-State Properties — Polymorphism & Crystallinity**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polymorphs** are different crystalline forms of the **same chemical compound** with **different internal lattice arrangements**. Each polymorph has a unique:
> - Melting point
> - Solubility
> - Stability
> - Bioavailability
> - Mechanical properties (compressibility, flowability)

**Types of Solid Forms:**
1. **Crystalline** (ordered, sharp melting point)
2. **Amorphous** (disordered, higher solubility, less stable)
3. **Polymorphs** (Forms I, II, III, IV...)
4. **Solvates/Hydrates** (contain solvent/water in lattice)
5. **Co-crystals** (drug + coformer in same crystal lattice)
6. **Salts** (drug + counterion)

> [!NOTE]
> **Famous KTU Case Study: Ritonavir (Abbott, 1998)**
> The drug had to be **withdrawn from the market** because a previously unseen, thermodynamically more stable polymorph (Form II) suddenly appeared, dramatically reducing solubility and bioavailability. This cost Abbott **>$250 million** and is the definitive case for **mandatory preformulation polymorphism screening**.

---

### **Stage 6: Stability Studies (The "Stress Test")**

Drug substances are subjected to **forced degradation (stress) studies** under ICH Q1A(R2) guidelines:

| Stress Condition | Typical Parameters | Degradation Pathway |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermal** | $40°\text{C}$, $60°\text{C}$, $80°\text{C}$ | Thermolysis, melting, polymorphic transition |
| **Hydrolytic** | $0.1\text{N HCl}$, $0.1\text{N NaOH}$, pH 7 buffer | Hydrolysis (esters, amides, lactams) |
| **Oxidative** | $3\%\ \text{H}_2\text{O}_2$, AAPH | Oxidation (thiols, phenols, tertiary amines) |
| **Photolytic** | ICH Q1B: 1.2 million lux·hr + 200 W·hr/m² UV | Photolysis (Nifedipine, Furosemide) |
| **Humidity** | $75\%\ \text{RH}$, $90\%\ \text{RH}$ | Hydrate formation, deliquescence |

**Arrhenius Equation** (for accelerated stability prediction):

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

$$\log k = \log A - \frac{E_a}{2.303 \, RT}$$

Where:
- $k$ = rate constant
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor
- $E_a$ = activation energy (kJ/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant $= 8.314\ \text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Rule of 10" / "Q10 Rule":** For every **10°C increase** in temperature, the rate of degradation **approximately doubles or triples** (Q10 ≈ 2–3). This is why accelerated stability is performed at $40°C/75\%\ RH$ for 6 months, predicting 2-year shelf life at $25°C$.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 Master Formula Card — Print This!**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Partition Coefficient** | $P = C_{oct} / C_{aq}$ | dimensionless | Predicts membrane permeability |
| 2 | **Log P** | $\log P = \log_{10}(C_{oct}/C_{aq})$ | dimensionless | Lipinski's Rule of 5 |
| 3 | **Henderson-Hasselbalch (acid)** | $pH = pK_a + \log([A^-]/[HA])$ | dimensionless | Salt selection, buffer prep |
| 4 | **Henderson-Hasselbalch (base)** | $pH = pK_a + \log([BH^+]/[B])$ | dimensionless | Salt selection, buffer prep |
| 5 | **Weak Acid Solubility** | $S = S_0(1 + 10^{(pK_a - pH)})$ | mg/mL | pH-solubility profile |
| 6 | **Weak Base Solubility** | $S = S_0(1 + 10^{(pH - pK_a)})$ | mg/mL | pH-solubility profile |
| 7 | **Ionization Fraction (acid)** | $\alpha_{ionized} = 1/(1+10^{(pK_a - pH)})$ | fraction | Speciation diagram |
| 8 | **Ionization Fraction (base)** | $\alpha_{ionized} = 1/(1+10^{(pH - pK_a)})$ | fraction | Speciation diagram |
| 9 | **Arrhenius Equation** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | s⁻¹ (rate) | Shelf-life prediction |
| 10 | **Q10 Rule** | $Q_{10} = (k_2/k_1)^{10/(T_2 - T_1)}$ | dimensionless | Accelerated stability |
| 11 | **Dose Number (BCS)** | $D_0 = (M_0/V_0) / S$ | dimensionless | BCS solubility class |
| 12 | **Higuchi Diffusion** | $Q = \sqrt{D \cdot (2A - C_s) \cdot C_s \cdot t}$ | amount/time | Drug release matrix |

**Standard Constants to Memorize:**
- $R = 8.314\ \text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$
- $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$
- Body fluid volume $V_0 = 250\ \text{mL}$ (gastric) or **900 mL** (intestinal) — BCS standard
- $25°C = 298.15\ \text{K}$
- $37°C = 310.15\ \text{K}$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Henderson-Hasselbalch Equation (Full Derivation)

**Starting Point:** Dissociation of a weak acid in water

$$\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^-$$

**Step 1:** Write the equilibrium constant expression (acid dissociation constant, $K_a$):

$$K_a = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

**Step 2:** Take the negative logarithm (base 10) of both sides:

$$-\log K_a = -\log [\text{H}^+] - \log \frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that $-\log K_a = pK_a$ and $-\log [\text{H}^+] = pH$:

$$pK_a = pH - \log \frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange by bringing $pH$ to the left side:

$$pH = pK_a + \log \frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

**This is the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation for a weak acid.**

> [!NOTE]
> For a weak base ($\text{BH}^+ \rightleftharpoons \text{B} + \text{H}^+$), the same derivation gives:
> $$pH = pK_a + \log \frac{[\text{B}]}{[\text{BH}^+]}$$

**Logic Check:** When $pH = pK_a$, the logarithm term becomes $\log(1) = 0$, meaning the drug is 50% ionized and 50% unionized. **This confirms the definition of $pK_a$.** ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Solubility of a Weak Electrolyte

**Starting Point:** Total solubility = concentration of all dissolved species

$$S_{total} = S_0 + S_{ionized}$$

For a weak acid:
$$S_{total} = S_0 + [\text{A}^-]$$

**Step 1:** From the $K_a$ expression, solve for $[\text{A}^-]$:

$$[\text{A}^-] = \frac{K_a \cdot [\text{HA}]}{[\text{H}^+]} = K_a \cdot \frac{S_0}{[\text{H}^+]}$$

(Substituting $[\text{HA}] \approx S_0$, since only unionized form is "undissolved")

**Step 2:** Substitute into the total solubility expression:

$$S_{total} = S_0 + \frac{K_a \cdot S_0}{[\text{H}^+]}$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $S_0$:

$$S_{total} = S_0 \left( 1 + \frac{K_a}{[\text{H}^+]} \right)$$

**Step 4:** Convert $[\text{H}^+]$ to pH form. Since $[\text{H}^+] = 10^{-pH}$ and $K_a = 10^{-pK_a}$:

$$\frac{K_a}{[\text{H}^+]} = \frac{10^{-pK_a}}{10^{-pH}} = 10^{(pH - pK_a)}$$

**Step 5:** Final result for **weak acid**:

$$\boxed{S_{total} = S_0 \left[ 1 + 10^{(pH - pK_a)} \right] }$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** Some textbooks write this with a flipped sign convention as $S = S_0[1 + 10^{(pK_a - pH)}]$ for **weak bases** (because bases are more soluble at *low* pH). Always double-check the species — this is a common KTU exam trap.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Solubility Calculation (KTU Numerical Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Question:** A weak acid drug has $pK_a = 4.5$ and intrinsic solubility $S_0 = 0.1\ \text{mg/mL}$. Calculate its total solubility at (a) pH 1.2 (gastric) and (b) pH 6.8 (intestinal).

**Solution:**

**Part (a): pH 1.2**

$$S_{total} = 0.1 \times \left[ 1 + 10^{(1.2 - 4.5)} \right]$$

$$= 0.1 \times \left[ 1 + 10^{-3.3} \right]$$

$$= 0.1 \times \left[ 1 + 0.000501 \right]$$

$$= 0.1 \times 1.000501$$

$$= 0.1001\ \text{mg/mL}$$

**At gastric pH 1.2 → drug is essentially unionized → low solubility** ✓

**Part (b): pH 6.8**

$$S_{total} = 0.1 \times \left[ 1 + 10^{(6.8 - 4.5)} \right]$$

$$= 0.1 \times \left[ 1 + 10^{2.3} \right]$$

$$= 0.1 \times \left[ 1 + 199.53 \right]$$

$$= 0.1 \times 200.53$$

$$= 20.05\ \text{mg/mL}$$

**At intestinal pH 6.8 → drug is highly ionized → ~200× higher solubility** ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This 200-fold increase is exactly why weak acid drugs (e.g., NSAIDs like ibuprofen) are best absorbed in the **small intestine** (higher pH) rather than the stomach, despite the stomach's larger surface area. The solubility "wins" over the surface area "loss".

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Drug–Excipient Compatibility Outcomes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🧫 Compatibility Test Interpretation Matrix** (DSC, FTIR, HPLC peak purity)

| Observation | Interpretation | Formulation Action |
|---|---|---|
| **DSC**: No shift in drug melting peak; no new endotherm | ✅ **Compatible** | Proceed with excipient |
| **DSC**: Disappearance of drug peak | ⚠️ **Amorphization** (not always incompatibility) | Check by XRPD; may improve stability |
| **DSC**: New endotherm at lower T | 🚨 **Eutectic formation** | Avoid excipient |
| **DSC**: Broad peak + exotherm | 🚨 **Solid-state reaction** | Avoid excipient |
| **FTIR**: New peak / loss of functional group peak | 🚨 **Chemical interaction** | Avoid excipient |
| **HPLC**: New degradation peak > 1% in 4 weeks | 🚨 **Incompatibility** | Avoid excipient |
| **Visual**: Discoloration, gas, liquefaction | 🚨 **Incompatibility** | Discard batch |

---

## 3.5 BCS Classification (Biopharmaceutics Classification System)

**Dose Number (Dose Solubility Ratio):**

$$D_0 = \frac{M_0 / V_0}{S}$$

Where:
- $M_0$ = highest single dose (mg)
- $V_0$ = volume of water in GI tract = **250 mL** (FDA standard)
- $S$ = solubility (mg/mL) of drug at pH 1.2, 4.5, and 6.8

> [!NOTE]
> **BCS Decision Rule:** Drug is "highly soluble" if $D_0 \leq 1$ at **all three** pH values (1.2, 4.5, 6.8) across the physiological pH range.

| BCS Class | Solubility | Permeability | Examples | Formulation Challenge |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I** | High | High | Metoprolol, Paracetamol | Easy (IR dosage form) |
| **II** | Low | High | Nifedipine, Griseofulvin | Solubility enhancement |
| **III** | High | Low | Cimetidine, Atenolol | Permeation enhancement |
| **IV** | Low | Low | Furosemide, Hydrochlorothiazide | Both — very difficult |

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[pH much less than pKa] --> B[Unionized drug dominates]
    B --> C[Solubility near S0 - LOW]
    
    D[pH equals pKa] --> E[50 percent ionized]
    E --> F[Solubility doubles]
    
    G[pH much greater than pKa] --> H[Fully ionized]
    H --> I[Solubility much higher - HIGH]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Salt Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    API[Drug Substance pKa Known] --> Q1{Is drug ionizable?}
    Q1 -- No --> NEUTRAL[Use Co-crystal or Amorphous form]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Acid or Base?}
    
    Q2 -- Acid --> AC1[pKa 3 to 5 = Ideal for salt]
    Q2 -- Base --> BC1[pKa 6 to 8 = Ideal for salt]
    
    AC1 --> ANION[Choose Cation from Na, K, Ca, Mg, Choline, Arginine]
    BC1 --> CATION[Choose Anion from HCl, HBr, H2SO4, Mesylate, Tartrate, Citrate]
    
    ANION --> TEST[Compatibility and Stability Testing]
    CATION --> TEST
    NEUTRAL --> TEST
    
    TEST --> BEST[Best Salt Form Selected]

```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Solid-State Form Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SS[Drug Substance] --> STABLE{Stable Form?}
    STABLE -- Yes --> C[Crystalline Form Selected]
    STABLE -- No --> AM[Amorphous Form Selected]
    
    C --> P1{Polymorphs Present?}
    P1 -- Yes --> P2[Most Stable Polymorph: Form A]
    P1 -- No --> SING[Single Crystal Form]
    
    AM --> AM1[Higher Solubility]
    AM1 --> AM2[Lower Physical Stability]
    AM2 --> AM3[Needs Stabilizer or Polymer Carrier]
    
    C --> SALT{Salt or Free Form?}
    SALT -- Salt --> S1[Counterion Selection Done]
    SALT -- Free --> F1[Free Acid or Base Used]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`
**Define preformulation. List any four objectives of preformulation studies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Preformulation is the systematic study of the physical, chemical, and physicochemical properties of a drug substance in isolation and in combination with excipients, performed prior to the development of a stable, effective, and bioavailable dosage form.

**Four Objectives (0.5 mark each = 2 marks):**
1. To determine the physicochemical properties of the drug (solubility, pKa, log P, polymorphism).
2. To establish stability of the drug under various stress conditions (thermal, hydrolytic, oxidative, photolytic).
3. To assess drug–excipient compatibility for rational excipient selection.
4. To select the appropriate salt, polymorph, and dosage form for optimal bioavailability.

---

### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`
**Differentiate between polymorphism and amorphism with two pharmaceutical examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Polymorphism (1.5 marks):** Existence of a drug substance in **two or more crystalline forms** with **different internal lattice arrangements** but **same chemical composition**. Each polymorph has different melting point, solubility, and stability.
> *Examples:* Chloramphenicol palmitate (Forms A and B), Ritonavir (Forms I and II).

> [!NOTE]
> **Amorphism (1.5 marks):** A **non-crystalline, disordered solid-state form** with **higher free energy, higher solubility, but lower physical stability** than crystalline forms. May convert to crystalline on storage.
> *Examples:* Amorphous insulin, amorphous indomethacin (used in spray-dried dispersions like Noxafil).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **🔹 Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 — Q. 11(a)]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **pH-solubility profile** and **pKa determination methods** for ionizable drugs. Explain the significance of the **Henderson-Hasselbalch equation** in preformulation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A weak base drug has $pK_a = 8.2$ and intrinsic solubility $S_0 = 0.05\ \text{mg/mL}$. Calculate its total solubility at (i) pH 1.2 and (ii) pH 6.8. Comment on the implications for its absorption in the GI tract.

---

#### **Model Answer for Q.A(a) [7 Marks]:**

**1. pH-Solubility Profile (3 marks):**
The pH-solubility profile is a **graphical/analytical representation** of the variation in drug solubility as a function of pH. For ionizable drugs, solubility changes dramatically around the $pK_a$:

- For **weak acids** → solubility **increases exponentially** as pH rises above $pK_a$.
- For **weak bases** → solubility **increases exponentially** as pH falls below $pK_a$.

The **plateau region** (low solubility, independent of pH) corresponds to the **unionized form** ($S_0$). The **rising region** reflects the contribution of the **ionized species** governed by $pK_a$.

**2. Methods of $pK_a$ Determination (2 marks):**
1. **Potentiometric Titration** — most common; plot of pH vs volume of titrant, $pK_a$ = pH at half-equivalence point.
2. **Spectrophotometric Method** — measures UV absorbance of ionized vs unionized form at different pH.
3. **Solubility Method** — $pK_a$ = pH at the **inflection point** of the pH-solubility profile.
4. **Conductometric / Capillary Electrophoresis** — for difficult cases.

**3. Henderson-Hasselbalch Significance (2 marks):**
- Predicts **degree of ionization** at any pH.
- Guides **buffer selection** for stability and dissolution.
- Forms the basis for **salt selection** (e.g., choosing Na-salt of acid drugs).
- Predicts **absorption site** (pH-partition hypothesis).

---

#### **Model Answer for Q.A(b) [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** $pK_a = 8.2$ (weak base), $S_0 = 0.05\ \text{mg/mL}$

**Formula for weak base:**

$$S_{total} = S_0 \left[ 1 + 10^{(pH - pK_a)} \right]$$

**Part (i): pH 1.2**

$$S_{total} = 0.05 \times \left[ 1 + 10^{(1.2 - 8.2)} \right]$$

$$= 0.05 \times \left[ 1 + 10^{-7.0} \right]$$

$$= 0.05 \times \left[ 1 + 1 \times 10^{-7} \right]$$

$$= 0.05 \times 1.0000001$$

$$\boxed{S_{total} \approx 0.05\ \text{mg/mL}}$$

**At gastric pH 1.2 → drug is highly ionized → fully soluble** (well, *more* soluble; here the unionized is so insoluble it doesn't contribute).

**Part (ii): pH 6.8**

$$S_{total} = 0.05 \times \left[ 1 + 10^{(6.8 - 8.2)} \right]$$

$$= 0.05 \times \left[ 1 + 10^{-1.4} \right]$$

$$= 0.05 \times \left[ 1 + 0.0398 \right]$$

$$= 0.05 \times 1.0398$$

$$\boxed{S_{total} \approx 0.052\ \text{mg/mL}}$$

**Comment on GI Absorption (3 marks):**
- At **gastric pH 1.2** → $pH < pK_a$ → drug is **ionized (protonated)** → highly soluble, but **poorly absorbed** across gastric mucosa (charged molecules don't cross lipid membranes easily).
- At **intestinal pH 6.8** → $pH < pK_a$ still (6.8 < 8.2) → drug is **mostly ionized** → still soluble, permeability now depends on $\log P$.
- **Implication:** This drug is best formulated as a **salt form** to enhance solubility across the entire GI range. Example: Diphenhydramine (base $pK_a$ 8.98) is formulated as **diphenhydramine HCl** salt to improve gastric solubility.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Many students **forget the "+1"** inside the bracket, leading to wrong values.
> - **Sign convention confusion:** For *weak bases*, the exponent is $(pH - pK_a)$, not $(pK_a - pH)$. Writing it the wrong way gives you a 200× wrong answer.
> - **Always mention** the assumption: intrinsic solubility is the **unionized form only**, and the activity coefficients are unity.
> - Students lose 1–2 marks for **not commenting on biological implications** (e.g., the pH-partition hypothesis).

---

### **🔹 Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 — Q. 11(b)]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **polymorphism** in pharmaceutical solids. Discuss its impact on drug **solubility, stability, bioavailability**, and **manufacturability** with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **methods used for polymorphism screening** (XRPD, DSC, TGA, hot-stage microscopy). Add a note on the **regulatory importance** of polymorph characterization.

---

#### **Model Answer for Q.B(a) [7 Marks]:**

**1. Definition (1.5 marks):**
Polymorphism is the ability of a **solid material to exist in more than one crystal structure** with **identical chemical composition** but **different physical properties** (melting point, density, hardness, solubility, stability). The **most stable form** has the lowest free energy; **metastable forms** have higher free energy and higher solubility (but may transform on storage).

**2. Types (1.5 marks):**
- **Enantiotropes:** Two forms have a **transition temperature** below melting point; one is stable below, the other above.
- **Monotropes:** One form is **always more stable** than the other at all temperatures.

**3. Pharmaceutical Impact (4 marks):**

| Property | Impact | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Solubility** | Metastable form → 2–10× higher solubility | Chloramphenicol palmitate Form B > Form A |
| **Stability** | Metastable form may convert to stable form on storage | Theophylline Form II → Form I |
| **Bioavailability** | Solubility difference → variable absorption | Ritonavir Form II caused >50% BA drop |
| **Manufacturability** | Different hardness, flow, compressibility | Paracetamol Form I (brittle) vs Form II (pliable) |
| **Patentability** | New polymorphs can be patented | Glivec (imatinib) — Novartis polymorphism patents |

---

#### **Model Answer for Q.B(b) [7 Marks]:**

**1. Polymorphism Screening Methods (5 marks):**

| Method | Principle | Information Provided |
|---|---|---|
| **X-Ray Powder Diffraction (XRPD)** | Bragg's Law: $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ | Crystal structure, polymorphic identity, amorphous content |
| **Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)** | Heat flow vs T | Melting point, transition T, enthalpy, polymorphism |
| **Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA)** | Mass change vs T | Solvate/hydrate content (mass loss step) |
| **Hot-Stage Microscopy (HSM)** | Visual under polarized light with heating | Crystalline transitions, dehydration in real time |
| **FTIR / Raman Spectroscopy** | Vibrational mode fingerprint | Polymorph-specific spectra, hydrogen bonding |
| **Solution Calorimetry** | Heat of dissolution | Solubility ranking, lattice energy |
| **Slurry Experiments** | Equilibration in solvents | Stable form at given T |
| **Variable-Temperature XRPD** | Crystal structure vs T | Enantiotropic transition temperature |

**2. Regulatory Importance (2 marks):**
- **ICH Q6A** requires control of polymorphic form in drug substance.
- **FDA Guidance (2007)** mandates characterization of all solid forms, including amorphous.
- **Patent litigation:** Polymorph patents are billion-dollar assets.
- **Generic bioequivalence:** Generic drugs must match the **same polymorph** as the innovator (or scientifically justify a different form).

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Do **not confuse solvates/hydrates with polymorphs** — solvates are *pseudopolymorphs* (contain solvent), polymorphs contain *only* the drug.
> - **Forgetting examples** costs easy marks. Always cite 2–3 examples: Ritonavir, Chloramphenicol palmitate, Carbamazepine, Theophylline.
> - Do **not skip regulatory content** — ICH Q6A and FDA guidance are favourite 2-mark questions.
> - Writing only "XRD" without expansion (X-Ray Diffraction) = lose 0.5 mark.

---

## 5.3 Quick-Recall Conceptual Questions (Module 3 — Practice)

> [!NOTE]
> **Try these 2-mark KTU model questions yourself (answers in next class):**
> 1. What is the **"Rule of 10"** in accelerated stability?
> 2. Define **hygroscopicity** and name the 4 USP classes.
> 3. What is a **co-crystal**? How is it different from a **salt**?
> 4. Explain the **pH-partition hypothesis** of drug absorption.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Final Rapid-Revision Card — Module 3: Preformulation**

### **Key Definitions to Memorize (Definitive KTU List)**
- ✅ **Preformulation** — the bridge between drug discovery and dosage form development.
- ✅ **Polymorphism** — multiple crystal forms of the same drug.
- ✅ **Amorphism** — disordered, higher-energy solid form.
- ✅ **Solvate vs Hydrate** — solvate contains organic solvent; hydrate contains water.
- ✅ **Co-crystal** — drug + neutral coformer in same lattice (vs salt which is ionized).
- ✅ **pKa** — pH at which 50% ionization occurs.
- ✅ **log P** — log of octanol-water partition coefficient (lipophilicity).
- ✅ **Intrinsic Solubility (S₀)** — solubility of the unionized form.
- ✅ **Hygroscopicity** — tendency to absorb atmospheric moisture.

### **Formulas You Must Know Cold**
- ✅ $pH = pK_a + \log([A^-]/[HA])$ — **Henderson-Hasselbalch (acid)**
- ✅ $S_{total} = S_0[1 + 10^{(pH-pK_a)}]$ — **Weak base solubility**
- ✅ $S_{total} = S_0[1 + 10^{(pK_a-pH)}]$ — **Weak acid solubility**
- ✅ $\log P = \log(C_{oct}/C_{aq})$ — **Partition coefficient**
- ✅ $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ — **Arrhenius equation**
- ✅ $D_0 = (M_0/V_0)/S$ — **BCS dose number**

### **Numerical Values to Memorize**
- ✅ $R = 8.314\ \text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$
- ✅ BCS $V_0 = 250\ \text{mL}$ (gastric) or 900 mL (intestinal)
- ✅ $25°C = 298.15\ \text{K}$
- ✅ ICH accelerated stability = $40°C / 75\%\ \text{RH}$ / 6 months
- ✅ ICH photostability = 1.2 million lux·hr + 200 W·hr/m² UV
- ✅ Q10 ≈ 2 to 3 (rate doubles per 10°C rise)

### **Case Studies KTU Loves to Ask**
- ✅ **Ritonavir** — Form II polymorphism crisis (Abbott, 1998).
- ✅ **Chloramphenicol palmitate** — Form A vs Form B bioavailability.
- ✅ **Griseofulvin** — BCS Class II solubility-limited absorption.
- ✅ **Ibuprofen** — weak acid, absorbed in intestine (H-H calculation).
- ✅ **Ranitidine HCl** — salt selection for improved stability.

### **Regulatory Bullet Points**
- ✅ **ICH Q1A(R2)** — accelerated and long-term stability.
- ✅ **ICH Q1B** — photostability testing.
- ✅ **ICH Q6A** — specifications for new drug substances (polymorph control).
- ✅ **ICH Q3A(R2)** — impurity thresholds in drug substance.
- ✅ **FDA 2007 Guidance** — ANDAs: pharmaceutical solid polymorphism.

### **One-Liners to Write in Margin of Answer Book**
> *Polymorphs are chemically identical but physically different.*
> *Salt = ionized; Co-crystal = neutral components.*
> *Metastable = higher solubility, lower stability.*
> *Amorphous = no long-range order, max free energy.*
> *Weak acids → salt with Na⁺/K⁺/Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺.*
> *Weak bases → salt with Cl⁻/SO₄²⁻/mesylate/tartrate.*

> - **Module:** Module 3
