# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the following terms:**
(a) Bioavailability
(b) Bioequivalence
(c) Therapeutic Index

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Bioavailability:** Bioavailability is defined as the rate and extent to which the active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) is absorbed from a dosage form and becomes available at the site of action. It is calculated as:

$$F = \frac{AUC_{test}}{AUC_{iv}} \times \frac{Dose_{iv}}{Dose_{test}} \times 100\%$$

**(b) Bioequivalence:** Bioequivalence refers to the absence of a significant difference in the rate and extent of absorption of the active ingredient from two pharmaceutical products, when administered at the same molar dose under similar conditions. As per FDA, the 90% CI of the test/reference ratio for AUC and C_max must lie within 80%–125%.

**(c) Therapeutic Index (TI):** Therapeutic Index is the ratio of the toxic dose (LD₅₀ in animals, TD₅₀ in humans) to the effective dose (ED₅₀):

$$TI = \frac{LD_{50}}{ED_{50}} \quad \text{or} \quad TI = \frac{TD_{50}}{ED_{50}}$$

A larger TI indicates a safer drug.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List the different phases of clinical trials and give their main objectives.**

**Model Answer:**

| Phase | Subjects | Main Objective |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase 0** | 10–15 volunteers | Microdosing, pharmacokinetic screening |
| **Phase I** | 20–100 healthy volunteers | Safety, tolerability, dose-ranging |
| **Phase II** | 100–300 patients | Efficacy, dose-response |
| **Phase III** | 1,000–3,000 patients | Confirm efficacy, monitor ADRs |
| **Phase IV** | Thousands (post-marketing) | Long-term safety, rare adverse reactions |

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts: Part (a) for **7 marks** and Part (b) for **7 marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Module 3 Choice 1**
**[KTU University Exam - Sample Question Modeled on 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **phases of clinical trials** with their objectives, sample sizes, and outcomes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **design and conduct of a bioequivalence study**. Include the acceptance criteria as per FDA guidelines. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Introduction:**
Clinical trials are research studies performed on human volunteers to evaluate the safety and efficacy of a new drug. They are the final and most critical step before a drug can be marketed.

**Detailed Explanation of Each Phase:**

**Phase 0 (Microdosing Studies):**
- Subjects: 10–15 healthy volunteers
- Objective: Pharmacokinetic screening using sub-pharmacological doses (≤ 100 μg)
- Duration: A few days
- Benefit: Reduces cost and time of early development

**Phase I (Safety & Pharmacokinetics):**
- Subjects: 20–100 healthy volunteers
- Objective: Determine maximum tolerated dose (MTD), dose-ranging, ADME
- Design: Open-label, single ascending dose (SAD) and multiple ascending dose (MAD)
- Outcome: Safe dose range for Phase II

**Phase II (Efficacy & Dose-Finding):**
- Subjects: 100–300 patients with the target disease
- Objective: Evaluate efficacy and side effects; determine optimal dose
- Design: Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled
- Subdivided into IIa (dose-finding) and IIb (efficacy confirmation)

**Phase III (Large-Scale Comparative Trials):**
- Subjects: 1,000–3,000 patients
- Objective: Confirm efficacy, monitor adverse drug reactions (ADRs), compare with standard treatment
- Design: Multicentric, randomized controlled trials (RCTs)
- Outcome: Data for regulatory submission (NDA/ANDA)

**Phase IV (Post-Marketing Surveillance):**
- Subjects: Thousands in real-world clinical practice
- Objective: Detect rare ADRs, long-term safety, drug interactions
- Conducted by: Pharmacovigilance programs

> **Conclusion:** Clinical trials progress from small, controlled safety studies to large, real-world efficacy studies, ensuring that only safe and effective drugs reach the public.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Introduction:**
Bioequivalence (BE) studies demonstrate that a generic drug product performs similarly to the innovator (brand) product in terms of rate and extent of absorption.

**Study Design — Standard Crossover Design:**

1. **Subject Selection:**
   - Healthy adult volunteers (18–55 years)
   - Normal BMI, non-smokers, no concomitant medication
   - Sample size: Typically 24–36 subjects (powered to detect 20% difference)

2. **Study Design: Two-Way Crossover**
   - Sequence TR: Test → Washout → Reference
   - Sequence RT: Reference → Washout → Test
   - Washout period: 5–7 elimination half-lives

3. **Dosing and Sampling:**
   - Single dose of test or reference under fasting conditions
   - Blood samples collected at predefined intervals (0, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24 h)

4. **Bioanalytical Method:**
   - HPLC-MS/MS validated per ICH M10
   - Validated for accuracy, precision, linearity, recovery

5. **Pharmacokinetic Analysis:**
   - C_max, AUC₀₋ₜ, AUC₀₋∞ calculated by non-compartmental methods
   - t_max noted directly from concentration-time curve

6. **Statistical Analysis:**
   - Log-transformed AUC and C_max
   - ANOVA: factors = sequence, subject(sequence), period, formulation
   - 90% Confidence Interval of Test/Reference ratio calculated

**Acceptance Criterion (per FDA):**
> The 90% CI for both AUC and C_max must lie within **80.00% – 125.00%**.

If the criterion is met → **Bioequivalence is established** → Generic product approved via ANDA (Abbreviated New Drug Application).

> **Conclusion:** BE studies form the scientific and regulatory basis for approval of generic medicines, ensuring therapeutic equivalence with the innovator product.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students often **lose 2–3 marks** by:
> - Confusing the **90% CI criterion** with a simple mean ratio test
> - Not mentioning the **washout period** calculation (5–7 t½)
> - Omitting the **log-transformation** step in statistical analysis
> - Forgetting to state the use of **HPLC-MS/MS** for bioanalysis

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Module 3 Choice 2**
**[KTU University Exam - Sample Question Modeled on 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Define and classify **toxicity studies**. Discuss the determination of **LD₅₀** and its significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **pharmaceutical evaluation of solid dosage forms** with reference to physical, chemical, and biological tests as per pharmacopoeial standards. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition of Toxicity Studies:**
Toxicity studies are systematic investigations of the adverse effects a substance can produce on living organisms, conducted as part of pre-clinical drug evaluation.

**Classification of Toxicity Studies:**

| Type | Duration | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Acute (Single dose)** | 24 h – 14 days | Determine LD₅₀, identify target organs |
| **Sub-acute (Repeated)** | 28–90 days | Identify cumulative toxicity |
| **Sub-chronic** | 90 days – 6 months | Dose-response relationship |
| **Chronic** | > 6 months – 2 years | Carcinogenicity, long-term safety |
| **Genotoxicity** | Variable | Ames test, chromosomal aberration |
| **Reproductive Toxicity** | Variable | Teratogenicity, fertility effects |

**Determination of LD₅₀:**

LD₅₀ (Lethal Dose 50) is the dose of a substance that kills 50% of the test animal population.

**Methods of Determination:**
1. **Litchfield-Wilcoxon Method** (Graphical method using log-dose vs. probit mortality)
2. **Karber's Method** (Arithmetic calculation)
3. **Miller-Tainter Method** (Graphical)
4. **OECD-fixed dose method** (Modern alternative; reduces animal use)

**Karber's Formula:**

$$LD_{50} = LD_{100} - \frac{\sum(a \cdot b)}{n}$$

where:
- $LD_{100}$ = Least dose causing 100% mortality
- $a$ = Difference between successive doses
- $b$ = Mean mortality at each dose
- $n$ = Number of animals per group

**Significance of LD₅₀:**
- Helps classify the toxicity of compounds (extremely toxic, highly toxic, etc.)
- Used to calculate **Therapeutic Index (TI)**: $TI = LD_{50}/ED_{50}$
- Higher LD₅₀ and lower ED₅₀ = Safer drug
- Helps determine the **starting dose for Phase I clinical trials**

> **Conclusion:** Toxicity studies and LD₅₀ determination are fundamental to establishing the safety profile of a drug before human exposure.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Pharmaceutical Evaluation of Solid Dosage Forms:**

Solid dosage forms (tablets, capsules, powders) must undergo rigorous quality evaluation per **USP, BP, IP** standards.

**A. Physical Evaluation:**

| Test | Method | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| **Appearance** | Visual inspection | Uniform color, no cracks |
| **Hardness** | Monsanto / Pfizer tester | 4–10 kg/cm² (typical) |
| **Friability** | Roche friabilator | ≤ 1.0% weight loss |
| **Weight Variation** | 20 tablets weighed individually | ± 5% (USP) |
| **Disintegration** | USP disintegration apparatus | ≤ 15 min (uncoated) |
| **Thickness & Diameter** | Vernier caliper | ± 5% of specification |
| **Moisture Content** | Karl Fischer titration | As per specification |

**B. Chemical Evaluation:**

- **Assay (Content Uniformity):** Determines % of active ingredient; must be **90%–110%** (USP)
- **pH:** For solutions and suspensions
- **Impurity Profiling:** Related substances, residual solvents (ICH Q3C, Q3D)
- **Stability Testing:** ICH Q1A (long-term, accelerated, intermediate conditions)

**C. Biological Evaluation:**

- **Sterility Test:** For sterile products (membrane filtration)
- **Microbial Limit Test (MLT):** Total aerobic count, total yeast/mold count
- **Endotoxin (LAL) Test:** For parenteral products
- **Dissolution Test:** USP Type I/II apparatus — measures rate of drug release

**Dissolution Testing Procedure:**
- Basket (USP Type I) at 50–100 rpm or Paddle (USP Type II) at 50–75 rpm
- 900 mL of specified medium at 37 ± 0.5 °C
- Samples withdrawn at intervals, analyzed by UV or HPLC
- Results expressed as cumulative % dissolved

**D. Performance Tests:**

- **In-Vitro In-Vivo Correlation (IVIVC):**
  - Level A: Point-to-point correlation
  - Level B: Statistical moment correlation
  - Level C: Single point correlation

> **Conclusion:** Comprehensive pharmaceutical evaluation ensures that solid dosage forms are safe, effective, stable, and of uniform quality throughout their shelf life.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Common mistakes in this question:
> - Confusing **disintegration** with **dissolution** (DT is physical breakdown; dissolution is molecular solubilization)
> - Forgetting **friability limit = ≤ 1.0%**
> - Omitting **bioburden / sterility / LAL tests** for parenterals
> - Not mentioning **ICH guidelines** for stability testing

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 Rapid Revision Checklist for "Evaluation" — Module 3**

### 🔑 Key Definitions
- **Bioavailability (F):** Rate and extent of drug reaching systemic circulation
- **Bioequivalence (BE):** Comparable rate and extent between two products
- **Bioassay:** Quantitative determination of drug potency using biological systems
- **Therapeutic Index (TI):** $LD_{50}/ED_{50}$ — higher is safer
- **AUC:** Total drug exposure; main BE parameter
- **C_max / t_max:** Peak concentration / time to peak

### 🔬 Evaluation Hierarchy
1. **In-vitro** (cells/tissues) → **In-vivo** (animals) → **Clinical** (humans) → **Post-marketing** (real-world)
2. **ADME:** Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion

### 📊 Clinical Trial Phases (Memory Trick: "**SAFE**")
- **Phase 0:** Microdosing (Screening)
- **Phase I:** Safety
- **Phase II:** Efficacy (Early)
- **Phase III:** Efficacy (Large-scale confirmation)
- **Phase IV:** Follow-up / Pharmacovigilance

### 📈 Toxicity Study Types (Memory Trick: "**ASCCR**")
- **A**cute, **S**ub-acute, **C**hronic, **C**arcinogenicity, **R**eproductive

### 📐 Critical Numerical Criteria
- **BE acceptance:** 90% CI within **80%–125%** for AUC and C_max
- **Friability:** ≤ **1.0%**
- **Weight variation:** ± **5%** (USP)
- **Assay:** **90%–110%** of label claim
- **f₂ similarity:** ≥ **50**
- **Washout period:** **5–7 × t½**
- **TI for safety:** ≥ **10**

### 🧪 Pharmaceutical Quality Tests
- **Physical:** Hardness, friability, DT, weight variation
- **Chemical:** Assay, impurity, stability, pH
- **Biological:** Sterility, MLT, LAL, dissolution

### ⚖️ Regulatory Guidelines to Remember
- **Schedule Y** — India (Clinical trials)
- **ICH-GCP** — International Good Clinical Practice
- **ICH Q1A** — Stability testing
- **ICH M10** — Bioanalytical method validation
- **Declaration of Helsinki** — Ethical principles for human research
- **CPCSEA** — Committee for Control and Supervision of Experiments on Animals (India)

### 💡 Common Exam Pitfalls
1. ❌ Confusing **disintegration** with **dissolution**
2. ❌ Saying **mean ratio** instead of **90% CI** for BE
3. ❌ Forgetting to **log-transform** BE data before ANOVA
4. ❌ Mixing up **LD₅₀** (toxicology) with **ED₅₀** (efficacy)
5. ❌ Missing the **washout period** concept in crossover design

> **Final Tip:** Always remember the **pyramid of evaluation** — molecules climb the ladder one step at a time, from test tubes to patients. Each step filters out the unsafe and ineffective, ensuring only the best reach the market.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Large Scale Manufacture and Packaging with Focus on Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Large Scale Manufacture and Packaging with Focus on Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: Large Scale Pharmaceutical Manufacture

**Large Scale Pharmaceutical Manufacture** is the industrial production of drug substances (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients, APIs) and drug products (tablets, capsules, injectables, syrups, ointments) in batch sizes typically ranging from **kilograms to multi-ton quantities**, carried out in **cGMP-compliant (current Good Manufacturing Practice)** facilities using specialized, validated process equipment designed for reproducibility, sterility, safety, and regulatory compliance.

The scope covers the entire chain from **API synthesis → Intermediate isolation → Formulation → Primary packaging → Secondary packaging → Tertiary packaging → Warehousing → Distribution**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PECHT751 – Module 3):**
> "Large scale manufacture and packaging of dosage forms — emphasis on **equipment selection, operating principles, material of construction, capacity calculation, and scale-up strategies** in compliance with **Schedule M / USFDA / WHO-GMP** norms."

### 1.2 Formal Definition: Pharmaceutical Packaging

**Pharmaceutical Packaging** is the science, art, and technology of enclosing or protecting drug products for **distribution, storage, sale, and use**. It is the coordinated system of preparing drug articles for transport, warehousing, logistics, and final presentation to the consumer, ensuring **product identity, stability, patient safety, regulatory compliance, and tamper evidence**.

Packaging is officially classified by the **United States Pharmacopoeia (USP)** and the **WHO** into three hierarchical tiers:

| Tier | Function | Examples |
|------|----------|----------|
| **Primary Packaging** | Direct contact with the dosage form | Blister foil, glass vial, ampoule, HDPE bottle, tube, sachet, strip |
| **Secondary Packaging** | Outermost retail container holding primary packs | Carton box, printed labels, patient information leaflet (PIL) |
| **Tertiary Packaging** | Bulk transport & logistics | Corrugated shipper box, shrink wrap, pallet |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture — The "Medicine Bakery + Amazon Warehouse" Analogy:**
>
> Think of a pharmaceutical plant as a **specialized bakery** that bakes *tablets* instead of biscuits. The **reactors** are the mixing bowls where Active Ingredients (the "flour") are blended with **excipients** (the "sugar, butter, baking powder"). The **granulator** is the dough-kneader, the **dryer** is the oven, the **tablet press** is the cookie-cutter stamping machine, and the **coater** is the chocolate-dipping station.
>
> Once the tablets are ready, the **packaging line** behaves like an **Amazon warehouse conveyor**:
> - **Blister machine** = wrapping each tablet individually (like bubble wrap).
> - **Bottle filler** = placing many tablets in a sealed jar.
> - **Cartoner** = slipping the wrapped pack into a gift box with a leaflet.
> - **Case packer** = loading 100 gift boxes into a shipping carton.
>
> The **regulatory "Health Inspector"** (USFDA/WHO) walks through periodically — if your "bakery" has dust, unlabeled jars, or open doors, your license is revoked. Hence **cGMP** (cleanliness, documentation, calibration, validation) is non-negotiable.

### 1.4 Industry-Standard Metrics (Bold Definitions)

- **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** is a multiplicative metric combining **Availability × Performance × Quality**, each expressed as a percentage. World-class pharmaceutical plants target **OEE ≥ 85%**.
- **cGMP (current Good Manufacturing Practice)** is the regulatory code enforced by 21 CFR Parts 210–211 (US) and Schedule M (India) governing every aspect of manufacture.
- **Scale-Up Factor (SF)** is the ratio of the **commercial batch size** to the **development/pilot batch size**, dimensionless.
- **Yield (%)** is the mass of product obtained from a process divided by the maximum theoretically possible mass, multiplied by 100.
- **Cleanroom Classification** is defined by **ISO 14644-1** limits on particles of size ≥ 0.5 µm per cubic meter (e.g., ISO 5 = Class 100, ISO 7 = Class 10,000).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Logic: The Manufacturing Logic Chain

A typical **large-scale solid oral dosage form (tablet) manufacturing** flow is sequential, with each step adding a specific physical/chemical transformation:

1. **Dispensing** — Controlled weighing of API and excipients under HEPA-filtered laminar flow booths.
2. **Sizing / Milling** — Reduction of particle size to ensure uniform content and dissolution.
3. **Blending** — Homogenization of dry powders in a **double-cone** or **V-blender** or high-shear **bin blender**.
4. **Granulation** — Aggregation of fines into free-flowing granules using a **high-shear wet granulator** or **fluid bed granulator**.
5. **Drying** — Removal of granulation solvent (water/IPA) using a **fluid bed dryer (FBD)** or **tray dryer**.
6. **Sizing of Granules** — Passing dried granules through a **cone mill** or **oscillating granulator**.
7. **Lubrication** — Addition of magnesium stearate, talc, colloidal silica in a final blender.
8. **Compression** — Tablet formation in a **rotary tablet press** (multi-station).
9. **Coating** — Application of film, sugar, or enteric coat in a **perforated pan coater**.
10. **Packaging** — Blistering, bottling, cartoning, labeling, and case-packing.

### 2.2 Key Equipment: Operating Principle & Construction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equipment List — Must-Memorize for KTU Exam**

| # | Equipment | Working Principle | Capacity (Industrial) | Material of Construction |
|---|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Stirred Tank Reactor (STR)** | Mechanical agitation + jacket heat transfer | 500 L – 20,000 L | SS 316L (pharma-grade) |
| 2 | **High-Shear Wet Granulator** | Impeller + chopper for powder wetting | 50 L – 1200 L | SS 316L, mirror-polished |
| 3 | **Fluid Bed Dryer/Granulator** | Pneumatic fluidization | 30 kg – 800 kg batch | SS 316L with blow-back filters |
| 4 | **Rotary Tablet Press** | Compression via upper/lower punches in rotating die table | 5,000 – 1,000,000 tablets/hr | Tooling: D2 / S7 tool steel |
| 5 | **Perforated Pan Coater** | Spray application of coating dispersion on tumbling tablets | 30 kg – 500 kg load | SS 316L with anti-static finish |
| 6 | **Blister Packaging Machine** | Thermoforming of PVC/PVDC foil + lidding Al foil | 200 – 800 blisters/min | Pharma-grade Al alloy dies |
| 7 | **Bottle Filling Line** | Volumetric / gravimetric dosing + capping + induction sealing | 60 – 300 bottles/min | SS 316L contact parts |
| 8 | **Cartoning Machine** | Horizontal pick-and-place of blister strips into cartons | 80 – 250 cartons/min | SS frame with carton magazine |
| 9 | **Autoclave (Sterilization)** | Saturated steam at **121 °C, 15 psi, 20 min** (F₀ ≥ 8) | 100 L – 5000 L chamber | SS 316L with bioseal |
| 10 | **Aseptic Filling Line (Vial/Ampoule)** | Restricted Access Barrier System (RABS) under ISO 5 LAF | 100 – 600 vials/min | SS 316L + borosilicate glass |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are mathematically verifiable and tested in KTU University Examinations. The notation uses standard chemical engineering conventions.

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Units | Engineering Application |
|---|-------------------|------------------|-------|--------------------------|
| 1 | $Y \,(\%) = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | Process yield | % | Batch documentation, validation |
| 2 | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall equipment effectiveness | dimensionless (×100) | Plant KPI benchmarking |
| 3 | $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot n^{3} \cdot D^{5}$ | Impeller power consumption (Newtonian fluid) | W | Mixer scale-up |
| 4 | $N_p = f(\text{Re})$ | Power number correlation | dimensionless | Mixer selection |
| 5 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot n \cdot D^{2}}{\mu}$ | Impeller Reynolds number | dimensionless | Flow regime classification |
| 6 | $\dfrac{Q_{p}}{Q_{m}} = \left(\dfrac{D_{p}}{D_{m}}\right)^{2.5}$ | Geometric scale-up for stirred tanks (equal P/V) | dimensionless | Constant power per unit volume |
| 7 | $\text{SF} = \dfrac{M_{\text{commercial}}}{M_{\text{pilot}}}$ | Scale-up factor | dimensionless | Batch size jump |
| 8 | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot (R_{c}+R_{m})}$ | Darcy filtration equation | m³/s | Filter sizing |
| 9 | $C = N_{\text{stations}} \times n_{\text{rev}} \times t$ | Tablet press capacity (tablets produced) | tablets | Production planning |
| 10 | $R_{\text{drying}} = -\dfrac{m_{s}}{A} \cdot \dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | Drying rate (mass transfer) | kg/(m²·s) | FBD / tray dryer design |
| 11 | $F_{0} = \Delta t \cdot 10^{\,(T-121)/z}$ | Sterilization lethality (z = 10 °C) | min | Autoclave validation |
| 12 | $X_{\text{water}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{evap}}}{m_{\text{solid}}} \times 100$ | Moisture content (% w.b.) | % | Endpoint of drying |
| 13 | $\eta_{\text{util}} = \dfrac{t_{\text{op}}}{t_{\text{avail}}} \times 100$ | Equipment utilization | % | OEE sub-component |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic tip for the KTU board exam:** "*Yield × OEE × Time = Money.*" Always associate process efficiency questions with these three multipliers.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Mixer scale-up (Eq. 3–6)** is used when a lab formulation (1 kg) is migrated to a commercial plant (500 kg) — engineers rely on constant Power per Volume (P/V) or constant tip speed to maintain granulation kinetics.
- **F₀ (Eq. 11)** is the **bioburden safety number** evaluated by **FDA inspectors** for terminal sterilization of parenterals.
- **OEE (Eq. 2)** is the single most-watched KPI in any **WHO-GMP audit**.
- **Darcy's law (Eq. 8)** drives the design of **Nutsche filters** and **rotary vacuum filters** used for API isolation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Yield and Material Balance (Tablet Batch)

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A pharmaceutical company wants to manufacture **Paracetamol tablets 500 mg** in a batch of **200,000 tablets**. The formulation contains 60% w/w Paracetamol API, 30% Lactose, 8% Starch paste, and 2% Magnesium stearate. Calculate:
> 1. Mass of API per tablet
> 2. Total batch mass
> 3. Mass of each ingredient
> 4. Theoretical yield and actual yield if 198,500 good tablets are produced

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the unit dose of API**

$$m_{\text{API per tablet}} = 500 \;\text{mg} \times 0.60 = 300 \;\text{mg} = 0.3 \;\text{g}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the total batch theoretical mass**

$$M_{\text{theoretical}} = 200{,}000 \times 0.5 \;\text{g} = 100{,}000 \;\text{g} = 100 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the mass of each component (formulation table)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{API}} &= 0.60 \times 100 \;\text{kg} = 60 \;\text{kg} \\
M_{\text{Lactose}} &= 0.30 \times 100 \;\text{kg} = 30 \;\text{kg} \\
M_{\text{Starch}} &= 0.08 \times 100 \;\text{kg} = 8 \;\text{kg} \\
M_{\text{Mg stearate}} &= 0.02 \times 100 \;\text{kg} = 2 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Material balance check (input vs. output)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{input}} &= 60 + 30 + 8 + 2 = 100 \;\text{kg} \\
M_{\text{output, theoretical}} &= 200{,}000 \times 0.5 \;\text{g} = 100 \;\text{kg} \\
\text{Mass loss (granulation + drying)} &\approx 2 \;\text{kg} \;\Rightarrow\; M_{\text{dry granules}} = 98 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical yield of tablets**

$$Y_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{200{,}000 \times 0.5 \;\text{g}}{100{,}000 \;\text{g (input)}} \times 100 = 100\%$$

**Step 6 — Actual yield (with rejects, samples, in-process losses)**

$$Y_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{198{,}500 \times 0.5 \;\text{g}}{100{,}000 \;\text{g}} \times 100 = 99.25\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A yield of **99.25%** is excellent. Pharmaceutical industry benchmark for tablet compression is **≥ 98%**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Scale-Up of a High-Shear Granulator

> **Problem:** A lab-scale high-shear granulator has the following operating parameters:
> - **Pilot volume** $V_{p} = 10 \;\text{L}$, impeller diameter $D_{p} = 0.15 \;\text{m}$, speed $n_{p} = 300 \;\text{rpm}$
> - It must be scaled to a **production scale** of $V_{m} = 600 \;\text{L}$ using geometric similarity and **constant Power per Unit Volume (P/V)**.
> - Granulator density $\rho = 1100 \;\text{kg/m}^{3}$ is assumed constant.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Determine the scale-up diameter using volume ratio (cubic similarity)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{V_{m}}{V_{p}} &= \left(\dfrac{D_{m}}{D_{p}}\right)^{3} \\
\dfrac{D_{m}}{D_{p}} &= \left(\dfrac{600}{10}\right)^{1/3} = (60)^{1/3} = 3.915 \\
D_{m} &= 3.915 \times 0.15 = 0.587 \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the constant (P/V) scale-up criterion (Eq. 6 form)**

$$
\dfrac{n_{m}}{n_{p}} = \left(\dfrac{D_{p}}{D_{m}}\right)^{2/3}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{m} &= 300 \times (1/3.915)^{2/3} \\
&= 300 \times (0.2554)^{0.6667} \\
&= 300 \times 0.4029 = 120.9 \;\text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute actual power required using $P = N_{p}\rho n^{3} D^{5}$ with $N_{p} = 5.0$ (assumed from pilot at turbulent Re)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{p} &= 5.0 \times 1100 \times (5)^{3} \times (0.15)^{5} \\
&\approx 5.0 \times 1100 \times 125 \times 7.59 \times 10^{-5} \\
&\approx 52.2 \;\text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Power at production scale**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{m} &= 5.0 \times 1100 \times (2.014)^{3} \times (0.587)^{5} \\
&\approx 5.0 \times 1100 \times 8.18 \times 0.0695 \\
&\approx 3126 \;\text{W} \approx 3.13 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify P/V constant (sanity check)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(P/V)_{p} &= 52.2 / 10 = 5.22 \;\text{W/L} \\
(P/V)_{m} &= 3126 / 600 = 5.21 \;\text{W/L} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** A 60× volume scale-up requires a **1.4× impeller speed reduction** and a **60× power increase**, while keeping the hydrodynamic regime identical.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Equipment Selection Decision Tree

| Selection Criterion | Best Equipment | Reason |
|---------------------|---------------|--------|
| Heat-sensitive API (avoid high T) | **Fluid Bed Dryer** | Short residence, mild T ≈ 50 °C |
| Sterile liquid parenteral | **Aseptic Filling + Autoclave (F₀ ≥ 8)** | USP <71> compliance |
| High-output OSD (>500 k tab/hr) | **Rotary Tablet Press (multi-station)** | Continuous indexing of dies |
| Effervescent tablets | **Tube filling + Strip Pack** | Moisture-proof Al-Al foil |
| Photosensitive drug | **Amber glass bottle + opaque blister** | UV protection |
| Hygroscopic API | **Strip pack (Al/Al blister)** | Low MVTR ≈ 0.0 g/m²/day |
| High-viscosity ointment | **Tube filling + induction sealer** | Hermetic closure |

---

### 3.4 Equipment Validation & Qualification (Process Performance Qualification)

Under **USFDA Process Validation Guidance (2011)**, every equipment in pharma manufacture is qualified in three stages:

1. **IQ (Installation Qualification)** — Verifies correct installation per URS (User Requirement Specification).
2. **OQ (Operational Qualification)** — Confirms operation across defined ranges using challenge tests.
3. **PQ (Performance Qualification)** — Demonstrates consistent product quality using actual commercial batches (typically n = 3 consecutive successful batches).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** A 14-mark question on Module 3 often asks: *"Explain the process validation lifecycle for an equipment used in tablet manufacture."* Always include the **V-Model**: URS → FRS → DS → IQ → OQ → PQ.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**Differentiate between primary, secondary, and tertiary packaging with two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — distribute as 1 + 1 + 1):**

**Primary Packaging** is in direct contact with the dosage form. It protects the drug from environmental degradation and must be **pharmacopoeia-grade** (non-reactive, non-leaching). *Examples:* Blister foil (PVC/PVdC) for tablets, **Type-I borosilicate glass ampoule** for injectables.

**Secondary Packaging** is the outer carton or container that holds the primary pack. It carries **patient information, batch details, MRP, bar code, and PIL (Patient Information Leaflet)**. *Examples:* Printed cardboard carton, shrink-wrapped bundle of blister strips.

**Tertiary Packaging** is used for **bulk transport and storage**. It is typically a corrugated shipper box on a wooden/plastic pallet. *Examples:* 5-ply corrugated carton, stretch-wrapped pallet.

---

**Q.A2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Explain the principle of operation of a fluid bed dryer. Mention any two advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — distribute as 2 + 1):**

A **Fluid Bed Dryer (FBD)** operates on the principle of **fluidization**, where heated air at a carefully controlled velocity passes upward through a bed of wet granules, suspending them in a dynamic "boiling" state. This maximizes the **heat-transfer coefficient (h ≈ 200–500 W/m²·K)** and exposes enormous surface area, giving very rapid and uniform drying. The granules are held on a perforated stainless-steel retaining plate, with HEPA-filtered process air (typically inlet at 50–80 °C) fluidizing the bed. Moisture-laden air exits through bag filters at the top, while the dried granules are discharged from the side.

**Advantages (any two for 1 mark):**
1. Drying is **5–10× faster** than tray drying.
2. Combined **granulation + drying** in single unit (FBDG).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice Provided)

---

**Q.B.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**(a) Describe the construction and working of a rotary tablet press with a neat diagram. List four process parameters that must be controlled during compression. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the equipment and material requirements for the manufacture of sterile parenteral dosage forms as per Schedule M. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Rotary Tablet Press (7 marks)**

A **rotary tablet press** is a high-speed, multi-station continuous tableting machine. Its main parts are:

- **Die table / turret:** A rotating circular disc carrying **multiple die cavities** (typically 8, 10, 16, 27, 36, 45, 61, or 75 stations). Material: hardened tool steel.
- **Upper and lower punches:** Slides vertically in punch guides. Cam tracks dictate vertical motion. Tips made of D2/S7 steel.
- **Dies:** Cylindrical cavities in the die table that hold powder.
- **Feed frame:** Rotates with a paddle to feed granules from the hopper into the die cavity.
- **Cam tracks:** Three (top punch, bottom punch) precisely engineered to control fill → compress → eject.
- **Pre-compression station:** Optional but desirable for double compression.
- **Take-off bar:** Pushes the tablet out of the die for discharge.

**Working Cycle (per punch pair):**
1. **Fill (die at hopper position)** — Granules gravity-fed into die.
2. **Dosing** — Excess scraped off by the feed frame paddle.
3. **Pre-compression** (if present) — Light compaction to remove entrapped air.
4. **Main compression** — Both punches converge via cams at peak pressure **5–30 kN**.
5. **Ejection** — Lower punch rises; take-off bar deflects tablet out.

**Process parameters to control (any 4 for 2 marks):**
1. Tablet weight / fill depth
2. Pre-compression force
3. Main compression force
4. Tablet hardness, thickness, friability
5. Turret speed (rpm)
6. Feeder speed (granule flow rate)

**(b) Sterile Parenteral Manufacture per Schedule M (7 marks)**

Schedule M (India) and 21 CFR 211.94 (US) impose **stringent requirements** for parenterals:

**Building & Cleanroom:**
- **Classified clean areas** as per ISO 14644: Grade A (ISO 5) LAF at filling point, surrounding Grade B (ISO 6) background, with adjacent Grade C (ISO 7) and Grade D (ISO 8) areas.
- **HVAC** with HEPA (H13/H14) filters, ≥ 20 air changes/hour, controlled T (21 ± 3 °C), RH (45 ± 5 %), positive pressure cascade.

**Equipment:**
- **Stainless steel 316L** (electropolished, Ra ≤ 0.8 µm) for all contact parts.
- **Autoclave** validated for F₀ ≥ 8 min at 121 °C, 15 psi.
- **Aseptic filling line** under Restricted Access Barrier System (RABS) or isolator.
- **Aseptic gowning** for personnel.
- **WFI (Water for Injection)** storage and distribution loop at > 80 °C.
- **Vial washers, depyrogenation tunnels** (300 °C), laminar airflow workbenches.
- **Sterility testing (membrane filtration)** per USP <71>.

**Materials:** Type-I borosilicate glass vials, butyl rubber stoppers, flip-off aluminum seals, validated sterilization.

**Personnel:** Trained in aseptic technique, gowning qualification, microbiological monitoring.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**(a) Explain the construction, working, and applications of a blister packaging machine. Discuss the materials of construction for blister foil. (7 marks)**

**(b) With the help of a flow diagram, describe the large-scale manufacturing of paracetamol tablets. Indicate the equipment used at each stage. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Blister Packaging Machine (7 marks)**

A **blister packaging machine** forms a cavity in a thermoformable plastic film, fills it with a unit dose, and seals it with a lidding aluminum foil — producing unit-dose packs.

**Construction:**
- **Indexing chain / cam-driven indexing** with a forming station, feeding station, sealing station, perforating, cutting, and ejection.
- **Heating system** for the plastic film (radiant or contact heaters).
- **Forming dies** (female mold) for cavity shaping.
- **Filling hopper with dosing disc / auger** for tablets, capsules.
- **Sealing dies** (heated platen, 150–200 °C) for lidding.
- **Coding station** for batch number / MRP.
- **Cutting / punching dies** for finished blister cards.

**Working:**
1. PVC/PVdC film unwound from reel.
2. Heated to its softening temperature (≈ 130–160 °C).
3. Vacuum-formed or pressure-formed into cavities.
4. Product (tablet / capsule) dropped into each cavity.
5. Aluminum lidding foil (20–30 µm) heat-sealed.
6. Perforation, batch coding, cutting into strips/cards.

**Materials of Construction for Foils:**
- **Forming film:** PVC (250 µm) — for non-hygroscopic drugs; **PVC/PVdC** (250/40 or 60/90 g·m⁻²) — moisture barrier; **Aclar/PCTFE** — high-barrier; **Aluminum cold-form foil (Al/Al)** — for hygroscopic or photosensitive drugs (push-through design, not applicable).
- **Lidding foil:** 20 µm hard aluminum + 6–8 g·m⁻² heat-seal lacquer (vinyl, acrylic, or surlyn).
- Sealing temperature: 150–200 °C; pressure 2–4 bar; dwell time 0.5–2 s.

**Applications:** Unit-dose tablets, capsules, lozenges; hospital unit-dose dispensing; clinical trial supplies; OTC products.

**(b) Paracetamol Tablet Manufacturing (7 marks)**

Refer to **Section 4.1 flow diagram**. The complete process with equipment:

| Stage | Operation | Equipment |
|-------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | Dispensing of API and excipients | **Laminar Air Flow (LAF) booth** with calibrated balances |
| 2 | Sizing of API crystals | **Cone mill / hammer mill** (sieve 1–2 mm) |
| 3 | Dry mixing | **V-blender / double-cone blender** (10–30 min) |
| 4 | Wet granulation | **High-shear wet granulator** (impeller + chopper) — binder addition |
| 5 | Drying | **Fluid Bed Dryer (FBD)** — inlet 60 °C, endpoint ≈ 2 % LOD |
| 6 | Sizing of dry granules | **Oscillating granulator / cone mill** (sieve 0.8–1.0 mm) |
| 7 | Lubrication | **Bin blender** — Mg stearate (2 min only) |
| 8 | Compression | **Rotary tablet press** (hardness 80–120 N, weight 500 mg ± 5 %) |
| 9 | Coating (optional) | **Perforated pan coater** (HPMC, PEG, TiO₂) |
| 10 | Blistering | **Blister packaging machine** (PVC/Al) |
| 11 | Cartoning | **Cartoning machine** (with PIL) |
| 12 | Case packing | **Automatic case packer** |
| 13 | Storage | **Climate-controlled warehouse** (T 25 ± 2 °C, RH 60 ± 5 %) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Students often confuse *primary* and *secondary* packaging. Always state which is in **direct contact** with the drug.
> - **Common mistake 2:** In scale-up questions, students forget the **2/3 exponent** in impeller speed scaling under constant P/V — leading to wrong power calculation. (Use $n_{m} = n_{p} \cdot (D_{p}/D_{m})^{2/3}$.)
> - **Common mistake 3:** Forgetting to mention **material of construction (SS 316L)** in equipment-based questions. Examiners specifically allocate 1 mark for this.
> - **Common mistake 4:** In sterilization, students write "autoclave at 121 °C" without specifying **15 psi and 20 min F₀ ≥ 8**. This is a half-mark deduction per checklist.
> - **Common mistake 5:** Describing *what* an equipment does but not *why* it is used (e.g., why SS 316L? because of corrosion resistance and GMP-required low surface roughness Ra ≤ 0.8 µm).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read the night before the KTU exam**

- **cGMP (current Good Manufacturing Practice)** is the legal backbone of every pharmaceutical operation. Always reference Schedule M, 21 CFR 210/211, or EU GMP.
- **Material of construction** for almost every contact part is **SS 316L** (austenitic, low carbon, Mo-stabilized, electropolished, Ra ≤ 0.8 µm).
- **Tablet press types:** Single-station (eccentric) vs **rotary** (multi-station) — for scale production, rotary is the industry standard.
- **High-shear granulator** uses **impeller + chopper**; **fluid bed granulator** uses **air-fluidization with top-spray binder**.
- **FBD (Fluid Bed Dryer)** is faster and gentler than tray drying. Monitor with **inlet/outlet ΔT and LOD (Loss on Drying).**
- **Coater** types: sugar coating (pan), film coating (perforated pan), enteric coating (spray). Polymer examples: HPMC, ethyl cellulose, Eudragit L/S.
- **Packaging tiers:** Primary (in contact) → Secondary (carton + label) → Tertiary (shipper).
- **Foil systems:** PVC (low barrier), **PVC/PVdC** (medium), **Al/Al cold form** (highest, for hygroscopic drugs), Aclar/PCTFE (high moisture barrier).
- **Sterilization** for parenterals: terminal moist heat (F₀ ≥ 8) or aseptic filtration (0.22 µm) followed by sterile filling.
- **Yield benchmarks (KTU expects these numbers):**
  - API synthesis: 70–95 %
  - Granulation + drying: 96–99 %
  - Compression: 98–99.5 %
  - Coating: 96–99 %
  - Packaging: 99–99.9 %
- **Scale-up golden rule:** Maintain **P/V (Power per Volume) constant** ⇒ $n_{m} = n_{p} \cdot (D_{p}/D_{m})^{2/3}$.
- **OEE = A × P × Q**, target **≥ 85 %** for a world-class pharmaceutical plant.
- **Validation** is IQ → OQ → PQ, and 3 consecutive successful PQ batches are required to declare commercial readiness.
- **Common KTU asked equipment:** Rotary tablet press, FBD, coater, blister machine, autoclave, aseptic filling line.
- **Always write the formula** if a numerical is asked — even a wrong numerical with a correct formula gets partial marks.
- **Mnemonic to remember the four coater polymers: "HECP"** — HPMC, Ethyl cellulose, Cellulose acetate phthalate, Polymethacrylates (Eudragit).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line exam anchor:** *"Pharmaceutical manufacture is the marriage of chemistry (synthesis), physics (formulation), engineering (equipment), and law (cGMP)."* Memorize this line — it opens 2-mark definition questions in KTU 2024 scheme ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Biphasic - Emulsions ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Biphasic Liquid Dosage Form – Emulsion** > An **emulsion** is a thermodynamically unstable, heterogeneous biphasic liquid dosage system consisting of **two immiscible liquids** (typically an oil phase and an aqueous phase), where one phase is uniformly dispersed as **droplets (dispersed/internal phase)** within the other continuous phase (external/dispersion medium), stabilized kinetically by a third component known as the **emulsifying agent (emulsifier or surfactant)**. **KTU 2024 Biphasic Classification – Emulsions:** | Type | Abbreviation | Continuous Phase | Dispersed Phase | Common Name | |---|---|---|---|---| | Oil-in-Water | **O/W** | Water (aqueous) | Oil | Milk-type | | Water-in-Oil | **W/O** | Oil | Water | Butter-type | | Multiple Emulsion W/O/W | **W/O/W** | Water | Water-in-Oil | Double emulsion | | Multiple Emulsion O/W/O | **O/W/O** | Oil | Oil-in-Water | Inverse double emulsion | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Emulsion Droplet Size Standard (KTU/Pharmaceutics Convention):** > - **Macroemulsions:** 0.1 – 100 μm (turbid, milky) > - **Microemulsions:** 10 – 100 nm (transparent/translucent, thermodynamically stable) > - **Nanoemulsions:** 20 – 200 nm (kinetically stable, transparent) > - Droplet sizes follow **Stokes' Law** settling behavior. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy – "The Mayonnaise Jar & The Cleaning Solvent"** > Imagine a salad dressing bottle. When you shake **olive oil + vinegar** vigorously, you get a cloudy, milky mixture. But the moment you stop shaking, the oil and vinegar separate into two layers. **This is exactly what an emulsion is** — a temporary forced marriage between two liquids that fundamentally refuse to mix (like oil and water). > > The reason they separate is **surface tension** — each liquid wants to minimize its contact with the other. The **emulsifying agent** (egg yolk in mayonnaise) acts like a **diplomatic translator** standing at the oil-water border. It has one end (hydrophilic/lipophobic) that "speaks water" and another end (lipophilic/hydrophobic) that "speaks oil," allowing the two to coexist in a temporarily stable mixture. > > If you use **egg yolk (lecithin)** as in mayonnaise → you get an **O/W emulsion** (oil droplets in water/vinegar). > If you use **heavy butter (w/o emulsifier)** → you get a **W/O emulsion** (water droplets trapped in fat). ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Interfacial tension (γ) of pure oil–water interface:** approximately **30 – 50 mN/m** (dynes/cm) without surfactant. - **Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC)** of typical surfactants: **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ M**. - **Hydrophile-Lipophile Balance (HLB) scale:** 0 to 20 (Griffin scale, 1949). - **Kinematic viscosity** of pharmaceutical emulsions: typically **0.5 – 5 Pa·s** depending on phase ratio. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Griffin's HLB Rule (KTU 2024 Highlight):** > - **HLB 3 – 6:** W/O emulsifiers (e.g., Span 20 = 8.6, Sorbitan oleate) > - **HLB 8 – 18:** O/W emulsifiers (e.g., Tween 80 = 15, Polysorbate 80) > - **HLB 7:** Wetting agents (transition region) > - **HLB 15 – 18:** Solubilizers ### 1.4 Symbols & Nomenclature (KTU Board Standard) | Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit | |---|---|---| | γ | Interfacial tension | mN/m or N/m | | η | Viscosity of continuous phase | Pa·s | | r | Radius of dispersed droplet | m | | ρ₁, ρ₂ | Densities of dispersed and continuous phases | kg/m³ | | g | Acceleration due to gravity | 9.81 m/s² | | v | Velocity of sedimentation | m/s | | Δρ | Density difference (ρ₁ − ρ₂) | kg/m³ | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Concept – The Four Emulsification Theories Emulsions require a **third substance** to form and remain stable. Four classical theories explain how this emulsifier works at the molecular level. KTU examiners frequently ask this as a 7-mark question. #### Theory 1: **Surface Tension Theory (Interfacial Tension Theory) – Bancroft (1913)** - **Why:** Pure oil-water interface has high interfacial tension (γ ≈ 30–50 mN/m), which causes droplets to coalesce. - **How:** The emulsifier **adsorbs at the oil-water interface**, lowering γ to **< 10 mN/m**. - **Result:** Reduced γ means reduced free energy of the system (ΔG = γ·ΔA), so the system becomes more thermodynamically favorable. #### Theory 2: **Oriented-Wedge Theory – Harkins (1917)** - **Why:** Emulsifier molecules have **asymmetric shapes**. - **How:** A surfactant with a **bulky hydrophilic head and a thin lipophilic tail** wedges into the O/W interface, curving the interface such that the **larger group** becomes the **continuous phase**. - **Rule:** The phase in which the emulsifier is **more soluble** forms the **continuous phase** (Bancroft's rule, equivalent statement). #### Theory 3: **Plastic Film Theory – Briggs (1920s)** - **Why:** A monomolecular film of emulsifier surrounds each droplet. - **How:** A **condensed, strong, coherent film** acts as a mechanical barrier, preventing droplet coalescence. The **stronger the film**, the **more stable the emulsion**. #### Theory 4: **Electrical Double-Layer Theory (DLVO-Inspired)** - **Why:** Droplets carry **electrical charge** (e.g., ionized surfactants give ±Z potential). - **How:** Like charges on droplets cause **electrostatic repulsion** → prevents coalescence. - **Magnitude:** Zeta potential > **±30 mV** is generally considered a stable colloidal system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bancroft's Rule (KTU 2024 – High-Yield):** > *"The phase in which the emulsifier is more soluble becomes the continuous (external) phase."* > - Sodium stearate is **water-soluble** → forms **O/W** emulsion. > - Calcium stearate is **oil-soluble** → forms **W/O** emulsion. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates **all KTU-board-tested equations** for emulsions. These appear repeatedly in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) problems. | # | Equation | Description | Standard Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\Delta G = \gamma \cdot \Delta A$ | Free energy of emulsification | J (joules) | | 2 | $v = \dfrac{2r^2 (\rho_1 - \rho_2) g}{9\eta}$ | **Stokes' Law** – sedimentation velocity of droplet | m/s | | 3 | $\text{HLB} = 20 \cdot \dfrac{M_h}{M_h + M_l}$ | Griffin HLB (saponification-based for fatty esters) | dimensionless (0–20) | | 4 | $\text{HLB}_{\text{required}} = \text{HLB}_{\text{oil}} \pm 1$ | Required HLB for an oil phase (1-unit tolerance) | dimensionless | | 5 | $\text{HLB}_{\text{mix}} = \sum_i (f_i \cdot \text{HLB}_i)$ | HLB of surfactant blend (mass fraction $f_i$) | dimensionless | | 6 | $P = \dfrac{4\gamma}{r}$ | **Laplace Pressure** inside a droplet (P = internal pressure) | Pa | | 7 | $\text{Creaming rate} \propto \dfrac{r^2 \cdot \Delta\rho}{\eta}$ | Derived from Stokes' Law for cream/settle velocity | m/s | | 8 | $W_{\text{coalescence}} = \pi r^2 \gamma (1 - \cos\theta)$ | Work of adhesion for droplet coalescence | J | | 9 | $V_{\text{emulsion}} = V_{\text{oil}} + V_{\text{water}} + V_{\text{emulsifier}}$ | Volume additivity (approximation) | mL or L | | 10 | $\phi_c = 0.74$ | **Maximum packing fraction** of monodisperse spheres (random close packing) | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units Conversion Quick-Reference for KTU Calculations:** > - 1 poise = 0.1 Pa·s > - 1 dyne/cm = 1 mN/m > - 1 μm = 10⁻⁶ m > - 1 cP (centipoise) = 10⁻³ Pa·s ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Pharmaceutical Utility > [!TIP] > - **Pharmaceutical O/W emulsions** deliver **poorly water-soluble drugs** (e.g., cyclosporine, ritonavir) for oral and IV administration. > - **Cosmetic W/O emulsions** form **waterproof cold creams** and **barrier ointments**. > - **IV lipid emulsions** (e.g., Intralipid® 20%) deliver calories and fat-soluble vitamins to patients unable to eat orally. > - **Topical corticosteroid emulsions** (e.g., hydrocortisone cream) use O/W for easy washability. > - **Vaccine adjuvants** (e.g., Freund's adjuvant – W/O) use emulsion technology to enhance immune response. > - **Multiple emulsions W/O/W** are used for **sustained/controlled release** of drugs (entrapping water-soluble drugs in inner aqueous phase). ### 2.4 Classification of Emulsifying Agents (KTU 2024 Categorization) | Class | Examples | HLB Range | Emulsion Type Formed | |---|---|---|---| | **Natural** (Anionic) | Sodium lauryl sulfate, Sodium oleate | ~40 | O/W | | **Natural** (Non-ionic) | Lecithin (egg yolk), Cholesterol | 8 – 10 | O/W or W/O | | **Semisynthetic** | Methylcellulose, Sodium CMC, Tween 80 | 10 – 16 | O/W | | **Synthetic Non-ionic** | Span 20, Span 80, Polysorbates | 1.8 – 16.7 | Span → W/O; Tween → O/W | | **Synthetic Anionic** | Sodium stearate, SLS | High | O/W | | **Synthetic Cationic** | Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) | High | O/W | | **Finely divided solids** | Bentonite, Veegum, Cab-O-Sil | — | Pickering emulsions (both) | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stokes' Law (Applied to Emulsion Creaming/Sedimentation) **Starting Premise:** A spherical droplet of radius $r$ moving under gravity through a viscous continuous phase experiences three forces: gravitational pull downward, buoyancy upward, and viscous drag opposing motion. **Step 1: Identify the forces on a single droplet.** The net downward force (gravity minus buoyancy) is: $$F_{\text{net}} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 (\rho_1 - \rho_2) g$$ **Why this form?** The volume of a sphere is $\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$. Multiplying by the density difference gives the effective mass per unit acceleration. The acceleration is $g$. **Step 2: Apply Stokes' Law for viscous drag on a sphere.** For low Reynolds number (laminar flow, Re < 1), the drag force is: $$F_{\text{drag}} = 6 \pi \eta r v$$ **Why this form?** This is the well-known Stokes' drag equation derived from solving the Navier-Stokes equations for creeping flow around a sphere. $\eta$ is the dynamic viscosity of the continuous phase. **Step 3: At terminal velocity, forces balance.** The droplet accelerates until $F_{\text{net}} = F_{\text{drag}}$: $$\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 (\rho_1 - \rho_2) g = 6 \pi \eta r v$$ **Step 4: Solve algebraically for v.** Cancel $\pi$ from both sides: $$\frac{4}{3} r^3 (\rho_1 - \rho_2) g = 6 \eta r v$$ Divide both sides by $r$: $$\frac{4}{3} r^2 (\rho_1 - \rho_2) g = 6 \eta v$$ Multiply both sides by $\frac{1}{6\eta}$: $$v = \frac{4 r^2 (\rho_1 - \rho_2) g}{3 \cdot 6\eta}$$ Simplify the denominator: $$v = \frac{4 r^2 (\rho_1 - \rho_2) g}{18\eta} = \frac{2 r^2 (\rho_1 - \rho_2) g}{9\eta}$$ **Final Stokes' Law Equation for Emulsion Droplet Velocity:** $$v = \frac{2 r^2 (\rho_1 - \rho_2) g}{9\eta}$$ **Physical Interpretation for KTU Answer Writing:** - **Doubling the droplet radius** → velocity increases by **4×** (quadratic dependence). - **Halving the viscosity** → velocity doubles. - **Density difference** dictates whether droplet **creams (rises)** or **sediments (sinks)**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Laplace Pressure Inside an Emulsion Droplet **Starting Premise:** A curved liquid-liquid interface exerts a higher pressure on the concave (inner) side than the convex (outer) side. This pressure difference maintains droplet sphericity. **Step 1: Consider a soap-film analogue first.** For a soap bubble of radius $r$ with surface tension $\gamma$, the pressure difference across the film is given by the **Young-Laplace equation** for a single interface: $$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$ **Why the factor of 2?** A soap bubble has *two* interfaces (inner and outer), so the total surface tension contribution doubles. **Step 2: For an emulsion droplet, there is only ONE interface.** The droplet of oil (or water) is surrounded by a single liquid-liquid interface. Therefore, the Young-Laplace equation becomes: $$P_{\text{inside}} - P_{\text{outside}} = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$ But KTU board convention simplifies this for a droplet with a single interface as: $$\boxed{P = \frac{4\gamma}{r}}$$ **Why the factor of 4 in some textbooks?** This version comes from the **2D surface energy balance** for a fluid sphere where the pressure-volume work $P \cdot dV$ equals surface energy change $\gamma \cdot dA$. Some references include the interfacial tension acting on both sides of the mathematical surface, giving $4\gamma/r$. Both $2\gamma/r$ and $4\gamma/r$ forms appear in pharmaceutical textbooks — KTU accepts both with proper explanation. **Step 3: Practical KTU Conclusion.** For an emulsion droplet of radius $r = 1\ \mu\text{m} = 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$ with $\gamma = 10\ \text{mN/m} = 10^{-2}\ \text{N/m}$: $$P = \frac{4 \times 10^{-2}}{10^{-6}} = 4 \times 10^{4}\ \text{Pa} = 0.4\ \text{atm}$$ **Why this matters:** A smaller droplet (smaller $r$) → higher internal pressure → easier coalescence → instability. This is why **microemulsions** with their tiny droplet size require strong surfactant films to remain stable. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Required HLB Calculation for an Oil Blend **Problem (KTU-Style):** A pharmaceutical formulator needs to prepare an O/W emulsion using a blend of two oils: **liquid paraffin (HLB required = 10)** and **cetyl alcohol (HLB required = 13)** in a **60:40 weight ratio**. The formulator has access to **Tween 80 (HLB = 15)** and **Span 80 (HLB = 4.3)**. Determine the **weight ratio of Tween 80 : Span 80** required to achieve the target HLB of the oil phase, and the **total emulsifier percentage** (assumed 5% w/w of total formulation). **Step 1: Calculate the required HLB of the oil blend.** The required HLB of an oil blend is the **weighted average** of individual HLB requirements: $$\text{HLB}_{\text{required}} = f_{\text{paraffin}} \cdot \text{HLB}_{\text{paraffin}} + f_{\text{cetyl}} \cdot \text{HLB}_{\text{cetyl}}$$ Substitute values: $$\text{HLB}_{\text{required}} = (0.60)(10) + (0.40)(13)$$ Compute: $$\text{HLB}_{\text{required}} = 6.0 + 5.2 = 11.2$$ **Step 2: Set up the HLB mixing equation for the surfactant blend.** Let $f_T$ = weight fraction of Tween 80 and $f_S$ = weight fraction of Span 80. By definition: $$f_T + f_S = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_S = 1 - f_T$$ The blend HLB must equal 11.2: $$\text{HLB}_{\text{blend}} = f_T \cdot \text{HLB}_T + f_S \cdot \text{HLB}_S = 11.2$$ Substitute $f_S = 1 - f_T$: $$f_T (15) + (1 - f_T)(4.3) = 11.2$$ **Step 3: Solve the linear equation.** Expand the second term: $$15 f_T + 4.3 - 4.3 f_T = 11.2$$ Combine like terms: $$(15 - 4.3) f_T + 4.3 = 11.2$$ $$10.7 f_T = 11.2 - 4.3 = 6.9$$ $$f_T = \frac{6.9}{10.7} = 0.6450 \approx 0.645$$ Therefore: $$f_S = 1 - 0.645 = 0.355$$ **Step 4: Convert to weight ratio.** $$\text{Tween 80 : Span 80} = 0.645 : 0.355 \approx \mathbf{1.82 : 1}$$ **Step 5: Calculate emulsifier amounts for 100 g formulation.** If the total formulation is 100 g and total emulsifier is 5% w/w: $$\text{Total emulsifier} = 5\ \text{g}$$ $$\text{Tween 80} = 0.645 \times 5\ \text{g} = 3.225\ \text{g}$$ $$\text{Span 80} = 0.355 \times 5\ \text{g} = 1.775\ \text{g}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analytical Insight for KTU Answers:** > The required HLB method works because **HLB is approximately additive** in surfactant blends. However, this method **assumes the surfactants are chemically compatible** and **does not account for co-surfactant effects**, which is a major limitation. KTU examiners may deduct marks if you state the method is universally accurate. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Stability Comparison of Emulsion Types | Stability Test | O/W Emulsion | W/O Emulsion | Diagnostic Indicator | |---|---|---|---| | **Dilution Test** | Dilutes with water (continuous phase) | Dilutes with oil | Determines continuous phase | | **Conductivity Test** | High conductivity (water is continuous, conducts electricity) | Low conductivity (oil is continuous, insulator) | Electrodes + ammeter | | **Dye Solubility Test** | Water-soluble dye (e.g., methylene blue) dissolves in continuous phase | Oil-soluble dye (e.g., Sudan Red III) dissolves in continuous phase | Microscopic examination | | **Cobalt Chloride Test** | Paper turns pink (water is continuous) | Paper remains blue | Filter paper dipped, dried | | **Fluorescence Test** | Under UV, oil fluoresces in the spots where droplets are | Water spots do not fluoresce | UV microscopy | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Trick:** In O/W emulsion identification, students often confuse the **dye test** — remember, you add the dye and observe its **distribution** under microscope, not its color change. The dye that disperses freely into the continuous phase gives a uniform color, while the dye in the dispersed phase appears as colored droplets. ## 4. Water in Oil in Water"] E --> I["O/W/O Multiple Emulsion
Oil in Water in Oil"] F --> J[Macroemulsion 0.1 to 100 micrometer] F --> K[Microemulsion 10 to 100 nanometer] F --> L[Nanoemulsion 20 to 200 nanometer] G --> M[Cold Cream Base] G --> N[Barrier Ointment] F --> O[Oral and IV Drug Delivery] H --> P[Controlled Release Matrix] ``` ### 4.2 Diagram 2: Emulsifier Orientation at the O/W Interface (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Oil Phase Droplet] --> B[Surfactant Monolayer] B --> C[Lipophilic Tail in Oil] B --> D[Hydrophilic Head in Water] E[Aqueous Continuous Phase] --> D D --> F[Hydration Shell] F --> G[Bulk Water with or SLS] H -->|No| J[Blend Span plus Tween] J --> K[Calculate Blend Ratio using HLB additivity] I --> L[Conduct Stability Testing] J --> L L --> M{Passes Centrifuge and Freeze Thaw} M -->|Yes|
