**X-ray Diffraction (XRD) Analysis** is a non-destructive analytical technique used in pharmaceutical and materials sciences to identify the crystalline phases, polymorphic forms, crystal structure, unit cell dimensions, and degree of crystallinity of a solid drug substance. It is based on the constructive interference of monochromatic X-rays with the regularly spaced atomic planes within a crystal lattice, described mathematically by **Bragg's Law**.

In pharmaceutical technology, XRD is officially classified by the **United States Pharmacopeia (USP)** and the **International Council for Harmonisation (ICH Q6A)** as a definitive characterization method for **polymorph identification**, **crystal form verification**, and **quantitative phase analysis** of Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients (APIs) and excipients.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 4):**
> X-ray diffraction is listed as a primary instrumental method for solid-state characterization of drugs, alongside DSC, TGA, and SEM. It is mandatory for studying **drug polymorphism**, a critical quality attribute governed by **ICH Q6A** guidelines.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a calm lake and you throw two small stones into the water at exactly the same time, but at slightly different locations. The ripples (waves) from each stone spread outward. Where the crests of two ripples meet, the water rises higher (constructive interference); where a crest meets a trough, the water becomes still (destructive interference).

Now imagine the atomic planes inside a crystal as a series of perfectly arranged, parallel "mirrors" separated by a tiny distance *d*. When X-rays (which are simply electromagnetic waves with very short wavelengths, typically **0.05 nm to 0.25 nm**) hit these planes, they reflect off. If the reflected waves from successive planes arrive back in step (in phase), they reinforce each other and produce a strong, measurable signal at a specific angle. If they are out of step, they cancel out and no signal is observed.

By measuring the **angle (2θ)** at which this reinforcement happens, scientists can mathematically work backward to discover:
1. The distance between atomic planes (*d-spacing*).
2. The arrangement of atoms in the unit cell.
3. The size of the crystallites (tiny crystal grains).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why X-rays?** X-rays have wavelengths on the same order of magnitude as interatomic distances (about **0.1–0.3 nm**, roughly **1 Angstrom**). Visible light has wavelengths of 400–700 nm, which is too large to "see" atomic structure. This is why we need X-rays to probe the atomic world.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| X-ray wavelength (Cu Kα) | **λ = 0.15406 nm (1.5406 Å)** | Most common laboratory source |
| X-ray wavelength (Mo Kα) | **λ = 0.07107 nm (0.7107 Å)** | Used for smaller unit cells |
| Typical 2θ scan range | **5° to 80°** | Pharmaceutical XRD working range |
| d-spacing range probed | **~0.1 to 1.5 nm (1–15 Å)** | Matches interatomic distances |
| Speed of light | **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** | Used in wave calculations |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The XRD experiment follows a logical sequence that every KTU student must internalize:

1. **X-ray Generation**: A cathode filament (typically tungsten) emits electrons via thermionic emission. These electrons are accelerated by a high voltage (**30–50 kV**) and strike a metal target (usually **copper**). The sudden deceleration produces **bremsstrahlung** (continuous spectrum) X-rays plus characteristic **Kα and Kβ** lines of the target metal.

2. **Monochromatization**: A **monochromator** (graphite crystal or nickel filter) isolates the Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) and removes Kβ and white radiation. This ensures a single, known wavelength strikes the sample.

3. **Sample Irradiation**: The monochromatic X-ray beam strikes the powdered (or single-crystal) drug sample held in a sample holder. The sample is usually mounted on a flat plate or in a capillary.

4. **Diffraction and Detection**: The detector (scintillation counter or solid-state detector) moves along a goniometer circle. It records the intensity of diffracted X-rays as a function of the **2θ angle** (the angle between the incident beam and the diffracted beam).

5. **Data Output**: The instrument plots **Intensity (counts) vs. 2θ (degrees)** — this is the **diffractogram**, the fingerprint of the crystalline material.

## 2.2 Bragg's Law — The Heart of XRD

When X-rays strike parallel atomic planes separated by distance *d*, the path difference between rays reflecting from successive planes must equal an integer number of wavelengths for constructive interference to occur. This is **Bragg's Law**, derived by **W.H. Bragg and W.L. Bragg** (Nobel Prize, 1915).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Equation:**
> $$n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$$
> where *n* is the order of diffraction (usually taken as 1), *λ* is the X-ray wavelength, *d* is the interplanar spacing, and *θ* is the angle of incidence (Bragg angle).

## 2.3 Pharmaceutical Applications — The "Why" for KTU

- **Polymorph Identification**: Different crystal forms of the same drug (e.g., **Ritonavir Form I vs Form II** — the famous 1998 recall) produce unique diffraction patterns. Each polymorph is a regulatory-distinct entity.
- **Crystallinity Quantification**: Amorphous vs. crystalline content can be estimated by comparing peak area to total area.
- **API Identification**: The diffraction pattern serves as a **fingerprint**, comparable with reference patterns in the **Powder Diffraction File (PDF)** database maintained by the **International Centre for Diffraction Data (ICDD)**.
- **Co-crystal and Salt Screening**: New solid forms are characterized by XRD.
- **Process Monitoring**: Batch-to-batch consistency of crystalline drug products.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is essential for solving numerical problems in KTU university exams. The vertical bar symbol is rendered as `\vert` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| Formula | Equation | Variables | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bragg's Law | $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ | n, λ, d, θ | n is a positive integer (n = 1, 2, 3...) |
| Interplanar spacing (cubic) | $d_{hkl} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}$ | a, h, k, l | Valid only for cubic systems |
| Scherrer Equation | $D = \dfrac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}$ | D, K, λ, β, θ | D is crystallite size in nm |
| Bragg angle relation | $\sin\theta = \dfrac{n\lambda}{2d}$ | — | Maximum when nλ ≤ 2d |
| Volume of unit cell (cubic) | $V = a^3$ | a | Length cubed |
| Density from unit cell | $\rho = \dfrac{nM}{N_A V}$ | n, M, N_A, V | n = molecules per unit cell |

### Variable Definitions in Scherrer Equation

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| D | Mean crystallite size | **1–100 nm** |
| K | Shape factor (Scherrer constant) | **0.9** (spherical) or **0.94** (common) |
| λ | X-ray wavelength (Cu Kα) | **0.15406 nm** |
| β | Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) of peak | **radians** |
| θ | Bragg angle (in radians for the equation) | degrees → convert |

## 2.5 Real-World Pharmaceutical Engineering Utility

In a **Quality Control (QC)** laboratory of a pharmaceutical company, XRD is used to verify that a batch of **Paracetamol** received is Form I (monoclinic) and not Form II (orthorhombic), because the two forms have different compressibility, solubility, and bioavailability. In **Preformulation**, XRD guides the selection of the most stable polymorph for commercial development. In **Patent Litigation**, XRD patterns often serve as evidence of a unique crystalline form.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Bragg's Law

**Statement to Prove:** Constructive interference of X-rays reflected from parallel atomic planes occurs when the path difference equals an integer multiple of the wavelength.

### Step 1: Setup the Geometry

Consider two parallel atomic planes separated by an interplanar distance *d*. A monochromatic X-ray beam of wavelength *λ* strikes the first plane at an incident angle *θ* (measured from the plane surface). A second, parallel ray strikes the next plane at the same angle.

### Step 2: Identify the Path Difference

The second ray must travel an **extra distance** to reach the second plane and reflect back to the same horizontal level as the first ray. This extra path consists of two segments.

- The ray travels from point A to point B (down to the second plane): distance = *d* / sin θ
- The ray travels from point B to point C (back up to the first plane level): distance = *d* / sin θ

### Step 3: Calculate Total Path Difference

$$
\text{Path difference} = AB + BC = \frac{d}{\sin\theta} + \frac{d}{\sin\theta} = \frac{2d}{\sin\theta}
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Condition for Constructive Interference

For constructive interference (peaks aligning with peaks), the path difference must be an integer multiple of the wavelength:

$$
\frac{2d}{\sin\theta} = n\lambda
$$

### Step 5: Rearrange into Final Form

Multiply both sides by sin θ:

$$
2d = n\lambda \sin\theta
$$

Divide both sides by 2 sin θ:

$$
d = \frac{n\lambda}{2\sin\theta}
$$

Equivalently (and more commonly written as):

$$
n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta
$$

This is the **Bragg Equation** — the fundamental law governing all X-ray diffraction experiments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation**: For a given *d* and *λ*, diffraction is observed only at specific angles θ₁, θ₂, θ₃... corresponding to n = 1, 2, 3... This is why a diffractogram consists of sharp peaks at discrete angles, not a continuous spectrum.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem:** A powdered drug sample is analyzed using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). A sharp diffraction peak is observed at 2θ = 28.44°. Calculate the interplanar spacing *d* for this set of planes. If the drug crystallizes in a cubic system with lattice parameter *a* = 4.50 Å, identify the Miller indices (hkl) of this reflection.

### Given Data

$$
\lambda = 1.5406 \text{ Å}, \quad 2\theta = 28.44°, \quad a = 4.50 \text{ Å}
$$

### Step 1: Determine the Bragg Angle θ

$$
\theta = \frac{2\theta}{2} = \frac{28.44°}{2} = 14.22°
$$

### Step 2: Apply Bragg's Law for n = 1

$$
n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta
$$

Solving for *d*:

$$
d = \frac{n\lambda}{2\sin\theta}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute Values

$$
d = \frac{1 \times 1.5406 \text{ Å}}{2 \times \sin(14.22°)}
$$

Evaluate sin(14.22°):

$$
\sin(14.22°) = 0.2456
$$

### Step 4: Final Calculation

$$
d = \frac{1.5406}{2 \times 0.2456} = \frac{1.5406}{0.4912}
$$

$$
\boxed{d = 3.137 \text{ Å}}
$$

### Step 5: Identify Miller Indices (hkl)

For a cubic system, the interplanar spacing is:

$$
d_{hkl} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2} = \frac{a}{d}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2} = \frac{4.50 \text{ Å}}{3.137 \text{ Å}} = 1.4345
$$

Square both sides:

$$
h^2 + k^2 + l^2 = (1.4345)^2 = 2.058
$$

The closest integer is **2**, which corresponds to the Miller indices (110) because:

$$
1^2 + 1^2 + 0^2 = 1 + 1 + 0 = 2
$$

**Verification:**

$$
d_{110} = \frac{4.50}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{4.50}{1.4142} = 3.182 \text{ Å}
$$

The slight difference (3.137 vs 3.182) is within experimental rounding tolerance.

$$
\boxed{(hkl) = (110)}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Scherrer Equation (Crystallite Size)

The **Scherrer Equation** estimates the size of crystalline domains (crystallites) from peak broadening. The full derivation links the finite size of a crystallite to the angular spread of a diffraction peak.

Consider a crystallite of size *D* containing *N* parallel atomic planes. The path difference between X-rays reflecting from the top and bottom planes is *N* × (2d sin θ). When *N* is small (small crystallite), the diffraction condition is relaxed over a small angular range, producing **peak broadening**.

The condition for the first minimum of destructive interference adjacent to the principal maximum gives:

$$
N \cdot \Delta(2d\sin\theta) = \lambda
$$

For small changes in θ:

$$
N \cdot 2d\cos\theta \cdot \Delta\theta = \lambda
$$

Define the **angular spread** $\beta$ (FWHM in radians) as $2\Delta\theta$:

$$
N \cdot 2d\cos\theta \cdot \frac{\beta}{2} = \lambda
$$

But $N \cdot d \approx D$ (total crystallite thickness):

$$
2D\cos\theta \cdot \frac{\beta}{2} = \lambda
$$

Rearranging:

$$
D = \frac{\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}
$$

Introducing the dimensionless shape factor K (typically 0.9):

$$
\boxed{D = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of XRD Techniques

| Feature | Powder XRD (PXRD) | Single Crystal XRD (SC-XRD) |
|---|---|---|
| Sample form | Polycrystalline powder | Single, well-formed crystal |
| Data type | 1D pattern (Intensity vs 2θ) | 3D diffraction spots |
| Information obtained | d-spacing, phase ID, crystallite size | Full 3D electron density map |
| Pharmaceutical use | Routine polymorph screening | Absolute structure determination |
| Instrument cost | Moderate (₹15–50 lakhs) | Very high (₹1–5 crores) |
| Sample preparation | Simple (grind & pack) | Difficult (need single crystal) |

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Components of a Powder X-ray Diffractometer

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional architecture and signal flow of a typical powder XRD instrument, mapping the interactions between the X-ray source, monochromator, sample, detector, and data acquisition system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HV Power Supply 30 to 50 kV] --> B[X-Ray Tube Cathode Filament]
    B --> C[Copper Target Anode]
    C --> D[Emission of Cu K alpha Radiation Lambda 0.15406 nm]
    D --> E[Monochromator Graphite Crystal]
    E --> F[Incident Beam Slit and Divergence Slit]
    F --> G[Drug Sample Powder on Flat Holder]
    G --> H[Diffracted Beams from Atomic Planes]
    H --> I[Receiving Slit and Scatter Slit]
    I --> J[Detector Scintillation Counter]
    J --> K[Goniometer Angle 2 theta]
    K --> L[Amplifier and Pulse Height Discriminator]
    L --> M[Data Acquisition Computer]
    M --> N[Diffractogram Plot Intensity versus 2 theta]
    N --> O[PDF Database ICDD Search Match]
    O --> P[Polymorph Identification and Crystal System Report]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow: Bragg Diffraction Geometry

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the geometric principle of Bragg diffraction — how X-rays reflect from successive atomic planes and produce constructive interference at a specific angle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident X-Ray Beam of Wavelength Lambda] --> B[Strikes First Atomic Plane at Angle Theta]
    B --> C[Penetrates to Second Atomic Plane]
    C --> D[Reflects from Second Plane at Angle Theta]
    D --> E[Path Difference Computed as 2d sine theta]
    E --> F{Is 2d sine theta equal to n times lambda?}
    F -- YES --> G[Constructive Interference Strong Peak Detected]
    F -- NO --> H[Destructive Interference No Signal Recorded]
    G --> I[Goniometer Records Intensity at this 2 theta Value]
    H --> J[Goniometer Continues to Next Angle]
    I --> K[Diffractogram Generated for Sample]
    J --> F
    K --> L[Compare with Reference PDF Card Index]
    L --> M[Identify Polymorph Crystal Form and Unit Cell]
```

## 4.3 Process Map: Pharmaceutical XRD Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Receive Drug Sample in QC Lab] --> B[Grind Sample to Fine Powder in Mortar]
    B --> C[Load Powder into XRD Sample Holder]
    C --> D[Mount Holder in Goniometer]
    D --> E[Set Scan Parameters 2 theta 5 to 80 degrees]
    E --> F[Run Scan Duration 10 to 30 minutes]
    F --> G[Collect Raw Diffractogram]
    G --> H[Subtract Background and Smooth Data]
    H --> I[Identify Peak Positions and d spacings]
    I --> J[Search ICDD PDF Database for Match]
    J --> K[Compare with Reference Polymorph Pattern]
    K --> L{Is Match within Tolerance?}
    L -- YES --> M[Accept Batch and Release for Production]
    L -- NO --> N[Reject Batch and Investigate Form Change]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model Question]**

**Q: Define X-ray diffraction. State Bragg's Law and explain the significance of the order of diffraction (n).**

**Model Answer:**

X-ray diffraction (XRD) is an analytical technique used to study the crystal structure of solid materials by directing monochromatic X-rays at a sample and measuring the angles and intensities of the diffracted beams. It is widely used in pharmaceutical analysis for polymorph identification and crystallinity assessment.

**Bragg's Law** states that constructive interference of X-rays reflected from parallel atomic planes occurs when the path difference equals an integer number of wavelengths:

$$
n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta
$$

where *n* is a positive integer (1, 2, 3...) called the **order of diffraction**, *λ* is the wavelength, *d* is the interplanar spacing, and *θ* is the Bragg angle.

**Significance of n:** The order of diffraction determines which multiple of the fundamental d-spacing produces a peak. For a given set of planes with spacing *d*, peaks appear at multiple angles corresponding to n = 1, 2, 3..., with the first-order (n = 1) being the most intense. The integer *n* also allows the equation to be simplified to $λ = 2d\sin\theta$ when n = 1, which is the most commonly used form in pharmaceutical analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):** Definition — 1 mark | Bragg's Law statement — 1 mark | Significance of n — 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Question]**

**Q: Differentiate between crystalline and amorphous solids as detected by XRD. Why is the amorphous form pharmaceutically less preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Crystalline Solid | Amorphous Solid |
|---|---|---|
| Atomic arrangement | Long-range, periodic, ordered | Short-range, random, disordered |
| XRD pattern | Sharp, intense, well-defined peaks | Broad, diffuse "hallo" or no peaks |
| d-spacing | Well-defined, discrete values | No single d-spacing |
| Energy state | Lower energy, thermodynamically stable | Higher energy, metastable |
| Solubility | Generally lower | Generally higher |
| Stability | Higher shelf-life | May recrystallize over time |

**Pharmaceutical Preference for Crystalline Form:**

The amorphous form, despite its higher solubility, is **pharmaceutically less preferred** because:

1. **Physical instability** — amorphous drugs tend to recrystallize during storage, leading to changes in dissolution and bioavailability.
2. **Reproducibility issues** — the random structure makes batch-to-batch consistency difficult to control.
3. **Handling problems** — amorphous solids are often hygroscopic and difficult to process (poor flow, compressibility).
4. **Regulatory concerns** — the metastable nature complicates shelf-life prediction and regulatory approval.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):** Tabular difference — 1.5 marks | Pharmaceutical reasoning — 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice (i.e., OR option). Each part (a) and (b) is typically 7 marks. You must answer either **Question A** OR **Question B** in full.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**

**Q (a):** Describe the construction and working of a powder X-ray diffractometer with a neatly labeled block diagram. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A **Powder X-ray Diffractometer (PXRD)** consists of the following major components:

**1. X-ray Source (X-ray Tube):**
- A sealed tube containing a tungsten filament (cathode) and a copper target (anode).
- High voltage (**30–50 kV**) accelerates electrons from the cathode to the anode.
- The sudden deceleration of electrons on striking the Cu target produces **bremsstrahlung** (continuous) and **Cu Kα characteristic** X-rays.
- The dominant Cu Kα radiation has wavelength **λ = 1.5406 Å**.

**2. Monochromator and Slits:**
- A graphite crystal monochromator or nickel filter isolates the Cu Kα line, removing Kβ and white radiation.
- **Divergence slit** controls the spread of the incident beam.
- **Receiving slit** and **scatter slit** define the beam reaching the detector.

**3. Sample Stage (Goniometer):**
- The powdered drug sample is packed into a flat aluminum or glass holder.
- The sample is mounted on the goniometer, which rotates the sample by angle θ and the detector by angle 2θ simultaneously (Bragg-Brentano geometry).

**4. Detector:**
- A **scintillation counter** (NaI crystal with photomultiplier) or **solid-state detector** (Si-Li) measures the intensity of diffracted X-rays.
- Output is in counts per second.

**5. Data Acquisition and Processing Unit:**
- A computer records intensity vs. 2θ.
- Software plots the **diffractogram**, which is the fingerprint of the crystalline sample.

**Working Principle:**

The X-ray beam strikes the sample at angle θ. Atomic planes with d-spacing satisfying $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ produce constructive interference. The detector records the intensity at each 2θ value. The output diffractogram is compared with standard **ICDD PDF database** cards for phase identification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split:** Source — 1.5 | Monochromator/Slits — 1.5 | Goniometer — 1.5 | Detector — 1 | Working — 1.5.

---

**Q (b):** A drug sample exhibits an XRD peak at 2θ = 31.68° using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). Calculate the d-spacing of the corresponding planes. If the unit cell is cubic with *a* = 5.64 Å, identify the Miller indices (hkl) of the reflection. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
$$
\lambda = 1.5406 \text{ Å}, \quad 2\theta = 31.68°, \quad a = 5.64 \text{ Å}, \quad n = 1
$$

**Step 1: Find Bragg angle θ**

$$
\theta = \frac{2\theta}{2} = \frac{31.68°}{2} = 15.84°
$$

**Step 2: Apply Bragg's Law to find d**

$$
n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta
$$

Rearranging:

$$
d = \frac{n\lambda}{2\sin\theta}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute values**

$$
\sin(15.84°) = 0.2730
$$

$$
d = \frac{1 \times 1.5406}{2 \times 0.2730} = \frac{1.5406}{0.5460}
$$

$$
\boxed{d = 2.821 \text{ Å}}
$$

**Step 4: Find Miller indices (hkl) for cubic system**

For cubic:

$$
d_{hkl} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2} = \frac{a}{d} = \frac{5.64}{2.821} = 1.9993
$$

Squaring:

$$
h^2 + k^2 + l^2 = (1.9993)^2 = 3.997 \approx 4
$$

**Step 5: Identify (hkl)**

The integer sum 4 corresponds to (200) because:

$$
2^2 + 0^2 + 0^2 = 4
$$

**Verification:**

$$
d_{200} = \frac{5.64}{\sqrt{4}} = \frac{5.64}{2.0} = 2.82 \text{ Å} \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
\boxed{(hkl) = (200)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split:** Step 1 (θ) — 1 | Step 2 (formula) — 1 | Step 3 (d calculation) — 1.5 | Step 4 (hkl logic) — 1.5 | Verification — 2.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**

**Q (a):** Explain the Scherrer equation for determining crystallite size. A nanocrystalline drug sample gives an XRD peak with FWHM (β) = 0.0080 radians at 2θ = 25.00° using Cu Kα radiation. Calculate the average crystallite size. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Scherrer Equation Explanation:**

The **Scherrer equation**, proposed by **Paul Scherrer (1918)**, relates the broadening of an XRD peak to the finite size of crystallites (coherently diffracting domains) in a polycrystalline sample. Smaller crystallites produce broader diffraction peaks because there are fewer parallel atomic planes to produce sharp constructive interference.

The equation is:

$$
D = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}
$$

where:
- *D* = average crystallite size (in nm)
- *K* = dimensionless shape factor (Scherrer constant), typically **0.9** for spherical crystallites
- *λ* = X-ray wavelength (0.15406 nm for Cu Kα)
- *β* = full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the diffraction peak, in **radians**
- *θ* = Bragg angle (must be in radians for this equation)

**Assumptions and Limitations:**

1. The broadening is purely due to **size effect** (not strain).
2. Crystallites are assumed to be **spherical** or of regular shape.
3. K depends on the shape and is typically 0.9 (or 0.94 as some references use).
4. The sample must be **monocrystalline** within each grain (no defects contributing to broadening).

**Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
$$
\beta = 0.0080 \text{ rad}, \quad 2\theta = 25.00°, \quad \lambda = 0.15406 \text{ nm}, \quad K = 0.9
$$

**Step 1: Find θ in radians**

$$
\theta = \frac{2\theta}{2} = \frac{25.00°}{2} = 12.50°
$$

Convert to radians:

$$
\theta_{rad} = 12.50° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.2182 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate cos θ**

$$
\cos(12.50°) = 0.9763
$$

**Step 3: Apply Scherrer equation**

$$
D = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}
$$

$$
D = \frac{0.9 \times 0.15406 \text{ nm}}{0.0080 \times 0.9763}
$$

**Step 4: Numerator**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 0.9 \times 0.15406 = 0.13865 \text{ nm}
$$

**Step 5: Denominator**

$$
\text{Denominator} = 0.0080 \times 0.9763 = 0.007810
$$

**Step 6: Final calculation**

$$
D = \frac{0.13865}{0.007810} = 17.75 \text{ nm}
$$

$$
\boxed{D \approx 17.75 \text{ nm}}
$$

This indicates the drug is in the **nanocrystalline** range (1–100 nm), which often exhibits enhanced solubility and dissolution rate per the **Noyes-Whitney equation** as described in pharmaceutics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split:** Scherrer equation explanation — 2.5 | Variable definitions — 1.5 | Numerical calculation — 3.

---

**Q (b):** Discuss the role of XRD in pharmaceutical polymorphism studies. Mention any two real-world case studies where XRD was critical. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Role of XRD in Polymorphism Studies:**

**Polymorphism** is the ability of a drug substance to exist in two or more crystalline forms with different arrangements of molecules in the solid state. Each polymorph has distinct physicochemical properties — solubility, melting point, density, stability, dissolution rate, and bioavailability. **XRD is the gold-standard technique for identifying and differentiating polymorphs** because each crystal form produces a unique diffraction pattern (a "fingerprint").

**Key Applications:**

1. **Polymorph Screening**: During preformulation, drug candidates are crystallized under varied conditions (solvents, temperatures, anti-solvents, pH) and analyzed by XRD to discover all possible crystal forms.

2. **Form Identification**: The diffractogram of a sample is matched against the **ICDD PDF database** and in-house reference patterns of known polymorphs.

3. **Stability Studies**: XRD is used to monitor whether a drug undergoes **solid-state transformation** during storage (e.g., hydrate formation, polymorphic transition).

4. **Patent Protection**: Novel polymorphs discovered by XRD are often patented as new intellectual property.

5. **QC and Batch Release**: Incoming raw materials are verified by XRD to ensure the correct polymorph is used in production.

**Real-World Case Studies:**

**Case 1: Ritonavir (Norvir) — 1998 Recall**

**Abbott Laboratories** launched Ritonavir (an HIV protease inhibitor) in 1996 as **Form I**. In 1998, a previously unseen **Form II** appeared in production batches. Form II was **less soluble** and had lower bioavailability, causing therapeutic failure. The discovery of Form II was confirmed by XRD, which showed a distinctly different pattern from Form I. Abbott had to **withdraw the product from the market** for over a year, reformulate, and switch to Form II as the new commercial form. The incident cost Abbott an estimated **$250 million** and led to major changes in FDA polymorph screening requirements. This case established XRD as a **mandatory** tool in pharmaceutical development.

**Case 2: GlaxoSmithKline — Paroxetine HCl**

**Paroxetine hydrochloride** (Paxil/Seroxat, an antidepressant) exists in multiple anhydrous and hydrated forms. GSK used XRD extensively to characterize and patent the **hemihydrate form** as the stable, marketable form. The XRD pattern of the hemihydrate was central to patent claims and regulatory submissions. The case demonstrated how XRD enables **patent protection** of specific crystal forms and supports **regulatory documentation** of solid-state properties.

**Additional Note — Carbamazepine**: The transition between **Carbamazepine Form III (anhydrate)** and the **dihydrate** is a classic example studied via XRD to understand humidity-induced phase transformations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split:** Role of XRD in polymorphism — 3 marks | Case study 1 (Ritonavir) — 2 marks | Case study 2 (Paroxetine or Carbamazepine) — 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to halve 2θ**: Many students use 2θ directly in Bragg's Law instead of θ. This is the **#1 calculation error** in XRD problems. Always compute θ = 2θ / 2.
>
> 2. **Angle unit confusion**: Bragg's Law uses the **angle in degrees** for sin and cos, but the **Scherrer equation requires θ and β in radians** for consistency. Mixing units is a 1–2 mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Cubic-only formula misuse**: The formula $d = a/\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$ is valid **only for cubic systems**. Using it for tetragonal, orthorhombic, or monoclinic systems will give wrong answers.
>
> 4. **Confusing Miller indices sum**: Students often forget to **square the indices** before summing. $h^2 + k^2 + l^2$ is NOT the same as $h + k + l$.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the n factor**: In higher-order diffraction, the path difference is $2d\sin\theta$ and the equation is $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$. Forgetting n=1 is a common error.
>
> 6. **Missing Scherrer constant K**: Writing $D = \lambda / (\beta\cos\theta)$ without K loses a mark. K = 0.9 must be included.
>
> 7. **No diagram in long answers**: Part B questions asking about instrument construction **require a labeled block diagram** for full marks. A textual description alone loses 2–3 marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — XRD Analysis (Module 4)**
>
> - **Bragg's Law**: $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ — the central equation of XRD. Memorize and apply correctly.
> - **Cu Kα wavelength**: $\lambda = 1.5406$ Å (1.5406 × 10⁻¹⁰ m) — used in 90% of pharmaceutical XRD experiments.
> - **Step 1 of every problem**: Convert 2θ to θ by halving. Step 2: Apply Bragg's Law.
> - **Cubic interplanar formula**: $d_{hkl} = a / \sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}$ — square the indices, sum them, then take square root.
> - **Scherrer equation**: $D = K\lambda / (\beta\cos\theta)$ — used for crystallite size; K = 0.9; β and θ in radians.
> - **Diffractogram**: Intensity (y-axis) vs. 2θ (x-axis). Sharp peaks = crystalline; broad humps = amorphous.
> - **Polymorph fingerprint**: Each polymorph gives a unique XRD pattern — the primary method of identification.
> - **ICDD PDF database**: Standard reference database for phase identification.
> - **Components of XRD**: X-ray tube → Monochromator → Slits → Sample → Detector → Computer.
> - **Bragg-Brentano geometry**: Sample rotates by θ, detector by 2θ simultaneously.
> - **Real-world impact**: Ritonavir 1998 case established XRD as a regulatory requirement for polymorph screening.
> - **USP and ICH Q6A**: Recognize XRD as a definitive solid-state characterization method.
> - **Common Miller indices to remember**: (100), (110), (111), (200), (210), (211), (220) — sums of squares: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8.
> - **Units consistency**: nm vs. Å — convert using 1 nm = 10 Å; angle: degrees vs. radians — convert using 1° = π/180 rad.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fourier Transform Spectroscopy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fourier Transform Spectroscopy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fourier Transform Spectroscopy (FTS)** is a measurement technique in which the spectrum of a substance is obtained by measuring the **coherent interference pattern (interferogram)** of radiation as a function of optical path difference, and subsequently computing the **Fourier Transform** of this interferogram to recover the conventional frequency-domain spectrum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Definition (Verbatim Recommended):**
> *"Fourier Transform Spectroscopy is a non-dispersive spectroscopic method that employs a Michelson interferometer to modulate the incoming radiation. The detector records the interferogram — a time-domain (or path-difference domain) signal — and a digital computer applies the Fourier Transform to convert it into a frequency-domain spectrum $B(\nu)$, which is the true absorption/emission spectrum of the sample."*

In pharmaceutical technology, the two principal implementations are:

| Acronym | Full Form | Spectral Region | Pharmaceutical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FT-IR** | Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy | $4000 \text{ to } 400 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ | Functional group identification, polymorphism, drug-excipient compatibility |
| **FT-NMR** | Fourier Transform Nuclear Magnetic Resonance | Radiofrequency (MHz) | Structural elucidation of API, impurity profiling |
| **FT-NIR** | Fourier Transform Near-Infrared | $12800 \text{ to } 4000 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ | PAT (Process Analytical Technology), blend uniformity |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check — A Cup of Biryani Spectrum**
> Imagine a song being played by an entire orchestra. A *dispersive* spectrometer is like standing at the door of the concert hall with a *single ear* tilted to hear only one note at a time — you must wait for the next note (slow scanning).
> 
> A *Fourier Transform* spectrometer is like having a microphone recording **the whole orchestra simultaneously** for a few seconds. The recording sounds like a buzz (the *interferogram*). When you mathematically "sort out" the buzz (Fourier Transform), you get **every instrument's contribution at every frequency — all at once**.
> 
> **The buzz = interferogram (time/path domain).**
> **The sorted song = spectrum (frequency domain).**

This simultaneous multi-frequency acquisition is the source of the famed **Fellgett (multiplex) advantage**, which makes FT instruments dramatically faster and more sensitive than dispersive counterparts.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light:** $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$
- **Mirror velocity (typical):** $v_{m} = 0.1 \text{ to } 10 \text{ cm/s}$
- **Spectral range covered simultaneously:** entire IR region (FT-IR)
- **Spectral resolution:** $\Delta \nu \approx 1 / \delta_{max}$, where $\delta_{max}$ is the **maximum optical path difference (OPD)**.
- **Typical resolution:** $0.5 \text{ to } 4 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ for routine pharmaceutical FT-IR.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The instrument is built around a **Michelson Interferometer**. The logical flow is:

1. **Source emission:** A broadband polychromatic IR source (e.g., Globar — silicon carbide rod) emits radiation of intensity $B(\nu)$ across all frequencies $\nu$.
2. **Beam splitting:** The beam strikes a **beamsplitter** (typically a thin germanium or KBr film coated on a substrate). Half the light is reflected, half is transmitted.
3. **Path-length modulation:**
   - The **fixed mirror** reflects its beam back to the beamsplitter.
   - The **moving mirror** (translated by a precision motor) reflects its beam back, with path length changing by $2x$ when the mirror moves a distance $x$.
   - The two beams recombine at the beamsplitter and **interfere** — constructively if in-phase, destructively if out-of-phase.
4. **Interferogram formation:** The detector records intensity $I(\delta)$ as a function of optical path difference $\delta = 2x$ (called the *retardation* or *OPD*).
5. **Fourier Transformation:** A digital computer (Cooley–Tukey FFT algorithm) computes:
   $$B(\nu) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} I(\delta) \, \cos(2\pi \nu \delta) \, d\delta$$
   to recover the spectrum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why cosine?**
> Because the interferogram of a true broadband source is a *real, even* function of $\delta$ (it is symmetric about zero path difference), only the cosine transform is needed — no imaginary sine component.

## 2.2 The Michelson Interferometer — Component Functions

| Component | Material (FT-IR) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Globar / Nernst glower / Tungsten-halogen (NIR) | Emits polychromatic radiation |
| **Beamsplitter** | Ge on KBr substrate (mid-IR); CaF₂ (NIR); quartz (UV-vis) | Splits 50:50, recombines beams |
| **Fixed mirror** | Front-coated Al or Au on Pyrex | Returns beam to beamsplitter |
| **Moving mirror** | Same, mounted on air-bearing carriage | Modulates path difference $\delta$ |
| **Detector** | DTGS (room temp) / MCT (cryo-cooled) | Converts IR signal → electrical signal |
| **He-Ne laser** | Red 632.8 nm | Reference for mirror position & sampling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **internal He-Ne laser** is the *soul* of FT accuracy. Its interference fringes trigger data sampling with a precision of $\lambda_{He-Ne}/2 \approx 316 \text{ nm}$, which is why FT instruments achieve wavelength accuracy better than $0.01 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ — far superior to dispersive instruments.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I(\delta) = \int_{0}^{\infty} B(\nu) \cos(2\pi \nu \delta) \, d\nu$ | Interferogram (Fourier cosine transform of spectrum) | a.u. |
| 2 | $B(\nu) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} I(\delta) \cos(2\pi \nu \delta) \, d\delta$ | Spectrum (Inverse Fourier transform) | a.u. |
| 3 | $\delta = 2x$ | Optical path difference vs mirror displacement $x$ | cm |
| 4 | $\Delta \nu = 1 / \delta_{max}$ | Spectral resolution | cm$^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $S/N \;\propto\; \sqrt{N_{scans}}$ | Signal-to-noise improves with co-added scans (signal averaging) | — |
| 6 | $t_{FT} = \delta_{max} / v_{m}$ | Time to complete one scan | s |
| 7 | $\nu = 1 / \lambda$ | Wavenumber–wavelength relation | cm$^{-1}$ |
| 8 | $E_{mirror} = \pm v_{m} \, t$ | Mirror position encoder equation | cm |
| 9 | $I_{max} - I_{min} = 2 I_0$ | Constructive vs destructive interference contrast | — |
| 10 | $\text{Apodization: } I_{corr}(\delta) = I(\delta) \cdot A(\delta)$ | Reduces truncation artifacts (e.g., Happ–Genzel, triangular) | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not write** absolute-value bars `|x|` inside markdown tables — they break the column rendering. Always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX form.

## 2.4 The Three Classical "Advantages" of FT Spectroscopy

These are **board-exam favourites**:

1. **Fellgett (Multiplex) Advantage** — All frequencies measured simultaneously. For a spectrum with $M$ resolution elements, the S/N improves by a factor of $\sqrt{M}$ in a time-limited measurement, compared to a dispersive instrument.
2. **Connes' Advantage** — The internal He-Ne laser provides an internal wavelength calibration. Wavenumber precision $< 0.01 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ is routinely achievable.
3. **Jacquinot (Throughput) Advantage** — No narrow slits required; the interferometer has a large circular optical aperture (étendue), allowing more radiation to reach the detector → higher sensitivity.

> [!NOTE]
> Fellgett's advantage applies only when detector noise dominates. For detectors limited by *photon shot noise* (e.g., MCT in mid-IR), the multiplex advantage is **partially negated** — but the throughput and wavelength advantages still make FT superior.

## 2.5 Real-World Pharmaceutical Utility

- **Polymorph screening** of an API (e.g., carbamazepine Form I vs Form III shows distinct FT-IR signatures in the $1700 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ C=O region).
- **Blend uniformity** monitoring via FT-NIR in tablet manufacturing (PAT per FDA guidance).
- **Identity testing** of raw materials per pharmacopeial methods (USP <854>, EP 2.02.24).
- **Reaction monitoring** of asymmetric synthesis in flow chemistry.
- **Quantitative analysis** of multicomponent dosage forms using PLS chemometric models on FT-IR/FT-NIR data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Interferogram Equation from First Principles

Let the source emit monochromatic radiation of frequency $\nu_0$ with amplitude $E_0$. The electric field is:

$$E(t) = E_0 \cos(2\pi \nu_0 t)$$

When the beam is split and travels through two arms of unequal path length $\delta$, the two returning waves at the beamsplitter are:

$$E_1(t) = \tfrac{1}{2} E_0 \cos(2\pi \nu_0 t)$$

$$E_2(t) = \tfrac{1}{2} E_0 \cos\bigl(2\pi \nu_0 (t + \tau)\bigr), \quad \tau = \delta / c$$

**Step 1 — Superpose the two waves:**

$$E_{tot}(t) = \tfrac{1}{2} E_0 \bigl[\cos(2\pi \nu_0 t) + \cos(2\pi \nu_0 t + 2\pi \nu_0 \tau)\bigr]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the trigonometric identity** $\cos A + \cos B = 2 \cos\!\bigl(\tfrac{A-B}{2}\bigr)\cos\!\bigl(\tfrac{A+B}{2}\bigr)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{tot}(t) &= \tfrac{1}{2} E_0 \cdot 2 \cos(\pi \nu_0 \tau) \cos\bigl(2\pi \nu_0 t + \pi \nu_0 \tau\bigr) \\
&= E_0 \cos(\pi \nu_0 \tau) \cos\bigl(2\pi \nu_0 t + \pi \nu_0 \tau\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Square to obtain intensity** (detector responds to $E^2$ averaged over optical period):

$$I(\delta) = \tfrac{1}{2} E_0^{\,2} \cos^2(\pi \nu_0 \tau) = \tfrac{1}{2} E_0^{\,2} \cdot \tfrac{1 + \cos(2\pi \nu_0 \tau)}{2}$$

Substituting $\tau = \delta / c$ and defining $B(\nu_0) = \tfrac{1}{4} E_0^{\,2}$:

$$I(\delta) = B(\nu_0) \bigl[1 + \cos(2\pi \nu_0 \delta / c)\bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Recognise wavenumber** $\tilde{\nu} = \nu_0 / c$ (units cm$^{-1}$), so $2\pi \nu_0 \delta / c = 2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta$:

$$\boxed{\;I(\delta) = B(\nu_0) \bigl[1 + \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \, \delta)\bigr]\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **constant term** $B(\nu_0)$ is the DC bias; only the **cosine term** carries spectral information and is the AC interferogram.

## 3.2 Generalisation to a Polychromatic Source

For a broadband source with spectral distribution $B(\tilde{\nu})$, sum (integrate) over all frequencies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(\delta) &= \int_{0}^{\infty} B(\tilde{\nu}) \bigl[1 + \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta)\bigr] \, d\tilde{\nu} \\
&= \underbrace{\int_{0}^{\infty} B(\tilde{\nu}) \, d\tilde{\nu}}_{I_0 \; \text{(DC constant)}} \;+\; \int_{0}^{\infty} B(\tilde{\nu}) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta) \, d\tilde{\nu}
\end{aligned}
$$

Define the AC interferogram $I_{AC}(\delta) = I(\delta) - I_0$:

$$\boxed{\;I_{AC}(\delta) = \int_{0}^{\infty} B(\tilde{\nu}) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta) \, d\tilde{\nu}\;}$$

This is the **forward Fourier cosine transform** of the spectrum. It is a Fourier-cosine pair with:

$$\boxed{\;B(\tilde{\nu}) = 2 \int_{0}^{\infty} I_{AC}(\delta) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta) \, d\delta\;}$$

(The factor of 2 arises from extending the integration from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$, since $I_{AC}(\delta)$ is an even function.)

## 3.3 Resolution Derivation — Why $\Delta \tilde{\nu} = 1 / \delta_{max}$

In practice, the mirror can travel only a finite distance, so the integral is truncated at $\delta_{max}$:

$$B_{obs}(\tilde{\nu}) = 2 \int_{0}^{\delta_{max}} I_{AC}(\delta) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta) \, d\delta$$

This is mathematically equivalent to **convolving the true spectrum with the Instrument Line Shape (ILS)**, which is the Fourier transform of the truncation function (a boxcar of width $\delta_{max}$). The boxcar function has FT equal to $\text{sinc}(\tilde{\nu} \delta_{max})$. The first zero of $\text{sinc}$ occurs at $\tilde{\nu} = 1/\delta_{max}$:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta \tilde{\nu} = \frac{1}{\delta_{max}}\;}$$

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Numerical illustration:** To achieve a resolution of $1 \text{ cm}^{-1}$, the mirror must travel $\delta_{max}/2 = 0.5 \text{ cm} = 5 \text{ mm}$. For $0.5 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ resolution, $\delta_{max} = 2 \text{ cm} = 20 \text{ mm}$. This is why high-resolution FT-IR instruments have long mirror travels.

## 3.4 Apodization — Removing the Sinc Side-Lobes

The sinc ILS has **secondary maxima** of $\approx 22\%$ of the central peak — they would appear as false spectral features ("feet" on absorption bands). Apodization multiplies $I_{AC}(\delta)$ by a window $A(\delta)$ that smoothly decays to zero:

$$I_{apod}(\delta) = I_{AC}(\delta) \cdot A(\delta)$$

| Apodization Function | $A(\delta)$ Form | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Boxcar** | $1$ for $0 \le \delta \le \delta_{max}$, else $0$ | Best resolution, worst side-lobes |
| **Triangular** | $1 - \vert \delta \vert / \delta_{max}$ | Good compromise, mild resolution loss |
| **Happ–Genzel** | $0.54 + 0.46 \cos(\pi \delta / \delta_{max})$ | Smooth decay, slight broadening |
| **Norton–Beer (weak/medium/strong)** | Cosine polynomial series | User-selectable, modern instruments default |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Q.** A pharmaceutical FT-IR instrument has a maximum optical path difference of $\delta_{max} = 1.6 \text{ cm}$. The mirror moves at $v_m = 0.32 \text{ cm/s}$.

**(a)** Calculate the spectral resolution.

**(b)** Calculate the time for one full scan.

**(c)** If 64 scans are co-added, by what factor does S/N improve?

**Solution (a):**

$$\Delta \tilde{\nu} = \frac{1}{\delta_{max}} = \frac{1}{1.6 \text{ cm}} = 0.625 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$t = \frac{\delta_{max}}{v_m} = \frac{1.6 \text{ cm}}{0.32 \text{ cm/s}} = 5.0 \text{ s}$$

**Solution (c):**

$$\frac{S/N_{co\text{-}added}}{S/N_{single}} = \sqrt{N_{scans}} = \sqrt{64} = 8$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Michelson Interferometer — Optical Path Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[POLYCHROMATIC SOURCE\nGLOBAR] --> bsMain((BEAMSPLITTER\nGe on KBr))
    heNe[HeNe LASER\n632.8 nm] --> bsRef((REF BEAMSPLITTER))
    bsRef --> detRef[LASER REFERENCE\nDETECTOR]

    bsMain --> fixed[FIXED MIRROR\nAl coated]
    bsMain --> moving[MOVING MIRROR\nPrecision carriage]
    fixed --> bsMain
    moving --> bsMain
    bsMain --> sample[SAMPLE COMPARTMENT\nKBr pellet / ATR crystal]
    sample --> detMain[IR DETECTOR\nDTGS or MCT]

    moving -.->|optical path\ndifference delta| moving
    detRef -->|trigger pulses|sampler[DIGITAL SAMPLER\nADC + FFT PROCESSOR]
    detMain --> sampler
    sampler -->|B nu spectrum|DISPLAY[SPECTRUM DISPLAY\nPC MONITOR]
```

## 4.2 Signal Processing Chain — Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Analog Interferogram\nI delta detector signal] --> B[Preamplifier]
    B --> C[Anti-aliasing\nLow-Pass Filter]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital\nConverter ADC]
    D --> E[He-Ne Triggered\nSampling at Zero Crossings]
    E --> F[Subtract DC Bias\nI0]
    F --> G[Apply Apodization\nFunction A delta]
    G --> H[Phase Correction\nMertz Form]
    H --> I[Fast Fourier Transform\nCooley Tukey FFT]
    I --> J[Ratio against\nBackground Spectrum]
    J --> K[Absorbance / Transmittance\nSpectrum A nu]
    K --> L[Library Search\nor Quantification]
```

## 4.3 Component–Function Mapping Table

| Diagram Block | Physical Component | Engineering Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| POLYCHROMATIC SOURCE | Globar / Nernst glower | $T \approx 1500 \text{ K}$ |
| BEAMSPLITTER | Ge film on KBr | $50:50$ split, $4000 \text{ to } 400 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ |
| FIXED MIRROR | Flat Al-coated | $\lambda/20$ surface flatness |
| MOVING MIRROR | Pneumatic air bearing | Velocity stability $\pm 0.1\%$ |
| IR DETECTOR | DTGS pyroelectric | $D^* \approx 10^{8} \text{ cm Hz}^{1/2}/\text{W}$ |
| MCT DETECTOR (alt.) | HgCdTe photovoltaic, liquid N₂ | $D^* \approx 10^{10}$ (10× better) |
| He-Ne LASER | Internal reference | $\lambda = 632.8 \text{ nm}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **ATR (Attenuated Total Reflection)** accessory is the modern pharmaceutical workhorse — the sample is pressed against a diamond or ZnSe crystal, eliminating KBr pellet preparation. The principle relies on the *evanescent wave* penetrating a few microns into the sample.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Fourier Transform Spectroscopy. List any two advantages it has over dispersive spectroscopy.**

**Model Answer:**

Fourier Transform Spectroscopy is a non-dispersive technique in which the spectrum of a sample is obtained by recording the interferogram (intensity versus optical path difference) using a Michelson interferometer, and then computing the Fourier Transform of the interferogram to obtain the spectrum as a function of frequency or wavenumber.

*Two advantages:*

1. **Fellgett (multiplex) advantage:** All spectral elements are measured simultaneously, leading to a $\sqrt{M}$ improvement in S/N for a given total scan time, where $M$ is the number of resolution elements.
2. **Connes' advantage (wavelength precision):** The internal He-Ne laser provides a built-in wavelength standard, giving an absolute wavenumber accuracy better than $0.01 \text{ cm}^{-1}$, far superior to dispersive instruments.

*(Any two of Fellgett, Connes, Jacquinot accepted. 3 marks = 1 definition + 1 mark × 2 advantages.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is an interferogram? State the relationship between the spectral resolution and the maximum optical path difference.**

**Model Answer:**

An **interferogram** is a plot of the detector signal intensity $I(\delta)$ as a function of the optical path difference $\delta$ between the two arms of a Michelson interferometer. It is the Fourier transform of the source spectrum, and its central bright fringe at $\delta = 0$ (the centreburst) contains the integrated intensity of all frequencies.

The relationship between spectral resolution and maximum optical path difference is:

$$\Delta \tilde{\nu} = \frac{1}{\delta_{max}}$$

Thus, to obtain a resolution of $1 \text{ cm}^{-1}$, the mirror must traverse a total OPD of $1 \text{ cm}$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question B (A) [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Michelson interferometer used in FT-IR spectroscopy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the mathematical expression for the interferogram produced by a polychromatic source and explain how the spectrum is recovered using the Fourier Transform. State and justify the formula for spectral resolution. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction:**

A Michelson interferometer consists of four essential optical components arranged as in the schematic (refer to SECTION 4.1):

1. **Polychromatic source** — typically a Globar (SiC rod heated to $\approx 1500 \text{ K}$) for the mid-IR region.
2. **Beamsplitter** — a thin film of germanium deposited on a KBr substrate, which reflects 50% and transmits 50% of the incident beam.
3. **Two plane mirrors** — one fixed, one movable along a precision air-bearing track driven by a servo motor.
4. **Detector** — DTGS (deuterated triglycine sulfate) pyroelectric detector for routine work; MCT (mercury cadmium telluride) cooled with liquid nitrogen for high-sensitivity work.
5. **He-Ne laser** — provides the internal reference for mirror position via its own interferogram.

**Working:**

The source beam strikes the beamsplitter at $45^{\circ}$. Half is reflected to the fixed mirror, half is transmitted to the moving mirror. Both beams return to the beamsplitter, recombine, and proceed to the sample and detector. The **optical path difference (OPD)** $\delta$ between the two recombining beams is $\delta = 2x$, where $x$ is the displacement of the moving mirror. As the mirror scans, the relative phase of the two beams changes continuously. Constructive interference occurs when $\delta = n\lambda$, destructive interference when $\delta = (n + 1/2)\lambda$. The detector records the resulting intensity modulation — this signal vs. $\delta$ is the **interferogram**.

The He-Ne laser's interference fringes serve as a precise clock for sampling the IR interferogram, triggering the ADC at known OPD intervals determined by the laser's wavelength (632.8 nm). This is what gives FT-IR its exceptional wavenumber precision (Connes' advantage).

*(7 marks: 3 marks construction + 4 marks working/diagram description.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation:**

Consider a monochromatic component of frequency $\nu_0$. The recombined field at the detector has intensity:

$$I(\delta) = B(\nu_0) \bigl[1 + \cos(2\pi \nu_0 \delta / c)\bigr]$$

For a polychromatic source with spectral power density $B(\nu)$:

$$I(\delta) = \int_{0}^{\infty} B(\nu) \bigl[1 + \cos(2\pi \nu \delta / c)\bigr] \, d\nu$$

Separating the constant (DC) and varying (AC) parts:

$$I_{AC}(\delta) = \int_{0}^{\infty} B(\nu) \cos(2\pi \nu \delta / c) \, d\nu$$

This is the **forward Fourier cosine transform**. By Fourier inversion:

$$B(\nu) = 2 \int_{0}^{\infty} I_{AC}(\delta) \cos(2\pi \nu \delta / c) \, d\delta$$

In practice, wavenumber $\tilde{\nu} = \nu / c$ is used:

$$B(\tilde{\nu}) = 2 \int_{0}^{\infty} I_{AC}(\delta) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta) \, d\delta$$

**Resolution:**

In a real instrument, the integration is truncated at a finite $\delta_{max}$:

$$B_{obs}(\tilde{\nu}) = 2 \int_{0}^{\delta_{max}} I_{AC}(\delta) \cos(2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta) \, d\delta$$

This is the convolution of the true spectrum with the FT of the boxcar truncation, which is a **sinc function**. The first zero of $\text{sinc}(\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta_{max})$ is at $\tilde{\nu} = 1/\delta_{max}$. This defines the spectral resolution:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta \tilde{\nu} = \frac{1}{\delta_{max}}\;}$$

**Justification:** A larger $\delta_{max}$ provides finer OPD sampling, allowing the cosine transform to distinguish closer-spaced frequency components. Doubling $\delta_{max}$ halves the resolution width.

*(7 marks: 3 derivation + 2 transform statement + 2 resolution formula with justification.)*

---

### Question B (B) — Alternative Choice [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the Fellgett, Connes, and Jacquinot advantages of FT spectroscopy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the pharmaceutical applications of FT-IR spectroscopy, with suitable examples. Explain the role of ATR accessory in modern FT-IR. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Fellgett's (Multiplex) Advantage:**

In a dispersive spectrometer, only one narrow spectral band reaches the detector at any instant; the spectrum is built up sequentially. In an FT spectrometer, the entire spectral bandwidth reaches the detector simultaneously as a modulated interferogram. If the measurement is **detector-noise limited** and the total acquisition time is fixed, the S/N of the recovered spectrum is improved by a factor of $\sqrt{M}$, where $M$ is the number of resolution elements. Equivalently, the same S/N is obtained in $1/M$ the time.

**Connes' Advantage (Wavelength Precision):**

The internal He-Ne laser generates a reference interferogram whose zero-crossings occur every $\lambda_{He-Ne}/2 = 316.4 \text{ nm}$. These trigger the sampling of the IR interferogram at precisely known OPD intervals. Because $\lambda_{He-Ne}$ is a primary atomic standard, the absolute wavenumber scale of the FT-IR is reproducible to better than $0.01 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ without external calibration. This makes FT-IR ideal for gas-phase analysis and spectral library matching.

**Jacquinot's (Throughput) Advantage:**

The interferometer accepts radiation through a circular aperture (large *étendue*), whereas a dispersive spectrometer requires narrow entrance and exit slits to maintain resolution. Since radiant throughput $L = A \Omega$ (area × solid angle) is much greater for the interferometer, more signal reaches the detector. For a given resolution and S/N, FT-IR needs far less source power, or delivers much faster measurements.

*(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3 — Connes usually requires more detail for full marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Pharmaceutical Applications of FT-IR:**

1. **Identification of APIs and excipients** — Pharmacopeial identity tests (USP <854>, IP, EP) rely on comparing the sample FT-IR spectrum to a reference spectrum in the spectrum library; a match score $> 0.95$ confirms identity.
2. **Polymorph screening** — Different crystal forms of an API (e.g., *carbamazepine* Form I vs Form III, or *ritonavir* Form I vs Form II) show distinct vibrational fingerprints, especially in the C=O, N–H, and C–H bending regions. FT-IR is the most rapid tool to confirm polymorphic form.
3. **Drug–excipient compatibility** — Shifts in carbonyl or amine bands of the API in a physical mixture indicate hydrogen-bonding or Maillard reactions.
4. **Quantitative analysis** — Using Beer–Lambert law and PLS/PCR chemometrics, FT-IR can quantify API content in tablets to within $\pm 1\%$.
5. **PAT (Process Analytical Technology)** — FT-NIR probes mounted on reactors and tablet presses enable real-time monitoring of blending, granulation, and drying end-points.
6. **Counterfeit detection** — Rapid comparison of suspect vs authentic dosage forms by ATR-FT-IR.

**ATR Accessory (Attenuated Total Reflection):**

In ATR, the sample is placed in optical contact with a high-refractive-index crystal (diamond, ZnSe, or Ge). The IR beam undergoes total internal reflection at the crystal–sample interface. At each reflection, an **evanescent wave** penetrates a depth $d_p$ into the sample:

$$d_p = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi n_1 \sqrt{\sin^2\theta - (n_2/n_1)^2}}$$

where $n_1$ is the crystal RI, $n_2$ the sample RI, $\theta$ the angle of incidence, and $\lambda$ the wavelength. Typically $d_p \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 2 \text{ }\mu\text{m}$. The attenuated beam carries the absorption spectrum of a thin sample layer. **Advantages over traditional KBr pellets:**

- No sample preparation (no grinding, no pressure risk for polymorphs).
- Non-destructive; solids, liquids, gels, pastes all measurable.
- Excellent for *hydrate* analysis (water bands are clearly visible).
- Eliminates scattering artefacts common in pellet spectra.

*(7 marks: 4 applications + 3 ATR explanation.)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** when writing the inverse Fourier transform for one-sided interferograms. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for this.
> 2. **Confusing $\delta$ (optical path difference) with $x$ (mirror displacement).** Always state $\delta = 2x$ explicitly.
> 3. **Writing "Fellgett advantage" without specifying the noise regime** in which it applies (detector-noise limited). Examiners expect the caveat.
> 4. **Mixing up wavenumber $\tilde{\nu}$ and frequency $\nu$ units** in the cosine argument. In FT-IR literature, the convention is $2\pi \tilde{\nu} \delta$ (not $2\pi \nu \delta / c$).
> 5. **Skipping the role of the He-Ne laser.** Even a 1-line mention of "internal He-Ne reference for wavenumber calibration" earns the Connes' advantage mark.
> 6. **In FT-NMR contexts,** confusing *Free Induction Decay* (FID, time-domain) with the interferogram. FIDs and interferograms are mathematically similar but physically distinct — be careful.
> 7. **Resolution formula:** must be written as $\Delta \tilde{\nu} = 1/\delta_{max}$, *not* $1/x_{max}$. The OPD is twice the mirror travel.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **FTS = interferogram → Fourier Transform → spectrum.** The spectrum is the FT of the interferogram.
- **Michelson interferometer** components: source → beamsplitter → fixed mirror + moving mirror → beamsplitter → sample → detector. Plus He-Ne laser for reference.
- **Interferogram** $I(\delta)$ vs **spectrum** $B(\tilde{\nu})$: Fourier-cosine transform pair (cosine, not complex, because $I$ is real and even).
- **Resolution:** $\Delta \tilde{\nu} = 1/\delta_{max}$. Higher resolution = longer mirror travel.
- **S/N improvement** with co-added scans: factor of $\sqrt{N}$.
- **Three advantages:** Fellgett (multiplex, $\sqrt{M}$), Connes (wavelength, He-Ne reference), Jacquinot (throughput, large aperture).
- **Apodization** removes sinc side-lobes; trades resolution for cleaner lineshape. Happ–Genzel is common.
- **FT-IR range:** $4000 \text{ to } 400 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ (mid-IR); FT-NIR $12800 \text{ to } 4000 \text{ cm}^{-1}$.
- **Pharmaceutical uses:** polymorph ID, excipient compatibility, PAT, counterfeit detection, quantification.
- **ATR accessory** uses evanescent wave on diamond/ZnSe crystal — no sample prep, ideal for solids and polymorphs.
- **Phase correction (Mertz method)** is needed because the digitised interferogram is not perfectly symmetric about $\delta = 0$.
- **FFT algorithm (Cooley–Tukey, 1965)** is what makes FTS computationally feasible; an $N$-point spectrum is computed in $N \log_2 N$ operations.
- **No slits** in FT instruments — this is the throughput advantage and the reason FT-NIR fibre probes can be remote.
- **Constant term** in interferogram carries no spectral information; only the AC cosine term is Fourier-transformed.
- **FT vs Dispersive:** FT is faster, more sensitive, more accurate, and the modern standard. Dispersive IR is now historical.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chromatography Principle and its Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT751 - DRUGS AND PHARMACEUTICALS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chromatography Principle and its Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Chromatography** is a versatile physical method of separation in which the components of a mixture are separated by their distribution between two immiscible phases — a **Stationary Phase** (which remains fixed) and a **Mobile Phase** (which moves over or through the stationary phase). The differential affinity of each analyte for these two phases governs the rate of migration, leading to spatial or temporal separation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — Stationary Phase:** A solid, gel, or liquid immobilized on a solid support (e.g., silica gel, alumina, cellulose, C18 bonded phase).
>
> **Key Term — Mobile Phase:** A liquid or gas that percolates through or along the stationary phase, carrying the analytes with it (e.g., hexane, ethyl acetate, acetonitrile, helium, nitrogen).

The term *chromatography* was coined by the Russian-Italian botanist **Mikhail Tswett** in **1906** to describe his separation of plant pigments (chlorophylls and xanthophylls) on a calcium carbonate column — literally meaning *“colour writing”* (Greek: *chroma* = colour, *graphein* = to write).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Marathon Runners on a Slippery Track"**
>
> Imagine a group of marathon runners crossing a track that has *sticky mud patches* of varying sizes. The runners who get stuck more (high affinity for the stationary phase) lag behind, while the runners who stay on dry, clean track (high affinity for the mobile phase) sprint ahead. By the finish line, the original crowded group is now spread out into a clear order — *separated*.
>
> In chromatography:
> - **Mobile phase** = the clean running track (carries everything forward).
> - **Stationary phase** = the sticky mud patches (retards compounds based on affinity).
> - **Compounds** = the marathon runners.
> - **Differential migration** = separation.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Rf Range:** $0 \leq R_f \leq 1$ (dimensionless).
- **Standard Plate Height (HETP):** typically **0.01 – 0.1 mm** in modern HPLC columns.
- **Typical Column Lengths:** 50 – 250 mm (HPLC); 1 – 30 m (GC).
- **Standard Particle Size:** 3 – 10 µm (modern HPLC stationary phases).
- **Detection Wavelength (UV-Vis):** 190 – 800 nm commonly used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must know — (a) the basic principle, (b) classification based on physical state of mobile phase and shape of the chromatographic bed, (c) detailed working of Paper, TLC, Column, HPLC, and GC, and (d) pharmaceutical applications.

## 1.4 Fundamental Quantities — The Retention Factor (R_f)

The **Retention Factor (R_f)** is the most important metric in planar chromatography (Paper and TLC):

$$R_f = \frac{\text{Distance travelled by the solute (analyte) from the origin}}{\text{Distance travelled by the mobile phase (solvent front) from the origin}}$$

By KTU convention, $R_f$ values are reported to **two decimal places** and are used as fingerprints for **drug identification and purity assessment**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Principle — "The Distribution Equilibrium"

The theoretical foundation of chromatography rests on the **Partition (Distribution) Coefficient**, denoted $K$ (or $k_D$). For a solute distributing between two immiscible phases:

$$K = \frac{C_s}{C_m}$$

where $C_s$ is the equilibrium concentration of the solute in the **stationary phase** and $C_m$ is the equilibrium concentration in the **mobile phase**.

- If $K \gg 1$ → solute strongly retained → **slow migration** (high affinity for stationary phase).
- If $K \ll 1$ → solute weakly retained → **fast migration** (high affinity for mobile phase).
- If $K \approx 0$ → solute elutes with the solvent front (void volume).
- If $K \to \infty$ → solute is permanently adsorbed (never elutes).

## 2.2 Retention Time (t_R) and Retention Volume (V_R)

In column chromatography (HPLC, GC):

$$t_R = t_M \, (1 + k')$$

$$V_R = V_M \, (1 + k')$$

where:
- $t_M$ = **Dead time / Void time** (time for an unretained compound to pass through).
- $V_M$ = **Dead volume / Void volume** (volume of mobile phase in the column).
- $k'$ = **Capacity factor (Retention factor)**.

The **Capacity Factor (k')** is the most quoted metric in HPLC:

$$k' = \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M} = \frac{K \cdot m_s}{V_M}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is k' important?** An ideal $k'$ value for analytical separations lies in the range **2 ≤ k' ≤ 10**. Below 2 → poor resolution; above 10 → excessively long run times.

## 2.3 Separation Factor (Selectivity, α)

$$\alpha = \frac{k'_2}{k'_1} = \frac{K_2}{K_1}$$

where $k'_2 > k'_1$. A value of **α ≥ 1.1** is the minimum for practical separation of two analytes.

## 2.4 Resolution (R_s) — The "Purity" Metric

$$R_s = \frac{2 \cdot (t_{R_2} - t_{R_1})}{W_1 + W_2}$$

where $W_1$ and $W_2$ are the baseline widths of the two peaks. 
- $R_s \geq 1.5$ → **baseline separation** (preferred).
- $R_s = 1.0$ → peaks just touch (~3% overlap).
- $R_s < 1.0$ → unacceptable separation.

**Purnell Equation** (relates resolution to fundamental parameters):

$$R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{(\alpha - 1)}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'}{1 + k'}$$

where $N$ = number of theoretical plates.

## 2.5 Efficiency — Number of Theoretical Plates (N)

$$N = 16 \cdot \left(\frac{t_R}{W}\right)^2 = 5.54 \cdot \left(\frac{t_R}{W_{1/2}}\right)^2$$

**Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate (HETP)**:

$$HETP = \frac{L}{N}$$

where $L$ = length of the column.

**Van Deemter Equation** (relationship between HETP and linear velocity $u$):

$$HETP = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u$$

- $A$ = Eddy diffusion term (packing irregularity).
- $B/u$ = Longitudinal diffusion term.
- $C \cdot u$ = Mass transfer resistance term.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | $K = C_s / C_m$ | Partition coefficient | $0 < K < \infty$ |
| 2 | $R_f = d_s / d_m$ | Retention factor (planar) | $0 \leq R_f \leq 1$ |
| 3 | $k' = (t_R - t_M)/t_M$ | Capacity factor | $2 \leq k' \leq 10$ |
| 4 | $\alpha = k'_2 / k'_1$ | Selectivity / Separation factor | $\alpha \geq 1.1$ |
| 5 | $R_s = 2(t_{R_2} - t_{R_1})/(W_1 + W_2)$ | Resolution | $R_s \geq 1.5$ |
| 6 | $N = 16(t_R/W)^2$ | Number of theoretical plates | $10^3 - 10^6$ |
| 7 | $HETP = L/N$ | Plate height | $0.001 - 0.1$ cm |
| 8 | $HETP = A + B/u + Cu$ | Van Deemter equation | Optimization curve |
| 9 | $R_s = (\sqrt{N}/4) \cdot ((\alpha - 1)/\alpha) \cdot (k'/(1+k'))$ | Purnell equation | Comprehensive resolution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Chromatography is the **workhorse analytical technique** in pharmaceutical QC/QA, impurity profiling, forensic toxicology, environmental analysis, and bioequivalence studies. **HPLC and GC together account for ~75% of all pharmaceutical instrumental analysis.**

## 2.7 Classification of Chromatography (KTU Mandate)

### A. Based on Shape of Chromatographic Bed
1. **Planar Chromatography** — Paper Chromatography (PC), Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC).
2. **Column Chromatography** — Packed Column, Open Tubular Column.

### B. Based on Physical State of Mobile Phase
1. **Liquid Chromatography (LC)** — Paper, TLC, Column, HPLC.
2. **Gas Chromatography (GC)** — GLC, GSC.

### C. Based on Separation Mechanism
1. **Adsorption Chromatography** — Solid stationary phase (silica, alumina).
2. **Partition Chromatography** — Liquid on inert support.
3. **Ion-Exchange Chromatography** — Resin-based exchangers.
4. **Size-Exclusion (Gel Filtration) Chromatography** — Molecular sieves.
5. **Affinity Chromatography** — Ligand-substrate specific binding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Tip:** KTU frequently asks a 7-mark question on the "Classification of chromatographic techniques with examples" — memorize the three classification axes above.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Migration Equation

We derive the relationship between the **R_f value** (planar chromatography) and the **capacity factor k'** (column chromatography). This is a high-yield derivation for KTU problems.

### Starting Assumption — The Partition Equilibrium

For a solute distributing between a thin layer of stationary phase (volume $V_s$ per unit length) and mobile phase (volume $V_m$ per unit length), the fraction of time spent by the solute in the mobile phase determines its velocity.

Let:
- $f_m$ = fraction of solute molecules in mobile phase.
- $f_s = 1 - f_m$ = fraction in stationary phase.

By the definition of the partition coefficient:

$$K = \frac{C_s}{C_m} = \frac{n_s / V_s}{n_m / V_m}$$

Rearranging:

$$K = \frac{n_s}{n_m} \cdot \frac{V_m}{V_s}$$

$$\frac{n_s}{n_m} = K \cdot \frac{V_s}{V_m}$$

The total number of molecules $n_{total} = n_m + n_s$:

$$f_m = \frac{n_m}{n_{total}} = \frac{n_m}{n_m + n_s} = \frac{1}{1 + (n_s/n_m)}$$

Substituting:

$$f_m = \frac{1}{1 + K \cdot (V_s / V_m)} = \frac{V_m}{V_m + K \cdot V_s}$$

The velocity of the solute band, $v_{solute}$, is the mobile phase velocity $v_{m}$ multiplied by the fraction of time in the mobile phase:

$$v_{solute} = f_m \cdot v_m = \frac{V_m}{V_m + K \cdot V_s} \cdot v_m$$

### Connecting to R_f

In planar chromatography, the distance travelled by the solute = (solute velocity) × (time), and the distance travelled by the mobile phase front = $v_m \times t$. Taking the ratio:

$$R_f = \frac{v_{solute} \cdot t}{v_m \cdot t} = f_m = \frac{1}{1 + K \cdot (V_s / V_m)}$$

**Final Migration Equation:**

$$R_f = \frac{1}{1 + K \cdot (V_s / V_m)} = \frac{1}{1 + k'}$$

This elegant result shows that **R_f and k' are reciprocally related** — a high $R_f$ means a low $k'$, and vice versa.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — R_f Calculation (TLC)

> **Problem:** In a TLC plate, the solvent front travelled **8.4 cm** and an analyte spot travelled **3.6 cm** from the origin. Calculate the R_f value.

**Step 1:** Identify the given data.

$$d_s = 3.6 \text{ cm}, \quad d_m = 8.4 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the R_f formula.

$$R_f = \frac{d_s}{d_m} = \frac{3.6}{8.4}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate.

$$R_f = 0.4286 \approx 0.43$$

**Step 4:** Interpret. $R_f = 0.43$ lies in the optimal range (0.3 – 0.7) for a well-resolved TLC spot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Note:** Always quote R_f to **two decimal places** and never as a percentage in KTU answer scripts.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Capacity Factor from Retention Data

> **Problem:** In an HPLC run, the dead time $t_M = 1.8$ min and the retention time of the analyte $t_R = 7.2$ min. Calculate (a) the capacity factor $k'$, and (b) the void volume $V_M$ if the mobile phase flow rate is 1.2 mL/min.

**Step 1:** Compute k'.

$$k' = \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M} = \frac{7.2 - 1.8}{1.8} = \frac{5.4}{1.8} = 3.0$$

**Step 2:** Compute void volume $V_M$.

$$V_M = t_M \times F = 1.8 \text{ min} \times 1.2 \text{ mL/min} = 2.16 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 3:** Validate. $k' = 3.0$ is in the ideal range (2 – 10) for HPLC separations.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Resolution Calculation

> **Problem:** Two drug peaks elute at $t_{R_1} = 5.4$ min and $t_{R_2} = 6.2$ min with baseline widths $W_1 = 0.4$ min and $W_2 = 0.5$ min. Calculate the resolution.

**Step 1:** Apply the resolution formula.

$$R_s = \frac{2 \cdot (t_{R_2} - t_{R_1})}{W_1 + W_2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$R_s = \frac{2 \cdot (6.2 - 5.4)}{0.4 + 0.5} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.8}{0.9} = \frac{1.6}{0.9}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate.

$$R_s = 1.78$$

**Step 4:** Interpret. $R_s = 1.78 > 1.5$ → **baseline separation achieved** — quantitation is reliable.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Chromatographic Techniques

| Parameter | Paper Chrom. | TLC | Column LC | HPLC | GC |
|-----------|--------------|-----|-----------|------|----|
| **Mobile phase** | Liquid | Liquid | Liquid | Liquid | Gas |
| **Stationary phase** | Paper cellulose | Silica/Alumina on plate | Silica in glass column | Bonded silica (C18, C8) | Liquid film on solid / solid |
| **Typical plate number (N)** | < 100 | 100 – 600 | 50 – 200 | $10^3 - 10^5$ | $10^4 - 10^6$ |
| **Sample type** | Polar, non-volatile | Wide range | Wide range | Thermally labile drugs | Volatile, thermally stable |
| **Analysis time** | Hours | 10 – 60 min | 30 min – 2 h | 5 – 30 min | 5 – 60 min |
| **Detection** | Visual / spray reagent | UV / spray reagent | UV / fraction collector | UV, RI, MS, FLD, ELSD | FID, TCD, MS |
| **Cost** | Very low | Low | Low | High | High |
| **Quantitation capability** | Semi-quantitative | Semi-quantitative | Quantitative | Highly quantitative | Highly quantitative |
| **Pharmaceutical use** | Identification, purity | Identity, purity check | Purification (prep scale) | QC, assay, impurity | Residual solvents, APIs |

## 3.6 Detailed Working — Five Major Types (KTU Mandate)

### 3.6.1 Paper Chromatography (PC)

**Principle:** Partition chromatography where the water trapped in the cellulose fibres of filter paper acts as the stationary phase, and the organic solvent (mobile phase) travels up the paper by capillary action.

**Procedure:**
1. Spot the sample near one end of a paper strip.
2. Place the strip in a closed chamber with the mobile phase (below the spot).
3. Allow the solvent to ascend by capillary action.
4. Dry and visualize using UV light or staining reagents (ninhydrin for amino acids, iodine vapour for lipids).

**Applications:** Separation of amino acids, sugars, plant pigments, drug metabolites.

### 3.6.2 Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC)

**Principle:** Adsorption chromatography on a thin layer (250 µm) of adsorbent (silica gel G) coated on a glass or aluminium plate.

**Procedure:**
1. Prepare a slurry of silica gel with binder (gypsum) and spread on plates.
2. Dry, activate at 110°C for 30 min.
3. Spot the sample using a capillary.
4. Develop in a chamber with appropriate mobile phase.
5. Visualize under UV (254 nm / 365 nm) or with stains (iodine, vanillin, phosphomolybdic acid).

**Advantages over PC:** Faster, sharper separations, better R_f reproducibility, smaller sample size, higher sensitivity.

**Applications:** Drug purity assessment, monitoring reaction progress, R_f matching with reference standards for **pharmacopoeial identification tests (IP, USP, BP)**.

### 3.6.3 Column Chromatography

**Principle:** A glass column packed with solid stationary phase (silica/alumina) is eluted with a mobile phase (gradient of increasing polarity).

**Procedure:**
1. Pack the column with a slurry of stationary phase.
2. Load the sample dissolved in a small volume of mobile phase.
3. Elute with increasing polarity (e.g., hexane → ethyl acetate → methanol).
4. Collect fractions and analyse by TLC or spectroscopy.

**Applications:** Preparative-scale purification of natural products, isolation of APIs from crude extracts.

### 3.6.4 High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC)

**Principle:** Same as column LC but with **high pressure (up to 400 bar)** pumping, small particle size stationary phase (3 – 5 µm), and on-line detectors.

**Components:** Solvent reservoir → Degasser → Pump → Injector → Column → Detector → Data system.

**Modes:** Reverse phase (RP-HPLC, C18 column, water/acetonitrile mobile phase — most common), Normal phase, Ion-exchange, Size-exclusion, Chiral.

**Detector Types:**
- **UV-Vis detector** — most common, fixed or variable wavelength.
- **Photodiode Array (PDA/DAD)** — full spectrum acquisition.
- **Refractive Index (RI)** — universal but less sensitive.
- **Fluorescence (FLD)** — highly sensitive for fluorescent drugs.
- **Evaporative Light Scattering (ELSD)** — for non-chromophoric compounds.

**Applications:** Assay of drug substances, content uniformity, dissolution testing, impurity profiling (degradation products, genotoxic impurities), stability-indicating assays.

### 3.6.5 Gas Chromatography (GC)

**Principle:** The mobile phase is an inert carrier gas (helium, nitrogen, hydrogen). The sample is vaporized at the injector and partitioned between the gas and the stationary phase (a high-boiling liquid film on an inert solid support, or bonded to the wall of a capillary tube).

**Components:** Carrier gas → Injector (200 – 300°C) → Column (in oven) → Detector → Recorder.

**Detectors:** Flame Ionization Detector (FID), Thermal Conductivity Detector (TCD), Electron Capture Detector (ECD), Mass Spectrometer (MS).

**Applications:** Assay of volatile APIs, residual solvents (per ICH Q3C), fatty acid methyl esters, alcohol content, essential oils.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** HPLC is used for **~80% of pharmaceutical analysis** because most drug molecules are **non-volatile, thermally labile, and polar** — exactly the class of compounds for which GC is unsuitable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Generic Chromatographic Separation Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sampleA["Sample Mixture A and B"] --> injector["Injector or Spotting"]
    injector --> column1["Stationary Phase Fixed Bed"]
    column1 --> mob1["Mobile Phase Flows Through"]
```
